    钦定四库全书　　　　　子部十二
    　　朝野佥载　　　　　　　小説家一【杂事之属】提要
    　　【臣】等谨案朝野佥载六卷旧本题唐张鷟撰鷟有龙筋鳯髓判已着録此书新唐书艺文志作三十卷宋史艺文志作佥载二十卷又佥载补遗三卷文献通考则但有佥载遗三卷此本六卷防考诸书皆不合晁公武读书志又谓其分三十五门而今本乃逐条聫缀不分门目亦与晁氏所纪不同又莫休符桂林风土记载鷟在开元中姚崇诬其奉使江南受遗赐死其子上表请代减死流岭南数年起为长史而卒计其时尚在天寳之前而书中有寳厯元年资阳石走事寳厯乃敬宗年号又有孟宏微对宣宗 [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 钦定四库全书 - Imperially designated Four Treasuries, a monumental collection of Chinese literature and scholarship.
    • 子部十二 - Subdivision Twelve, a specific classification within the Chinese library system.
    • 小说家类一【杂事之属】 - First category of the Miscellaneous section in the Novels category, indicating the classification of the text within the library system.
    • 提要 - Abstract or summary, typically found at the beginning of a book or chapter.
    • 臣等谨案 - We, the subjects, respectfully present, a formal way to introduce the abstract.
    • 朝野佥载 - Collective Records of the Court and the People, a historical record of anecdotes and events from the Tang dynasty.
    • 张鷟 - Zhang Zuo, a notable figure in the Tang dynasty, known for his literary works.
    • 龙筋凤髓判 - Dragon Sinews and Phoenix Marrow Judgment, another work by Zhang Zuo, known for its literary and legal content.
    • 新唐书艺文志 - Literary Records of the New History of the Tang Dynasty, a historical record detailing the literary works of the Tang dynasty.
    • 宋史艺文志 - Literary Records of the History of Song, a historical record detailing the literary works of the Song dynasty.
    • 文献通考 - Comprehensive Examination of Literature and History, a comprehensive historical and cultural record.
    • 开元中 - During the Kaiyuan period, a significant period in the Tang dynasty, marked by cultural and economic prosperity.
    • 姚崇 - Yao Chong, a prominent statesman and chancellor during the Tang dynasty.
    • 奉使江南 - Sent as an envoy to Jiangnan, a diplomatic mission to the southern regions of China.
    • 受遗赐死 - Accused of accepting a legacy and sentenced to death, a serious accusation.
    • 上表请代 - Submitted a petition to be substituted, a request to take someone else's place in a punishment.
    • 减死流岭南 - Reduced to exile to Lingnan instead of execution, a lesser punishment.
    • 数年起为长史 - After several years, appointed as the Chief Clerk, a promotion.
    • 宝历元年 - The first year of the Baoli era, a specific year in the reign of Emperor Jingzong of Tang.
    • 资阳石走 - The stone of Ziyang moved, a reference to a historical event.
    • 宝历 - Baoli, the era name of Emperor Jingzong of Tang.
    • 资阳 - Ziyang, a place name.
    • 孟宏微对宣宗 - Meng Hongwei's response to Emperor Xuanzong, a historical reference.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 钦定四库全书 - A monumental collection of Chinese literature and scholarship.
    • 子部十二 - A specific classification within the Chinese library system.
    • 小说家类一【杂事之属】 - A category for miscellaneous historical and anecdotal records.
    • 提要 - A common feature in Chinese books, providing a summary or introduction.
    • 臣等谨案 - A formal way to introduce a summary or abstract in historical texts.
    • 朝野佥载 - A historical record of anecdotes and events from the Tang dynasty.
    • 张鷟 - A notable figure in the Tang dynasty, known for his literary works.
    • 龙筋凤髓判 - Another work by Zhang Zuo, known for its literary and legal content.
    • 新唐书艺文志 - A historical record detailing the literary works of the Tang dynasty.
    • 宋史艺文志 - A historical record detailing the literary works of the Song dynasty.
    • 文献通考 - A comprehensive historical and cultural record.
    • 开元中 - A significant period in the Tang dynasty, marked by cultural and economic prosperity.
    • 姚崇 - A prominent statesman and chancellor during the Tang dynasty.
    • 奉使江南 - A diplomatic mission to the southern regions of China.
    • 受遗赐死 - A serious accusation of accepting a legacy and being sentenced to death.
    • 上表请代 - A request to take someone else's place in a punishment.
    • 减死流岭南 - A lesser punishment, exile to the southern regions.
    • 数年起为长史 - A promotion to a higher position after several years.
    • 宝历元年 - A specific year in the reign of Emperor Jingzong of Tang.
    • 资阳石走 - A historical event involving the movement of a stone in Ziyang.
    • 宝历 - The era name of Emperor Jingzong of Tang.
    • 资阳 - A place name, Ziyang.
    • 孟宏微对宣宗 - A historical reference to a response given to Emperor Xuanzong.

    Proper Nouns

    • 钦定四库全书 - Imperially designated Four Treasuries
    • 子部十二 - Subdivision Twelve
    • 小说家类一【杂事之属】 - First category of the Miscellaneous section in the Novels category
    • 提要 - Abstract or summary
    • 臣等谨案 - We, the subjects, respectfully present
    • 朝野佥载 - Collective Records of the Court and the People
    • 张鷟 - Zhang Zuo
    • 龙筋凤髓判 - Dragon Sinews and Phoenix Marrow Judgment
    • 新唐书艺文志 - Literary Records of the New History of the Tang Dynasty
    • 宋史艺文志 - Literary Records of the History of Song
    • 文献通考 - Comprehensive Examination of Literature and History

    • 姚崇 - Yao Chong

    • 受遗赐死 - Accused of accepting a legacy and sentenced to death
    • 上表请代 - Submitted a petition to be substituted
    • 减死流岭南 - Reduced to exile to Lingnan instead of execution

    • 宝历元年 - The first year of the Baoli era

    The Four Treasuries, Imperially Designated, Subdivision Twelve
    Collective Records of the Court and the People
    First Category of the Miscellaneous Section in the Novels Category: Abstract

    We, the subjects, respectfully present the Collective Records of the Court and the People in six volumes, originally titled as authored by Zhang Zuo of the Tang dynasty. Zhang Zuo has another work, Dragon Sinews and Phoenix Marrow Judgment, already recorded. This book is listed in the Literary Records of the New History of the Tang Dynasty as thirty volumes, in the Literary Records of the History of Song as twenty volumes, and in the Comprehensive Examination of Literature and History as three supplementary volumes. This edition, however, differs from these records. According to the Reading Records of Zhao Gongwu, it is divided into thirty-five sections, but the current edition is a continuous compilation without sections, differing from Zhao Gongwu's account. Additionally, Mo Xiufu's Records of the Customs and Geography of Guilin mention that during the Kaiyuan period, Yao Chong falsely accused Zhang Zuo of accepting a legacy and sentenced him to death. His son submitted a petition to be substituted, and Zhang Zuo was reduced to exile to Lingnan instead of execution. After several years, he was appointed as the Chief Clerk and died. At that time, it was before the Tianbao era, yet the book contains events from the first year of the Baoli era, when the stone of Ziyang moved. Baoli is the era name of Emperor Jingzong of Tang. There is also a reference to Meng Hongwei's response to Emperor Xuanzong. [1]

    事时代皆不相及案尤袤遂初堂书目亦分朝野佥载及佥载补遗为二书疑佥载乃鷟所作补遗则为后人附益凡阑入中唐后事者皆应为补遗之文而陈振孙所谓书本三十卷此其节畧者当即此本葢尝经宋人摘録合佥载补遗为一删并门已非原书又不知何时析三卷为六卷也其书皆纪唐代故事而于谐噱怪纎悉胪载未免失于杂故洪迈容斋随笔讥其记事屑擿裂且多媟语然耳目所接可据者多故司马光作通鉴亦引用之兼收博采固未尝无稗于见闻也乾隆四十六年十一月恭校上 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 事时代皆不相及 - Literally means 'the times and eras do not correspond,' suggesting that the content and era of the text are not aligned.
    • 耳目所接可据者多 - Means that there are many reliable and verifiable accounts.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 尤袤 - A scholar and poet of the Song Dynasty known for his bibliographic works.
    • 遂初堂 - The name of You Mao's study or library.
    • 朝野佥载 - A historical record compiled during the Tang Dynasty, known for its anecdotes and stories.
    • 张鷟 - A Tang Dynasty scholar and writer, believed to be the author of 'Chao Ye Qian Zai.'
    • 陈振孙 - A scholar of the Song Dynasty known for his bibliographic catalog.
    • 洪迈 - A scholar and writer of the Song Dynasty, known for his 'Yongzai Suibi.'
    • 司马光 - A prominent historian of the Song Dynasty, best known for compiling the 'Zizhi Tongjian.'
    • 乾隆四十六年 - The 46th year of the Qianlong Emperor's reign, corresponding to 1781 CE.

    Proper Nouns

    • 尤袤 - You Mao, a Song Dynasty scholar and poet.
    • 遂初堂 - Suichutang, You Mao's study or library.
    • 朝野佥载 - Chao Ye Qian Zai, a historical record from the Tang Dynasty.
    • 张鷟 - Zhang Zuo, a Tang Dynasty scholar and writer.
    • 陈振孙 - Chen Zhensun, a Song Dynasty scholar.
    • 洪迈 - Hong Mai, a Song Dynasty scholar and writer.
    • 司马光 - Sima Guang, a Song Dynasty historian.
    • 乾隆四十六年 - The 46th year of the Qianlong Emperor's reign (1781 CE).

    The times and eras of the events do not align. In the Suichutang book catalog compiled by You Mao, "Chao Ye Qian Zai" and its supplement are listed as two separate works. It is suspected that "Chao Ye Qian Zai" was written by Zhang Zuo, while the supplement was added by later scholars. All entries from the mid-Tang period and later should be considered part of the supplement. Chen Zhensun mentioned that the original thirty-volume version is a condensed version of the current text. It is believed that the text has been compiled and edited by Song scholars, combining the main text and supplement, and modifying the classification, which differs from the original. It is unclear when the text was divided into six volumes from the original three. The book records Tang dynasty stories, including humorous and strange tales, which makes it somewhat chaotic. Therefore, Hong Mai criticized in his "Yongzai Suibi" that the book's records are fragmented and contain inappropriate language. However, there are many reliable and verifiable accounts. Sima Guang also used this text as a reference in his "Zizhi Tongjian." By collecting and selecting widely, the text has contributed to the expansion of knowledge. The imperial review and publication took place in the eleventh month of the 46th year of the Qianlong era. [2]

    总纂官【臣】纪昀【臣】陆锡熊【臣】孙士毅
    　　总　校　官　【臣】　陆　费　墀

    　　钦定四库全书
    　　朝野佥载卷一
    　　唐　张鷟　撰
    　　贞观年中定州鼓城县人魏全家富母忽然失明问卜者王子贞子贞为卜之曰明年有人从东来青衣者三月一日来疗必愈至时候见一人着青防襦遂邀为设饮食其人曰仆不解医但解作犁耳为主人作之持斧绕舎求犁辕见桑曲枝临井上遂斫下其母两眼焕然见物此曲桑葢井之所致也
    　　周郎中裴珪妾赵氏有美色曽就张璟藏卜年命藏曰夫人目长而慢视准相书猪视者淫妇人目有四白五夫守宅夫人终以奸废宜慎之赵笑而去后果与人奸没入掖庭 [3]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [3]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 總纂官 - Title meaning 'chief compiler' or 'chief editor', indicating a high-ranking official responsible for overseeing the compilation of a text.
    • 總校官 - Title meaning 'chief proofreader' or 'chief reviser', indicating a high-ranking official responsible for reviewing and correcting the text.
    • 欽定 - Term meaning 'imperial decree' or 'royally sanctioned', indicating that the text is officially endorsed by the emperor.
    • 朝野佥载 - Title meaning 'Records of the Court and the World', referring to a historical text documenting events and anecdotes from both the court and the general populace.
    • 贞观年中 - Time phrase meaning 'during the Zhenguan period', referring to the era of Emperor Taizong of Tang (627–649).
    • 定州鼓城县人 - Phrase meaning 'a person from Gu County, Ding Prefecture', providing the geographical and administrative context of the subject.
    • 问卜者 - Phrase meaning 'a fortune-teller' or 'a diviner', referring to someone who predicts the future or interprets omens.
    • 犁耳 - Term meaning 'plow ear', referring to a part of the plow, which is used metaphorically in the text.
    • 曲桑 - Term meaning 'crooked mulberry tree', referring to a specific type of tree mentioned in the text.
    • 盖井之所致也 - Phrase meaning 'caused by the well', indicating a causal relationship.
    • 周郎中裴珪 - Title and name meaning 'Péi Guī, the Langzhong of Zhou', referring to a high-ranking official.
    • 妾赵氏 - Phrase meaning 'mistress Zhao', referring to a concubine.
    • 美色 - Term meaning 'beautiful appearance' or 'good looks', describing a person's physical attractiveness.
    • 卜年命 - Phrase meaning 'predicting one's destiny', referring to the practice of fortune-telling.
    • 猪视者 - Term meaning 'pig-eyed', referring to a specific type of eye shape in traditional Chinese physiognomy.
    • 淫妇人 - Term meaning 'promiscuous woman', describing a woman with a bad reputation for sexual behavior.
    • 四白 - Term meaning 'four whites', referring to the white parts of the eye in traditional Chinese physiognomy.
    • 五夫守宅 - Phrase meaning 'five husbands protecting the home', a traditional belief in Chinese culture.
    • 奸废 - Term meaning 'degradation due to adultery', referring to the consequences of immoral behavior.
    • 慎之 - Phrase meaning 'be cautious', advising someone to be careful.
    • 与人奸 - Phrase meaning 'commit adultery with someone', describing an act of infidelity.
    • 没入掖庭 - Phrase meaning 'sent to the imperial harem', indicating a severe punishment for immoral behavior.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 贞观年中 - Refers to the Zhenguan period (627–649), a golden age of the Tang Dynasty under Emperor Taizong.
    • 定州鼓城县 - Refers to Gu County, a county in Ding Prefecture, providing the geographical and administrative context of the events.
    • 张鷟 - Refers to Zhang Zuo, a prominent Tang Dynasty writer and official known for his literary works.
    • 魏全 - Refers to Wei Quan, a person from Gu County, Ding Prefecture, who is the subject of the first anecdote.
    • 王子贞 - Refers to Wang Zhen, a fortune-teller who provides a diagnosis for Wei Quan's mother.
    • 犁耳 - Refers to a part of the plow, which is used metaphorically in the text.
    • 曲桑 - Refers to a specific type of mulberry tree mentioned in the text.
    • 周郎中裴珪 - Refers to Péi Guī, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 妾赵氏 - Refers to Mistress Zhao, a concubine of Péi Guī.
    • 张璟藏 - Refers to Zhang Jingcang, a fortune-teller who predicts the future of Mistress Zhao.

    Proper Nouns

    • 总纂官 - Chief compiler
    • 总校官 - Chief proofreader
    • 纪昀 - Ji Yun
    • 陆锡熊 - Lu Xixiong
    • 孙士毅 - Sun Shiyi
    • 陆费墀 - Lu Feichi
    • 欽定四库全书 - The Four Treasuries (officially endorsed)
    • 朝野佥载 - Records of the Court and the World
    • 贞观年中 - During the Zhenguan period
    • 定州鼓城县 - Gu County, Ding Prefecture
    • 魏全 - Wei Quan
    • 王子贞 - Wang Zhen
    • 犁耳 - Plow ear
    • 曲桑 - Crooked mulberry tree
    • 周郎中裴珪 - Péi Guī, the Langzhong of Zhou
    • 妾赵氏 - Mistress Zhao
    • 美色 - Beautiful appearance
    • 张璟藏 - Zhang Jingcang
    • 猪视者 - Pig-eyed
    • 淫妇人 - Promiscuous woman
    • 四白 - Four whites
    • 五夫守宅 - Five husbands protecting the home
    • 奸废 - Degradation due to adultery
    • 慎之 - Be cautious
    • 与人奸 - Commit adultery with someone
    • 没入掖庭 - Sent to the imperial harem

    Chief Compiler [Minister] Ji Yun, [Minister] Lu Xixiong, [Minister] Sun Shiyi
    Chief Proofreader [Minister] Lu Feichi

    Imperial Decree: The Four Treasuries
    Records of the Court and the World, Volume One
    By Zhang Zuo of the Tang Dynasty

    During the Zhenguan period, there was a wealthy man named Wei Quan from Gu County, Ding Prefecture. His mother suddenly went blind. He consulted a fortune-teller named Wang Zhen. Wang Zhen divined and said, "Next year, there will be a man wearing blue clothing who will come from the east. On the first day of the third month, he will cure her blindness." As the time arrived, a man wearing a blue jacket appeared. Wei Quan invited him to have a meal. The man said, "I do not know medicine, but I know how to make a plow." He worked for the host and took an axe to search for the plow's beam. He found a crooked mulberry tree near a well and cut it down. Wei Quan's mother's eyes suddenly regained their sight, and she could see clearly again. This was caused by the crooked mulberry tree near the well.

    Péi Guī, the Langzhong of Zhou, had a concubine named Mistress Zhao, who was very beautiful. She once consulted a fortune-teller named Zhang Jingcang about her destiny. Zhang Jingcang said, "Your eyes are long and look downward. According to physiognomy, those with pig-like eyes are promiscuous women. Your eyes have four whites, and according to the Five Husbands Protecting the Home theory, you will eventually be degraded due to adultery. Be cautious." Mistress Zhao laughed and left. Later, she indeed committed adultery and was sent to the imperial harem. [3]

    杜景佺信都人也本名元方垂拱中更为景佺刚直严正进士擢第后为鸾台侍郎平章事时内史李昭徳以刚直下狱景佺廷诤其公清正直则天怒以为面欺左授溱州刺史初任溱州防善筮者于路言其当重入相得三品而不着紫袍至是夏中服紫衫而终
    　　瀛州人安县令张怀礼沧州弓高令晋行忠就蔡微逺卜转式讫谓礼曰公大亲近位至方伯谓忠曰公得京官今年禄尽宜致仕可也二人皆应举怀礼授左补阙后至和复二州刺史行忠授城门郎至秋而卒
    　　开元二年梁州道士梁虚州以九宫推算张鷟云五加年天罡临命一生之大厄以周易筮之遇观之涣主惊恐后风水行上事即散安国观道士李虚不告姓名暗使推之云此人今年身在天牢大辟之罪乃可以免不然病当死无救法果被御史李全交致其罪勅令处尽而刑部尚书李知白左丞张廷珪崔升侍郎程行谋咸请之乃免死配流岭南二道士之言信有征矣 [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 垂拱中 - A time period during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang (684-685 AD).
    • 平章事 - A high-ranking official position, equivalent to a chancellor or prime minister.
    • 廷诤 - To argue or debate in court.
    • 左授 - To demote or transfer to a lower position, often to a distant place.
    • 三品 - A rank in the imperial bureaucracy, indicating a high-level official.
    • 紫袍 - A purple robe, a symbol of high rank and honor.
    • 夏中服紫衫而终 - Wearing a purple shirt in summer and dying, suggesting a premature death.
    • 瀛州 - A prefecture in ancient China, located in modern-day Hebei Province.
    • 安縣令 - The county magistrate of An County.
    • 沧州弓高令 - The county magistrate of Gonggao County in Cang Prefecture.
    • 九宫推算 - A method of divination using the nine palaces of the Luo Shu (a magic square).
    • 五鬼加年 - A term in Chinese astrology indicating a year of bad luck or misfortune.
    • 天罡临命 - A term in Chinese astrology indicating a significant influence on one's fate.
    • 周易筮之 - Divination using the *I Ching* (Book of Changes).
    • 遇观之涣 - Encountering the hexagram *View* (观) and *Scattering* (涣) in a divination.
    • 主惊恐 - Indicating fear or anxiety.
    • 风水行上事 - A term related to geomancy or feng shui, suggesting a favorable or unfavorable situation.
    • 安国观道士 - A Taoist priest at An Guo Temple.
    • 不告姓名 - Not revealing one's name.
    • 暗使推之 - Secretly instructing someone to perform divination.
    • 身在天牢 - Being imprisoned in the heavenly jail, a metaphor for severe punishment.
    • 大辟之罪 - A capital crime.
    • 可免 - Can be avoided.
    • 病当死 - Will die of illness.
    • 无救法 - No remedy.
    • 被御史李全交致其罪 - Accused by the censor Li Quan Jiao.
    • 勅令处尽而刑部尚书李知白左丞张廷珪崔玄升侍郎程行谋咸请之乃免死配流岭南 - Ordered to be executed, but the Minister of Justice Li Zhibai, the Left Censor Zhang Tinggui, Cui Xuansheng, and others petitioned for clemency, and he was spared and exiled to Lingnan.
    • 信有征矣 - Indeed, there is evidence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 杜景佺 - A historical figure, a Tang dynasty official known for his integrity and administrative skills.
    • 信都 - A place name, modern-day Xingtai, Hebei Province.
    • 李昭德 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty, known for his strict and sometimes harsh policies.
    • 则天 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 溱州 - A remote prefecture, likely in modern-day Sichuan or Yunnan Province.
    • 张鷟 - A famous Tang dynasty writer and official.
    • 梁州 - A prefecture in ancient China, located in modern-day Shaanxi Province.
    • 道士 - Taoist priests.
    • 九宫 - The nine palaces of the Luo Shu, a fundamental concept in Chinese numerology and divination.
    • 五鬼加年 - A term in Chinese astrology indicating a year of bad luck or misfortune.
    • 天罡临命 - A term in Chinese astrology indicating a significant influence on one's fate.
    • 周易 - The *I Ching* (Book of Changes), an ancient Chinese divination text.
    • 观之涣 - Hexagrams in the *I Ching*, *View* (观) and *Scattering* (涣).
    • 主惊恐 - Indicating fear or anxiety.
    • 风水 - Feng shui, a traditional Chinese practice of harmonizing individuals with their environment.
    • 安国观 - A Taoist temple.
    • 李全交 - A censor in the Tang dynasty.
    • 刑部尚书 - The Minister of Justice.
    • 李知白 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
    • 张廷珪 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
    • 崔玄升 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
    • 程行谋 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
    • 岭南 - A region in southern China, including modern-day Guangdong, Guangxi, and Hainan provinces.

    Proper Nouns

    • 杜景佺 - A Tang dynasty official.
    • 信都 - A place name, modern-day Xingtai, Hebei Province.
    • 李昭德 - A Tang dynasty official.
    • 则天 - Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 溱州 - A remote prefecture.
    • 张鷟 - A Tang dynasty writer and official.
    • 梁州 - A prefecture in ancient China.
    • 道士 - Taoist priests.
    • 九宫 - The nine palaces of the Luo Shu.
    • 五鬼加年 - A term in Chinese astrology.
    • 天罡临命 - A term in Chinese astrology.
    • 周易 - The *I Ching* (Book of Changes).
    • 观之涣 - Hexagrams in the *I Ching*.
    • 主惊恐 - Indicating fear or anxiety.
    • 风水 - Feng shui.
    • 安国观 - A Taoist temple.
    • 李全交 - A censor in the Tang dynasty.
    • 刑部尚书 - The Minister of Justice.
    • 李知白 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
    • 张廷珪 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
    • 崔玄升 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
    • 程行谋 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
    • 岭南 - A region in southern China.

    Dù Jǐngquán, originally from Xindu, was known by his birth name Yuánfāng. During the Chóngdé period, he was renamed Jǐngquán. He was upright and stern, passed the imperial examination, and later became a Langtaishanglang and Pingzhāngshì. At the time, Lǐ Zhāodé, the Neishǐ (Chancellor), was imprisoned for his strictness. Dù Jǐngquán argued in court that Lǐ Zhāodé was fair and upright, which angered Empress Wu Zetian, who believed he was face-saving. As a result, Dù Jǐngquán was demoted to the position of Zhìzhōu Zhìshǐ. Initially, while serving as the governor of Zhìzhōu, a skilled diviner on the road predicted that he would be reappointed as a high-ranking official, reaching the rank of Sanpǐn but without wearing a purple robe. By this time, he was wearing a purple shirt in summer and died prematurely.

    A man from Yíngzhōu, Zhang Huàilǐ, the county magistrate of Ānxīàn, and Jìn Xíngzhōng, the county magistrate of Gōnggāo in Cāng Prefecture, consulted with Cài Wēiyuǎn for divination. After completing the divination, Cài Wēiyuǎn told Zhang Huàilǐ, "You will soon rise to a high position, reaching the rank of Fāngbó (a high-ranking official)." To Jìn Xíngzhōng, he said, "You will become a capital official this year, but your term will end, and you should retire." Both men took the imperial examinations. Zhang Huàilǐ was appointed as Zuǒ Bǔquē (a censor) and later served as the governor of two prefectures. Jìn Xíngzhōng was appointed as Chéngménláng (a gatekeeper) and died in autumn.

    In the second year of the Kaiyuan era, a Taoist priest from Liángzhōu, Liáng Xūzhōu, used the Nine Palaces to predict that Zhang Zhuo would face a year of great misfortune, with the Five Ghosts adding to his year and the Heavenly Warrior influencing his fate. Using the I Ching for divination, he encountered the hexagrams View (观) and Scattering (涣), indicating fear and anxiety. Later, the situation related to feng shui improved. A Taoist priest at An Guo Temple, Lǐ Míng, without revealing his name, secretly instructed someone to perform divination, predicting that Zhang Zhuo would be imprisoned in the Heavenly Jail and face a capital crime, but could be spared. If not, he would die of illness with no remedy. Indeed, Zhang Zhuo was accused by the censor Li Quan Jiao and ordered to be executed. However, the Minister of Justice Li Zhibai, the Left Censor Zhang Tinggui, Cui Xuansheng, and others petitioned for clemency, and he was spared and exiled to Lingnan. The predictions of the two Taoist priests were indeed verified. [4]

    泉州有客卢元钦染大疯惟鼻根未倒属五月五日官取蚺蛇胆欲进或言肉可治疯遂取一截蛇肉食之三五日后渐可百日平复又商州有人患大疯家人恶之山中为起茅舎有乌蛇坠酒罂中病人不知饮酒渐差罂底见蛇骨方知其由也
    　　则天时凤阁侍郎周允元朝罢入阁太平公主唤一医人自光政门入见一撮允元头二持棒随其后直入景运门医白公主公主奏之上令给使觇问在阁无事食讫还房午后如厠长参典怪其久私往候之允元踣面于厠上目直视不语口中涎落给使奏之上问医曰此可得防时对曰缓者三日急者一日上与锦被覆之幷牀舁送宅止夜半而卒上自为诗以悼之 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 大疯 - Severe skin disease, likely leprosy, in the context of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 蚺蛇胆 - Gall of the king cobra, believed to have medicinal properties.
    • 肉可治疯 - Suggests that consuming snake meat could cure the disease, reflecting a folk belief in the medicinal value of certain animals.
    • 渐可 - Means 'gradually improved' or 'showed signs of recovery.'
    • 百日平复 - Means 'fully recovered within a hundred days.'
    • 商州 - Refers to a region in Shaanxi Province, China.
    • 茅舎 - Means 'hut made of straw,' indicating a simple, rural dwelling.
    • 乌蛇 - Refers to a black snake, likely a water snake or another species.
    • 罂 - Refers to a large earthenware jar, often used for storing liquids.
    • 踣面 - Means 'face down' or 'lying face down.'
    • 目直视不语 - Means 'staring straight ahead without speaking.'
    • 口中涎落 - Means 'saliva dripping from the mouth.'
    • 给使 - Refers to attendants or servants.
    • 长参典 - Refers to a senior official or attendant.
    • 怪其久 - Means 'wondered why it took so long.'
    • 私往候之 - Means 'went privately to check on him.'
    • 直入景运门 - Means 'entered the Jingyun Gate directly.'
    • 光政门 - Refers to a gate in the palace complex.
    • 太平公主 - Refers to Princess Taiping, a powerful figure during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 凤阁侍郎 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty government.
    • 周允元 - The name of the official mentioned in the text.
    • 朝罢入阁 - Means 'after court, entered the government office.'
    • 医人 - Refers to a doctor or physician.
    • 锦被覆之 - Means 'covered with a brocade quilt.'
    • 舁送宅 - Means 'carried to his residence.'
    • 夜半而卒 - Means 'died in the middle of the night.'
    • 悼之 - Means 'to mourn or lament.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 大疯 - In the context of the Tang Dynasty, '大疯' likely refers to leprosy or another severe skin disease.
    • 蚺蛇胆 - The belief in the medicinal properties of snake bile was common in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 太平公主 - Princess Taiping was a powerful and influential figure during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 凤阁侍郎 - This was a high-ranking position in the Tang Dynasty government, equivalent to a modern minister.
    • 周允元 - A historical figure who served as a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 景运门 - One of the gates in the Tang Dynasty palace complex, symbolizing the imperial authority.
    • 锦被 - A luxurious quilt, indicating the high status of the person being treated.
    • 则天 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.

    Proper Nouns

    • 泉州 - Refers to Quanzhou, a city in Fujian Province, China.
    • 卢元钦 - The name of the person who recovered from a severe skin disease.
    • 商州 - Refers to Shangzhou, a region in Shaanxi Province, China.
    • 太平公主 - Refers to Princess Taiping, a powerful figure during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 凤阁侍郎 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty government.
    • 周允元 - The name of the official mentioned in the text.
    • 则天 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 光政门 - One of the gates in the Tang Dynasty palace complex.
    • 景运门 - One of the gates in the Tang Dynasty palace complex.
    • 锦被 - A luxurious quilt, indicating the high status of the person being treated.

    In Quanzhou, a guest named Lu Yuanqin contracted a severe skin disease, but his nose remained unaffected. On the fifth day of the fifth month, officials obtained the gall of a king cobra, intending to present it to the emperor. Someone suggested that snake meat could cure the disease, so they took a piece of snake meat and ate it. After a few days, Lu Yuanqin showed signs of improvement and fully recovered within a hundred days.

    In Shangzhou, another person contracted a severe skin disease. His family, fearing the disease, isolated him in a straw hut in the mountains. A black snake fell into a wine jar. The patient drank the wine without knowing it contained snake, and he gradually improved. Later, he found snake bones at the bottom of the jar, realizing the cause of his recovery.

    During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, the Fengge Shilang Zhou Yunyuan entered the government office after court. Princess Taiping summoned a doctor who entered through the Guangzheng Gate. The doctor saw a ghost grabbing Zhou Yunyuan's head, with two other ghosts following with sticks. They entered the Jingyun Gate directly. The doctor reported this to Princess Taiping, who then informed the emperor. The emperor sent attendants to investigate, finding no issues in the office. Zhou Yunyuan returned to his room and later went to the toilet. A senior official wondered why it took so long and went privately to check on him. Zhou Yunyuan was lying face down, staring straight ahead without speaking, with saliva dripping from his mouth. The attendants reported this to the emperor, who asked the doctor. The doctor said it could be prevented if treated within a certain time. The emperor covered Zhou Yunyuan with a brocade quilt and had him carried to his residence. He died in the middle of the night. The emperor wrote a poem to mourn him. [5]

    久视年中襄州人杨元亮年二十余于防州汶山观佣力昼梦见天尊云我堂舎破坏汝为我脩造遣汝能医一切病悟而説之试疗无不愈者贑县里正背有肿大如拳亮以刀割之数日平复疗病日获十千造天尊堂成疗病渐无效
    　　如意年中洛州人赵景病卒五日而苏云见一僧与一木长尺余教曰人有病者汝以此木拄之即愈景后见机上尺乃是僧所与者试将疗病拄之立差门庭每日数百人御史马知己以其聚众追之禁左台病者满于台门则天闻之追入内宫人病拄之即愈放出任救百姓病数月以后得钱七百余贯后渐无验遂絶
    　　洛州有人患应语病语即喉中应之以问善医张文仲经夜思之乃得一法即取本草令读之皆应至其所畏者即不言仲乃録取药合和为丸服之应时而愈一云问医苏澄云
    　　郝公景于泰山采药经市过有见者怪羣见公景皆走避之遂取药和为杀丸有病患者服之差 [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 久视年中 - Refers to the year 700 AD during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 襄州人 - Refers to someone from Xiangzhou (modern-day Xiangyang, Hubei).
    • 于防州汶山观佣力 - Means working at the Wenshan Temple in Fangzhou (modern-day Chengdu, Sichuan).
    • 昼梦见天尊云 - Describes a dream where the person sees a deity (天尊, usually referring to the Jade Emperor or a high-ranking Taoist deity) speaking.
    • 我堂舎破坏汝为我脩造 - The deity asks the person to repair its temple.
    • 遣汝能医一切病 - The deity grants the person the ability to cure all diseases.
    • 悟而説之 - The person wakes up and realizes what happened in the dream.
    • 试疗无不愈者 - The person tests the ability and finds that it works.
    • 贑县里正背有肿大如拳 - Describes a village headman from Gan County with a swelling as big as a fist on his back.
    • 亮以刀割之数日平复 - The person cuts the swelling, and it heals within a few days.
    • 疗病日获十千 - The person earns ten thousand coins per day by treating patients.
    • 造天尊堂成疗病渐无效 - After building the temple for the deity, the person's healing ability gradually diminishes.
    • 如意年中 - Refers to the year 696 AD during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 洛州人赵景病卒五日而苏 - A person from Luozhou (modern-day Luoyang, Henan) dies and comes back to life after five days.
    • 云见一僧与一木长尺余 - The person claims to have seen a monk giving him a piece of wood about a foot long.
    • 教曰人有病者汝以此木拄之即愈 - The monk instructs the person to use the wood to treat illnesses.
    • 景后见机上尺乃是僧所与者 - The person later finds the wood on a table.
    • 试将疗病拄之立差 - The person tests the wood, and it immediately cures the patient.
    • 门庭每日数百人 - The person's home becomes crowded with hundreds of patients every day.
    • 御史马知己以其聚众追之 - The censor Ma Jizhi investigates the person for gathering a crowd.
    • 禁左台病者满于台门 - The person's patients fill the left office of the censor's headquarters.
    • 则天闻之追入内宫 - Empress Wu Zetian hears about the person and summons him to the palace.
    • 人病拄之即愈 - The person cures the emperor's patients with the wood.
    • 放出任救百姓病 - The emperor allows the person to treat the common people.
    • 数月以后得钱七百余贯 - After several months, the person earns more than seven hundred strings of cash.
    • 后渐无验遂絶 - The wood eventually loses its effectiveness and disappears.
    • 洛州有人患应语病 - A person from Luozhou suffers from a condition where his throat responds to speech.
    • 语即喉中应之 - When the person speaks, his throat responds.
    • 以问善医张文仲 - The person seeks help from the skilled doctor Zhang Wenzhong.
    • 经夜思之乃得一法 - Zhang Wenzhong thinks about it overnight and comes up with a method.
    • 即取本草令读之皆应至其所畏者即不言 - He reads the *Bencao* (Herbal Medicine) aloud, and the person's throat stops responding when it reaches a particular herb.
    • 仲乃録取药合和为丸服之应时而愈 - Zhang Wenzhong collects the herbs, combines them into pills, and the person takes them, and the condition is cured immediately.
    • 一云问医苏澄云 - Another account says the person consulted the doctor Su Cheng.
    • 郝公景于泰山采药经市过有见者怪羣见公景皆走避之 - Hao Gongjing, while collecting herbs on Mount Tai, encounters a ghost who sees him and runs away.
    • 遂取药和为杀丸 - Hao makes a pill to kill ghosts.
    • 有病患者服之差 - The pill cures the patient's illness.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 久视年 - Refers to the year 700 AD during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 如意年 - Refers to the year 696 AD during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 天尊 - Refers to a high-ranking Taoist deity, often the Jade Emperor.
    • 天尊堂 - A temple dedicated to the deity.
    • 御史 - A high-ranking official responsible for supervision and investigation.
    • 左台 - The left office of the censor's headquarters.
    • 则天 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 本草 - The *Bencao* (Herbal Medicine), a classical text on traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai, one of the Five Great Mountains in China, known for its spiritual and cultural significance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 久视年 - 700 AD
    • 如意年 - 696 AD
    • 襄州 - Modern-day Xiangyang, Hubei
    • 防州 - Modern-day Chengdu, Sichuan
    • 汶山观 - Wenshan Temple
    • 天尊 - A high-ranking Taoist deity
    • 天尊堂 - Temple dedicated to the deity
    • 贑县 - Modern-day Gan County, Jiangxi
    • 洛州 - Modern-day Luoyang, Henan
    • 赵景 - Zhao Xuanjing
    • 御史马知己 - Censor Ma Jizhi
    • 则天 - Empress Wu Zetian
    • 张文仲 - Zhang Wenzhong
    • 苏澄 - Su Cheng
    • 郝公景 - Hao Gongjing
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai

    In the year of Longsight, there was a man named Yang Yuanliang from Xiangzhou, who was around twenty years old. He worked at the Wenshan Temple in Fangzhou. One day, he dreamed of a divine being who said, "My temple is in disrepair. You should repair it for me. I will grant you the ability to cure all diseases." Upon waking, Yang realized the dream and began to test his newfound ability. He found that he could indeed cure all illnesses. In Gan County, there was a village headman whose back had a swelling as large as a fist. Yang cut it with a knife, and within a few days, it healed. He earned ten thousand coins per day by treating patients. After building the temple for the divine being, his healing ability gradually diminished.

    In the year of Ruyi, there was a man named Zhao Xuanjing from Luozhou who died and came back to life after five days. He claimed to have seen a monk who gave him a piece of wood about a foot long and instructed him to use it to treat illnesses. Zhao later found the wood on a table. He tested the wood, and it immediately cured the patient. His home became crowded with hundreds of patients every day. The censor Ma Jizhi investigated Zhao for gathering a crowd. The patients filled the left office of the censor's headquarters. Empress Wu Zetian heard about Zhao and summoned him to the palace. She had her own patients treated with the wood, and they were cured. She allowed Zhao to treat the common people. After several months, Zhao earned more than seven hundred strings of cash. Eventually, the wood lost its effectiveness and disappeared.

    In Luozhou, there was a man who suffered from a condition where his throat responded to speech. When he spoke, his throat would respond. He sought help from the skilled doctor Zhang Wenzhong. Zhang thought about it overnight and came up with a method. He read the Bencao (Herbal Medicine) aloud, and the man's throat stopped responding when it reached a particular herb. Zhang collected the herbs, combined them into pills, and the man took them. The condition was cured immediately. Another account says he consulted the doctor Su Cheng.

    Hao Gongjing was collecting herbs on Mount Tai when he encountered a ghost who saw him and ran away. Hao made a pill to kill ghosts. There was a patient who suffered from an illness, and when he took the pill, he was cured. [6]

    定州人崔务坠马折足医令取铜末和酒服之遂痊平及亡后十余年改视其胫骨折处有铜末束之
    　　岭南风俗多为毒药令奴食冶葛死埋之土中蕈生正当腹上食之立死手足额上生者当日死旁自外者数日死渐逺者或一月或两月全逺者一年二年三年亦即死惟陈怀卿家药能解之或以涂马鞭头控上拂着手即毒拭着口即死
    　　赵延禧云遭恶蛇虺所螫处贴之艾炷其上灸之立差不然即死凡蛇囓即当囓处灸之引去毒气即止
    　　冶葛食之立死有冶葛处即有白藤花能解冶葛毒鸩鸟食水之处即有犀牛不濯角其水物食之必死为鸩食蛇之故
    　　医书言虎中药箭食清泥野猪中药箭豗荠苨而食雉被鹰伤以地黄叶帖之又礜石可以害鼠张鷟曾试之鼠中毒如醉亦不识人犹知取泥汁饮之须臾平复鸟兽虫物解毒何况人乎被蠭齧者以甲虫末傅之被马咬者以烧鞭鞘灰涂之葢取其相服也蜘蛛齧者雄黄末傅之筋断须续者取旋复根绞取汁以筋相对以汁涂而封之即相续如故蜀儿奴逃走多刻筋以此续之百不失一 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 定州人崔務墜馬折足 - A person from Dingzhou, Cui Wu, fell off a horse and broke his foot.
    • 醫令取銅末和酒服之遂痊平 - The doctor ordered him to take copper powder mixed with wine, and he was completely cured.
    • 及亡後十余年改視其胫骨折處有銅末束之 - After his death, more than ten years later, when he was reburied, it was seen that his fractured shinbone was bound with copper powder.
    • 岭南风俗多为毒药令奴食冶葛死埋之土中蕈生正当腹上食之立死 - In the Lingnan region, it is a custom to make poison, give it to slaves to eat, and bury them in the ground. Mushrooms grow directly over their stomachs, and eating them causes immediate death.
    • 手足额上生者当日死旁自外者数日死渐远者或一月或两月全远者一年二年三年亦即死 - Those that grow on hands, feet, and forehead die on the same day; those growing from the sides die in a few days; those growing further away may die in a month, two months, or even up to three years.
    • 惟陈怀卿家药能解之或以涂马鞭头控上拂着手即毒拭着口即死 - Only Chen Huaiqing's family medicine can cure it; if it is applied to the head of a horsewhip and waved around, it is toxic, and touching it or wiping it on the mouth will cause death.
    • 赵延禧云遭恶蛇虺所螫处贴之艾炷其上灸之立差不然即死凡蛇囓即当囓处灸之引去毒气即止 - Zhao Yanxi said that for a bite from a venomous snake, apply moxa to the spot and burn it, which will immediately heal the wound; otherwise, it will be fatal. For any snake bite, burn the spot where it bit, and the poison will be drawn away.
    • 冶葛食之立死有冶葛处即有白藤花能解冶葛毒鸩鸟食水之处即有犀牛不濯角其水物食之必死为鸩食蛇之故 - Eating wild gourd causes immediate death; where wild gourd grows, there is white morning glory that can cure its poison. Where the kingfisher drinks, there is a rhinoceros that does not wash its horn; anything that drinks that water will die because the kingfisher eats snakes.
    • 医书言虎中药箭食清泥野猪中药箭豗荠苨而食雉被鹰伤以地黄叶帖之又礜石可以害鼠张鷟曾试之鼠中毒如醉亦不识人犹知取泥汁饮之须臾平复鸟兽虫物解毒何况人乎被蠭齧者以甲虫末傅之被马咬者以烧鞭鞘灰涂之葢取其相服也蜘蛛齧者雄黄末傅之筋断须续者取旋复根绞取汁以筋相对以汁涂而封之即相续如故蜀儿奴逃走多刻筋以此续之百不失一 - Medical books say that a tiger bitten by a poisoned arrow eats clear mud; a wild boar bitten by a poisoned arrow eats wild ginger and wild ginger; a pheasant wounded by an eagle has its wound treated with rehmannia leaves. Orpiment can harm mice; Zhang Zhuo once tried it, and the mice became intoxicated, not recognizing people, but still knew to drink muddy water to recover. Birds, beasts, insects, and other creatures can be cured of poison; how much more so humans? For bee stings, apply beetle powder; for horse bites, apply burned horsewhip ash. It is taken from their mutual compatibility. For spider bites, apply realgar powder; for broken tendons, take the root of *Inula japonica*, squeeze out the juice, place the tendons opposite each other, and apply the juice and seal them; they will reconnect as before. In Sichuan, children who run away often cut their tendons and use this method to reconnect them, with no failures.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 定州 - A historical region in northern China.
    • 崔務 - A historical figure, possibly a local official or notable person.
    • 冶葛 - A type of wild gourd known for its toxicity.
    • 白藤花 - White morning glory, a plant known for its medicinal properties.
    • 鸩鸟 - A mythical bird known for its deadly poison.
    • 犀牛 - A large mammal, often associated with mythical or symbolic significance in Chinese culture.
    • 陈怀卿 - A historical figure, possibly a renowned physician.
    • 赵延禧 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or medical expert.
    • 雉 - Pheasant, a bird often mentioned in classical Chinese texts.
    • 地黄叶 - Rehmannia leaves, used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 礜石 - Orpiment, a mineral used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 张鷟 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or medical expert.
    • 旋复根 - The root of *Inula japonica*, used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 蜀儿奴 - Children or slaves from Sichuan, a historical region in southwestern China.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sentence describes a sequence of events: falling off a horse, breaking a foot, receiving a treatment, and being cured.
    • This sentence describes a posthumous observation, noting the presence of copper powder on a fractured shinbone.
    • This sentence describes a cultural practice involving poisoning and the growth of mushrooms over the buried bodies.
    • This sentence details the effects of the poison based on where it grows, using a series of conditional statements.
    • This sentence describes a specific antidote and its method of use, emphasizing its effectiveness and the danger of improper application.
    • This sentence describes a traditional remedy for snake bites, emphasizing the importance of immediate treatment.
    • This sentence describes a complex interplay of plants, animals, and poisons, using a series of conditional statements.
    • This sentence describes various traditional remedies for different ailments and injuries, using a series of conditional statements and examples.

    Proper Nouns

    • 定州 - A historical region in northern China.
    • 崔務 - A historical figure, possibly a local official or notable person.
    • 陈怀卿 - A historical figure, possibly a renowned physician.
    • 赵延禧 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or medical expert.
    • 雉 - Pheasant, a bird often mentioned in classical Chinese texts.
    • 张鷟 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or medical expert.

    A person from Dingzhou, Cui Wu, fell off a horse and broke his foot. The doctor ordered him to take copper powder mixed with wine, and he was completely cured. After his death, more than ten years later, when he was reburied, it was seen that his fractured shinbone was bound with copper powder.

    In the Lingnan region, it is a custom to make poison, give it to slaves to eat, and bury them in the ground. Mushrooms grow directly over their stomachs, and eating them causes immediate death. Those that grow on hands, feet, and forehead die on the same day; those growing from the sides die in a few days; those growing further away may die in a month, two months, or even up to three years. Only Chen Huaiqing's family medicine can cure it; if it is applied to the head of a horsewhip and waved around, it is toxic, and touching it or wiping it on the mouth will cause death.

    Zhao Yanxi said that for a bite from a venomous snake, apply moxa to the spot and burn it, which will immediately heal the wound; otherwise, it will be fatal. For any snake bite, burn the spot where it bit, and the poison will be drawn away.

    Eating wild gourd causes immediate death; where wild gourd grows, there is white morning glory that can cure its poison. Where the kingfisher drinks, there is a rhinoceros that does not wash its horn; anything that drinks that water will die because the kingfisher eats snakes.

    Medical books say that a tiger bitten by a poisoned arrow eats clear mud; a wild boar bitten by a poisoned arrow eats wild ginger and wild ginger; a pheasant wounded by an eagle has its wound treated with rehmannia leaves. Orpiment can harm mice; Zhang Zhuo once tried it, and the mice became intoxicated, not recognizing people, but still knew to drink muddy water to recover. Birds, beasts, insects, and other creatures can be cured of poison; how much more so humans? For bee stings, apply beetle powder; for horse bites, apply burned horsewhip ash. It is taken from their mutual compatibility. For spider bites, apply realgar powder; for broken tendons, take the root of Inula japonica, squeeze out the juice, place the tendons opposite each other, and apply the juice and seal them; they will reconnect as before. In Sichuan, children who run away often cut their tendons and use this method to reconnect them, with no failures. [7]

    永徽中有崔爽者每食生鱼三斗乃足于后饥作鲙未成爽忍饥不禁遂吐一物状如虾蟇自此之后不复能食鲙矣
    　　国子司业知制诰崔融病百余日腹中虫蚀极痛不可忍有一物如守宫从下部出须防而卒
    　　后魏孝文帝定四姓陇西李氏大姓恐不入星夜乗鸣駞倍程至洛时四姓已定讫故至今谓之駞李焉
    　　张文成曰干封以前选人每年不越数千垂拱以后每嵗常至五万人不加众选人益繁者葢有由矣尝试论之秪如明经进士十周三卫勲散杂色国官直司妙简实材堪入流者十分不过一二选司考练总是假手冒名势家嘱请手不把笔即送东司眼不识文被举南馆正员不足权补试摄检校之官贿货纵横汚狼籍流外行署钱多即留或帖司助曹或员外行案更有挽郎辇脚营田当屯无尺寸工夫并优与处分皆不事学问唯求财贿是以选人冗冗甚于羊羣吏部喧喧多于蚁聚若铨实用百无一人积薪化薪所从来逺矣 [8]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [8]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 每食生鱼三斗乃足 - He would eat three dou of raw fish to be satisfied.
    • 于后饥作鲙未成 - Later, when he was hungry and preparing to make sashimi, it was not yet ready.
    • 爽忍饥不禁 - Cui Shuang could not resist his hunger.
    • 遂吐一物状如虾蟆 - He then vomited an object shaped like a toad.
    • 自此之后不复能食鲙矣 - From then on, he could no longer eat sashimi.
    • 腹中虫蚀极痛不可忍 - He suffered from internal parasites that caused extreme pain he could not endure.
    • 有一物如守宫从下部出 - He expelled an object shaped like a gecko from his lower body.
    • 须防而卒 - He needed to be cautious and eventually passed away.
    • 星夜乘鸣驼倍程至洛 - They traveled at night on a neighing camel, covering double the usual distance to Luoyang.
    • 故至今谓之驼李焉 - Therefore, they are still called 'Camel Li' today.
    • 干封以前选人每年不越数千 - Before the Danyang era, the number of candidates selected each year did not exceed several thousand.
    • 垂拱以后每岁常至五万人 - After the Chui Gong era, the number of candidates selected each year often reached fifty thousand.
    • 秪如明经进士十周三卫勋散杂色国官直司妙简实材堪入流者十分不过一二 - Among the ten categories of officials, including those from the Mingjing and Jinshi examinations, the Three Guards, and other miscellaneous positions, only a fraction of the truly capable individuals were selected.
    • 选司考练总是假手冒名势家嘱请手不把笔即送东司眼不识文被举南馆正员不足权补试摄检校之官 - The selection process was often manipulated by powerful families, and those who could not write were sent to the Eastern Office, while those who could not recognize writing were sent to the Southern Pavilion.
    • 更有挽郎辇脚营田当屯无尺寸工夫并优与处分皆不事学问唯求财贿 - There were also draftsmen, carriage drivers, and farmers who did not perform any actual work, and they were all given preferential treatment. They did not engage in learning but only sought wealth.
    • 是以选人冗冗甚于羊群吏部喧喧多于蚁聚 - Therefore, the selection process became so crowded that it was like a flock of sheep, and the Ministry of Personnel was so noisy that it was like an ant colony.
    • 若铨实用百无一人积薪化薪所从来逺矣 - If the selection process were truly based on merit, there would be no one suitable. The problem has been accumulating for a long time.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 永徽 - The reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang, 650-655 AD.
    • 崔爽 - A historical figure during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 崔融 - A historical figure during the Tang Dynasty, known for his literary works.
    • 国子司业 - The head of the Imperial College, responsible for education and the drafting of imperial edicts.
    • 知制诰 - The position responsible for drafting imperial edicts.
    • 后魏孝文帝 - Emperor Xiaowen of the Northern Wei, who reigned from 471-499 AD.
    • 陇西李氏 - The Li family of Longxi, a prominent family during the Northern Wei period.
    • 四姓 - The four major families during the Northern Wei period.
    • 干封 - The reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang, 666-668 AD.
    • 垂拱 - The reign title of Empress Wu Zetian, 684-688 AD.
    • 明经进士 - Two types of imperial examinations during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 三卫 - The Three Guards, a military organization during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 勋散杂色国官 - Various types of officials, including those with military merit and those in miscellaneous positions.
    • 直司 - The position responsible for direct service to the emperor.
    • 妙简实材 - Selecting the most capable individuals.
    • 堪入流者 - Those who are qualified to enter the official ranks.
    • 选司 - The officials responsible for the selection process.
    • 考练 - The process of evaluating and selecting candidates.
    • 假手冒名 - Manipulating the selection process by using others' names.
    • 势家嘱请 - Powerful families making requests.
    • 东司 - The Eastern Office, a department in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 南馆 - The Southern Pavilion, a department in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 正员不足 - Insufficient regular positions.
    • 权补试摄检校之官 - Temporarily filling positions with candidates who are tested and appointed.
    • 挽郎 - Draftsmen.
    • 辇脚 - Carriage drivers.
    • 营田 - Farmers.
    • 当屯 - Those who should be stationed.
    • 无尺寸工夫 - Not performing any actual work.
    • 优与处分 - Given preferential treatment.
    • 不事学问 - Not engaging in learning.
    • 唯求财贿 - Only seeking wealth.
    • 铨实用 - Truly based on merit.
    • 积薪化薪 - Accumulating problems over time.

    Proper Nouns

    • 永徽 - The reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang, 650-655 AD.
    • 崔爽 - A historical figure during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 崔融 - A historical figure during the Tang Dynasty, known for his literary works.
    • 国子司业 - The head of the Imperial College, responsible for education and the drafting of imperial edicts.
    • 知制诰 - The position responsible for drafting imperial edicts.
    • 后魏孝文帝 - Emperor Xiaowen of the Northern Wei, who reigned from 471-499 AD.
    • 陇西李氏 - The Li family of Longxi, a prominent family during the Northern Wei period.
    • 四姓 - The four major families during the Northern Wei period.
    • 干封 - The reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang, 666-668 AD.
    • 垂拱 - The reign title of Empress Wu Zetian, 684-688 AD.
    • 明经进士 - Two types of imperial examinations during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 三卫 - The Three Guards, a military organization during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 勋散杂色国官 - Various types of officials, including those with military merit and those in miscellaneous positions.
    • 直司 - The position responsible for direct service to the emperor.
    • 妙简实材 - Selecting the most capable individuals.
    • 堪入流者 - Those who are qualified to enter the official ranks.
    • 选司 - The officials responsible for the selection process.
    • 考练 - The process of evaluating and selecting candidates.
    • 假手冒名 - Manipulating the selection process by using others' names.
    • 势家嘱请 - Powerful families making requests.
    • 东司 - The Eastern Office, a department in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 南馆 - The Southern Pavilion, a department in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 正员不足 - Insufficient regular positions.
    • 权补试摄检校之官 - Temporarily filling positions with candidates who are tested and appointed.
    • 挽郎 - Draftsmen.
    • 辇脚 - Carriage drivers.
    • 营田 - Farmers.
    • 当屯 - Those who should be stationed.
    • 无尺寸工夫 - Not performing any actual work.
    • 优与处分 - Given preferential treatment.
    • 不事学问 - Not engaging in learning.
    • 唯求财贿 - Only seeking wealth.
    • 铨实用 - Truly based on merit.
    • 积薪化薪 - Accumulating problems over time.

    During the Yonghui period, there was a man named Cui Shuang who would eat three dou of raw fish to be satisfied. Later, when he was hungry and preparing to make sashimi, it was not yet ready. Cui Shuang could not resist his hunger and then vomited an object shaped like a toad. From then on, he could no longer eat sashimi.

    During the illness of Cui Rong, who served as the head of the Imperial College and was in charge of drafting imperial edicts, he was sick for over a hundred days. He suffered from internal parasites that caused extreme pain he could not endure. He expelled an object shaped like a gecko from his lower body and needed to be cautious, eventually passing away.

    During the reign of Emperor Xiaowen of the Northern Wei, the four major families, including the Li family of Longxi, feared they would not be included. They traveled at night on a neighing camel, covering double the usual distance to Luoyang. By the time they arrived, the four major families had already been determined. Therefore, they are still called "Camel Li" today.

    Zhang Wencheng said that before the Danyang era, the number of candidates selected each year did not exceed several thousand. After the Chui Gong era, the number of candidates selected each year often reached fifty thousand. Without increasing the number of officials, the selection process became more complex. There must be a reason for this. Let me try to explain.

    For example, among the ten categories of officials, including those from the Mingjing and Jinshi examinations, the Three Guards, and other miscellaneous positions, only a fraction of the truly capable individuals were selected. The selection process was often manipulated by powerful families, and those who could not write were sent to the Eastern Office, while those who could not recognize writing were sent to the Southern Pavilion. There were also draftsmen, carriage drivers, and farmers who did not perform any actual work, and they were all given preferential treatment. They did not engage in learning but only sought wealth. Therefore, the selection process became so crowded that it was like a flock of sheep, and the Ministry of Personnel was so noisy that it was like an ant colony. If the selection process were truly based on merit, there would be no one suitable. The problem has been accumulating for a long time. [8]

    郑愔为吏部侍郎掌选汚狼籍引铨有选人系百钱于靴带上愔问其故答曰当今之选非钱不行愔黙而不言时崔湜亦为吏部侍郎掌选有铨人引过分疏云某能翘闗负米湜曰君壮何不兵部选答曰外边人皆云崔侍郎下有气力者即存
    　　景龙年中斜封得官者三百人从屠贩而践高位景云践祚尚书宋璟御史大夫毕构奏停斜封人官璟构出后见人彭卿受斜封人贿赂奏云见孝和怒曰我与人官何因夺却于是斜封皆复旧职伪周革命之际十道使人天下选残明经进士及下邨教童博士皆被搜不曾试练并与美职尘黩士人之品诱悦愚夫之心庸才者得官以为荣有才者得官以为辱昔赵王伦之篡也天下孝亷秀才茂异并不简试雷同与官市道屠沽亡命不轨皆封侯略尽太府之铜不供铸印至有白版侯者朝防之服貂者大半故謡云貂不足狗尾续小人多幸君子耻之无道之朝一何连也惜哉 [9]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [9]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為吏部侍郎掌選 - As the head of the Ministry of Personnel in charge of selection.
    • 汚狼籍 - Corrupt and disorderly, describing the chaotic and unethical nature of the selection process.
    • 引铨 - To be called for selection.
    • 系百钱于靴带上 - Tied a hundred coins to the belt of his boots, indicating bribery.
    • 黙而不言 - Silently without comment, suggesting tacit acceptance of the bribery.
    • 从屠贩而践高位 - Rising to high positions from the ranks of butchers and peddlers, highlighting the corruption of the selection process.
    • 景龙年中 - During the Jinglong era, referring to a specific period in Chinese history.
    • 斜封得官者三百人 - Three hundred officials appointed through backdoor channels.
    • 景云践祚 - The ascension of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, marking a significant change in leadership.
    • 尚书宋璟御史大夫毕构奏停斜封人官 - Shangshu Song Jing and Yushi Daifu Bi Gou petitioned to stop the appointments of those who were appointed through backdoor channels.
    • 见孝和怒曰 - Seeing Emperor Xuanzong's anger, he said, indicating a reaction to the emperor's displeasure.
    • 伪周革命之际 - During the revolution of the false Zhou dynasty, referring to the period when Wu Zetian established the Zhou dynasty.
    • 十道使人 - Officials sent to the ten circuits, indicating a widespread administrative effort.
    • 天下选残 - The selection process was disrupted, suggesting that the process was compromised.
    • 明经进士及下邨教童博士 - Those who passed the Mingjing and Jinshi exams and village teachers, referring to different levels of scholars and educators.
    • 被搜扬 - Were searched and promoted, indicating that they were selected for positions.
    • 不曾试练 - Without being tested or trained, suggesting that the selection process was flawed.
    • 并与美职 - Were given good positions, indicating the rewards of the flawed selection process.
    • 尘黩士人之品 - Degrading the reputation of scholars, highlighting the negative impact on the scholarly community.
    • 诱悦愚夫之心 - Appealing to the hearts of the ignorant, suggesting that the process was manipulated to satisfy the less educated.
    • 庸才者得官以为荣 - Those with mediocre talent gained positions and felt honored, indicating a sense of achievement among the less qualified.
    • 有才者得官以为辱 - Those with real talent gained positions and felt humiliated, highlighting the frustration of the more capable.
    • 昔赵王伦之篡也 - When Zhao Wang Lun usurped power, referring to a historical event.
    • 天下孝亷秀才茂异并不简试 - The whole country's filial and clean officials, talented scholars, and extraordinary individuals were not selected through proper testing, indicating a flawed selection process.
    • 雷同与官 - Were given positions indiscriminately, suggesting a lack of merit-based selection.
    • 市道屠沽亡命不轨皆封侯 - Merchants, butchers, and fugitives who were not law-abiding were all made marquises, highlighting the corruption of the selection process.
    • 略尽太府之铜不供铸印 - Almost all the copper from the Taifu was used up and not enough was left to make seals, indicating the extent of the corruption.
    • 至有白版侯者 - Even those with white board marquis titles, suggesting a form of honorary title without real power.
    • 朝防之服貂者大半 - More than half of the court officials wore sable fur hats, indicating a widespread corruption.
    • 小人多幸君子耻之 - The common people were fortunate, but the virtuous felt ashamed, highlighting the moral implications of the situation.
    • 无道之朝一何连也 - How corrupt the court was, summarizing the overall state of affairs.
    • 惜哉 - Alas, expressing regret or sorrow.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 郑愔 - A historical figure who served as the head of the Ministry of Personnel during the Tang dynasty.
    • 崔湜 - Another historical figure who also served as the head of the Ministry of Personnel during the Tang dynasty.
    • 景龙年中 - The Jinglong era, a period during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 景云践祚 - The ascension of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, marking the beginning of a new era.
    • 尚书宋璟 - Song Jing, a famous official known for his integrity and reforms.
    • 御史大夫毕构 - Bi Gou, another official known for his integrity.
    • 伪周革命之际 - The period when Wu Zetian established the Zhou dynasty, a significant event in Chinese history.
    • 十道使人 - Officials sent to the ten circuits, a system of administrative divisions in China.
    • 明经进士及下邨教童博士 - Scholars and educators who passed various exams, reflecting the educational system of the time.
    • 太府 - The Taifu, a government department responsible for financial matters.
    • 朝防之服貂者大半 - Sable fur hats, a symbol of high-ranking officials in the Tang dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 郑愔 - A historical figure.
    • 崔湜 - A historical figure.
    • 宋璟 - A historical figure.
    • 毕构 - A historical figure.
    • 景龙 - A period during the Tang dynasty.
    • 景云 - A period during the Tang dynasty.
    • 伪周 - The false Zhou dynasty, established by Wu Zetian.
    • 十道 - The ten circuits, a system of administrative divisions.
    • 太府 - The Taifu, a government department.
    • 朝防之服貂者 - Sable fur hats, a symbol of high-ranking officials.

    During the reign of Emperor Zhongzong, Vizier Zheng Yin, as the head of the Ministry of Personnel, presided over a corrupt and disorderly selection process. Candidates would be called for selection, and one candidate tied a hundred coins to the belt of his boots. Zheng Yin asked him why, and the candidate replied, "In today's selection, without money, it cannot be done." Zheng Yin remained silent. At the same time, Cui Shi, also serving as the head of the Ministry of Personnel, had a candidate who, when called for selection, claimed he could lift heavy gates and carry grain. Cui Shi said, "If you are so strong, why not apply to the Ministry of War?" The candidate replied, "Everyone outside says that those with strength under Cui Vizier will be selected."

    During the Jinglong era, three hundred officials were appointed through backdoor channels, rising to high positions from the ranks of butchers and peddlers. When Emperor Xuanzong ascended the throne, Shangshu Song Jing and Yushi Daifu Bi Gou petitioned to stop the appointments of those who were appointed through backdoor channels. After Song Jing and Bi Gou left office, a ghost named Peng Qing accepted bribes from those who were appointed through backdoor channels and reported to the emperor, saying, "I saw Emperor Xuanzong's anger and he said, 'I gave people positions, why did you take them away?' As a result, those appointed through backdoor channels were all restored to their old positions."

    During the revolution of the false Zhou dynasty, officials were sent to the ten circuits, and the selection process was disrupted. Those who passed the Mingjing and Jinshi exams and village teachers were all searched and promoted without being tested or trained, and were given good positions, degrading the reputation of scholars and appealing to the hearts of the ignorant. Those with mediocre talent gained positions and felt honored, while those with real talent gained positions and felt humiliated.

    When Zhao Wang Lun usurped power, the whole country's filial and clean officials, talented scholars, and extraordinary individuals were not selected through proper testing and were given positions indiscriminately. Merchants, butchers, and fugitives who were not law-abiding were all made marquises, almost all the copper from the Taifu was used up and not enough was left to make seals, and even those with white board marquis titles. More than half of the court officials wore sable fur hats. The common people were fortunate, but the virtuous felt ashamed. How corrupt the court was, alas! [9]

    天后中契丹李尽忠万荣之破营府也以地牢囚汉俘数百人闻麻仁节等诸君欲至乃令守囚霫等绐之曰家口饥寒不能存活求待国家兵到吾等即降其囚日别与一顿粥引出安慰曰吾此无饮食养汝又不忍杀汝总放归何众皆拜伏乞命乃绐放去至幽州具说饥冻逗遛兵士闻之争欲先入至黄麞峪贼又令老者投官军送遗老牛瘦马于道侧仁节等三军弃步卒将马先争入被贼设伏横截军将被索防之生擒节等死者填山谷罕有一遗
    　　景龙四年洛州凌空观失火万物并尽惟有一真人岿然独存乃泥塑为之后改为圣真观
    　　西京朝堂北头有大槐树隋曰唐兴材门首文皇帝移长安城将作大匠高颎常坐此树下检校后栽树行不正欲去之帝曰颎坐此树下不须杀之至今先天一百三十年其树尚在柯叶森竦株根盘礴与诸树不同承天门正当唐兴材门首今唐家居焉 [10]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [10]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 天后中 - Period during the reign of Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 契丹李尽忠万荣之破营府也 - Describes the defeat of the Tang Dynasty's military camp by the Khitan leader Li Jinzhong and his ally Wan Rong.
    • 地牢囚汉俘数百人 - Refers to the imprisonment of hundreds of Han captives in underground prisons.
    • 守囚霫等 - Refers to the guards or captors of the prisoners.
    • 绐之曰 - Means 'deceived them by saying,' indicating a false statement made to mislead.
    • 家口饥寒不能存活 - Means 'our families are starving and freezing, unable to survive,' a fabricated excuse to manipulate the prisoners.
    • 求待国家兵到吾等即降 - Means 'we will wait for the arrival of the national army and then surrender,' another deceptive statement.
    • 引出安慰曰 - Means 'led them out to comfort them by saying,' indicating a false promise to ease their minds.
    • 吾此无饮食养汝 - Means 'I have no food to feed you,' a lie to manipulate the prisoners.
    • 又不忍杀汝 - Means 'but I cannot bear to kill you,' another false statement.
    • 总放归 - Means 'let them all go,' indicating the release of the prisoners.
    • 逗遛兵士 - Means 'delaying the soldiers,' indicating that the prisoners were used to delay the Tang army.
    • 争欲先入 - Means 'racing to enter first,' indicating the eagerness of the soldiers to reach a destination.
    • 黄麞峪 - Refers to Huangzhang Valley, a location where the Tang army was ambushed.
    • 被贼设伏横截军将被索防之生擒节等死者填山谷罕有一遗 - Describes the ambush and capture of the Tang generals, with many soldiers dying in the valley.
    • 景龙四年 - Refers to the year 708 AD, during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 洛州凌空观失火 - Refers to a fire at Lingkong Temple in Luoyang.
    • 万物并尽 - Means 'all things were destroyed,' indicating the complete destruction of the temple.
    • 惟有一真人岿然独存 - Means 'only one statue of a Taoist deity remained standing,' indicating the survival of a single statue amidst the destruction.
    • 乃泥塑为之后改为圣真观 - Means 'it was then made into a clay statue, and the temple was renamed Shengzhen Temple,' indicating the transformation of the temple after the fire.
    • 西京朝堂北头有大槐树 - Refers to a large locust tree in front of the northern gate of the imperial court in Chang'an.
    • 隋曰唐兴材门首 - Means 'during the Sui Dynasty, it was called the Tangxing Gate,' indicating the historical name of the gate.
    • 文皇帝移长安城 - Refers to Emperor Wen of Sui, who moved the capital from Chang'an to Luoyang.
    • 将作大匠高颎常坐此树下检校 - Means 'the chief architect Gao Jing often sat under this tree to inspect,' indicating Gao Jing's habit of inspecting the tree.
    • 栽树行不正欲去之 - Means 'the tree was planted crookedly, and there was a desire to remove it,' indicating the tree's poor condition.
    • 帝曰颎坐此树下不须杀之 - Means 'the emperor said, 'Gao Jing sat under this tree, so do not cut it down,' indicating the emperor's decision to preserve the tree.
    • 至今先天一百三十年其树尚在柯叶森竦株根盘礴与诸树不同 - Means 'until today, one hundred and thirty years after the beginning of the Tianbao era, the tree still stands with its branches and leaves towering and its roots sprawling, different from other trees,' indicating the tree's longevity and unique appearance.
    • 承天门正当唐兴材门首今唐家居焉 - Means 'Chenting Gate is directly opposite the Tangxing Gate, and now the Tang family resides there,' indicating the current location of the Tang family.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天后 - Refers to Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history, who ruled during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 契丹 - Refers to the Khitan people, a nomadic group that frequently clashed with the Tang Dynasty.
    • 李尽忠万荣 - Refers to Li Jinzhong and Wan Rong, Khitan leaders who led a rebellion against the Tang Dynasty.
    • 营府 - Refers to the military camps of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 地牢 - Refers to underground prisons, a form of punishment in ancient China.
    • 汉俘 - Refers to Han Chinese captives taken during the Khitan rebellion.
    • 霫 - Refers to the Xianbei people, a nomadic group that was often used as guards or captors.
    • 绐 - Refers to deception or trickery, a common tactic in ancient warfare.
    • 家口 - Refers to family members, often used in the context of captivity or enslavement.
    • 逗遛 - Refers to delaying or detaining, often used in the context of military strategy.
    • 黄麞峪 - Refers to Huangzhang Valley, a location where the Tang army was ambushed.
    • 景龙四年 - Refers to the year 708 AD, during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 洛州 - Refers to Luoyang, one of the most important cities in Chinese history and the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 凌空观 - Refers to Lingkong Temple, a Taoist temple in Luoyang.
    • 圣真观 - Refers to Shengzhen Temple, the renamed temple after the fire.
    • 西京 - Refers to Chang'an, the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 朝堂 - Refers to the imperial court, the center of political power.
    • 隋 - Refers to the Sui Dynasty, which preceded the Tang Dynasty.
    • 唐兴材门 - Refers to the Tangxing Gate, a historical gate in Chang'an.
    • 文皇帝 - Refers to Emperor Wen of Sui, who moved the capital from Chang'an to Luoyang.
    • 将作大匠 - Refers to the chief architect, a high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 高颎 - Refers to Gao Jing, a famous chief architect and statesman during the Sui Dynasty.
    • 先天 - Refers to the Tianbao era, a period during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 承天门 - Refers to Chenting Gate, a historical gate in Chang'an.
    • 唐家居焉 - Refers to the Tang family, indicating their residence in the area.

    Proper Nouns

    • 天后 - Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 契丹 - The Khitan people, a nomadic group that frequently clashed with the Tang Dynasty.
    • 李尽忠万荣 - Li Jinzhong and Wan Rong, Khitan leaders who led a rebellion against the Tang Dynasty.
    • 营府 - The military camps of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 地牢 - Underground prisons, a form of punishment in ancient China.
    • 汉俘 - Han Chinese captives taken during the Khitan rebellion.
    • 霫 - The Xianbei people, a nomadic group that was often used as guards or captors.
    • 绐 - Deception or trickery, a common tactic in ancient warfare.
    • 家口 - Family members, often used in the context of captivity or enslavement.
    • 逗遛 - Delaying or detaining, often used in the context of military strategy.
    • 黄麞峪 - Huangzhang Valley, a location where the Tang army was ambushed.
    • 景龙四年 - The year 708 AD, during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 洛州 - Luoyang, one of the most important cities in Chinese history and the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 凌空观 - Lingkong Temple, a Taoist temple in Luoyang.
    • 圣真观 - Shengzhen Temple, the renamed temple after the fire.
    • 西京 - Chang'an, the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 朝堂 - The imperial court, the center of political power.
    • 隋 - The Sui Dynasty, which preceded the Tang Dynasty.
    • 唐兴材门 - The Tangxing Gate, a historical gate in Chang'an.
    • 文皇帝 - Emperor Wen of Sui, who moved the capital from Chang'an to Luoyang.
    • 将作大匠 - The chief architect, a high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 高颎 - Gao Jing, a famous chief architect and statesman during the Sui Dynasty.
    • 先天 - The Tianbao era, a period during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 承天门 - Chenting Gate, a historical gate in Chang'an.
    • 唐家居焉 - The Tang family, indicating their residence in the area.

    During the reign of Empress Wu, when the Khitan leaders Li Jinzhong and Wan Rong attacked and defeated the Tang military camps, they imprisoned hundreds of Han captives in underground prisons. When they learned that General Ma Renjie and other officials were approaching, they ordered the guards to deceive the prisoners by saying, "Our families are starving and freezing, unable to survive. We will wait for the arrival of the national army and then surrender." The prisoners were given a bowl of porridge each day and led out to be comforted by the statement, "I have no food to feed you, but I cannot bear to kill you. I will let you all go." The prisoners bowed and begged for their lives, and so they were deceived and released. Upon reaching Youzhou, the prisoners told of their hunger and cold, which delayed the Tang soldiers. Upon hearing this, the soldiers raced to enter first, reaching Huangzhang Valley where the Khitans had set up an ambush. The Khitans then sent elderly men to the Tang army, offering old cows and skinny horses on the roadside. The Tang generals, Ma Renjie and others, abandoned their infantry and led their horses ahead, racing to enter. They were ambushed and captured by the Khitans, who had set up a trap and cut off their retreat. The generals were seized and killed, and the valley was filled with the dead, few of whom survived.

    In the fourth year of the Jinglong era, a fire broke out at Lingkong Temple in Luoyang, destroying everything. Only one statue of a Taoist deity remained standing. It was then made into a clay statue, and the temple was renamed Shengzhen Temple.

    At the northern gate of the imperial court in Chang'an, there was a large locust tree. During the Sui Dynasty, it was called the Tangxing Gate. Emperor Wen of Sui moved the capital from Chang'an to Luoyang. The chief architect Gao Jing often sat under this tree to inspect. The tree was planted crookedly, and there was a desire to remove it. The emperor said, "Gao Jing sat under this tree, so do not cut it down." Until today, one hundred and thirty years after the beginning of the Tianbao era, the tree still stands with its branches and leaves towering and its roots sprawling, different from other trees. Chenting Gate is directly opposite the Tangxing Gate, and now the Tang family resides there. [10]

    永徽年以后人唱桑条歌有桑条韦女韦也乐至神龙年中逆韦应之谄佞者郑愔作桑条乐词十余首进之逆韦大喜擢之为吏部侍郎赏缣百疋
    　　龙朔已来人唱歌名突厥盐后周圣厯年中差阎知防和匈奴授三品春官尚书送武延秀往娶黙啜女送金银器物锦防衣裳以为礼聘不可胜纪突厥翻动汉使并没立知微为可汗乃突厥盐之应
    　　调露中大帝欲封中岳属突厥而止后又欲封土番入寇遂停止永淳年又驾幸嵩岳謡曰嵩山凡防层不畏登不得只畏不得登三度征兵马傍道打腾腾岳下遘疾不愈回至宫而崩
    　　永淳之后天下皆唱杨栁杨栁漫头駞后徐敬业犯事出栁州司马遂作伪勅自授州司马杀长史陈敬之据江淮反使李孝逸讨之斩业首驿马防入洛阳杨栁杨栁漫头駞后此其应也 [11]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [11]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 永徽年以后 - Refers to the years after the Yonghui era (650-655 AD) of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 唱桑条歌 - Means 'singing the song of mulberry branches.'
    • 桑条韦女 - Refers to a woman named Wei who sang the mulberry song.
    • 乐至神龙年中 - Means 'the joy reached the Shendong era (705-707 AD).'
    • 逆韦应之 - Refers to Wei, who responded to the song.
    • 谄佞者郑愔 - Refers to Zheng Yin, a flatterer.
    • 作桑条乐词十余首 - Means 'composed over ten songs of mulberry branches.'
    • 擢之为吏部侍郎 - Means 'promoted him to the position of Secretary of the Ministry of Personnel.'
    • 赏缣百疋 - Means 'rewarded him with a hundred bolts of silk.'
    • 龙朔已来 - Refers to the years after the Longshuo era (661-662 AD).
    • 歌名突厥盐 - Means 'singing the song of Turkic salt.'
    • 后周圣厯年中 - Refers to the years during the Shengli era (699-700 AD) of the Zhou Dynasty.
    • 差阎知防和匈奴 - Means 'dispatched Yan Zhifang to negotiate with the Xiongnu.'
    • 授三品春官尚书 - Means 'appointed him as the Spring Official (Ministry of Rites) with the rank of third grade.'
    • 送武延秀往娶黙啜女 - Means 'sent Wu Yanshou to marry the daughter of Moche.'
    • 送金银器物锦防衣裳 - Means 'sent gold and silver vessels, brocade, and clothing as gifts.'
    • 不可胜纪 - Means 'too numerous to count.'
    • 突厥翻动汉使并没立知微为可汗 - Means 'the Turks rebelled, captured the Han envoys, and made Yan Zhifang the Khan.'
    • 突厥盐之应 - Means 'the response to the Turkic salt song.'
    • 调露中 - Refers to the years during the Tiaolu era (679-680 AD).
    • 大帝欲封中岳 - Means 'the Great Emperor wanted to perform a ritual to honor Mount Song.'
    • 属突厥而止 - Means 'but it was stopped due to the Turks.'
    • 后又欲封土番入寇 - Means 'later, he wanted to perform a ritual to honor the soil, but the Tibetans invaded.'
    • 遂停止 - Means 'so it was stopped.'
    • 永淳年又驾幸嵩岳 - Refers to the years during the Yongchun era (682-683 AD), when the emperor visited Mount Song.
    • 謡曰 - Means 'a folk song said.'
    • 嵩山凡防层不畏登不得只畏不得登 - Means 'Mount Song has many layers, not afraid of not being able to climb, but afraid of not being able to climb.'
    • 三度征兵马傍道打腾腾 - Means 'three times the army was mobilized, and the roads were crowded with soldiers.'
    • 岳下遘疾不愈回至宫而崩 - Means 'the emperor fell ill at the foot of the mountain and died on his way back to the palace.'
    • 永淳之后天下皆唱杨栁杨栁漫头駞 - Refers to the years after Yongchun, when people sang the song of willow trees.
    • 后徐敬业犯事出栁州司马 - Means 'later, Xu Jingye committed a crime and was exiled to be the governor of Liuzhou.'
    • 遂作伪勅自授州司马 - Means 'he then forged an edict to appoint himself as the governor of Yangzhou.'
    • 杀长史陈敬之据江淮反 - Means 'killed the Chief Secretary Chen Jingzhi and rebelled in the Jianghuai region.'
    • 使李孝逸讨之斩业首驿马防入洛阳 - Means 'sent Li Xiaoyi to suppress the rebellion, and the head of Xu Jingye was brought back to Luoyang by fast horse.'
    • 杨栁杨栁漫头駞后此其应也 - Means 'the song of willow trees was a response to these events.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 永徽年 - The Yonghui era (650-655 AD) of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 神龙年 - The Shendong era (705-707 AD) of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 龙朔年 - The Longshuo era (661-662 AD) of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 后周圣厯年 - The Shengli era (699-700 AD) of the Zhou Dynasty.
    • 突厥 - The Turkic people, a powerful nomadic group in Central Asia.
    • 匈奴 - The Xiongnu, a nomadic group that historically posed a threat to China.
    • 武延秀 - Wu Yanshou, a Tang prince who was sent to marry a Turkic princess.
    • 黙啜 - Moche, a Turkic leader.
    • 调露年 - The Tiaolu era (679-680 AD) of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 大帝 - The Great Emperor, likely referring to Emperor Gaozong of Tang.
    • 中岳 - Mount Song, one of the Five Great Mountains in China.
    • 封中岳 - A ritual to honor a mountain, often performed by emperors.
    • 永淳年 - The Yongchun era (682-683 AD) of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 嵩岳 - Mount Song, also known as Songshan.
    • 杨栁 - Willow trees, often used in Chinese poetry and folk songs.
    • 徐敬业 - Xu Jingye, a Tang general who rebelled against the government.
    • 栁州 - Liuzhou, a city in modern-day Guangxi.
    • 州 - Yangzhou, a city in modern-day Jiangsu.
    • 李孝逸 - Li Xiaoyi, a Tang general who suppressed Xu Jingye's rebellion.

    After the Yonghui era, people sang the song of mulberry branches, with a woman named Wei singing it. The joy reached the Shendong era, and Wei responded to the song. Zheng Yin, a flatterer, composed over ten songs of mulberry branches and presented them to Wei, who was very pleased and promoted Zheng Yin to the position of Secretary of the Ministry of Personnel, rewarding him with a hundred bolts of silk.

    Since the Longshuo era, people have been singing the song of Turkic salt. During the Shengli era, Yan Zhifang was dispatched to negotiate with the Xiongnu and was appointed as the Spring Official with the rank of third grade. Wu Yanshou was sent to marry the daughter of Moche, and gold and silver vessels, brocade, and clothing were sent as gifts. The Turks rebelled, captured the Han envoys, and made Yan Zhifang the Khan. This was the response to the Turkic salt song.

    During the Tiaolu era, the Great Emperor wanted to perform a ritual to honor Mount Song but it was stopped due to the Turks. Later, he wanted to perform a ritual to honor the soil but the Tibetans invaded, so it was stopped. During the Yongchun era, the emperor visited Mount Song, and a folk song said, "Mount Song has many layers, not afraid of not being able to climb, but afraid of not being able to climb." Three times the army was mobilized, and the roads were crowded with soldiers. The emperor fell ill at the foot of the mountain and died on his way back to the palace.

    After the Yongchun era, people all sang the song of willow trees. Later, Xu Jingye committed a crime and was exiled to be the governor of Liuzhou. He then forged an edict to appoint himself as the governor of Yangzhou, killed the Chief Secretary Chen Jingzhi, and rebelled in the Jianghuai region. Li Xiaoyi was sent to suppress the rebellion, and the head of Xu Jingye was brought back to Luoyang by fast horse. The song of willow trees was a response to these events. [11]

    周如意年中已来始唱黄麞歌其词曰黄麞黄麞草里藏弯弓射你伤俄而契丹反叛杀都督赵翽营府陷没差总管曹仁师张遇麻仁节王孝杰前后百万众被贼败于黄麞谷诸军并没罔有孑遗黄麞之歌斯为验矣
    　　周垂拱已来苾拏儿歌词皆是邪曲后张易之小名苾拏
    　　景龙年安乐公主洛州道光坊造安乐寺用钱数百万童謡曰可怜安乐寺了了树头悬后诛逆韦并杀安乐斩首悬于竿上改为悖逆庶人
    　　神龙以后謡曰山南乌鹊窠山北金骆駞鎌柯不凿孔斧子不施柯此突厥彊盛百姓不得斫桑养蚕种木刈谷之应也
    　　景龙中谣曰可怜圣善寺身着緑毛衣牵来河里饮踏杀鲤鱼儿至景云中谯王从均州入都作乱败走投洛州而死
    　　景云中謡曰一条麻线挽天枢絶去也神武即位敕令推倒天枢收铜并入尚方此其应兆
    　　景龙中謡曰黄栢犊子挽纼断两脚地鞋绳断六月平王诛逆韦欲作乱鞋绳断者事不成阿韦是黄犊之后也 [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 俄而 - Indicates a rapid succession of events.
    • 斯为验矣 - Means 'thus proving to be true.'
    • 黄麞之歌 - A song that became a symbol or omen.
    • 邪曲 - Means 'evil and crooked.'
    • 童謡 - Means 'children's rhyme.'
    • 诛逆韦 - Means 'executed the rebel Wei.'
    • 悖逆庶人 - Means 'traitorous commoner.'
    • 乌鹊窠 - A metaphor used in the rhyme.
    • 金骆駞 - A metaphor used in the rhyme.
    • 天枢 - A monument.
    • 阿韦 - A historical figure.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周如意年中 - A specific year during the reign of Emperor Ruyi of Zhou.
    • 契丹 - A historical ethnic group.
    • 都督赵翽 - A high-ranking military official.
    • 曹仁师张遇麻仁节王孝杰 - Names of military leaders.
    • 黄麞谷 - A specific location.
    • 周垂拱 - A reign title.
    • 苾拏儿 - A term used in the song lyrics.
    • 张易之 - A historical figure.
    • 安乐公主 - A princess who built a temple.
