    • 周瀛州刺史独孤庄酷虐有贼问不承庄引前曰若健儿一一具吐放汝遂还巾带贼并吐之诸官以为必放顷庄曰将我作具来乃一铁钩长丈余甚铦利以绳挂于树间谓贼曰汝不闻健儿钩下死今以胲钩之遣壮士掣其绳则钩出于脑矣谓司法曰此法何似答曰吊民伐罪深得其宜庄大笑后庄左降施州刺史染病惟忆人肉部下有奴婢死者遣人割肋下肉食之嵗余卒 - During the Zhou dynasty, the governor of Yingzhou, Dugu Zhuang, was cruel, when a bandit did not confess, he said if he were a soldier, he would confess everything and be released, he then returned his headband, the bandit confessed everything, all officials thought he would be released, but soon Dugu said bring me a tool, it was a long iron hook, very sharp, and he hung it on a tree, he told the bandit, you have never heard of soldiers dying under hooks, now I will hook you and send a strong man to pull the rope, and the hook will come out of your brain, he asked the judge, how is this method? The judge said it is appropriate to pacify the people and punish the guilty, Dugu laughed, later he was demoted to be the governor of Shizhou, he fell ill and only thought of human flesh, he had his dead slaves and servants' rib meat cut and eaten, he died after a year.
    • 周推事使索元礼时人号为索使讯囚作铁笼头【呼角反】其头仍加揳焉多至脑裂髓出又为凤晒翅狝猴鑚火等以椽闗手足而转之并斫骨至碎又悬囚于梁下以石缒头其酷法如此元礼外域人薛师之假父后坐贿流死岭南 - During the Zhou dynasty, the interrogator Suyanli was called the 'Suo' interrogator, he interrogated prisoners by making an iron cage for their heads, he also added wedges, often causing their brains to split and their marrow to come out, he also made phoenixes spread their wings, macaques drill fire, etc., he bound their hands and feet with wooden poles and turned them, and chopped their bones to pieces, he also hung prisoners under the beams and lowered them with stones, his cruel methods were like this, Suyanli was an outsider, he was the foster father of Xue Shizhi, later he was convicted of corruption and exiled to die in Lingnan.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周 - Refers to the Zhou dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 临安 - The capital of the Southern Song dynasty, now known as Hangzhou.
    • 岭南 - Refers to the southern regions of China.
    • 瀛州 - An ancient Chinese state.
    • 施州 - An ancient Chinese state.
    • 岭南首领陈元光 - Chen Yuanguang, a historical figure who was a leader in the southern regions.
    • 独孤庄 - Dugu Zhuang, a historical figure who was a governor.
    • 索元礼 - Suyanli, a historical figure who was an interrogator.
    • 薛师之 - Xue Shizhi, a historical figure who was the foster son of Suyanli.

    During the Zhou dynasty, the magistrate of Lin'an, Xue Zhen, had a penchant for eating human flesh. A creditor and a slave arrived at Lin'an and stayed at an inn. Xue Zhen invited them to drink with him. He got them drunk, killed them, and chopped them into pieces, then cooked them with mercury and melted down to the bones. Later, he wanted to eat his wife, but she became aware of his intentions and fled. The county magistrate investigated and uncovered the truth. The recorder of the state of Lin'an reported this to the imperial court, and Xue Zhen was sentenced to a hundred strokes with a bamboo cane and died.

    During the Zhou dynasty, the leader of Lingnan, Chen Yuanguang, set up a banquet and ordered a servant to serve wine. Chen became angry and ordered him to be dragged out and killed. His body was boiled until it was soft and served to the guests. Later, his hands were shown to the guests, who were terrified and choked and vomited.

    During the Zhou dynasty, the governor of Yingzhou, Dugu Zhuang, was cruel. When a bandit did not confess, he said if he were a soldier, he would confess everything and be released. He then returned his headband, and the bandit confessed everything, and all officials thought he would be released. But soon Dugu said bring me a tool, it was a long iron hook, very sharp, and he hung it on a tree. He told the bandit, you have never heard of soldiers dying under hooks, now I will hook you and send a strong man to pull the rope, and the hook will come out of your brain. He asked the judge, how is this method? The judge said it is appropriate to pacify the people and punish the guilty, Dugu laughed. Later he was demoted to be the governor of Shizhou. He fell ill and only thought of human flesh. He had his dead slaves and servants' rib meat cut and eaten, and he died after a year.

    During the Zhou dynasty, the interrogator Suyanli was called the 'Suo' interrogator. He interrogated prisoners by making an iron cage for their heads and also added wedges, often causing their brains to split and their marrow to come out. He also made phoenixes spread their wings, macaques drill fire, etc., he bound their hands and feet with wooden poles and turned them, and chopped their bones to pieces. He also hung prisoners under the beams and lowered them with stones. His cruel methods were like this. Suyanli was an outsider, he was the foster father of Xue Shizhi. Later he was convicted of corruption and exiled to die in Lingnan. [24]

    周来俊臣罗织人罪皆先进状敕依奏即籍没徐有功出死囚亦先进状某人罪合免敕依然后断雪有功好出罪皆先奉进止非是自专张汤探人主之情葢为此也
    　　羽林将军元楷三代告密得官男彦玮告刘诚之破家彦玮处侍御史二年七月三日楷以反逆诛家口配流可谓积恶之家殃有余也
    　　周补阙乔知之有婢碧玉姝艳能歌舞有文华知之时幸为之不婚伪魏王武承嗣暂借教姬人妆梳纳之更不放还知之知之乃作緑珠怨以寄之其词曰石家金谷重新声明珠十斛买娉婷此日可怜偏自许此时歌舞得人情君家闺阁不曾观好将歌舞借人看意气雄豪非分理骄矜势力横相干辞君去君终不忍徒劳掩袂伤鈆粉百年离恨在高楼一代容顔为君尽碧玉读诗饮泣不食三日投井而死承嗣撩出尸于裙带上得诗大怒乃讽罗织人告之遂斩知之于南市破家籍没 [25]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [25]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 羅織人罪 - Fabricating charges against someone, often for political purposes.
    • 先進狀 - Submitting a petition first, indicating a procedural step in the legal process.
    • 敕依奏 - The imperial decree follows the petition, indicating that the emperor's decision is based on the submitted document.
    • 籍沒 - Confiscation and seizure of property, often as a punishment.
    • 出死囚 - Releasing a death row inmate, indicating a judicial decision to spare someone's life.
    • 進止 - Making a recommendation or proposal, often to a superior authority.
    • 自專 - Acting independently without seeking approval, often with negative connotations.
    • 探人主之情 - Anticipating the emperor's intentions, indicating a strategy of aligning actions with the ruler's preferences.
    • 積惡之家 - A family with accumulated evil, suggesting a lineage of wrongdoing.
    • 殃有余也 - Suffering remains, indicating that the consequences of past actions continue.
    • 告密得官 - Gaining an official position through denunciation, indicating a corrupt practice.
    • 破家 - Ruining a family, indicating severe punishment.
    • 侍御史 - A high-ranking official in the imperial court, responsible for various duties including investigation and oversight.
    • 反逆 - Treason or rebellion, indicating a serious crime against the state.
    • 配流 - Exiling, indicating a form of punishment.
    • 緑珠怨 - A poem written by Qiao Zhizhi, expressing sorrow and longing.
    • 石家金谷 - The famous garden of Shi Chong, a wealthy and influential figure during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 聲明珠十斛 - Ten measures of pearls, indicating immense wealth.
    • 娉婷 - Graceful and beautiful, describing a woman's appearance.
    • 君家闺阁 - Your family's inner chambers, referring to the private domain of a woman.
    • 意气雄豪 - Proud and heroic, describing a person's demeanor.
    • 骄矜势力 - Arrogant and powerful, describing a person's attitude and status.
    • 辞君去君终不忍 - To leave you, but I cannot bear to part, expressing deep emotional attachment.
    • 徒劳掩袂伤铅粉 - To vainly hide one's tears, indicating futile efforts to conceal sorrow.
    • 百年离恨 - A century of separation and sorrow, indicating long-lasting grief.
    • 一代容颜 - A generation's beauty, referring to the beauty of a particular era.
    • 碧玉读诗饮泣不食三日投井而死 - Describes the tragic fate of Bi Yu, who reads a poem and weeps so much that she refuses food for three days and ultimately drowns herself.
    • 撩出尸于裙带上得诗 - To pull out the poem from the belt, indicating the discovery of the poem after Bi Yu's death.
    • 罗织人告之 - To fabricate charges and report, indicating a false accusation.
    • 斩知之于南市 - To execute him in the southern market, indicating a public execution.
    • 破家籍没 - To ruin the family and confiscate their property, indicating severe punishment.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周来俊臣 - A notorious official during the Tang Dynasty known for his cruel and arbitrary methods of obtaining confessions.
    • 徐有功 - A respected judge during the Tang Dynasty known for his fairness and leniency.
    • 羽林将军元楷 - A high-ranking military official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 魏王武承嗣 - A powerful nobleman and relative of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 乔知之 - A poet and official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 碧玉 - A concubine of Qiao Zhizhi, known for her beauty and literary talent.
    • 緑珠怨 - A poem written by Qiao Zhizhi, expressing his sorrow over the loss of his concubine, Bi Yu.
    • 石家金谷 - A famous garden owned by Shi Chong, a wealthy and influential figure during the Jin Dynasty, known for its opulence and beauty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 周来俊臣 - A notorious official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 徐有功 - A respected judge during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 羽林将军元楷 - A high-ranking military official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 魏王武承嗣 - A powerful nobleman and relative of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 乔知之 - A poet and official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 碧玉 - A concubine of Qiao Zhizhi.
    • 緑珠怨 - A poem written by Qiao Zhizhi.
    • 石家金谷 - A famous garden owned by Shi Chong.

    Zhou Lai Junchen fabricated charges against people and submitted petitions first, and the imperial decree followed the petition, leading to the confiscation and seizure of their property. Xu Yougong released a death row inmate and also submitted a petition first, stating that a certain person's crime should be forgiven, and the imperial decree followed the petition, and then the case was cleared. Xu Yougong often released death row inmates and always made recommendations to the emperor, not acting independently. Zhou Buque Qiao Zhizhi had a concubine named Bi Yu, who was graceful, beautiful, and talented in poetry and music. Qiao Zhizhi favored her and did not marry her. False Wei Prince Wu Chengsi temporarily borrowed her to teach the women in his household how to dress and comb their hair, and then did not return her. Qiao Zhizhi knew this and wrote the "Green Pearl Lament" to express his sorrow. Bi Yu read the poem, wept, refused to eat for three days, and ultimately drowned herself. Wu Chengsi pulled the poem out of her belt and discovered it. The person who fabricated charges reported him, and he was executed in the southern market, and his family was ruined and their property confiscated. [25]

    周张易之为控监弟昌宗为秘书监昌仪为洛阳令竞为豪侈易之为大铁笼置鵞鸭于其内当中取起炭火铜盆贮五味汁鵞鸭绕火走渴即饮汁火炙痛即回表里皆熟毛落尽肉赤烘烘乃死昌宗活拦驴于小室内起炭火置五味汁如前法昌仪取铁橛钉入地缚狗四足于橛上放鹰鹞活按其肉食肉尽而死未死号呌酸楚不复可聴易之曾过昌仪忆马膓取从骑破脇取膓良久乃死后诛易之昌宗等百姓脔割其肉肥白如猪肪煎炙而食昌仪打双脚折抉取心肝而后死斩其首送都谚云走马报 [26]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [26]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 控监 - A title or position, possibly related to a type of official or supervisor. The exact meaning is unclear due to the rarity of the character ''.
    • 竞为豪侈 - A phrase meaning 'competing in extravagance,' indicating a rivalry in displaying wealth and luxury.
    • 大铁笼 - A large iron cage, used here to describe a device for cooking.
    • 鵞鸭 - Geese and ducks, common domesticated birds.
    • 铜盆贮五味汁 - A copper basin containing a mixture of five flavors, likely a broth or sauce.
    • 火炙痛即回 - The birds would run back to the fire when it was painful, indicating a method of cooking.
    • 表里皆熟 - The inside and outside of the birds would be cooked through.
    • 毛落尽肉赤 - The feathers would fall off, and the meat would turn red.
    • 烘烘乃死 - The birds would die when they were thoroughly cooked.
    • 活拦驴 - A live donkey, used here in a similar cooking method.
    • 铁橛钉入地 - An iron stake driven into the ground, used to secure the donkey.
    • 缚狗四足于橛上 - Tying the dog's four legs to the stake.
    • 放鹰鹞活按其肉 - Releasing eagles and falcons to attack the live dog, a cruel method of cooking.
    • 未死号呌酸楚 - The dog would still be alive and crying out in pain.
    • 不复可聴 - The sound would be unbearable to listen to.
    • 走马报 - A message delivered by horse, indicating the speed of communication.
    • 打双脚折 - The dog's legs would be broken.
    • 抉取心肝 - The heart and liver would be pulled out.
    • 斩其首送都 - The heads would be cut off and sent to the capital.
    • 谚云 - A proverb or saying, indicating a common belief or practice.
    • 肥白如猪肪 - The meat would be as white and fatty as pork fat.
    • 煎炙而食 - The meat would be fried or grilled and eaten.
    • 而后死 - The dog would die after this process.
    • 谚云走马报 - A proverb indicating that the news of their death was spread quickly.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周张易之 - Zhang Yizhi, a prominent figure during the Tang Dynasty, known for his close relationship with Emperor Zhongzong.
    • 昌宗 - Zhang Changzong, Zhang Yizhi's brother, also a close associate of Emperor Zhongzong.
    • 昌仪 - Zhang Changyi, another brother of Zhang Yizhi, also involved in the court.
    • 秘书监 - A high-ranking official responsible for managing the imperial library and archives.
    • 洛阳令 - The magistrate of Luoyang, a significant administrative position.
    • 马膓 - A horse's intestine.
    • 百姓 - The common people.
    • 猪肪 - Pork fat.
    • 谚云 - A proverb or saying, indicating a common belief or practice.
    • 都 - The capital.

    Proper Nouns

    • 周张易之 - Zhang Yizhi, a prominent figure during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 昌宗 - Zhang Changzong, Zhang Yizhi's brother.
    • 昌仪 - Zhang Changyi, another brother of Zhang Yizhi.
    • 控监 - A specific title, possibly related to a type of official or supervisor.
    • 秘书监 - A high-ranking official responsible for managing the imperial library and archives.
    • 洛阳令 - The magistrate of Luoyang.
    • 大铁笼 - A large iron cage.
    • 鵞鸭 - Geese and ducks.
    • 铜盆 - A copper basin.
    • 五味汁 - A mixture of five flavors.
    • 铁橛 - An iron stake.
    • 鹰鹞 - Eagles and falcons.
    • 马膓 - A horse's intestine.
    • 百姓 - The common people.
    • 猪肪 - Pork fat.
    • 谚云 - A proverb.
    • 都 - The capital.

    Zhang Yizhi, a prominent figure during the Tang Dynasty, held the position of 控监 (a specific title, possibly related to a type of official or supervisor). His brother Changzong served as the Secretary Monarch, and another brother Changyi was the magistrate of Luoyang. They competed in extravagance. Zhang Yizhi used a large iron cage to place geese and ducks inside. He lit a fire in the center, placed a copper basin with a mixture of five flavors, and the birds would run around the fire. When thirsty, they would drink the broth, and when the fire was painful, they would return. The birds were cooked through, their feathers fell off, and the meat turned red. They died when thoroughly cooked. Changzong used a live donkey in a similar manner, placing it in a small room, lighting a fire, and using the same method. Changyi used an iron stake driven into the ground, tying a dog's four legs to it, and releasing eagles and falcons to attack the live dog. The dog was eaten after its meat was consumed, but it was still alive and crying out in pain, making the sound unbearable to listen to. Zhang Yizhi once passed by Changyi and remembered a horse's intestine. He took a horse from his entourage, broke its ribs, and extracted the intestine, which took a long time to die. After their execution, the common people cut and ate the meat, which was as white and fatty as pork fat, fried or grilled. Changyi had his legs broken, his heart and liver pulled out, and then he died. His head was cut off and sent to the capital. A proverb says that the news of their death was spread quickly. [26]

    周秋官侍郎周兴推劾残忍法外苦楚无所不为时人号牛头阿婆百姓怨谤兴乃牓门判曰被告之人问皆称枉斩决之后咸悉无言
    　　周侍御史侯思止醴泉卖饼食人也罗告准例酧五品于上前索御史上曰卿不识字对曰獬豸岂识字但为国触罪人而已遂授之凡推勘杀戮甚众更无余语但谓囚徒曰不用你书言笔语但还我白司马若不肯来俊即与你孟青横遭苦楚非命者不可胜数白司马坂者北邙山司马坂也来俊者中丞来俊臣也孟青者将军孟青棒也后坐私蓄锦朝堂决杀之 [27]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [27]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 周秋官侍郎 - Refers to Zhou Xing, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his cruel methods of interrogation.
    • 推劾残忍 - Means 'cruel methods of investigation and prosecution.'
    • 法外苦楚 - Refers to 'tortures beyond the law.'
    • 牛头阿婆 - A nickname given to Zhou Xing due to his cruel and intimidating demeanor.
    • 百姓怨谤 - Means 'the people's complaints and slander.'
    • 牓门判曰 - A formal announcement or edict posted on the door.
    • 被告之人问皆称枉 - Means 'all accused people claim they are innocent.'
    • 斩决之后咸悉无言 - Means 'after execution, there is no more speech.'
    • 周侍御史侯思止 - Refers to Hou Sizhi, a low-born individual who rose to power through informer activities.
    • 醴泉卖饼食人也 - Means 'a person from Lique who sold bread and pies.'
    • 罗告准例酧五品 - Means 'he was rewarded with a fifth-rank official position for reporting crimes.'
    • 于上前索御史上曰 - Means 'he asked the emperor for a higher position.'
    • 卿不识字 - Means 'you don't know how to read.'
    • 獬豸岂识字 - Means 'a mythical creature (Xiezhi) doesn't need to know how to read.'
    • 但为国触罪人而已 - Means 'but it is for the country to touch the guilty.'
    • 推勘杀戮甚众 - Means 'he investigated and killed many people.'
    • 白司马坂者北邙山司马坂也 - Means 'the White司马Bamboo is the司马Bamboo on the North Mausoleum Hill.'
    • 来俊者中丞来俊臣也 - Means 'Lai Jun is the Censor-in-Chief Lai Junchen.'
    • 孟青者将军孟青棒也 - Means 'Meng Qing is the General Meng Qing's rod.'
    • 后坐私蓄锦朝堂决杀之 - Means 'later, he was executed for privately storing silk.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周兴 - A notorious Tang Dynasty official known for his cruel methods of interrogation.
    • 侯思止 - A low-born individual who became a high-ranking official through informer activities.
    • 醴泉 - A place name, likely referring to a specific region or town.
    • 北邙山 - A famous burial ground in Luoyang, Henan Province.
    • 来俊臣 - A notorious Tang Dynasty official known for his cruel methods of interrogation and political purges.
    • 孟青 - A fictional or symbolic name, possibly referring to a type of torture instrument or a person known for their harsh methods.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This long sentence describes Zhou Xing's cruel methods and the subsequent complaints from the people.
    • This sentence describes Hou Sizhi's rise to power and his cruel methods of interrogation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 周兴 - A high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 侯思止 - A low-born individual who became a high-ranking official.
    • 来俊臣 - A notorious Tang Dynasty official.
    • 北邙山 - A famous burial ground in Luoyang, Henan Province.

    During the Tang Dynasty, Zhou Xing, a high-ranking official known for his cruel methods of investigation and prosecution, was notorious for his use of torture beyond the law. People called him "Old Lady with a Bull's Head" due to his intimidating demeanor, and the populace complained and slandered him. Zhou Xing then posted an edict on the door, stating that all accused individuals claimed they were innocent, and after execution, there was no more speech.

    Zhou Sizhi, a low-born individual from Lique who sold bread and pies, rose to power through informer activities. He was rewarded with a fifth-rank official position for reporting crimes and asked the emperor for a higher position. When the emperor asked if he knew how to read, he replied, "A mythical creature (Xiezhi) doesn't need to know how to read; it is for the country to touch the guilty." The emperor then appointed him, and he investigated and killed many people. He had no other words but told the prisoners, "Don't use your written words, just return my White司马. If you refuse, Lai Jun will give you Meng Qing, and you will suffer greatly." Those who died by non-fate were countless. The White司马Bamboo is the司马Bamboo on the North Mausoleum Hill, Lai Jun is the Censor-in-Chief Lai Junchen, and Meng Qing is the General Meng Qing's rod. Later, he was executed for privately storing silk. [27]

    周明堂尉吉顼夜与监察御史王助同宿王助以亲故为说綦连耀男大觉小觉云应两角麒麟也耀字光翟言光宅天下也顼明日録状付来俊臣敕差河内王懿宗推诛王助等四十一人皆破家后俊臣犯事司刑断死进状三日不出朝野怪之上入苑吉顼拢马上问在外有何事意顼奏曰臣幸预控为陛下耳目在外惟怪来俊臣状不出上曰俊臣于国有功朕思之耳顼奏曰于安逺告虺贞反其事并验今贞为成州司马俊臣聚结不逞诬遘贤良贿如山寃魂满路国之贼也何足惜哉上令状出诛俊臣于西市敕追于安逺还除尚食奉御顼有力焉除顼中丞赐绯顼理綦连耀事以为己功授天官侍郎平章事与河内王竞出为温州司马卒 [28]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [28]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 周明堂尉 - A title indicating a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, specifically associated with the Temple of Heaven in the capital.
    • 监察御史 - A title for an official responsible for overseeing the conduct of other officials.
    • 两角麒麟 - A mythical creature with two horns, symbolizing auspiciousness and nobility.
    • 光宅天下 - A phrase suggesting that someone will rule the world, possibly a prophecy or a claim to power.
    • 破家 - To destroy a family or household, often used in the context of punishment or disgrace.
    • 控 - A term that is unclear, possibly referring to a type of control or restraint.
    • 聚结不逞 - To gather together and act in a lawless or rebellious manner.
    • 诬遘贤良 - To falsely accuse virtuous people.
    • 贿如山 - To accumulate bribes like a mountain, indicating corruption.
    • 冤魂满路 - The streets are filled with the spirits of the wrongfully accused, suggesting widespread injustice.
    • 国之贼也 - A traitor to the state.
    • 尚食奉御 - A title for an official responsible for the imperial kitchen.
    • 中丞 - A title for a high-ranking official, often associated with the censorate.
    • 天官侍郎 - A title for an official in the Ministry of Personnel.
    • 平章事 - A title for a senior minister, often equivalent to a chancellor.
    • 温州司马 - A title for an official in the state of Wenzhou, possibly a lower-ranking position.
    • 除 - To appoint or remove from office.
    • 赐绯 - To be granted a red robe, a sign of imperial favor or reward.
    • 理綦连耀事 - To handle the affairs of Qí Lián Yào, possibly referring to a legal or administrative matter.
    • 授 - To grant or appoint.
    • 天官侍郎平章事 - A high-ranking official with significant administrative and advisory roles.
    • 竞出为温州司马 - To be appointed to a position in Wenzhou, possibly indicating a demotion or transfer.
    • 卒 - To die, often used in official records to denote the end of a person's career or life.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 綦连耀 - Qí Lián Yào, a historical figure or person of significance, whose name is used in a cryptic phrase.
    • 两角麒麟 - A mythical creature symbolizing auspiciousness and nobility, often used in Chinese literature and folklore.
    • 光宅天下 - A phrase suggesting a claim to power or a prophecy of ruling the world.
    • 来俊臣 - Lái Jùnchén, a notorious eunuch and official known for his cruel methods of torture and persecution during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 河内王懿宗 - Hé Nèi Wáng Yìzōng, a high-ranking official or noble during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 于安远 - Yú Ānyuǎn, an official or person involved in a political case.
    • 虺贞 - Huǐ Zhēn, a person who was accused of treason.
    • 成州司马 - A position in the state of Chengzhou, possibly a lower-ranking official.
    • 尚食奉御 - An official responsible for the imperial kitchen, often a position of trust.
    • 中丞 - A high-ranking official, often associated with the censorate.
    • 天官侍郎 - An official in the Ministry of Personnel, responsible for appointments and promotions.
    • 平章事 - A senior minister, often equivalent to a chancellor.
    • 温州司马 - A position in the state of Wenzhou, possibly a lower-ranking official.
    • 赐绯 - A red robe, a sign of imperial favor or reward.
    • 理綦连耀事 - Handling the affairs of Qí Lián Yào, possibly a legal or administrative matter.
    • 授 - Granting or appointing a position.
    • 天官侍郎平章事 - A high-ranking official with significant administrative and advisory roles.
    • 竞出为温州司马 - Being appointed to a position in Wenzhou, possibly indicating a demotion or transfer.
    • 卒 - To die, often used in official records to denote the end of a person's career or life.

    Proper Nouns

    • 周明堂尉吉顼 - Ji Xū, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 监察御史王助 - Wang Zhù, an official responsible for oversight.
    • 綦连耀 - Qí Lián Yào, a historical figure or person of significance.
    • 两角麒麟 - A mythical creature symbolizing auspiciousness and nobility.
    • 光宅天下 - A phrase suggesting a claim to power or a prophecy of ruling the world.
    • 来俊臣 - Lái Jùnchén, a notorious eunuch and official known for his cruel methods of torture and persecution during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 河内王懿宗 - Hé Nèi Wáng Yìzōng, a high-ranking official or noble during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 于安远 - Yú Ānyuǎn, an official or person involved in a political case.
    • 虺贞 - Huǐ Zhēn, a person who was accused of treason.
    • 成州司马 - A position in the state of Chengzhou, possibly a lower-ranking official.
    • 尚食奉御 - An official responsible for the imperial kitchen, often a position of trust.
    • 中丞 - A high-ranking official, often associated with the censorate.
    • 天官侍郎 - An official in the Ministry of Personnel, responsible for appointments and promotions.
    • 平章事 - A senior minister, often equivalent to a chancellor.
    • 温州司马 - A position in the state of Wenzhou, possibly a lower-ranking official.
    • 赐绯 - A red robe, a sign of imperial favor or reward.
    • 理綦连耀事 - Handling the affairs of Qí Lián Yào, possibly a legal or administrative matter.
    • 授 - Granting or appointing a position.
    • 天官侍郎平章事 - A high-ranking official with significant administrative and advisory roles.
    • 竞出为温州司马 - Being appointed to a position in Wenzhou, possibly indicating a demotion or transfer.
    • 卒 - To die, often used in official records to denote the end of a person's career or life.

    The Minister of the Temple of Heaven, Ji Xu, spent the night with the Censorial Inspector, Wang Zhu, at an inn. Wang Zhu, due to their familial connection, spoke of a prophecy concerning Qí Lián Yào’s sons, Dà Jué and Xiǎo Jué, who were said to embody the auspicious symbol of a two-horned qilin (mythical creature). Qí Lián Yào himself was known by the alias "Guāng Zhì," which was interpreted as a foretelling of his dominion over the world. The next day, Ji Xu recorded the details and submitted them to Lái Jùnchén, who then had the case investigated by Hé Nèi Wáng Yìzōng. As a result, Wang Zhu and forty others were executed, and their families were disgraced. Later, when Lái Jùnchén himself became involved in a scandal, the judiciary sentenced him to death, but the emperor did not issue the order for three days, causing public astonishment. When the emperor entered the imperial garden, Ji Xu approached him on horseback and asked what was troubling him. Ji Xu replied that he was fortunate to serve as the emperor’s eyes and ears, and he found it strange that the execution order for Lái Jùnchén had not yet been issued. The emperor responded that Lái Jùnchén had served the state well, and he was still considering the matter. Ji Xu then reported that Yú Ānyuǎn had accused Huǐ Zhēn of treason, and the evidence supported the claim; Huǐ Zhēn was now serving as a magistrate in Chengzhou. Ji Xu further stated that Lái Jùnchén had gathered a group of lawless individuals, falsely accused virtuous people, and accumulated bribes like a mountain, leaving the streets filled with the spirits of the wrongfully accused. Such a person was a traitor to the state, and what was there to pity? The emperor then ordered the execution of Lái Jùnchén in the western market, reinstated Yú Ānyuǎn to his former position as a courtier responsible for the imperial kitchen, and rewarded Ji Xu with the title of Censorial Inspector and the honor of wearing a red robe. Ji Xu was later appointed to handle the affairs of Qí Lián Yào, and for this service, he was granted the titles of Minister of Personnel and Senior Minister. However, he was eventually demoted to the position of magistrate in Wenzhou, where he died. [28]

    成王千里使岭南取大蛇长八九尺以绳缚口横于门限之下州县参谒者呼令入门但知直视无复瞻仰蛇而惊惶惧僵仆被蛇绕数匝良久解之以为戏笑又取及鳖令人脱衣纵等齧其体终不肯放死而后已其人酸痛号呼不可复言王与姬妾共看以为玩乐然后以竹刺等口遂齧竹而放人艾炙鳖背炙痛而放口人被试者皆失魂至死不平复矣
    　　朔方总管张亶好杀时有突厥投化亶乃作檄文骂黙啜言词甚不逊书其腹背凿其肌肤湼之以墨炙之以火不胜楚痛日夜作虫鸟鸣然后送与黙啜识字者宣讫脔而杀之匈奴怨望不敢降 [29]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [29]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 成王千里使 - Refers to a king (possibly a historical figure) sending someone over a long distance.
    • 取大蛇 - Capturing or fetching a large snake.
    • 以绳缚口 - Tying the snake's mouth with a rope.
    • 横于门限之下 - Placing it horizontally under the threshold of a door.
    • 州县参谒者 - Officials from various states and counties who pay homage.
    • 呼令入门 - Calling them to enter.
    • 但知直视无复瞻仰 - They can only look straight ahead and not look around.
    • 惊惶惧 - Being frightened.
    • 僵仆 - Collapsing.
    • 被蛇绕数匝 - Being wrapped by the snake several times.
    • 良久解之 - After a long time, they are freed.
    • 以为戏笑 - It is treated as a joke.
    • 又取龟及鳖 - Capturing turtles and tortoises.
    • 令人脱衣 - Making people remove their clothes.
    • 纵龟等啮其体 - Allowing the turtles to bite their bodies.
    • 终不肯放死而后已 - They do not release them until they die.
    • 其人酸痛号呼 - Suffering pain and crying out.
    • 不可复言 - Unable to speak anymore.
    • 王与姬妾共看 - The king and his concubines watch together.
    • 以为玩乐 - Treating it as entertainment.
    • 然后以竹刺龟等口 - Then using bamboo to poke the turtles' mouths.
    • 遂啮竹而放人 - The turtles bite the bamboo and release the people.
    • 艾炙鳖背 - Burning the tortoise's back with mugwort.
    • 炙痛而放口 - Causing pain and releasing the people.
    • 人被试者皆失魂至死不平复矣 - All of them lose their souls and cannot recover even after death.
    • 朔方总管张亶好杀 - The governor of the northern border region, fond of killing.
    • 时有突厥投化 - The Turks who surrendered.
    • 作檄文骂黙啜 - Writing a proclamation to insult Mochuo.
    • 言词甚不逊 - The words are extremely disrespectful.
    • 书其腹背 - Writing on their backs.
    • 凿其肌肤 - Carving into their flesh.
    • 湼之以墨 - Staining with ink.
    • 炙之以火 - Roasting with fire.
    • 不胜楚痛 - Unbearable pain.
    • 日夜作虫鸟鸣 - They make sounds like insects and birds day and night.
    • 然后送与黙啜 - Then sending them to Mochuo.
    • 识字者宣讫 - The literate person reads the proclamation.
    • 脔而杀之 - Cutting them into pieces and killing them.
    • 匈奴怨望 - The Xiongnu are resentful.
    • 不敢降 - They dare not surrender.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 成王 - Likely refers to a historical king, possibly from the Han or Tang dynasties.
    • 岭南 - Refers to the southern regions of China, historically known for its exotic and dangerous wildlife.
    • 突厥 - Refers to the Turkic tribes, who were often in conflict with Chinese empires.
    • 黙啜 - Refers to Mochuo, a prominent Turkic leader during the Tang dynasty.
    • 朔方总管 - Refers to the governor of the northern border region, a key administrative position in Chinese history.

    Proper Nouns

    • 成王 - A historical king, possibly from the Han or Tang dynasties.
    • 岭南 - The southern regions of China.
    • 突厥 - The Turkic tribes.
    • 黙啜 - Mochuo, a prominent Turkic leader during the Tang dynasty.
    • 朔方总管 - The governor of the northern border region.
    • 张亶 - The governor of the northern border region.

    The King of Cheng sent someone over a thousand miles to the southern regions to fetch a large snake, about eight to nine feet long, and tied its mouth with a rope, placing it horizontally under the threshold of the door. Officials from various states and counties who paid homage were called to enter but could only look straight ahead and not look around. They were frightened, collapsed, and were wrapped by the snake several times. After a long time, they were freed and treated as a joke. The king then captured turtles and tortoises, made people remove their clothes, and allowed the turtles to bite their bodies. The turtles did not release them until they died. The people suffered severe pain, cried out, and could not speak anymore. The king and his concubines watched together, treating it as entertainment. Then, using bamboo, they poked the turtles' mouths, and the turtles bit the bamboo and released the people. They burned the tortoises' backs with mugwort, causing pain, and released the people who were tested. All of them lost their souls and could not recover even after death.

    The Governor of the Northern Border, Zhang Dan, was fond of killing. When the Turks surrendered, he wrote a proclamation to insult Mochuo, with words that were extremely disrespectful. He wrote on their backs, carved into their flesh, stained them with ink, and roasted them with fire, causing unbearable pain. They made sounds like insects and birds day and night. Then, he sent them to Mochuo, and the literate person read the proclamation. They were cut into pieces and killed. The Xiongnu were resentful and dared not surrender. [29]

    殿中侍御史王旭括宅中别宅女妇风声色目有稍不承者以绳勒其隂令壮士弹竹击之酸痛不可忍倒悬一女妇以石锤其发遣证与长安尉房恒奸经三日不承女妇曰侍郎如此苦毒儿死必诉于防司配入宫必申于主上终不相放旭惧乃舍之
    　　监察御史李嵩李全交殿中王旭京师号为三豹嵩为赤□豹交为白额豹旭为黒豹皆狼戾不轨鸩毒无仪礼性狂疎精神惨刻每讯囚必铺卧体削竹籖指方梁压髁碎瓦搘膝遣仙人献果玉女登梯犊子悬驹驴儿拔橛凤皇晒翅狝猴钻火上麦索下防单人不聊生囚皆乞死肆情锻錬证是为非任意指麾空为实周公孔子请伏杀人伯夷叔齐求其刼罪讯劾干壍水必有期推鞫湿泥尘非不久来俊臣乞为弟子索元礼求作门生被追者皆相谓曰牵牛付虎未有出期防鼠与猫终无脱日妻子永别友朋长辞京中人相要作咒曰若违心负教横遭三豹其毒害也如此 [30]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [30]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 殿中侍御史 - High-ranking official in the imperial court, responsible for oversight and investigation.
    • 括宅中别宅女妇风声色目 - Investigation into the women living in separate houses within a residence, focusing on their reputations and appearances.
    • 以绳勒其隂 - Cruel method of torture involving binding the victim’s genital area with a rope.
    • 遣壮士弹竹击之 - Inflicting pain by having strong men hit the victim with bamboo sticks.
    • 倒悬一女妇以石锤其发 - Method of torture where a woman is hung upside down and struck on the head with a stone.
    • 证与长安尉房恒奸 - Accusing someone of adultery with a government official.
    • 经三日不承 - Refusing to confess for three days.
    • 侍郎如此苦毒儿死必诉于防司 - Threatening to report the torture to the authorities if the victim dies.
    • 配入宫必申于主上 - Implies that if the victim is sent to the palace, they will appeal directly to the emperor.
    • 旭惧乃舍之 - Wang Xu fears the consequences and releases the victim.
    • 监察御史李嵩李全交殿中王旭京师号为三豹 - Refers to Wang Xu, Li Song, and Li Quan as the 'Three Tigers' of the capital, known for their brutal methods.
    • 嵩为赤□豹 - Describes Li Song as the 'Red Tiger.'
    • 交为白额豹 - Describes Li Quan as the 'White Tiger.'
    • 旭为黒豹 - Describes Wang Xu as the 'Black Tiger.'
    • 皆狼戾不轨 - Means they were all ruthless and lawless.
    • 鸩毒无仪 - Refers to using poison without restraint.
    • 性狂疎 - Describes their personalities as wild and careless.
    • 精神惨刻 - Means they were cruel and harsh in spirit.
    • 每讯囚必铺卧体削竹籖指方梁压髁碎瓦搘膝 - Detailed description of the tortures inflicted on prisoners, including binding them, poking them with bamboo sticks, pressing on their knees, and using broken tiles.
    • 遣仙人献果玉女登梯犊子悬驹驴儿拔橛凤皇晒翅狝猴钻火上麦索下防单人不聊生囚皆乞死 - List of tortures using various metaphors and objects, such as making prisoners climb ladders, hang upside down, and suffer from extreme conditions.
    • 肆情锻錬证是为非 - Describes the arbitrary and malicious nature of their interrogations.
    • 任意指麾空为实 - Means they would fabricate evidence and claim it as true.
    • 周公孔子请伏杀人 - Metaphorical reference to even revered figures like Zhou Gong and Confucius being forced to confess to crimes.
    • 伯夷叔齐求其刼罪 - Another metaphorical reference to the virtuous figures Bo Yi and Shu Qi being accused of crimes.
    • 讯劾干壍水必有期 - Implies that interrogations would continue until the victim was exhausted or dead.
    • 推鞫湿泥尘非不久来俊臣乞为弟子索元礼求作门生 - Describes the victims being treated like students or apprentices of these torturers.
    • 被追者皆相谓曰牵牛付虎未有出期防鼠与猫终无脱日 - Metaphorical expression comparing the victims' fate to that of a cow being led to a tiger or a mouse to a cat, implying inevitable death.
    • 妻子永别友朋长辞 - Describes the victims' families and friends being permanently separated from them.
    • 京中人相要作咒曰若违心负教横遭三豹 - People in the capital would swear oaths, threatening to face the 'Three Tigers' if they betrayed their principles.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 殿中侍御史 - High-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, responsible for oversight and investigation.
    • 长安尉房恒 - Government official in Chang'an (modern-day Xi'an), who was accused of adultery.
    • 周公孔子 - Revered figures in Chinese history and philosophy, often used as moral exemplars.
    • 伯夷叔齐 - Two virtuous figures from ancient Chinese history, known for their integrity and refusal to compromise their principles.
    • 来俊臣乞为弟子索元礼求作门生 - References to notorious torturers from the Tang Dynasty who were known for their brutal methods.
    • 牵牛付虎未有出期防鼠与猫终无脱日 - Metaphors from Chinese folklore and literature, emphasizing the inevitability of death when faced with powerful and ruthless adversaries.

    Proper Nouns

    • 殿中侍御史 - Title for a high-ranking official in the imperial court.
    • 长安尉房恒 - Government official in Chang'an.
    • 周公孔子 - Revered figures in Chinese history and philosophy.
    • 伯夷叔齐 - Two virtuous figures from ancient Chinese history.
    • 来俊臣 - Notorious torturer from the Tang Dynasty.
    • 索元礼 - Another notorious torturer from the Tang Dynasty.
    • 京师 - The capital city, referring to Chang'an (modern-day Xi'an).

    In the court of the palace, the Palace Attendant and Imperial Censor Wang Xu conducted an investigation into the women living in separate houses within a residence, focusing on their reputations and appearances. Those who did not comply were bound with ropes around their genital areas and struck with bamboo sticks, causing unbearable pain. One woman was hung upside down and struck on the head with a stone. They were accused of adultery with the magistrate of Chang'an, Fang Heng, and refused to confess for three days. The woman said, "If the magistrate treats me so cruelly, I will report him to the authorities when I die. If I am sent to the palace, I will appeal to the emperor. He will never let me go." Wang Xu, fearing the consequences, released her.

    The Imperial Censor Li Song and Li Quan, along with Wang Xu, were known in the capital as the "Three Tigers." Li Song was the "Red Tiger," Li Quan was the "White Tiger," and Wang Xu was the "Black Tiger." All three were ruthless and lawless, using poison without restraint. They had wild and careless personalities and were cruel and harsh in spirit. Whenever they interrogated prisoners, they would bind them, poke them with bamboo sticks, press on their knees, and use broken tiles. They would make prisoners climb ladders, hang upside down, and suffer from extreme conditions. The prisoners begged for death, and the "Three Tigers" would arbitrarily torture and fabricate evidence, claiming it as true. Even revered figures like Zhou Gong and Confucius would be forced to confess to crimes, and virtuous figures like Bo Yi and Shu Qi would be accused of crimes. Interrogations would continue until the victims were exhausted or dead. Victims were treated like students or apprentices of these torturers. Those being pursued would swear oaths, threatening to face the "Three Tigers" if they betrayed their principles. The victims' families and friends were permanently separated from them, and people in the capital would swear oaths, threatening to face the "Three Tigers" if they betrayed their principles. The cruelty of the "Three Tigers" was such that it instilled fear in the hearts of all. [30]

    京兆人高丽家贫于御史台替勲官逓送文牒其时令史作伪帖付高丽追人拟吓钱事败令史逃走追讨不获御史张孝嵩捉高丽栲膝骨落地两脚俱挛抑遣代令史承伪准法断死讫大理卿状上故事准名例律笃疾不合加刑孝嵩勃然作色曰脚挛何废造伪命乃舁上市斩之
    　　周黔府都督谢祐凶险忍毒则天朝徙曹王于黔中祐吓云则天赐自尽祐亲奉进止更无别勅王怖而缢死后祐于平阁上卧婢妾十余人同宿夜不觉刺客截祐首去后曹王破家簿録事得祐头漆之题谢祐字以为秽器方知王子令刺客杀之 [31]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [31]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 作伪帖 - To forge a document.
    • 承伪 - To bear false charges.
    • 勃然作色 - To suddenly change color with anger.
    • 脚挛何废 - Paralyzed legs, what use.
    • 造伪命 - To forge orders.
    • 吓云 - Threaten to say.
    • 赐自尽 - Grant self-slaughter.
    • 亲奉进止 - Personally follow orders.
    • 更无别勅 - No other edict.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 京兆 - The capital region of the Tang Dynasty, the center of political and administrative power.
    • 御史台 - The office of the Imperial Censorate, responsible for supervising officials and maintaining integrity.
    • 令史 - A lower-ranking official in the bureaucracy, often involved in administrative tasks.
    • 大理卿 - The Chief Justice, the highest judicial official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 名例律 - The penal code of the Tang Dynasty, which included laws and regulations for criminal cases.
    • 则天朝 - The reign of Empress Wu Zetian, a powerful and controversial figure who ruled during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 黔府 - A region in southwestern China, known for its rugged terrain and cultural diversity.
    • 曹王 - A title given to a member of the imperial family, indicating a high-ranking noble.

    Proper Nouns

    • 高丽 - A person's name.
    • 张孝嵩 - The name of the Imperial Censor Zhang Xiaosong.
    • 谢祐 - The name of the military governor Xie You.
    • 曹王 - A title given to a member of the imperial family.
    • 则天朝 - The reign of Empress Wu Zetian.

    A Man from Jingzhao Named Gao Li

    Gao Li, a poor man from the capital region, worked in the Imperial Censorate delivering official documents for an official. At that time, a lower-ranking official forged a document and gave it to Gao Li to chase after someone and threaten for money. The matter failed, the lower-ranking official fled, and the pursuit was unsuccessful. The Imperial Censor Zhang Xiaosong caught Gao Li and beat him until his kneecaps fell off, causing both legs to be paralyzed. He was then suppressed and sent to replace the lower-ranking official, who bore false charges, and was sentenced to death according to the law. The Chief Justice reported the case, stating that according to precedent and the penal code, a person with a severe illness should not be punished. Zhang Xiaosong suddenly changed color with anger and said, "Paralyzed legs, what use? He could still forge orders. Then carry him to the market and behead him."

    Xie You, the Military Governor of Qianfu, was cruel and dangerous. During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, a member of the imperial family was exiled to the Qianzhong region. Xie You threatened to say that Empress Wu Zetian had granted self-slaughter and personally followed orders, with no other edict. The prince was terrified and hanged himself. Afterward, Xie You slept on a flat platform with more than ten concubines. At night, he did not notice assassins cutting off his head and fleeing. Later, the family broke, and records were compiled. The head was painted and inscribed with Xie You's name, considered as a filthy object. Then it was known that the prince had ordered assassins to kill him. [31]

    周黙啜贼之陷恒定州和亲使杨齐庄教授三品入匈奴遂没贼将至赵州襄公段瓆同没唤庄共出走庄惧不敢发瓆遂先归则天赏之复旧任齐庄寻至敕付河内王懿宗鞫问庄曰昔有人相庄位至三品有刀箭厄庄走出被赶斫射不死走得脱来愿王哀之懿宗性酷毒奏庄初怀犹豫请杀之敕依引至天津桥南于卫士铺鼓格上缚磔手足令段瓆先射三发皆不中又段瑾射之中又令诸司百官射箭如猬毛仍气殜殜然微动即以刀当心直下破至隂割取心掷地仍趌趌跳数十回懿宗忍毒如此
    　　杨务亷孝和时造长宁安乐宅仓库成特授将作大匠坐数十万免官又上章奏闻陜州三门凿山烧石岩侧施栈道牵船河流湍急所顾夫并未与价直茍牵绳一断栈梁一絶则扑杀数十人取顾夫钱籴米充数即注夫逃走下本贯禁父母兄弟妻子牵船皆令系二鈲于胸背落栈着石百无一存满路悲号声动山谷皆称杨务亷人妖也天生此妖以破残百姓 [32]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [32]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 和亲使 - Envoy for marriage alliance, referring to a diplomatic mission involving marriage.
    • 刀箭厄 - Metaphor for danger or misfortune, often used in divination and fortune-telling.
    • 遂没贼 - Subsequently captured by the enemy.
    • 唤庄共出走 - Called for Yang Qi Zhang to escape together.
    • 庄惧不敢发 - Yang Qi Zhang was afraid and dared not act.
    • 瓆遂先归 - Duan Cong returned first.
    • 则天赏之 - Empress Wu Zetian rewarded him.
    • 复旧任 - Restored his former position.
    • 齐庄寻至 - Yang Qi Zhang arrived soon after.
    • 敕付河内王懿宗鞫问 - The emperor ordered the Duke of Hedong, Yi Zong, to interrogate him.
    • 庄曰昔有人相庄位至三品有刀箭厄 - Yang Qi Zhang said that someone had predicted he would reach the third rank but would face danger from knives and arrows.
    • 庄走出被赶斫射不死 - He escaped but was chased, cut, and shot, but did not die.
    • 愿王哀之 - I hope Your Highness will pity me.
    • 懿宗性酷毒 - Yi Zong had a cruel and vicious nature.
    • 奏庄初怀犹豫请杀之 - He reported that Yang Qi Zhang had initially hesitated and requested his execution.
    • 敕依 - The emperor agreed.
    • 引至天津桥南于卫士铺鼓格上缚磔手足 - He was led to the south of Tianjin Bridge and tied to the drum stand of the guardhouse, with his hands and feet spread out.
    • 令段瓆先射三发皆不中 - Duan Cong was ordered to shoot three arrows, none of which hit.
    • 又段瑾射之中 - Then Duan Jin shot and hit.
    • 令诸司百官射箭如猬毛 - He ordered all officials to shoot arrows, like a hedgehog's spines.
    • 仍气殜殜然微动 - He still breathed faintly.
    • 即以刀当心直下破至隂割取心掷地 - They then cut him open with a knife, took out his heart, and threw it on the ground.
    • 仍趌趌跳数十回 - He still twitched and jumped dozens of times.
    • 天生此妖以破残百姓 - Heaven created this monster to destroy the people.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周黙啜 - Prominent Uyghur leader during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 和亲 - Traditional Chinese diplomatic practice involving marriage alliances.
    • 杨齐庄 - High-ranking official who was captured by the Uyghurs.
    • 段瓆 - Loyal official who returned safely.
    • 武则天 - Only female emperor in Chinese history, known for her strong rule and reforms.
    • 天津桥 - Famous bridge in Luoyang, a significant city during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 河内王懿宗 - Powerful official during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 刀箭厄 - Metaphor for danger or misfortune, often used in divination and fortune-telling.

    Proper Nouns

    • 周黙啜 - Uyghur leader.
    • 杨齐庄 - High-ranking official.
    • 段瓆 - Loyal official.
    • 武则天 - Empress.
    • 天津桥 - Famous bridge.
    • 河内王懿宗 - Powerful official.

    The Uyghur leader Zhou Moche captured the city of Hengding and took the envoy for marriage alliance, Yang Qi Zhang, a high-ranking official of the third rank, into the territory of the Xiongnu, where he was subsequently captured. As the Uyghurs advanced towards Zhaozhou, the loyal official Duan Cong was also captured. Duan Cong called for Yang Qi Zhang to escape together, but Yang Qi Zhang was afraid and dared not act, so Duan Cong returned first. Empress Wu Zetian rewarded Duan Cong and restored his former position. Soon after, Yang Qi Zhang arrived, and the emperor ordered the Duke of Hedong, Yi Zong, to interrogate him. Yang Qi Zhang said that someone had predicted he would reach the third rank but would face danger from knives and arrows. He had escaped but was chased, cut, and shot, but did not die, and he hoped Yi Zong would pity him. Yi Zong had a cruel and vicious nature and reported that Yang Qi Zhang had initially hesitated and requested his execution. The emperor agreed and ordered Yang Qi Zhang to be led to the south of Tianjin Bridge and tied to the drum stand of the guardhouse, with his hands and feet spread out. Duan Cong was ordered to shoot three arrows, none of which hit. Then Duan Jin shot and hit. All officials were ordered to shoot arrows, like a hedgehog's spines. He still breathed faintly. They then cut him open with a knife, took out his heart, and threw it on the ground. He still twitched and jumped dozens of times. Yi Zong's cruelty was thus evident.

    During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, Yang Wuling constructed the warehouses for the Longning and Anle mansions. Upon completion, he was specially appointed as the Grand Constructor. However, he was involved in embezzlement of tens of thousands of coins and was dismissed from his position. He later submitted a memorial reporting that in the Sanmen Pass of Shaanxi, mountains were being blasted and rocks burned to create a栈道 (suspended path) for pulling ships. The river was swift, and the laborers were not paid. If the ropes broke or the栈道 (suspended path) collapsed, dozens of people would be killed. To cover up the lack of payment, the money from the laborers was used to buy rice to make up the numbers, and the laborers were reported as having escaped. Their parents, brothers, and wives were all imprisoned, and those pulling the ships were ordered to wear two iron balls on their chests and backs. When the栈道 (suspended path) collapsed and they fell onto the rocks, none survived. The roads were filled with the cries of the dying, and the sound echoed through the mountains. Everyone called Yang Wuling a monster, saying that Heaven had created this monster to destroy the people. [32]

    监察御史李全交素以罗织酷虐为业台中号为人头罗刹殿中王旭号为面夜叉讯囚引枷柄向前名为驴驹拔橛枷头着树名曰犊子悬车两手捧枷累砖于上号为仙人献果立高木之上枷柄向后拗之名玉女登梯考栁州典廖福司门令史张性并求神狐魅皆遣唤作凤证蛇成龙也
    　　陈怀卿岭南人也养鸭百余头后于鸭栏中除粪粪中有光爚爚然以盆水沙汰之得金十两乃觇所食处于舎后山足下因凿有麸金销得数十斤时人莫知卿遂巨富仕至梧州刺史
    　　周长安年初前遂州长江县丞夏文荣时人以为判防事张鷟时为御史出为处州司仓替归往问焉荣以杖画地作栁字曰君当为此州至后半年除栁州司后改徳州平昌令荣刻时日晷漏无差又苏州嘉兴令杨廷玉则天之表侄也贪狠无厌着词曰逥波尔时廷玉打獠取钱未足其姑既见作天子傍人不得枨触差摄御史康詈推奏断死时母在都见夏文荣荣索黄白纸各一千张 [33]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [33]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 羅織酷虐 - Fabricating charges with cruel and harsh methods.
    • 人頭羅刹 - Metaphorical term combining 'human head' with 'Rākṣasa' (a demon in Hindu mythology), likely referring to a person who is extremely cruel or fearsome.
    • 鬼面夜叉 - Metaphorical term combining 'ghost face' with 'Yakṣa' (a type of demon in Buddhist mythology), suggesting someone with a terrifying or monstrous appearance.
    • 驴驹拔橛 - Metaphor for a difficult or painful situation, possibly referring to a donkey pulling out a stake, symbolizing resistance or struggle.
    • 犊子悬车 - Metaphor for a situation where a young calf is suspended from a cart, possibly symbolizing helplessness or being in a precarious position.
    • 仙人献果 - Metaphor for offering fruit as a gift, possibly symbolizing something rare or valuable.
    • 玉女登梯 - Metaphor for ascending a ladder, possibly symbolizing reaching a higher level or achieving something difficult.
    • 求神狐魅 - Refers to the act of seeking help from spirits, ghosts, or supernatural beings, often associated with witchcraft or sorcery.
    • 唤作凤证蛇成龙 - Metaphorical expression, possibly referring to transforming something ordinary into something extraordinary, like turning a snake into a dragon or a bird into a phoenix.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 监察御史 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty responsible for overseeing and reporting on the actions of other officials.
    • 台中 - Refers to the central government or the imperial court.
    • 殿中王旭 - Refers to Wang Xu, a specific individual in the historical context.
    • 柳州 - Refers to Liuzhou, a city in Guangxi, China.
    • 典 - Refers to a minor official or clerk.
    • 司门令史 - Refers to an official responsible for managing the gates or doors of an institution.
    • 陈怀卿 - Refers to Chen Huaiqing, a historical figure.
    • 岭南 - Refers to the southern region of China, including modern-day Guangdong and Guangxi provinces.
    • 遂州长江县丞 - Refers to a county official in the Sui Dynasty.
    • 夏文荣 - Refers to Xia Wenrong, a historical figure.
    • 张鷟 - Refers to Zhang Zhe, a historical figure.
    • 御史 - Refers to an official responsible for oversight and investigation.
    • 处州 - Refers to a city in Zhejiang, China.
    • 司仓 - Refers to an official responsible for managing grain or storage.
    • 梧州 - Refers to Wuzhou, a city in Guangxi, China.
    • 德州平昌令 - Refers to a county official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 苏州嘉兴令 - Refers to a county official in Suzhou, China.
    • 杨廷玉 - Refers to Yang Tingyu, a historical figure.
    • 则天 - Refers to Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 表侄 - Refers to a nephew by marriage.
    • 贪狠无厌 - Refers to someone who is greedy and insatiable.
    • 康詈 - Refers to Kang Li, a historical figure.
    • 黄白纸 - Refers to yellow and white paper, possibly used for official documents or rituals.

    Proper Nouns

    • 监察御史李全交 - Li Quan Jiao, a historical figure.
    • 台中王旭 - Wang Xu, a historical figure.
    • 柳州典廖福 - Liao Fu, a historical figure.
    • 司门令史张性 - Zhang Xing, a historical figure.
    • 陈怀卿 - Chen Huaiqing, a historical figure.
    • 夏文荣 - Xia Wenrong, a historical figure.
    • 张鷟 - Zhang Zhe, a historical figure.
    • 处州司仓 - A historical official position.
    • 梧州刺史 - A historical official position.
    • 德州平昌令 - A historical official position.
    • 杨廷玉 - Yang Tingyu, a historical figure.
    • 则天 - Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 康詈 - Kang Li, a historical figure.
    • 黄白纸 - A historical term referring to yellow and white paper.

    The Imperial Censor Li Quan Jiao was notorious for his cruel and harsh methods of fabricating charges, earning him the nickname "Human Head Rākṣasa" in the imperial court. Wang Xu, a central official, was known as "Ghost-Faced Yakṣa" for his terrifying appearance. When interrogating prisoners, he would pull the wooden handle of the manacle forward, a method he called "Donkey Pulling Out a Stake." He would also hang the manacle on a tree, a practice he named "Calf Hanging from a Cart." He would have the prisoner hold the manacle while stacking bricks on top, a method he called "Immortal Offering Fruits." He would then stand the prisoner on a tall tree and twist the manacle backward, a technique he named "Maid Climbing a Ladder." He used these methods to interrogate Liao Fu, a minor official in Liuzhou, and Zhang Xing, a gatekeeper, and even summoned spirits and ghosts to help him, claiming that he could turn birds into phoenixes and snakes into dragons.

    Chen Huaiqing was a man from the southern region of China who raised over a hundred ducks. One day, while cleaning the duck pen, he noticed a bright light in the excrement. He used a basin of water to sift through it and found ten taels of gold. Curious, he observed where the ducks had been eating and discovered a spot under the foot of a mountain behind his house. Digging there, he found more gold, which he smelted into several dozen pounds. No one knew how he had become so wealthy, and he eventually rose to the position of Governor of Wuzhou.

    In the early years of the Zhou Dynasty, Xia Wenrong was a county magistrate in Changjiang, Suizhou. At the time, people believed he was skilled in divination and fortune-telling, and Zhang Zhe, a censor, was sent to serve as the warehouse manager in Chuzhou. After Zhang returned, he visited Xia and, with a stick, drew the character for "liu" (柳) on the ground, saying, "You will serve in this state." Six months later, Xia was indeed appointed to the same position, and later he was promoted to magistrate of Pingchang in Dezhou. Zhang was known for his precise calculations of time and the sun and moon, and his predictions were always accurate.

    In Suzhou, Yang Tingyu was the magistrate of Jiaxing. He was the nephew of Wu Zetian by marriage and was known for his greed and insatiable appetite for money. He wrote a poem that said, "Turning back the waves, Tingyu beat the barbarians to collect money, but it was not enough. His aunt, now the Empress, saw him as a king, and no one else could touch him." He was eventually accused by Kang Li, a censor, and sentenced to death. At the time, his mother was in the capital and saw Xia Wenrong, who demanded a thousand sheets of yellow and white paper from her. [33]

    曰至后十日来母如其言荣曰且免死矣后十日内有进止果六日有勅杨廷玉改尽老母残年又天官令史栁无忌造荣荣书卫汉栁氏曰卫多不成汉栁二州交加不定后果唱卫州録事闗重即唱汉州録事时鸾台凤阁令史进状诉天官注拟不平则天责侍郎崔暐暐奏臣注官极平则天曰若尔吏部令史官共鸾台凤阁交换遂以无忌为栁州平阳主簿鸾台令史为汉州録事焉
    　　周司礼卿张希望移旧居改造见人冯毅见之曰公新堂下有一伏尸晋朝三品将军极怒公可避之望笑曰吾少长已来未曾知此事父母多言后月余日毅入见持弓矢随希望后迨登阶引弓射中膊脾间望觉此自痛以手抚之其日卒
    　　周左司郎中郑从简所居防事常不佳令巫者观之果有伏尸姓宗妻姓寇在防基之下使问之曰君坐我门上我出入常值君君自不好非我之为也掘之三丈果得旧骸有铭如其言移出改于是遂絶 [34]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [34]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 曰至后十日来母如其言 - Formal expression meaning 'after ten days, Mother came as he had said.'
    • 荣曰且免死矣 - 'Rong said, 'I will be spared from death.''
    • 果六日有勅杨廷玉改尽老母残年 - 'Indeed, on the sixth day, an imperial decree came to change the remaining years of the old mother.'
    • 又天官令史栁无忌造荣荣书卫汉栁氏曰卫多不成汉栁二州交加不定 - 'Also, the Tian Guan commandant, Liu Wujì, forged a letter to Rong, saying that the Wei Han Liu clan was unstable, with Wei and Han states alternating unpredictably.'
    • 后果唱卫州録事闗重即唱汉州録事 - 'Indeed, later, Guan Zhong was appointed as the recorder of Wei state, and immediately after, he was appointed as the recorder of Han state.'
    • 时鸾台凤阁令史进状诉天官注拟不平则天责侍郎崔暐暐奏臣注官极平则天曰若尔吏部令史官共鸾台凤阁交换遂以无忌为栁州平阳主簿鸾台令史为汉州録事焉 - 'At that time, the Ling Tai Feng Ge commandant submitted a petition to the emperor, accusing the Tian Guan commandant of unfair appointments. The emperor blamed the Left Censor Cui Xuanwei, who replied, 'My appointments are extremely fair.' The emperor said, 'If so, let the Tian Guan commandant and the Ling Tai Feng Ge commandant exchange positions.' Thus, Liu Wujì was made the chief clerk of Liuzhou, and the Ling Tai commandant was made the recorder of Han state.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 至后 - Refers to the day after the winter solstice, a significant date in the Chinese calendar.
    • 杨廷玉 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official.
    • 天官 - Refers to the Tian Guan commandant, a high-ranking official in charge of appointments.
    • 鸾台凤阁 - Refers to the Ling Tai Feng Ge commandant, another high-ranking official.
    • 卫州、汉州 - Refers to administrative divisions or states in ancient China.
    • 栁州、平阳 - Refers to other administrative divisions or states in ancient China.
    • 崔暐 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sentence is straightforward but requires careful translation to maintain the formal tone.
    • This is a simple statement but requires context to understand the full meaning.
    • This sentence is clear but requires understanding of the historical context.
    • This sentence is complex and requires careful translation to maintain the meaning.
    • This sentence is clear but requires understanding of the administrative context.
    • This sentence is complex and requires careful translation to maintain the meaning.

    Proper Nouns

    • 杨廷玉 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official.
    • 天官 - Refers to the Tian Guan commandant, a high-ranking official in charge of appointments.
    • 鸾台凤阁 - Refers to the Ling Tai Feng Ge commandant, another high-ranking official.
    • 卫州、汉州 - Refers to administrative divisions or states in ancient China.
    • 栁州、平阳 - Refers to other administrative divisions or states in ancient China.
    • 崔暐 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official.

    After ten days, Mother came as he had said. Rong said, "I will be spared from death." Indeed, within ten days, an imperial decree came to change the remaining years of the old mother. Also, the Tian Guan commandant, Liu Wujì, forged a letter to Rong, saying that the Wei Han Liu clan was unstable, with Wei and Han states alternating unpredictably. Indeed, later, Guan Zhong was appointed as the recorder of Wei state, and immediately after, he was appointed as the recorder of Han state. At that time, the Ling Tai Feng Ge commandant submitted a petition to the emperor, accusing the Tian Guan commandant of unfair appointments. The emperor blamed the Left Censor Cui Xuanwei, who replied, "My appointments are extremely fair." The emperor said, "If so, let the Tian Guan commandant and the Ling Tai Feng Ge commandant exchange positions." Thus, Liu Wujì was made the chief clerk of Liuzhou, and the Ling Tai commandant was made the recorder of Han state.

    During the Zhou dynasty, the official Zhang Yiwang moved his old residence for renovation and saw a ghost. Feng Yi saw it and said, "There is a hidden corpse in your new hall, a third-rank general from the Jin dynasty. He is extremely angry. You should avoid it." Zhang smiled and said, "Since I was young, I have never known such a thing. My parents often talk about it." After more than a month, Feng entered and saw the ghost holding a bow and arrow following Zhang after he ascended the steps. The ghost drew his bow and shot Zhang in the armpit and spleen. Zhang felt the pain himself and touched it with his hand. On that day, he died.

    During the Zhou dynasty, the Left Censor Zheng Congjian's residence was often not good. He ordered a witch to observe it and found that there was a hidden corpse with the surname Zong and the wife's surname Kou under the foundation. He asked it, "You sit on my door. I often meet you when I enter and leave. It is not me who is bad, but you." They dug three meters deep and indeed found the old remains with an inscription as he had said. They moved it out and reburied it, and thus the problem was resolved. [34]

    周地官郎中房颖叔除天官侍郎明日欲上其夜有厨子王老夜半起忽闻外有人唤云王老不须起房侍郎不上后三日李侍郎上王老却卧至晓房果病起数日而卒所司奏状下即除李逥秀为侍郎其日谢即上王老以其言问诸人皆云不知方悟是神明所告也
    　　北齐稠禅师邺人也幼落发为沙弥时辈甚众每休暇常角力腾趠为戏而禅师以劣弱见陵绐侮殴撃者相继禅师羞之乃入殿中闭户抱金刚足而誓曰我以羸弱为等轻侮为辱己甚不如死也汝以力闻当佑我我捧汝足七日不与我力必死于此无还志约既毕因至心祈之初一两夕恒尔念益固至六日将曙金刚形见手执大鉢满中盛筋谓稠曰小子欲力乎曰欲念至乎曰至能食筋乎曰不能神曰何故稠曰出家人断肉故神因操鉢举匕以筋食之禅师未敢食乃怖以金刚杵稠惧遂食斯须食毕神曰汝已多力然善持教勉旃神去且晓乃还所居诸同列问曰竖子 [35]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [35]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 欲上 - Means 'was about to take office.'
    • 忽闻 - Means 'suddenly heard.'
    • 不须起 - Means 'don't get up.'
    • 不上 - Means 'is not taking office.'
    • 却卧至晓 - Means 'went back to sleep until dawn.'
    • 果病起 - Means 'indeed fell ill and got up.'
    • 所司 - Refers to the officials or the bureaucracy.
    • 奏状 - Means 'submitted a report.'
    • 下即除 - Means 'immediately appointed.'
    • 谢即上 - Means 'took office' or 'assumed the position.'
    • 以其言问诸人 - Means 'asked others about this.'
    • 皆云不知 - Means 'everyone said they didn't know.'
    • 方悟是神明所告也 - Means 'he then realized it was a message from the divine.'
    • 休暇 - Means 'break' or 'leisure time.'
    • 角力腾趠 - Means 'wrestle and jump around.'
    • 见陵绐侮 - Means 'was mocked and beaten.'
    • 殴撃者相继 - Means 'those who beat him came one after another.'
    • 羞之 - Means 'was ashamed.'
    • 殿中 - Means 'in the temple.'
    • 闭户 - Means 'closed the door.'
    • 抱金刚足 - Means 'held the foot of the statue of Vajrapani,' a Buddhist deity.
    • 誓曰 - Means 'vowed, saying.'
    • 羸弱 - Means 'weak and frail.'
    • 为等轻侮 - Means 'bullied by my peers.'
    • 为辱己甚 - Means 'this is a great shame.'
    • 不如死也 - Means 'it is better to die.'
    • 以力闻 - Means 'known for your strength.'
    • 当佑我 - Means 'should protect me.'
    • 捧汝足 - Means 'hold your foot.'
    • 七日不与我力 - Means 'do not give me strength within seven days.'
    • 必死于此无还 - Means 'I will die here, and there will be no return.'
    • 志约既毕 - Means 'after making this vow.'
    • 至心祈之 - Means 'prayed with all his heart.'
    • 恒尔念益固 - Means 'his resolve grew stronger.'
    • 将曙 - Means 'just before dawn.'
    • 金刚形见 - Means 'the figure of Vajrapani appeared.'
    • 手执大鉢满中盛筋 - Means 'holding a large bowl filled with tendons.'
    • 欲力乎 - Means 'do you want strength?'
    • 念至乎 - Means 'do you mean that my resolve is strong enough?'
    • 能食筋乎 - Means 'can you eat tendons?'
    • 不能 - Means 'no, I cannot.'
    • 何故 - Means 'why not?'
    • 断肉故 - Means 'because I abstain from eating meat.'
    • 操鉢举匕 - Means 'took the bowl and a spoon.'
    • 以筋食之 - Means 'forced the tendons into his mouth.'
    • 未敢食 - Means 'hesitated to eat.'
    • 怖以金刚杵 - Means 'threatened him with a vajra.'
    • 稠惧遂食 - Means 'Chou was afraid and ate the tendons.'
    • 斯须食毕 - Means 'in a short while, he finished eating.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 北齐 - Refers to the Northern Qi dynasty.
    • 稠禅师 - Name of the monk, with '禅师' meaning 'Zen master.'
    • 邺人 - Means 'from Ye,' where Ye was a city in ancient China.
    • 金刚 - Refers to Vajrapani, a Buddhist deity.
    • 金刚杵 - Refers to a vajra, a ritual object used in Buddhism.

    Proper Nouns

    • 周地官郎中房颖叔 - Specific official title and name.
    • 天官侍郎 - Specific official title.
    • 厨子王老 - Name of the kitchen worker.
    • 李侍郎 - Specific official title and name.
    • 李逥秀 - Name of the official.
    • 王老 - Name of the kitchen worker.
    • 房果病起 - Name of the official.
    • 所司 - Refers to the officials or the bureaucracy.
    • 稠禅师 - Name of the monk.
    • 北齐 - Name of the dynasty.
    • 邺人 - Place name.
    • 金刚 - Name of the Buddhist deity.
    • 金刚杵 - Name of the ritual object.

    Zhou 地官郎中 Fang Yingshu was appointed as 天官侍郎. The next day, as he was about to take office, at midnight, a kitchen worker named Wang Laoshi got up. Suddenly, he heard someone outside calling, "Wang Laoshi, don't get up; Fang Shi Lang is not taking office." Three days later, Li Shi Lang took office. Wang Laoshi went back to sleep until dawn; Fang Yingshu indeed fell ill, got up after several days, and then died. The officials reported this, and the emperor immediately appointed Li Hui Xiu as Shi Lang. On that day, he took office. Wang Laoshi asked others about this, and everyone said they didn't know; he then realized it was a message from the divine.

    In the Northern Qi dynasty, there was a monk named Chou, from Ye. When he was young, he shaved his head and became a novice, and there were many others of his age. Every time they had a break, they would often wrestle and jump around for fun, but the monk, being weak and frail, was repeatedly mocked and beaten. The monk was ashamed and went into the temple, closed the door, and held the foot of the statue of Vajrapani, vowing, "If I am weak and bullied by my peers, I would rather die than live in shame." He prayed with all his heart. For the first one or two nights, it was as he had hoped, but his resolve grew stronger. On the sixth day, just before dawn, the figure of Vajrapani appeared, holding a large bowl filled with tendons. Vajrapani said to Chou, "Do you want strength?" Chou replied, "Yes, I do. Do you mean that my resolve is strong enough?" Vajrapani said, "Yes, but can you eat tendons?" Chou replied, "No, I cannot." Vajrapani said, "Why not?" Chou replied, "Because as a monk, I abstain from eating meat." Vajrapani then took the bowl and a spoon, and forced the tendons into Chou's mouth. The monk was afraid and hesitated, but Vajrapani threatened him with a vajra, and Chou ate the tendons. Soon, he finished eating. Vajrapani said, "You now have great strength, but remember to uphold the teachings and strive diligently." The divine figure then disappeared, and just as dawn broke, Chou returned to his residence. His fellow monks asked, "What happened, young man?" [35]

    顷何至稠不答须臾于堂中防食食毕诸同列又戏殴禅师曰吾有力恐不堪于汝同列试引其臂筋骨强劲殆非人也方惊疑禅师曰吾为汝试之因入殿中横塌壁行自西自东凡数百步又跃首至于梁数四乃引重千钧其拳防骁武劲先轻侮者俯伏流汗莫敢仰视禅师后证果居于林虑山入山数十里精庐殿堂穷极壮大诸僧从而禅者常数千人齐文宣帝怒其聚众因领骁骑数万躬自往讨将加白刄焉禅师是日领僧徒谷口迎候文宣问曰师何遽此来稠曰陛下将杀贫僧恐山中血汚伽蓝故此谷口受戮文宣大惊降驾礼谒请许其悔过禅师亦无言文宣命设馔施毕 [36]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [36]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 试引其臂筋骨强劲殆非人也 - Phrase indicating the arm was tested and found to be extremely strong, almost superhuman.
    • 引重千钧 - Phrase indicating the ability to lift a weight of a thousand jin (an ancient unit of weight).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 禅师 - Refers to a Buddhist monk, likely a high-ranking or well-known figure.
    • 齐文宣帝 - Refers to Emperor Wenxuan of Qi, a ruler of the Northern Qi dynasty.
    • 林虑山 - Refers to Lintu Mountain, a location in China known for its historical and religious significance.
    • 伽蓝 - Refers to a Buddhist temple or monastery.
    • 白刄 - Refers to a white blade, often used symbolically to denote execution or severe punishment.

    Proper Nouns

    • 禅师 - The title or name of a Buddhist monk.
    • 齐文宣帝 - The title of Emperor Wenxuan of Qi.
    • 林虑山 - The name of Lintu Mountain.
    • 伽蓝 - The term for a Buddhist temple or monastery.
    • 白刄 - The term for a white blade, often used symbolically.

    Why did Cao Cao not respond immediately? After a short while, food was prepared in the hall. When they finished eating, the other officials began to playfully mock the monk, saying, "I have great strength, but I fear I cannot match you." They then tried to test his arm, finding it to be extremely strong, almost superhuman. The person was surprised and questioned the monk, who replied, "I will test it for you." He then entered the hall and walked along the wall from west to east for several hundred steps, jumping up to the beams multiple times. He could lift a weight of a thousand jin, and his fists were powerful and strong. Those who had previously mocked him crouched and sweated, too afraid to look up. The monk later achieved enlightenment and lived in Lintu Mountain. His retreat was several miles deep into the mountain, with grand halls and temples. Many monks followed him, and there were often thousands of people practicing meditation. Emperor Wenxuan of Qi was angry about the large gathering and led several thousand elite soldiers to personally go and punish them, intending to execute the monk. On that day, the monk led the monks to meet the emperor at the valley entrance. The emperor asked, "Why are you so hasty to come here?" The monk replied, "Your Majesty intends to kill me, but I fear that the blood will stain the temple. Therefore, I am here to accept my punishment." The emperor was greatly surprised and descended from his carriage to pay respects, requesting that the monk be allowed to repent. The monk remained silent. The emperor then ordered a meal to be prepared and distributed. [36]

    请曰闻师金刚处祈得力今欲见师效少力可乎稠曰昔力者人力耳今为陛下见神力欲见之乎文宣曰请与同行寓目先是禅师造寺诸方施木数千根卧在谷口禅师咒之诸木起立空中自相搏击声雷霆鬭触摧折缤纷如雨文宣大惧从官散走文宣叩头请止之因敕禅师度人造寺无得禁止后于幷州营幢子未成遘病临终叹曰夫生死者人之大分如来尚所未免但功徳未成以此为恨耳死后愿为大力长者继成此功言终而化至后三十年隋帝过幷州见此寺心中涣然记忆有似旧脩行处顶礼防敬无所不为处分幷州大兴营葺其寺遂成时人谓帝大力长者云 [37]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [37]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 請曰 - Formal expression meaning 'asked to say' or 'requested to speak,' indicating a polite request.
    • 聞師 - Means 'heard about the master' or 'knew of the teacher,' showing respect and acknowledgment.
    • 處祈得力 - Means 'in a place where one seeks strength' or 'where one prays for power,' suggesting a place of spiritual or physical empowerment.
    • 今欲見師效少力可乎 - Means 'now I wish to see the master and offer a little effort, is that possible?' This is a polite request for permission to assist.
    • 昔力者人力耳 - Means 'former strength was merely human strength,' contrasting past human strength with the current supernatural strength.
    • 今為陛下見神力 - Means 'now I will show the emperor divine power,' indicating a demonstration of supernatural abilities.
    • 欲見之乎 - Means 'do you wish to see it?' A direct question to the emperor.
    • 叩头请止之 - Means 'knelt down and begged to stop it,' indicating a plea to halt the demonstration.
    • 处分并州大兴营葺其寺 - Means 'ordered the reconstruction of the temple in Pingzhou,' indicating imperial command for the temple's restoration.
    • 大力长者 - A title given to someone who has great strength or power, often used in Buddhist contexts.
    • 功徳未成以此为恨耳 - Means 'the merit was incomplete, which is why I regret it,' expressing a sense of unfinished business.
    • 言终而化 - Means 'his words ended and he transformed,' indicating the end of his life and a transformation into another form.
    • 顶礼防敬无所不为 - Means 'paid homage, guarded reverence, and did nothing but pay respect,' indicating deep veneration.
    • 处分幷州大兴营葺其寺遂成 - Means 'ordered the reconstruction of the temple in Pingzhou, and it was completed,' indicating the completion of the temple's restoration.
    • 有似旧修行处 - Means 'like the place where he used to practice,' indicating a familiar or nostalgic connection.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 文宣 - Refers to Emperor Wenxuan of Northern Qi, a ruler known for his patronage of Buddhism.
    • 禅师 - Refers to a Buddhist monk or master, often associated with Zen Buddhism.
    • 金刚 - Refers to a type of Buddhist deity or a symbol of strength and indestructibility.
    • 幢子 - Refers to a type of Buddhist banner or flag used in religious ceremonies.
    • 隋帝 - Refers to Emperor Yang of Sui, a ruler who continued the support of Buddhism.

    Proper Nouns

    • 文宣 - Emperor Wenxuan of Northern Qi.
    • 禅师 - A Buddhist monk or master.
    • 金刚 - A type of Buddhist deity or symbol of strength.
    • 隋帝 - Emperor Yang of Sui.
    • 大力长者 - A title given to someone with great strength, often used in Buddhist contexts.

    The emperor asked, "I have heard that the master of the Diamond Buddha resides in a place where one prays for strength. Now I wish to see the master and offer a little effort, is that possible?" The monk replied, "In the past, strength was merely human strength. Now I will show the emperor divine power. Do you wish to see it?" The emperor said, "Please come with me to witness it." Before this, the monk had built a temple, and people from various places donated thousands of pieces of wood, which were piled up at the entrance of the valley. The monk chanted over them, and the wood rose into the air, striking each other with sounds like thunder, breaking and falling like rain. The emperor was greatly frightened, and his attendants scattered in panic. The emperor knelt down and begged the monk to stop the demonstration. He then ordered the monk to build the temple without hindrance. Later, in Pingzhou, when the monk was constructing a banner, he fell ill and, on his deathbed, lamented, "Life and death are part of human destiny, even the Buddha could not escape them. But my merit was incomplete, which is why I regret it. After my death, I wish to be reborn as a powerful elder to complete this work." With these words, he passed away. Thirty years later, Emperor Yang of Sui passed by Pingzhou and saw the temple. His heart was filled with a sense of familiarity, as if he remembered it from his past practice. He paid homage, guarded reverence, and did nothing but pay respect. He ordered the reconstruction of the temple in Pingzhou, and it was completed. At that time, people called him the "Great Power Elder." [37]

    真腊国在驩州南五百里其俗有客设槟榔龙脑香蛤屑等以为赏宴其酒比之淫秽私房与妻共饮对尊者避之又行房不欲令人见此俗与中国同国人不着衣服见衣服者共笑之俗无盐铁以竹弩射虫鸟
    　　五溪蛮父母死于村外閤其尸三年而打鼔路歌亲属饮宴舞戏一月余日尽产为棺于临江高山半腰凿龛以葬之自山上悬索下柩弥高者以为至孝即终身不复祠祭初遭丧三年不食盐
    　　岭南獠民好为蜜蝍即鼠胎未瞬通身赤蠕者饲之以蜜饤之筵上嗫匕而行以筯挟取啖之唧唧作声故曰蜜蝍 [38]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [38]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 真腊国 - Historical kingdom in Southeast Asia, corresponding to modern-day Cambodia.
    • 驩州 - Ancient Chinese administrative region, likely referring to a specific area in the southern part of China.
    • 槟榔 - Tropical fruit often used in traditional ceremonies and as a stimulant.
    • 龙脑香 - Aromatic resin, often used in incense and medicine.
    • 蛤屑 - Powdered shellfish, possibly used as a condiment or medicine.
    • 淫秽私房 - Private rooms or spaces where inappropriate activities take place.
    • 行房 - Refers to sexual intercourse.
    • 五溪蛮 - Indigenous people living in the Five Streams area, which is in present-day Hunan and Guizhou provinces.
    • 閤其尸三年而 - Ritual practice involving covering the body for three years before burial.
    • 打鼔路歌 - Folk song or dance performed with drumming.
    • 岭南獠民 - Indigenous people living in the southern regions of China, particularly in the Lingnan area.
    • 蜜蝍 - Type of insect, specifically a type of rat larva.
    • 通身赤蠕者 - Describes the appearance of the insect, indicating it is red and wriggling.
    • 饲之以蜜 - Feeding the insect with honey.
    • 饤之筵上 - Placing the insect on a table or serving dish.
    • 嗫匕而行 - Moving slowly and deliberately, possibly with a knife or fork.
    • 唧唧作声 - Making a small, continuous sound.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 真腊国 - Located in Southeast Asia, known for its rich cultural heritage and historical significance.
    • 驩州 - Ancient Chinese administrative region, indicating the geographical context of the text.
    • 槟榔 - Commonly used in Southeast Asian cultures for social and ceremonial purposes.
    • 龙脑香 - Used in traditional Chinese medicine and religious practices.
    • 蛤屑 - Traditional ingredient in Chinese cuisine and medicine.
    • 淫秽私房 - Refers to private spaces where inappropriate activities occur, reflecting social norms and taboos.
    • 行房 - Sexual intercourse, often discussed in the context of social and cultural norms.
    • 五溪蛮 - Indigenous people known for their unique customs and practices.
    • 閤其尸三年而 - Ritual practice reflecting the cultural beliefs and customs of the time.
    • 岭南獠民 - Indigenous people with distinct cultural practices and traditions.
    • 蜜蝍 - Type of insect with specific cultural significance in certain regions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 真腊国 - The historical kingdom of Chenla (modern-day Cambodia).
    • 驩州 - Ancient Chinese administrative region.
    • 五溪蛮 - Indigenous people of the Five Streams area.
    • 岭南獠民 - Indigenous people of the Lingnan region.

    The True Lao Kingdom is located 500 li south of the state of Huan. The customs of the people there include offering betel nuts, dragon brain incense, and powdered shellfish to guests as a form of hospitality. Their wine is considered to be of a similar nature to that consumed in private, inappropriate settings. They drink with their wives, but avoid doing so in the presence of elders. They also do not want others to see them during intimate moments. These customs are similar to those in China. The people of the True Lao Kingdom do not wear clothes, and those who do wear clothes are laughed at. They do not use salt or iron, and they use bamboo bows to hunt insects and birds.

    When the parents of the Five Streams people die, their bodies are covered for three years before burial. They perform drumming and singing rituals, and relatives gather for feasts and dances for more than a month. They use all their wealth to create coffins and carve niches in the cliffs of high mountains near rivers to bury the dead. The body is lowered from the mountain using ropes, and the higher the body is lowered, the more it is considered a sign of filial piety. After the initial mourning period, they do not perform further sacrifices. When they first encounter a death, they do not eat salt for three years.

    The Lingnan Liao people enjoy consuming a type of insect called "mì jié," which is a red, wriggling larva of a rat. They feed it honey and place it on the table. They move it slowly with a knife or fork and pick it up with chopsticks to eat it, making a small, continuous sound in the process. This is why it is called "mì jié." [38]

    梁有磕头师者极精进梁武帝甚敬信之后敕使唤磕头师帝适与人棊欲杀一段应声曰杀却使遽出而斩之帝棊罢曰唤师使荅曰向者陛下令人杀却臣已杀讫帝叹曰师临死之时有何言使曰师云贫道无罪前刼为沙弥时以鍫刬地误断一曲蟮帝时为蟮今此报也帝流泪悔恨亦无及焉
    　　建昌王武攸寜别置勾使法外枉征财物百姓破家者十而九告寃于天吁嗟满路为大库长百步二百余间所征获者贮在其中天火烧之一时荡尽众口所咒攸寜寻患足肿粗于瓮其酸楚不可数月而终
    　　干封年中京西明寺僧昙畅将一奴二骡向岐州棱法师处听讲道逢一道人着衲帽衣掐数珠自云贤者五戒薄暮至马嵬店宿五戒礼佛诵经半夜不歇畅以为精进并坐至四更即共同发去店十余里忽袖中出两刃刀矛便刺杀畅其奴下马入草走避五戒骑骡驱驮即去主人未晓梦畅告云昨夜五戒杀贫道须臾奴走到告之如梦时同宿三卫子执持弓箭乗马赶四十余里以弓箭拟之即下骡乞死防送县决杀之 [39]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [39]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 極精進 - Means 'extremely diligent' or 'highly devoted,' often used to describe someone's spiritual or religious practice.
    • 甚敬信 - Means 'highly respected and trusted,' indicating deep reverence and faith.
    • 敕使 - Refers to an imperial edict or command, often used to convey official instructions.
    • 應聲曰 - Means 'immediately replied,' indicating a quick verbal response.
    • 殺卻 - Means 'kill off' or 'execute,' often used in a decisive manner.
    • 棊 - Refers to the game of Go (Weiqi), a traditional Chinese board game.
    • 荅曰 - Means 'answered,' indicating a verbal reply.
    • 前刼 - Refers to a past life or previous incarnation, often used in Buddhist contexts.
    • 沙弥 - Refers to a novice monk, typically a young male who has taken initial vows but not yet full ordination.
    • 鍫 - Refers to a hoe or spade, a tool used for digging.
    • 斷一曲蟮 - Means 'cut a segment of a worm,' indicating a specific action.
    • 吁嗟 - Means 'groaning' or 'lamenting,' often used to express sorrow or distress.
