    • 大库 - Refers to a large warehouse or storage facility.
    • 天火烧之 - Means 'burned by heaven,' indicating a divine or supernatural fire.
    • 酸楚 - Means 'bitter and painful,' often used to describe emotional or physical suffering.
    • 尋患足肿 - Means 'soon suffered from swollen feet,' indicating a sudden onset of a physical ailment.
    • 粗于瓮 - Means 'as thick as a jar,' indicating a severe swelling.
    • 尋 - Means 'soon' or 'quickly,' indicating a short period of time.
    • 干封年中 - Refers to the year of Ganfeng, a reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang.
    • 京西明寺 - Refers to Jingximing Temple, a specific temple in the capital city.
    • 聽讲 - Means 'to listen to lectures' or 'to study,' indicating educational activities.
    • 道逢 - Means 'met on the road,' indicating an encounter.
    • 賢者 - Refers to a wise person or sage, often used to describe someone with high moral or spiritual qualities.
    • 五戒 - Refers to the Five Precepts, a set of basic ethical guidelines in Buddhism.
    • 馬嵬店 - Refers to Mawei Inn, a specific inn or lodging place.
    • 驅驮 - Means 'drive the load,' indicating the act of transporting goods.
    • 防送 - Means 'to arrest and escort,' indicating legal action.
    • 决杀 - Means 'execute by decision,' indicating a formal execution.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 梁武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Liang, a ruler of the Liang Dynasty known for his patronage of Buddhism.
    • 磕头师 - Refers to a monk or religious figure who practices prostration (kowtowing) as part of their spiritual discipline.
    • 干封年中 - Refers to the year of Ganfeng, a reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang, indicating a specific historical period.
    • 京西明寺 - Refers to Jingximing Temple, a significant temple in the capital city of Chang'an during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 马嵬店 - Refers to Mawei Inn, a specific location where historical events took place, notably associated with the An Lushan Rebellion.
    • 五戒 - Refers to the Five Precepts, a set of basic ethical guidelines in Buddhism, including not killing, stealing, lying, sexual misconduct, and intoxication.
    • 干封 - Refers to the reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang, indicating a specific historical period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 梁武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liang.
    • 磕头师 - Monk or religious figure.
    • 干封 - Reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang.
    • 京西明寺 - Jingximing Temple.
    • 马嵬店 - Mawei Inn.
    • 五戒 - Five Precepts.
    • 干封 - Reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang.
    • 建昌王武攸寜 - Duke Wuyouning.
    • 京西明寺僧昙畅 - Monk Tan Chang.
    • 岐州棱法师 - Master Ling of Qizhou.
    • 马嵬店 - Mawei Inn.
    • 三卫子 - Three Guardsmen.

    During the Liang Dynasty, there was a monk who was extremely diligent in his practice. Emperor Wu of Liang highly respected and trusted him. Later, the emperor issued an edict to summon the monk. At the time, the emperor was playing Go with someone and wanted to kill a piece. The monk immediately replied, "Kill it." The messenger then quickly went out and executed the person. After the game, the emperor said, "Summon the monk." The messenger replied, "The emperor had ordered the execution of the person, and I have already carried it out." The emperor sighed and said, "What did the monk say before he died?" The messenger replied, "The monk said, 'I have no sin. In my previous life as a novice monk, I accidentally cut a segment of a worm with a hoe. Now, I am that worm, and this is my retribution.' " The emperor shed tears and felt deep regret, but it was too late.

    In the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang, during the year of Ganfeng, there was a large storage facility at Jingximing Temple in the capital. Duke Wuyouning of Jianchang set up a special office to extort wealth from the people outside the law. Ninety percent of the people were ruined by this extortion, and they cried out to heaven in their suffering. The facility was so large that it was 100 steps long and had over 200 rooms. All the confiscated goods were stored there. One day, a divine fire burned the facility, destroying everything in an instant. The people cursed Duke Wuyouning, and he soon suffered from swollen feet as thick as a jar. His suffering was bitter and painful, and he died after several months.

    In the year of Ganfeng, a monk named Tan Chang from Jingximing Temple in the capital traveled with a slave and two mules to listen to lectures from Master Ling of Qizhou. On the road, he met a man wearing a patched hat and a rough robe, holding prayer beads and calling himself a wise man who observed the Five Precepts. As dusk approached, they reached Mawei Inn and stayed there. The wise man prayed and chanted scriptures all night without stopping. Tan Chang thought he was very diligent and sat with him until the fourth watch of the night. They then left the inn together. After traveling about ten miles, the wise man suddenly pulled out two daggers from his sleeves and stabbed Tan Chang, killing him. The slave dismounted and ran into the grass to hide. The wise man rode the mule and drove the load away. The innkeeper did not wake up until morning and had a dream in which Tan Chang told him, "Last night, the wise man killed me." Soon after, the slave came to tell the innkeeper what had happened, just as in the dream. Three guardsmen, armed with bows and arrows, chased the wise man for over forty miles. When they caught up with him, they shot him, and he dismounted the mule and begged for mercy. They arrested him and took him to the county for execution. [39]

    后魏末嵩阳杜昌妻栁氏甚妒有婢金荆昌沐令理发栁氏截其双指无何栁被狐刺螫指双落又有一婢名玉莲能唱歌昌爱而叹其善栁氏乃截其舌后栁氏舌疮烂事急就稠禅师懴悔禅师已先知谓栁氏曰夫人为妒前截婢指已失指又截婢舌今又合断舌悔过至心乃可以免栁氏顶礼求哀经七日禅师令大张口咒之有二蛇从口出一尺以上急呪之遂落地舌亦平复自是不复妒矣
    　　贞观中濮阳范略妻任氏畧先幸一婢任以刀截其耳鼻畧不能制有顷任有娠诞一女无耳鼻女年渐大其婢仍在女问具说所由女悲泣以恨其母母深有愧色悔之无及 [40]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [40]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 甚妒 - Means 'very jealous' or 'extremely envious.'
    • 沐令理发 - Means 'ordered to comb her hair' or 'ordered to have her hair combed.'
    • 截其双指 - Means 'cut off her two fingers.'
    • 无何 - A time marker meaning 'soon after' or 'shortly thereafter.'
    • 狐刺螫 - Means 'bitten by a fox' or 'stung by a fox.' *狐* (fox) here likely refers to a fox spirit or a supernatural creature.
    • 合断舌 - Means 'will cut off her tongue.'
    • 懴悔 - Means 'repentance' or 'atonement.'
    • 顶礼求哀 - Means 'prostrated herself in prayer and begged for mercy.'
    • 经七日 - Means 'for seven days.'
    • 大张口 - Means 'opened her mouth wide.'
    • 咒之 - Means 'chanted over her.'
    • 落地舌亦平复 - Means 'the tongue fell out, and it healed.'
    • 截其耳鼻 - Means 'cut off her ears and nose.'
    • 具说所由 - Means 'told her the whole story.'
    • 无不利於事也 - Means 'nothing is unhelpful in matters.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 后魏 - The Northern Wei dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 嵩阳 - A place name, likely referring to a region in Henan province.
    • 贞观 - The reign title of Emperor Taizong of Tang (627–649 CE).
    • 濮阳 - A place name, likely referring to a region in Henan province.
    • 禅师 - Means 'monk' or 'priest,' specifically a Buddhist monk.
    • 狐刺螫 - Refers to a supernatural event, possibly involving a fox spirit or a curse.
    • 懴悔 - A religious practice of repentance, often associated with Buddhism.
    • 顶礼 - A form of prostration, a common gesture in Buddhist rituals.

    Proper Nouns

    • 后魏 - The Northern Wei dynasty.
    • 嵩阳 - A place name, likely referring to a region in Henan province.
    • 杜昌 - The husband's name.
    • 柳氏 - The wife's name.
    • 金荆 - The first maid's name.
    • 玉莲 - The second maid's name.
    • 稠禅师 - The name of the monk.
    • 贞观 - The reign title of Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 濮阳 - A place name, likely referring to a region in Henan province.
    • 范略 - The husband's name.
    • 任氏 - The wife's name.

    During the late Northern Wei dynasty, in Songyang, Du Chang's wife, Suo Li, was extremely jealous. She had a maid named Jin Jing, and when Du Chang ordered her to comb her hair, Suo Li cut off her two fingers. Soon after, Suo Li was bitten by a fox and lost her fingers. She then had another maid named Yu Lian, who could sing beautifully. Du Chang admired her talent, but Suo Li, out of jealousy, cut off her tongue. Later, Suo Li developed a severe tongue ulcer and sought repentance from a monk named Cuo. The monk, who already knew about Suo Li's jealousy, told her, "Because of your jealousy, you have already lost fingers and cut off a tongue. If you continue, you will lose your tongue again. Only sincere repentance can save you." Suo Li prostrated herself in prayer and begged for mercy. After seven days, the monk made her open her mouth wide and chanted over her. Two snakes fell out of her mouth, and her tongue healed. From then on, she no longer acted out of jealousy.

    During the reign of Emperor Taizong in the Tang dynasty, in Puyang, Fan Lüe's wife, Ren, was also extremely jealous. She had a maid whom she favored, but she cut off her ears and nose with a knife. Fan Lüe could not stop her. Soon after, Ren became pregnant and gave birth to a daughter who was born without ears and nose. As the daughter grew older, the maid was still around. When the daughter asked about the reason, the maid told her the whole story. The daughter cried bitterly and blamed her mother. Ren felt deep remorse and regretted her actions, but it was too late. [40]

    广州化县丞胡亮从都督周仁轨讨獠得一首领妾幸之至县亮向府不在妻贺氏乃烧钉烙其双目妾遂自缢死后贺氏有娠产一蛇两目无晴以问禅师师曰夫人曾烧铁烙一女妇眼以夫人性毒故为蛇报此是被烙女妇也夫人好养此蛇可以免难不然祸及身矣贺氏养蛇一二年渐大不见物惟在衣被中亮不知也拨被见蛇大惊以刀斫杀之贺氏两目俱枯不复见物悔而无及焉
    　　梁仁裕为骁卫将军先幸一婢妻李氏甚妒而虐缚婢击其脑婢号呼曰在下卑贱势不自由娘子锁项苦毒何甚婢死后月余李氏病常见婢来唤李氏头上生四处瘅疽脑溃昼夜鸣呌苦痛不胜数月而卒
    　　荆州枝江县主簿夏荣判防司县丞张景先宠其婢厥妻杨氏妒之景出使不在妻杀婢投之于厠景至绐之曰婢逃矣景以妻酷虐不问也婢讼之于荣荣追对之问景曰公夫人病困说形状景疑其有私也怒之荣曰公夫人枉杀婢投于厠今见推勘公诚问之景悟问其妇妇病甚具首其事荣令厠内取其骸骨香汤浴之厚加殡婢不肯放月余而卒 [41]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [41]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 从都督周仁轨讨獠 - Followed General Zhou Rengui to campaign against the Lao (a tribal group).
    • 幸之 - Had sexual relations with or favored; in context, Hu Liang had sexual relations with the chief's concubine.
    • 烧钉烙其双目 - Burned iron nails to scald her eyes.
    • 贺氏乃烧钉烙其双目 - Huo's wife, He Shi, burned iron nails to scald her eyes.
    • 妾遂自缢死后 - The concubine hanged herself after her death.
    • 有娠产一蛇 - She was pregnant and gave birth to a snake.
    • 两目无晴 - Her eyes were without pupils or blind.
    • 夫人曾烧铁烙一女妇眼 - The lady once burned iron to scald a woman's eyes.
    • 以夫人性毒故为蛇报 - Because of the lady's poisonous nature, it is a snake's revenge.
    • 好养此蛇可以免难 - If you raise this snake, you can avoid difficulties.
    • 不然祸及身矣 - Otherwise, disaster will befall you.
    • 贺氏养蛇一二年渐大 - He Shi raised the snake for one or two years, and it gradually grew larger.
    • 不见物惟在衣被中 - It did not see anything and remained only in her clothes and bedding.
    • 亮不知也拨被见蛇大惊 - Liang did not know; when he pulled aside the bedding and saw the snake, he was greatly shocked.
    • 以刀斫杀之 - He killed it with a knife.
    • 两目俱枯不复见物 - Both her eyes became dry and she could no longer see anything.
    • 悔而无及焉 - She regretted it but it was too late.
    • 先幸一婢 - First had sexual relations with a maid.
    • 甚妒而虐缚婢击其脑 - She was very jealous and cruelly bound the maid, hitting her head.
    • 号呼曰在下卑贱势不自由 - She cried out, saying, 'I am lowly and cannot help it.'
    • 锁项苦毒何甚 - Why is the lock around my neck so painful?
    • 婢死后月余李氏病常见婢来唤 - After the maid died, about a month later, Li Shi fell ill and often saw the maid coming to call her.
    • 头上生四处瘅疽脑溃 - She developed four boils on her head, and her brain became ulcerated.
    • 昼夜鸣呌苦痛不胜 - She screamed day and night, suffering unbearable pain.
    • 数月而卒 - She died after several months.
    • 判防司县丞张景先宠其婢 - Zhang Jingxian, the county magistrate of Fangsi, favored his maid.
    • 景出使不在妻杀婢投之于厠 - When Jing went on a mission and was not around, his wife killed the maid and threw her into the latrine.
    • 景至绐之曰婢逃矣 - When Jing returned, he deceived him, saying, 'the maid has escaped.'
    • 公夫人病困说形状 - Your wife is very ill and describes the situation.
    • 具首其事 - She confessed the whole matter.
    • 令厠内取其骸骨香汤浴之厚加殡 - He ordered the bones to be taken from the latrine, bathed in fragrant water, and given a proper burial.
    • 婢不肯放月余而卒 - The maid did not want to be released and died after a month.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 都督周仁轨 - General Zhou Rengui was a military leader during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 獠 - The Lao were a tribal group often subject to military campaigns by the Tang Dynasty.
    • 蛇 - Snakes are often associated with supernatural or vengeful spirits in Chinese folklore.
    • 厠 - Refers to a latrine or toilet, a place of low status and often associated with impurity.
    • 防司 - A local administrative office in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 县丞 - A county magistrate, a local government official.
    • 厠内取其骸骨 - The act of retrieving bones from a latrine is a grim and unusual practice, reflecting the supernatural elements of the story.

    Proper Nouns

    • 广州化县丞胡亮 - Hu Liang, the county magistrate of Huazhao in Guangzhou.
    • 都督周仁轨 - General Zhou Rengui.
    • 獠 - The Lao, a tribal group.
    • 广州 - Guangzhou, a city in southern China.
    • 化县 - Huazhao County, a county in Guangzhou.
    • 防司县丞张景先 - Zhang Jingxian, the county magistrate of Fangsi.
    • 梁仁裕 - Liang Renyu, a general.
    • 荆州枝江县主簿夏荣 - Xia Rong, the deputy county magistrate of Zhiyang County in Jingzhou.
    • 张景先 - Zhang Jingxian, the county magistrate of Fangsi.
    • 夏荣 - Xia Rong, the deputy county magistrate of Zhiyang County.
    • 李氏 - Li Shi, the wife of Zhang Jingxian.
    • 杨氏 - Yang Shi, the wife of Xia Rong.
    • 婢 - A maid or concubine.
    • 厠 - A latrine or toilet.
    • 防司 - A local administrative office.
    • 县丞 - A county magistrate.
    • 主簿 - A deputy county magistrate.
    • 荆州 - Jingzhou, a city in central China.
    • 枝江县 - Zhiyang County, a county in Jingzhou.

    Hu Liang, the county magistrate of Huazhao in Guangzhou, followed General Zhou Rengui to campaign against the Lao and obtained a chief's concubine. Upon arriving at the county, Liang was not at the government office. His wife, He Shi, burned iron nails to scald her eyes. The concubine then hanged herself after her death. He Shi became pregnant and gave birth to a snake whose eyes were without pupils. She asked a Zen master about the matter. The master said, "Lady, you once burned iron to scald a woman's eyes. Because of your poisonous nature, this is the woman's revenge. She is the woman who was scalded. If you raise this snake, you can avoid difficulties; otherwise, disaster will befall you." He Shi raised the snake for one or two years, and it gradually grew larger. It did not see anything and remained only in her clothes and bedding. Liang did not know; when he pulled aside the bedding and saw the snake, he was greatly shocked and killed it with a knife. He Shi's eyes became dry, and she could no longer see anything. She regretted it but it was too late.

    Liang Renyu, a general, first had sexual relations with a maid. His wife, Li Shi, was very jealous and cruelly bound the maid, hitting her head. The maid cried out, saying, "I am lowly and cannot help it." She asked, "Why is the lock around my neck so painful?" After the maid died, about a month later, Li Shi fell ill and often saw the maid coming to call her. She developed four boils on her head, and her brain became ulcerated. She screamed day and night, suffering unbearable pain, and died after several months.

    Xia Rong, the deputy county magistrate of Zhiyang County in Jingzhou, favored his maid. When Zhang Jingxian, the county magistrate of Fangsi, went on a mission and was not around, his wife, Yang Shi, killed the maid and threw her into the latrine. When Zhang returned, he deceived him, saying, "The maid has escaped." Zhang thought his wife was harshly cruel and did not inquire further. The maid complained to Xia Rong, who investigated the matter. He asked Zhang, "Your wife is very ill and describes the situation." Zhang suspected there was something private and was angry. Xia Rong said, "Your wife wrongfully killed the maid and threw her into the latrine. Now I am investigating this matter. You should ask her about it." Zhang realized the truth and questioned his wife. She was very ill and confessed the whole matter. Xia Rong ordered the bones to be taken from the latrine, bathed in fragrant water, and given a proper burial. The maid did not want to be released and died after a month. [41]

    左仆射韦安石女适太府主簿李训训未婚以前有一妾成亲之后遂嫁之已易两主女患传尸瘦病恐妾厌祷之安石令河南令秦守一捉来榜掠楚苦竟以自诬前后决三百以上投井而死不出三日其女遂亡时人咸以为寃魂之所致也安石坐贬蒲州太极元年八月卒
    　　王冀州衡水人少无赖告密罗织善人曾游河北云具见老人每年入邑斋遂告二百人授游击将军俄除侍御史时有告胜州都督王安仁者密差往推索大枷夹颈安仁不承伏遂于枷上斫安仁死便即脱之其男从军亦擒而斩之至汾州与司马毛公食须臾喝下斩取首级百姓震悚后坐诬枉流雷州将少姬花严素所宠也于舟中伪作敕追花严諌曰事势如此何忍更为不轨乎怒曰此老妪欲败吾事缚其手足投之于江船人救得之又鞭二百而死埋于江上俄而伪敕发御史胡元礼推之锢身领回至花严死处忽云花严来唤对事左右皆不见唯称叩头死罪如受枷棒之声夜半而卒 [42]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [42]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 左仆射 - High-ranking official position in the Tang Dynasty, roughly equivalent to a prime minister.
    • 太府主簿 - Official position in the Tang Dynasty, responsible for managing the imperial treasury.
    • 传尸瘦病 - Term for a contagious disease that causes wasting and death.
    • 榜掠楚苦 - Refers to severe physical torture.
    • 决三百以上 - Refers to receiving a punishment of more than three hundred strokes with a whip or cane.
    • 投井而死 - To throw oneself into a well and die.
    • 坐贬 - To be demoted or punished for a crime.
    • 蒲州太极元年八月 - Refers to the month of August in the first year of the Taiji era in the state of Pu.
    • 王弘冀州衡水人 - Indicates that Wang Hong was from the state of Hebei in the prefecture of Hengshui.
    • 无赖 - Means a person who is lazy, idle, or lacking in moral principles.
    • 罗织善人 - To fabricate charges against virtuous people.
    • 告密 - To report someone to the authorities, often falsely.
    • 游击将军 - Military rank in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 侍御史 - Official position responsible for overseeing the conduct of other officials.
    • 胜州都督 - Military and administrative position in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 大枷夹颈 - Refers to a large wooden frame used to bind and torture a person by pressing their neck.
    • 伏 - To confess or admit guilt.
    • 斫 - To chop or strike with a weapon.
    • 从军 - To serve in the military.
    • 司马 - Official position in charge of military affairs.
    • 伪作敕 - To forge an imperial edict.
    • 锢身领回 - To arrest and bring back someone.
    • 花严 - A woman, likely a concubine or妾 (concubine).
    • 胡元礼 - Historical figure, likely a high-ranking official or judge.
    • 不轨 - To act in a way that is against the law or moral order.
    • 缚其手脚 - To bind someone's hands and feet.
    • 鞭二百 - To whip someone two hundred times.
    • 伪敕发 - To issue a false imperial edict.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 左仆射韦安石 - High-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 太府主簿李训 - Official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 蒲州太极元年八月 - Refers to the month of August in the first year of the Taiji era in the state of Pu.
    • 王弘冀州衡水人 - Indicates that Wang Hong was from the state of Hebei in the prefecture of Hengshui.
    • 胡元礼 - Historical figure, likely a high-ranking official or judge.

    Proper Nouns

    • 左仆射韦安石 - High-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 太府主簿李训 - Official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 蒲州太极元年八月 - Refers to the month of August in the first year of the Taiji era in the state of Pu.
    • 王弘冀州衡水人 - Indicates that Wang Hong was from the state of Hebei in the prefecture of Hengshui.
    • 胡元礼 - Historical figure, likely a high-ranking official or judge.

    The Left Shangshu (Left Minister) Wei Anshi's daughter married the Taifu Zhubu (Imperial Treasury Clerk) Li Xun. Before they were married, Li Xun had a concubine. After they were married, he gave the concubine away to another man, who became her new master. The daughter contracted a contagious wasting disease, and Wei Anshi feared that the concubine might have been the cause. He ordered the County Magistrate of Henan, Qin Shouyi, to bring the concubine in for severe torture. Under the pressure of the torture, she eventually confessed to fabricating the illness. She was punished with more than three hundred strokes and threw herself into a well, dying within three days. Her daughter also died shortly after. People at the time believed that the daughter's death was caused by the spirit of the wronged concubine. Wei Anshi was demoted and later died in August of the first year of the Taiji era in the state of Pu.

    Wang Hong, a man from Hengshui in the state of Hebei, was known for his laziness and lack of moral principles. He often reported innocent people to the authorities, fabricating charges against them. He once traveled to the region of Hebei and met an old man who told him that every year, the old man would enter the village temple to pray. Wang Hong then falsely accused two hundred people of a crime and was appointed as a Yuye Jiangjun (Guerrilla General). Soon after, he was promoted to Shiyushi (Inspector of Judicial Affairs). At one point, he was sent to investigate the case of Wang Anren, the Duke of Shengzhou, who was accused of a crime. Wang Hong used a large wooden frame to bind and torture Wang Anren, but Wang Anren did not confess. In a fit of rage, Wang Hong struck Wang Anren to death with a weapon. Wang Anren's son, who was serving in the military, was also captured and executed. Later, Wang Hong traveled to Fen Prefecture and had a meal with the Military Governor Mao Gong. After the meal, Wang Hong ordered Mao Gong to be beheaded, taking his head as a trophy. The people were terrified by his actions. Eventually, Wang Hong was punished for his false accusations and was exiled to Leizhou. He had a concubine named Huayuan, who was his favorite. While on a boat, Wang Hong forged an imperial edict to summon Huayuan. Huayuan advised him, saying, "Given the current situation, how can you afford to act so recklessly?" Wang Hong became angry and said, "This old woman wants to ruin my plans!" He then bound her hands and feet and threw her into the river. The crew saved her, but Wang Hong whipped her two hundred times and buried her on the shore. Soon after, a false imperial edict was issued, and the Inspector of Judicial Affairs Hu Yuanli investigated the matter. Wang Hong was arrested and brought back to the place where Huayuan died. Suddenly, it was said that Huayuan appeared and called out to Wang Hong, but no one else could see her. Wang Hong cried out, "I am guilty! I deserve to die!" He heard the sound of a wooden frame and a stick, and he died in the middle of the night. [42]

    余杭人陆彦夏月死十余日见王云命未尽放归左右曰宅舎亡坏不堪时沧州人李谈新来其人合死王曰取谈宅舎与之彦遂入谈柩中而苏遂作呉语不识妻子具说其事遂向余杭访得其家妻子不认具陈由来乃信之
    　　天后中涪州武龙界多虎暴有一兽似虎而絶大日正中逐一虎直入人家噬杀之亦不食其肉自是县界不复有虎矣録奏检瑞图乃酋耳不食生物有虎暴则杀之
    　　天后中成王千里将一虎子来宫中养损一宫人遂令生饿数日而死天后令之其上起塔设千人供勒碑号为虎塔至今犹在
    　　傅黄中为越州诸暨县令有部人饮大醉夜中山行临崖而睡忽有虎临其上而嗅之虎须入醉人鼻中遂喷嚏声震虎遂惊跃便即落崖腰胯不遂为人所得
    　　阳城居夏县拜諌议大夫郑纲【一本作锢】居阌乡拜拾遗李周南居曲江拜校书郎时人以为转远转高转近转卑 [43]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [43]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 命未尽放归 - Set phrase meaning 'not yet over, release back to life'; used to describe the revived man being allowed to return.
    • 遂入谈柩中而苏 - Narrative phrase describing the revived man entering Li Tan's coffin and coming back to life.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天后 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 虎塔 - A pagoda built to honor a tiger, still standing today.

    Proper Nouns

    • 余杭 - A place name in present-day Hangzhou, Zhejiang Province.
    • 沧州 - A place name in present-day Hebei Province.
    • 涪州 - A place name in present-day Fuzhou, Sichuan Province.
    • 成王 - Refers to the Prince Chengwang.
    • 夏县 - A place name in present-day Shanxi Province.
    • 諌议大夫 - A high-ranking official responsible for advising the emperor.
    • 拾遗 - An official responsible for remonstrating with the emperor.
    • 校书郎 - An official responsible for proofreading and editing books.

    A man from Yuhang, Lu Yan, died in the summer but was revived after seeing a king who said his fate was not yet over. The attendants said his house was ruined and unsuitable. A man from Cangzhou named Li Tan had recently arrived and was destined to die. The king ordered that Li Tan's house be given to Lu Yan. Lu Yan then entered Li Tan's coffin and came back to life. He began speaking Wu dialect and did not recognize his wife and children. He told his story and went to Yuhang to find his family. His wife and children did not recognize him until he told them his story.

    During the reign of Empress Wu, the Wulong area of Fuzhou was plagued by frequent tiger attacks. There was a beast resembling a tiger but much larger. One day, at noon, it chased a tiger and killed it inside a house. The beast did not eat the tiger's meat, and after that, there were no more tigers in the county. The event was recorded and reported, and consulting a book of auspicious signs identified the beast as a "chui er" (a mythical creature that does not eat living things). If there were tiger attacks, it would kill them.

    During the reign of Empress Wu, the Prince Chengwang brought a tiger cub to the palace. The tiger later killed a palace woman. The Empress ordered that the tiger be starved for several days before it died. The Empress ordered the burial of the tiger, building a pagoda, setting up a thousand-person offering, and erecting a stele named the "Tiger Pagoda," which still stands today.

    Fu Huangzhong was the magistrate of Zhuji County in the state of Yue. A man from the department of Zhuji County drank heavily, fell asleep near a cliff, and was saved by a tiger. Suddenly, a tiger smelled the man, its whiskers entered the man's nose, causing him to sneeze, and the tiger jumped away, leading to the man falling off the cliff and being saved by a passerby.

    Yang Cheng lived in Xian County and was appointed as a Counselor. Zheng Gang lived in Wenxiang and was appointed as a Remonstrant. Li Zhou Nan lived in Qujiang and was appointed as a Proofreader. People believed that the further away one was, the higher the position, and the closer one was, the lower the position. [43]

    袁守一性行浅促时人号为料鬭鳬翁歴任万年尉雍州长史窦怀贞每欲鞭之乃于中书令宗楚客门饷生菜除监察怀贞未知也贞高揖曰驾欲出公作如此检校守一即弹之月余贞除左台御史大夫守一请假不改出乞解贞呼而慰之守一兢惕不已楚客知之为除右台侍御史于朝堂抗衡于贞曰与公罗师罗师者市郭儿语无交涉也无何楚客以反诛守一以其党配流端州
    　　黄门侍郎崔泰之哭特进李峤诗曰台阁神仙地衣冠君子乡昨朝犹对坐今日忽云亡魂随司命魄逐阎罗王此时罢懽笑无复向朝堂
    　　尚书右丞陆余庆转洛州长史其子嘲之曰陆余庆笔头无力觜头硬一朝受词诏十日判不竟送案褥下余庆得而读之曰必是那狗遂鞭之
    　　周定州刺史孙彦高被突厥围城数十重不敢诣防文符须征发者于小窻接入锁州宅门及贼登垒乃入匮中藏令奴曰牢掌钥匙贼来索慎勿与昔有愚人入京选皮袋被贼盗去其人曰贼偷我袋将终不得我物用或问其故答曰钥匙尚在我衣带上彼将何物开之此孙彦高之流也 [44]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [44]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 性行浅促 - Compound term describing someone as having a shallow and limited nature or behavior.
    • 料鬭鳬翁 - Nickname given to someone, possibly referring to their occupation or behavior. '料鬭' could mean 'managing disputes' or 'arbitrating fights,' and '鳬翁' could refer to an elderly person or someone with a certain demeanor.
    • 歴任 - Means 'served in various positions' or 'held multiple roles.'
    • 万年尉 - Refers to an official title, '尉' being an administrative or military officer, and '万年' likely referring to a specific region or administrative division.
    • 雍州长史 - '长史' is a high-ranking official, and '雍州' is a historical region in China.
    • 中书令 - A high-ranking official in charge of the central government's secretariat.
    • 监察 - Refers to an official position, '监察御史' being a type of censor.
    • 左台御史大夫 - A high-ranking censorial official.
    • 右台侍御史 - Another type of censorial official.
    • 朝堂 - Refers to the imperial court or the central government.
    • 罗师 - A term or title, possibly referring to a type of official or a specific role.
    • 市郭儿 - Refers to someone from the market or urban area, possibly a commoner or a merchant.
    • 黄门侍郎 - A high-ranking official, '黄门' referring to the imperial palace, and '侍郎' being a deputy minister.
    • 特进 - A high-ranking official or honorific title.
    • 尚书右丞 - A high-ranking official in the Ministry of Works.
    • 洛州长史 - Refers to an official position in the state of Luo.
    • 笔头无力觜头硬 - Metaphorical expression describing someone as weak in writing but strong in speech or argument.
    • 受词诏 - Refers to receiving imperial edicts or commands.
    • 判不竟 - Means 'not completed' or 'unfinished.'
    • 端州 - A historical region in China.
    • 周定州刺史 - Refers to an official position in the state of Zhou.
    • 孙彦高 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official.
    • 突厥 - Refers to the Turkic people, a nomadic group that historically interacted with the Chinese empire.
    • 文符 - Refers to official documents or correspondence.
    • 锁州宅门 - Means 'locking the state residence's gate.'
    • 登垒 - Refers to climbing a fortress or defensive wall.
    • 匮中藏 - Means 'hiding in a chest' or 'locking away in a chest.'
    • 牢掌钥匙 - Means 'hold the key tightly' or 'keep the key secure.'
    • 阎罗王 - Refers to Yama, the god of death in Chinese mythology.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 袁守一 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 窦怀贞 - A prominent official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his political influence.
    • 宗楚客 - Another prominent official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his involvement in court politics.
    • 监察御史 - A type of censorial official responsible for overseeing government officials.
    • 左台御史大夫 - A high-ranking censorial official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 右台侍御史 - Another type of censorial official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 朝堂 - The imperial court, where major political decisions were made.
    • 罗师 - A term or title, possibly referring to a type of official or a specific role in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 市郭儿 - Refers to commoners or merchants in urban areas.
    • 特进 - A high-ranking official or honorific title in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 尚书右丞 - A high-ranking official in the Ministry of Works during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 洛州长史 - An official position in the state of Luo during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 端州 - A historical region in China, known for its cultural and administrative significance.
    • 周定州刺史 - An official position in the state of Zhou during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 孙彦高 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 突厥 - A nomadic group that historically interacted with the Chinese empire, particularly during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 文符 - Official documents or correspondence used in administrative and military contexts.
    • 阎罗王 - Yama, the god of death in Chinese mythology, often invoked in literature to describe death or judgment.

    Proper Nouns

    • 袁守一 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 窦怀贞 - A prominent official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his political influence.
    • 宗楚客 - Another prominent official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his involvement in court politics.
    • 崔泰之 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 李峤 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 陆余庆 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 孙彦高 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 特进 - A high-ranking official or honorific title in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 尚书右丞 - A high-ranking official in the Ministry of Works during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 洛州长史 - An official position in the state of Luo during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 端州 - A historical region in China, known for its cultural and administrative significance.
    • 周定州刺史 - An official position in the state of Zhou during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 突厥 - A nomadic group that historically interacted with the Chinese empire, particularly during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 阎罗王 - Yama, the god of death in Chinese mythology.

    袁守一性格浅薄，行为急促，当时人们称他为“料鬭鳬翁”。他历任万年尉、雍州长史等职。窦怀贞每次都想鞭打他，于是趁中书令宗楚客不在时，在宗楚客门前送生菜，以此除掉他。窦怀贞不知情，只是拱手作揖说：“皇上要出宫了，你如此检校，做得不错。”袁守一立即弹劾他。一个多月后，窦怀贞被任命为左台御史大夫，袁守一请假不改，请求解除职务，窦怀贞呼唤并安慰他，袁守一却惶恐不安。宗楚客得知此事后，将袁守一调任右台侍御史，在朝堂上与窦怀贞抗衡，说：“与你罗师，罗师是市郭儿，与我无交涉。”不久，宗楚客因谋反而被诛杀，袁守一因他是党羽而被流放至端州。

    黄门侍郎崔泰之哭悼特进李峤的诗曰：“台阁神仙地，衣冠君子乡。昨朝犹对坐，今日忽云亡。魂随司命去，鬼魄逐阎罗王。此时罢欢笑，无复向朝堂。”

    尚书右丞陆余庆转任洛州长史，其子嘲讽他说：“陆余庆笔头无力，觜头硬。一朝受词诏，十日判不竟。送案褥下，余庆得而读之，曰：‘必是那狗。’遂鞭之。”

    周定州刺史孙彦高被突厥围城数十重，不敢前往防御。需征发兵员时，他通过小窗接入文书，锁州宅门。等到贼人登垒，他才躲入柜中藏匿，吩咐奴仆说：“牢牢掌管钥匙，贼人来索要时，切勿给他。”昔日有愚人入京选皮袋，被贼盗去，那人说：“贼偷我袋，将终不得我物用。”有人问他原因，他答道：“钥匙尚在我衣带上，彼将何物开之？”此乃孙彦高之流也。 [44]

    姜师度好竒诡为沧州刺史兼按察造抢车运粮开河筑堰州县鼎沸于鲁城界内种稻置屯穂蟹食尽又差夫打蟹苦之歌曰地抑种稻一槩被水沫年年索蟹夫百姓不可活又为陜州刺史以永丰仓米运将别征三钱计以为费一夕忽云得计立注楼从仓建槽至于河长数千丈而令放米其不快处具大杷推之米皆积耗多为粉末兼风激凡一函失米百石而动即千万数遣典庾者偿之家产皆竭复遣输户自量至有偿数十斛者甚害人方停之
    　　岐王府参军石恵恭与监察御史李全交诗曰御史非长任参军不久居待君迁转后此职还到余因竞放牒往来全交为之判十余纸以报乃假手于拾遗张九龄
    　　御史中丞李谨度宋璟引致之遭母丧不肯举发哀讣到皆匿之官寮苦其无用令本贯瀛州申谨度母死尚书省牒御史台然后哭其庸猥皆此也 [45]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [45]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 好竒诡 - Compound adjective meaning 'loves strange and bizarre things,' indicating a penchant for unusual or extravagant projects.
    • 兼按察 - Means 'serving as an inspector' or 'with the additional duty of inspection,' referring to an official's dual role.
    • 差夫 - Means 'to conscript laborers,' often used in the context of forced labor.
    • 苦之 - Means 'to suffer from it' or 'to find it difficult,' expressing the hardship experienced by the people.
    • 歌曰 - Means 'a song goes,' introducing a folk song or lament.
    • 抑种稻 - Means 'but to plant rice,' indicating a contrast or limitation in the context of the song.
    • 一概被水沫 - Means 'all covered by water foam,' describing the widespread flooding.
    • 年年索蟹夫 - Means 'year after year, conscripting laborers to catch crabs,' indicating a recurring burden.
    • 不可活 - Means 'cannot survive' or 'cannot live on,' expressing the dire situation.
    • 又为陜州刺史 - Means 'then served as the governor of Shaan Prefecture,' indicating a change in position.
    • 永丰仓 - Refers to the Yongfeng granary, a large storage facility.
    • 别征三钱 - Means 'to levy an additional three coins,' indicating an extra tax.
    • 立注楼 - Means 'to build a water tower,' a structure for managing water flow.
    • 具大杷推之 - Means 'to use large rakes to push it,' describing the method of managing the grain flow.
    • 积耗多为粉末 - Means 'much of it accumulates as dust,' indicating inefficiency.
    • 风激扬 - Means 'wind blows it away,' describing the loss of grain due to wind.
    • 失米百石 - Means 'loses a hundred dan of grain,' indicating significant loss.
    • 典庾者 - Refers to the granary manager or official responsible for grain storage.
    • 偿之家产皆竭 - Means 'their family property is all exhausted,' indicating the financial ruin of those responsible.
    • 输户 - Refers to the taxpayers or those responsible for grain delivery.
    • 偿数十斛者 - Means 'those who have to pay tens of dou,' indicating the extent of the financial burden.
    • 甚害人 - Means 'very harmful,' expressing the negative impact.
    • 方停之 - Means 'just stopped it,' indicating the cessation of the practice.
    • 岐王府参军 - Refers to a military officer in the Prince of Qi's household.
    • 监察御史 - Refers to an official responsible for oversight and inspection.
    • 因竞放牒 - Means 'because of competing to issue documents,' indicating a bureaucratic struggle.
    • 假手于拾遗 - Means 'to use the services of a censor,' indicating a delegation of tasks.
    • 御史中丞 - Refers to the deputy head of the Censorate.
    • 宋璟引致之 - Means 'recommended by Song Jing,' indicating a recommendation for the position.
    • 遭母丧 - Means 'upon the death of his mother,' indicating a period of mourning.
    • 不肯举发哀 - Means 'refused to announce his mourning,' indicating a lack of public display of grief.
    • 讣到皆匿之 - Means 'when the news of her death arrived, he concealed it,' indicating a cover-up.
    • 官寮苦其无用 - Means 'officials complained about his uselessness,' indicating dissatisfaction with his performance.
    • 令本贯瀛州申谨度母死 - Means 'ordered the native place of Ying Prefecture to report the death of Ruan Jundu's mother,' indicating a bureaucratic action.
    • 尚书省牒御史台 - Means 'the Shangshu Province issued a decree to the Censorate,' indicating a formal communication.
    • 然后哭 - Means 'then he cried,' indicating the performance of mourning rituals.
    • 庸猥皆此様也 - Means 'his incompetence and triviality were all like this,' indicating a general assessment of his character.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 姜师度 - A historical figure known for his engineering projects and administrative roles.
    • 沧州 - Refers to Cang Prefecture, a region in modern-day Hebei Province.
    • 鲁城界内 - Refers to the area within the boundaries of Luzheng (Lu City), indicating a specific region.
    • 永丰仓 - A significant granary in the Tang Dynasty, used for storing grain.
    • 陜州 - Refers to Shaan Prefecture, a region in modern-day Henan Province.
    • 岐王府 - Refers to the household of the Prince of Qi, a member of the imperial family.
    • 监察御史 - A high-ranking official responsible for oversight and inspection.
    • 拾遗 - A low-ranking official with the duty of remonstrating and providing advice.
    • 宋璟 - A famous chancellor and statesman of the Tang Dynasty known for his integrity and administrative reforms.
    • 尚书省 - The central government office responsible for administrative affairs.
    • 御史台 - The central government office responsible for oversight and inspection.

    Proper Nouns

    • 姜师度 - A historical figure.
    • 沧州 - A region in modern-day Hebei Province.
    • 鲁城 - A specific region within Cang Prefecture.
    • 永丰仓 - A significant granary in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 陜州 - A region in modern-day Henan Province.
    • 岐王府 - The household of the Prince of Qi.
    • 监察御史 - A high-ranking official.
    • 拾遗 - A low-ranking official.
    • 宋璟 - A famous chancellor and statesman of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 尚书省 - The central government office responsible for administrative affairs.
    • 御史台 - The central government office responsible for oversight and inspection.
    • 石恵恭 - A military officer in the Prince of Qi's household.
    • 李全交 - An official responsible for oversight and inspection.
    • 张九龄 - A censor.
    • 李谨度 - A deputy head of the Censorate.
    • 瀛州 - A region in modern-day Hebei Province.

    姜师度喜欢奇异的事物，担任沧州刺史兼监察官，建造抢车运输粮食，开凿河流和修建堤坝，州县因此沸腾。在鲁城边界内种植水稻，设置屯田，但稻穗被螃蟹吃光，又征调劳力捕捉螃蟹，百姓苦不堪言，歌谣唱道：“土地虽好，却只能种稻，全部被水沫覆盖，年年征调捉蟹的劳力，百姓无法生存。”后来他又担任陜州刺史，用永丰仓的粮食运输，另征三钱作为费用。一夜之间，他忽然想出一个计策，建造水塔，从仓库到河边建槽，长达数千丈，命令放米，不顺畅的地方用大耙推动，但米大多积攒成粉末，加上风吹扬起，每运一次损失一百石，动辄损失成千上万，派遣仓库管理员赔偿，家产全部耗尽，又让纳税人自行测量，有的赔偿数十斛，非常损害百姓，最终停止了这种做法。

    岐王府参军石惠恭与监察御史李全交写诗说：“御史不是长期任职的职位，参军也不会久留，待你调任后，这个职位还会回到我这里。因为竞争发放文书，李全交为此判了十余纸以回报，实际上是借助拾遗张九龄的手。

    御史中丞李谨度由宋璟推荐，母亲去世后，他不肯公开表达哀悼，讣告到来时都隐瞒了，官员们抱怨他无用，命令他的原籍瀛州申报李谨度母亲去世的消息，尚书省向御史台发出文书，然后他才哭泣，他的庸碌无能都是这样的。 [45]

    王怡为中丞宪台之秽姜晦为掌选侍郎吏部之秽崔泰之为黄门侍郎门下之秽号为京师三秽
    　　阳滔为中书舎人时促命制敕令史持库钥他适无旧本检寻乃斵窻取得之时人号为斵窻舎人
    　　国子进士【一作祭酒】辛智诗云君为河边草逢春心剰生妾如堂上镜得照始分明同房学士常定宗为改始字为转字遂争此诗皆云我作乃下牒见博士罗为宗判云昔五字定表以理切称竒一言竞长取词多为主诗归智转还定宗以状牒知亦为公之念
    　　杭州参军独孤守忠领租船赴都夜半急追集船人更无他语乃曰逆风必不得张防众大哂焉
    　　王熊为泽州都督府法曹断掠粮贼惟各决杖一百通判熊曰总掠防人法曹曰掠七人熊曰掠七人合决七百法曹曲断府司科罪时人哂之前尹正义为都督公平后熊来替百姓歌曰前得尹佛子后得王癞獭判事驴咬唤人牛嚼沫见钱满面喜无镪从头喝尝逢饿夜叉百姓不可活 [46]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [46]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 中丞宪台之秽 - Refers to the corruption or scandal associated with the position of 中丞 (a high-ranking official in the imperial court).
    • 掌选侍郎吏部之秽 - Refers to the corruption or scandal associated with the position of 掌选侍郎 (a high-ranking official in the Ministry of Personnel).
    • 黄门侍郎门下之秽 - Refers to the corruption or scandal associated with the position of 黄门侍郎 (a high-ranking official in the inner court).
    • 京师三秽 - A term used to describe three notorious cases of corruption in the capital city.
    • 促命制敕 - Refers to the urgent command to draft an imperial edict.
    • 斵窻取得 - A literal expression meaning 'carved through the window to retrieve,' used metaphorically to describe an unconventional method of obtaining something.
    • 斵窻舎人 - A term used to describe a secretary who used an unconventional method to retrieve a document.
    • 国子进士 - Refers to a scholar or graduate from the Imperial Academy.
    • 同房学士 - Refers to fellow scholars or officials.
    • 改始字为转字 - Refers to changing the character '始' to '转' in a poem.
    • 牒见博士 - Refers to submitting a petition to the Imperial Academy for judgment.
    • 罗为宗判云 - Refers to the judgment given by the Imperial Academy.
    • 五字定表 - Refers to a five-character poem that was considered a standard for excellence.
    • 一言竞长 - Refers to a single word that was considered the best in a competition.
    • 诗归弘智 - Refers to the poem being attributed back to 辛弘智.
    • 转还定宗 - Refers to the poem being attributed back to 常定宗.
    • 逆风必不得张防 - A literal expression meaning 'in the face of the wind, one must not spread the sail,' used metaphorically to describe a situation where one must not take action.
    • 驴咬 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'a donkey biting a melon,' used to describe a situation where something is being done in an unconventional or absurd manner.
    • 唤人牛嚼沫 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'calling someone to chew on the foam,' used to describe a situation where someone is being asked to do something absurd or unnecessary.
    • 见钱满面喜 - A literal expression meaning 'seeing money makes one's face happy,' used to describe the greed or corruption of officials.
    • 无镪从头喝 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'without money, one is scolded from the beginning,' used to describe the harsh treatment of officials who are corrupt.
    • 尝逢饿夜叉 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'often encountering a hungry night demon,' used to describe the harsh and unpredictable nature of official life.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 中丞 - A high-ranking official in the imperial court responsible for judicial matters.
    • 宪台 - Refers to the imperial court or the central government.
    • 掌选侍郎 - A high-ranking official in the Ministry of Personnel responsible for selecting and appointing officials.
    • 黄门侍郎 - A high-ranking official in the inner court responsible for attending to the emperor.
    • 国子进士 - A scholar or graduate from the Imperial Academy, which was the highest institution of learning in the imperial court.
    • 同房学士 - Fellow scholars or officials who were often in the same office or academy.
    • 牒见博士 - The process of submitting a petition to the Imperial Academy for judgment, which was a common practice in the imperial court.
    • 罗为宗判云 - The judgment given by the Imperial Academy, which was considered authoritative and final.
    • 五字定表 - A five-character poem that was considered a standard for excellence in classical Chinese poetry.
    • 一言竞长 - A single word that was considered the best in a competition, which was a common practice in the imperial court to determine the best poet or writer.
    • 诗归弘智 - The poem being attributed back to 辛弘智, which was a common practice in the imperial court to determine the rightful owner of a poem.
    • 转还定宗 - The poem being attributed back to 常定宗, which was a common practice in the imperial court to determine the rightful owner of a poem.
    • 逆风必不得张防 - A metaphorical expression used to describe a situation where one must not take action, which was a common practice in the imperial court to determine the best course of action.
    • 驴咬 - A metaphorical expression used to describe a situation where something is being done in an unconventional or absurd manner, which was a common practice in the imperial court to determine the best course of action.
    • 唤人牛嚼沫 - A metaphorical expression used to describe a situation where someone is being asked to do something absurd or unnecessary, which was a common practice in the imperial court to determine the best course of action.
    • 见钱满面喜 - A literal expression used to describe the greed or corruption of officials, which was a common practice in the imperial court to determine the best course of action.
    • 无镪从头喝 - A metaphorical expression used to describe the harsh treatment of officials who are corrupt, which was a common practice in the imperial court to determine the best course of action.
    • 尝逢饿夜叉 - A metaphorical expression used to describe the harsh and unpredictable nature of official life, which was a common practice in the imperial court to determine the best course of action.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王怡 - A high-ranking official in the imperial court.
    • 姜晦 - A high-ranking official in the Ministry of Personnel.
    • 崔泰之 - A high-ranking official in the inner court.
    • 阳滔 - A secretary in the imperial court.
    • 辛弘智 - A scholar or graduate from the Imperial Academy.
    • 常定宗 - A scholar or graduate from the Imperial Academy.
    • 独孤守忠 - A military officer in the imperial court.

    Wang Yi was the corrupt official of the imperial court, Jiang Hui was the corrupt official of the Ministry of Personnel, and Cui Tai was the corrupt official of the inner court, and they were collectively known as the "Three Corruptions of the Capital."

    Yang Tao, when serving as a secretary in the imperial court, was urgently ordered to draft an imperial edict. The clerk holding the key to the treasury was elsewhere, and there was no old copy to retrieve. Yang Tao then carved through the window to retrieve the document, and people began to call him the "Window-Carving Secretary."

    A scholar or graduate from the Imperial Academy, Xin Hongzhi, wrote a poem: "You are the grass by the river, and in spring, your heart overflows. I am the mirror on the hall, and when I am reflected, I become clear." His fellow scholar, Chang D [46]

    冀州防军麴崇裕送司功入京诗曰崇裕有幸防得遇明流行司士向京去旷野哭声哀司功曰大才士先生其谁曰吴儿博士教此声韵司功曰师明弟子哲
    　　滑州灵昌尉梁士防官科鸟翎里正不送举牒判曰官唤鸟翎何物里正不送鸟翎佐使曰公大好判鸟翎太多防索笔曰官唤鸟翎何物里正不送鸟翎有识之士闻而笑之

    　　朝野佥载卷二
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,朝野佥载>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　朝野佥载卷三
    　　唐　张鷟　撰
    　　则天朝太仆卿来俊臣之强盛朝官侧目上林令侯敏偏事之其妻董氏谏止之曰俊臣国贼也势不久一朝事败党附先遭君可敬而逺之敏稍稍引退俊臣怒出为涪州武龙令敏欲弃官归董氏曰速去莫求住遂行至州投刺参州将错题一张州将展看尾后有字大怒曰脩名不了何以为县令不放上敏忧闷无已董氏曰且住莫求去停五十日忠州贼破武龙杀旧县令掠家口并尽敏以不计上获全后俊臣诛逐其党流岭南敏又获免 [47]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [47]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 明流行 - Metaphorical expression indicating a clear and upright path or a good opportunity.
    • 旷野哭声哀 - Vivid imagery describing the wilderness filled with sorrowful cries.
    • 大好判 - Idiomatic expression indicating a well-considered judgment or decision.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 冀州 - One of the Nine Provinces of ancient China, located in what is now Hebei Province.
    • 防军 - A type of military officer responsible for defense.
    • 司功 - A low-ranking official, often responsible for minor administrative tasks.
    • 上林 - The imperial garden, a place of leisure and recreation for the emperor.
    • 则天朝 - The reign of Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 太仆卿 - A high-ranking official in charge of the imperial stables and transportation.
    • 来俊臣 - A notorious official during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, known for his cruelty and power.
    • 涪州 - An ancient prefecture in Sichuan Province.
    • 武龙 - A county within the ancient prefecture of Fuzhou.
    • 朝野佥载 - A collection of anecdotes and stories from the Tang Dynasty, compiled by Zhang Zuo.
    • 钦定四库全书 - The official compilation of Chinese literature and historical texts commissioned by the Qing Dynasty.
    • 张鷟 - Author of the text.
    • 侯敏 - Official in charge of the imperial garden.
    • 董氏 - Wife of Hou Min.

    Proper Nouns

    • 冀州 - Ji Prefecture.
    • 防军 - Military officer.
    • 司功 - Low-ranking official.
    • 上林 - Imperial garden.
    • 则天朝 - Reign of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 太仆卿 - High-ranking official.
    • 来俊臣 - Notorious official.
    • 涪州 - Ancient prefecture in Sichuan.
    • 武龙 - County within Fuzhou.
    • 朝野佥载 - Collection of anecdotes.
    • 钦定四库全书 - Official compilation of Chinese literature.
    • 张鷟 - Author of the text.
    • 侯敏 - Official in charge of the imperial garden.
    • 董氏 - Wife of Hou Min.

    During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, Qu Chongyu, a military officer from Ji Prefecture, composed a poem as he sent Siguang, his subordinate, to the capital. The poem began with Qu Chongyu expressing his fortune in encountering a clear and upright path. As Siguang departed, the wilderness echoed with sorrowful cries. Siguang, upon hearing the cries, asked who the great scholar was. The response was that the sound and rhythm were taught by a scholar from Wu. Siguang then remarked that the teacher was wise and the disciple was intelligent.

    In another incident, the magistrate of Lingchang, Liang Shifang, called for a document known as "bird feathers" (a metaphorical term), but the village headman failed to deliver it. The magistrate issued a judgment, questioning why the village headman did not bring the "bird feathers." The assistant remarked that the magistrate had made a good judgment, but the "bird feathers" were too numerous. The magistrate then requested a pen and wrote, "The magistrate called for 'bird feathers,' what are these 'bird feathers'? The village headman did not bring them." Knowledgeable people found this situation amusing.

    The Chao Ye Qian Zai (A Collection of Anecdotes from the Court and the People) is a book compiled by Zhang Zuo during the Tang Dynasty. In the second volume, there is a story about the powerful and feared Lai Junchen, a high-ranking official during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian. His power caused other officials to be wary of him. Hou Min, an official in charge of the imperial garden, flattered Lai Junchen, but his wife, Dong Shi, advised him to stop. She argued that Lai Junchen was a traitor and that his power would not last. She suggested that Hou Min should respect and distance himself from him. Gradually, Hou Min withdrew his flattery, which angered Lai Junchen. As a result, Lai Junchen demoted Hou Min to the position of magistrate in Wulong, Fuzhou. Hou Min wanted to resign, but Dong Shi advised him to leave quickly and not seek a new position. So, he left and presented his credentials to the local governor, who made a mistake in writing his name. Upon seeing an additional name at the end of Hou Min's credentials, the governor became furious and refused to accept him, causing Hou Min to feel distressed. Dong Shi advised him to stay, and after fifty days, a rebel force attacked Wulong, killing the previous county magistrate and taking the entire family captive. Because Hou Min did not calculate his actions, he was spared. Later, when Lai Junchen was executed and his followers exiled to Lingnan, Hou Min was also saved. [47]

    唐冀州长史吉懋欲为男顼娶南宫县丞崔敬女敬不许因有故脇以求亲敬惧而许之择日下函并花车卒至门者敬妻郑氏初不知抱女大哭曰我家门户低不曽有吉郎女坚卧不起其小女白其母曰父有急难杀身救解设令为婢尚不合辞姓望之门何足为耻姊若不可儿自当之遂登车而去顼迁平章事贤妻达节谈者荣之顼坐与河内王武懿宗争竞出为温州司马而卒
    　　监察御史李畬母清素贞洁畬请禄米送至宅母遣量之賸三石问其故令史曰御史例不槩賸又问车脚几钱又曰御史例不还脚钱母怒令还所賸米及脚钱以责畬畬乃追仓官科罪诸御史皆有慙色
    　　文昌左丞卢献女第二先适郑氏其夫早亡誓不再醮姿容端秀言辞甚髙姊夫羽林将军李思冲姊亡之后奏请续亲许之兄弟并不敢白思冲择日备礼贽币甚盛执贽就宅卢氏拒闗抗声詈曰老奴我非汝匹也乃逾垣至所亲家截发思冲奏之敕不夺其志后为尼甚精进 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 冀州长史 - High-ranking official position in the Tang Dynasty, equivalent to a provincial governor's deputy.
    • 南宫县丞 - Lower-ranking official position, equivalent to a county deputy.
    • 脅以求亲 - Phrase indicating coercion or pressure to force someone into marriage.
    • 门户低 - Colloquial expression meaning 'low social status' or 'common family'.
    • 吉郎女 - Term of address for a woman from a common family, implying a lower social status.
    • 杀身救解 - Phrase indicating extreme self-sacrifice to resolve a difficult situation.
    • 设令为婢 - Hypothetical statement meaning 'even if she were to become a servant'.
    • 姓望之门 - Term referring to a family of noble lineage.
    • 达节 - Term indicating high moral character and understanding of social norms.
    • 谈者荣之 - Phrase indicating that people speak highly of someone.
    • 平章事 - High-ranking official position, equivalent to a chancellor or prime minister.
    • 羽林将军 - High-ranking military officer in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 续亲 - Term indicating remarriage or a new marriage.
    • 尼 - Term referring to a nun.
    • 精进 - Term indicating diligent practice and progress in religious or moral matters.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐冀州长史吉懋 - High-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 南宫县丞崔敬 - Lower-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 河内王武懿宗 - High-ranking official and member of the royal family in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 文昌左丞卢献 - High-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 监察御史李畬 - High-ranking official responsible for oversight and inspection in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 羽林将军李思冲 - High-ranking military officer in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 文昌左丞 - High-ranking official position in the Tang Dynasty, equivalent to a deputy minister of the central government.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐冀州长史吉懋 - High-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 南宫县丞崔敬 - Lower-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 河内王武懿宗 - High-ranking official and member of the royal family in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 文昌左丞卢献 - High-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 监察御史李畬 - High-ranking official responsible for oversight and inspection in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 羽林将军李思冲 - High-ranking military officer in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 文昌左丞 - High-ranking official position in the Tang Dynasty, equivalent to a deputy minister of the central government.

    During the Tang Dynasty, Ji Mao, the Chief Prefect of Jizhou, wished to marry his son Xiu to the daughter of Cui Jing, the County Magistrate of Nangong. Cui Jing refused, but due to a pressing matter, he was coerced into agreeing. Cui Jing's wife, Zheng, initially unaware, embraced her daughter and wept, saying, "Our family has low status; we have never had a daughter marry into a family of such standing." She lay down resolutely, refusing to move. Their younger daughter, however, told her mother, "Father is in urgent need; I am willing to sacrifice myself to resolve his difficulties. Even if I were to become a servant, it would not be fitting to decline a family of noble lineage. How can the door of such a family be a source of shame? If you cannot agree, I will take it upon myself." With that, she climbed into the carriage and departed.

    Xiu later became a Chancellor, and his wife was known for her high moral character. People spoke highly of her. However, Xiu once clashed with Heiwei Wang Wu Yizong, a member of the royal family, and was subsequently exiled to serve as a Military Governor in Wenzhou, where he died.

    The Inspector of Censorship, Li She, had a mother who was known for her purity and integrity. Li She requested grain and rice to be delivered to their home. His mother sent someone to measure it and found that three stones remained. She asked for the reason, and the official replied, "Inspectors are not allowed to keep any surplus." Li She then asked about the cost of the carriage, and the official said, "Inspectors are not allowed to reimburse carriage fees." His mother became angry and ordered the surplus grain and the carriage fee to be returned to hold Li She accountable. Li She then pursued the granary official and punished him with a fine. All the other censors were filled with shame.

    Lu Xian, the Left Censor of Wenchang, had a second daughter who was first married to Zheng. Her husband died early, and she vowed never to remarry. She was beautiful and eloquent. Her brother-in-law, Li Sichong, a General of the Imperial Guard, proposed to her after her sister's death. He requested permission to remarry her, and Lu Xian's brothers did not dare to inform him. They prepared a grand ceremony with rich gifts and presented them to the Lu residence. However, Lu Xian's wife, Lu, refused to open the gate and shouted, "Old man, I am not your match!" She then climbed over the wall to her relatives' home and cut her hair. Li Sichong reported this to the emperor, who did not force her to change her mind. She later became a nun and practiced diligently. [48]

    沧州髙邓亷妻李氏女嫁未周年而亷卒李年十八守志设灵几每日三上食临哭布衣蔬食六七年忽夜梦一男子容止甚都欲求李氏为偶李氏睡中不许之自后毎夜梦见李氏竟不受以为精魅书符呪禁终莫能絶李氏叹曰吾誓不移节而为此所挠盖吾容貌未衰故也乃防刀截发麻衣不濯蓬鬓不理垢面灰身其又谢李氏曰夫人竹栢之操不可夺也自是不复梦见郡守旌其门闾至今尚有节妇里
    　　杨盈川侄女曰容华幼善属文尝为新糚诗好事者多传之诗曰宿鸟惊眠罢房栊乗晓开鳯钗金作缕鸾镜玉为台糚似临池出人疑向月来自怜终不见欲去复徘徊
    　　初兵部尚书任环敕赐宫女二人皆国色妻妬烂二女头发秃尽太宗闻之令上官赍金壶瓶酒赐之云饮之立死环三品合置姬媵尔后不妬不须饮若妬即饮之栁氏拜敕讫曰妾与环结发夫妻俱出微贱更相辅翼遂致荣官环今多内嬖诚不如死饮尽而卧然实非酖也至半夜睡醒帝谓环曰其性如此朕亦当畏之因诏二女令别宅安置 [49]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [49]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 守志 - Means 'to adhere to one's resolve' or 'to remain steadfast in one's purpose.'
    • 设灵几 - Means 'to set up a spirit table' or 'to establish a shrine for the deceased.'
    • 三上食 - Means 'to offer food three times a day,' a ritual practice.
    • 布衣蔬食 - Means 'wearing plain clothes and eating simple food,' indicating a life of austerity.
    • 精魅 - Refers to a spirit or ghost that is considered malevolent or troublesome.
    • 书符呪禁 - Means 'to write charms and spells to banish' or 'to use magical texts to repel.'
    • 节妇 - Refers to a woman who remains faithful to her deceased husband, often used to describe a widow who remains chaste.
    • 杨盈川侄女 - Refers to a female relative of Yang Yingchuan, likely a historical figure.
    • 宿鸟惊眠罢 - A poetic expression meaning 'the birds have ceased their startled sleep,' suggesting a moment of quiet.
    • 房栊乘晓开 - Means 'the window opens with the dawn,' indicating the arrival of morning.
    • 凤钗金作缕 - Refers to a decorative hairpin made of gold, symbolizing elegance and beauty.
    • 鸾镜玉为台 - Refers to a mirror with a jade base, symbolizing clarity and purity.
    • 糚似临池出 - Means 'the bride appears as if she emerged from a pool,' suggesting a moment of beauty and grace.
    • 人疑向月来 - Means 'people suspect she came from the moon,' suggesting an ethereal or otherworldly quality.
    • 自怜终不见 - Means 'she laments that she will never be seen again,' expressing a sense of loss or longing.
    • 欲去复徘徊 - Means 'she wishes to leave but hesitates,' expressing a moment of indecision.
    • 兵部尚书 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the imperial government, responsible for military affairs.
    • 敕赐宫女 - Means 'imperial decree granting palace maidens,' indicating a formal gift from the emperor.
    • 国色 - Refers to a woman of exceptional beauty, often used to describe a concubine or maiden.
    • 妻妬 - Means 'the wife's jealousy,' indicating a conflict between a husband and his wife.
    • 烂二女头发秃尽 - Means 'the two maidens' hair fell out completely,' indicating a physical effect of jealousy or magic.
    • 太宗 - Refers to Emperor Taizong of Tang, a prominent ruler in Chinese history.
    • 上官赍金壶瓶酒 - Means 'an official carrying a golden flask of wine,' indicating a formal gift.
    • 饮之立死 - Means 'drink it and die immediately,' indicating a threat of death.
    • 合置姬媵 - Means 'it is appropriate to have concubines,' indicating a social norm or expectation.
    • 若妬即饮之 - Means 'if she is jealous, then drink it,' indicating a conditional threat.
    • 柳氏拜敕讫 - Means 'Liu's wife bowed after receiving the decree,' indicating a formal act of submission.
    • 结发夫妻 - Refers to a couple who have been married for a long time, often used to describe a long-lasting marriage.
    • 俱出微贱 - Means 'both came from humble origins,' indicating a shared background.
    • 更相辅翼 - Means 'they supported each other,' indicating mutual assistance.
    • 遂致荣官 - Means 'they achieved honor and official positions,' indicating success.
    • 多内嬖 - Means 'having many favored concubines,' indicating a preference for multiple partners.
    • 诚不如死 - Means 'it is truly better to die,' indicating a preference for death over a situation.
    • 至半夜睡醒 - Means 'until midnight when she woke up,' indicating a period of time.
    • 畏之 - Means 'to fear her,' indicating a sense of respect or caution.
    • 诏二女令别宅安置 - Means 'the emperor ordered the two maidens to be moved to a separate residence,' indicating a formal decision.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 沧州 - A place name, likely a city or region in ancient China.
    • 髙邓亷 - A historical figure, likely a local official or noble.
    • 杨盈川 - A historical figure, likely a poet or scholar.
    • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang, a prominent ruler known for his military conquests and administrative reforms.
    • 兵部尚书 - A high-ranking official in the imperial government, responsible for military affairs.
    • 宫女 - Palace maidens, often granted to officials or nobles as a reward or favor.
    • 国色 - A term used to describe women of exceptional beauty, often associated with concubines or favored maids.
    • 节妇 - A term used to describe a widow who remains faithful to her deceased husband, often celebrated for her virtue.
    • 结发夫妻 - A term used to describe a long-lasting marriage, often emphasizing mutual support and loyalty.
    • 微贱 - A term used to describe a humble or lowly background, often in contrast to nobility or wealth.
    • 内嬖 - A term used to describe favored concubines, often in contrast to a wife.

    Proper Nouns

    • 沧州 - A place name, likely a city or region in ancient China.
    • 髙邓亷 - A historical figure, likely a local official or noble.
    • 杨盈川 - A historical figure, likely a poet or scholar.
    • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang, a prominent ruler known for his military conquests and administrative reforms.
    • 兵部尚书 - A high-ranking official in the imperial government, responsible for military affairs.
    • 宫女 - Palace maidens, often granted to officials or nobles as a reward or favor.
    • 国色 - A term used to describe women of exceptional beauty, often associated with concubines or favored maids.
    • 节妇 - A term used to describe a widow who remains faithful to her deceased husband, often celebrated for her virtue.
    • 结发夫妻 - A term used to describe a long-lasting marriage, often emphasizing mutual support and loyalty.
    • 微贱 - A term used to describe a humble or lowly background, often in contrast to nobility or wealth.
    • 内嬖 - A term used to describe favored concubines, often in contrast to a wife.

    A Woman of Chaste Resolve from Cangzhou

    The wife of Gao Denglian from Cangzhou, Lady Li, was married for less than a year when her husband passed away. At the age of eighteen, she remained steadfast in her resolve, setting up a spirit table to honor her late husband. She offered food three times a day and mourned at the shrine, wearing plain clothes and eating simple fare for six or seven years. One night, she dreamt of a man of remarkable appearance who wished to be her companion. Lady Li refused him in her sleep, and from then on, she dreamed of this man every night but still did not accept him. She believed him to be a malevolent spirit and tried to banish him with charms and spells, but he could not be dispelled. Lady Li sighed, "I have sworn not to change my fidelity, but I am being troubled by this. It is likely because my appearance has not yet faded." She then took a knife to cut her hair, wore a coarse hemp dress, did not wash her hair, allowed her hair to grow wild, and covered her face in dirt. The spirit thanked her, saying, "Madam, your integrity is as unyielding as bamboo and cypress; it cannot be taken away." From then on, she no longer dreamed of him. The local magistrate honored her door with a plaque, and to this day, there is still a place called "Chaste Woman's Lane."

    Yang Yingchuan's Niece, Lady Ronghua, was skilled in writing from a young age and once composed a new poem about a bride. Many people admired and passed it around. The poem reads:

    "The birds have ceased their startled sleep,
    The window opens with the dawn.
    Her hairpin is made of gold threads,
    Her mirror rests on a jade stand.
    She appears as if she emerged from a pool,
    People suspect she came from the moon.
    She laments that she will never be seen again,
    She wishes to leave but hesitates."

    Initially, when the Minister of War, Ren Huan, was granted two palace maidens of exceptional beauty by imperial decree, his wife became jealous, and the two maidens' hair fell out completely. Emperor Taizong heard of this and ordered an official to bring a golden flask of wine, saying, "Drink it and die immediately." As a third-rank official, Ren Huan was entitled to have concubines. The emperor said, "If she is not jealous, then there is no need to drink. If she is jealous, then drink it." Liu, Ren Huan's wife, bowed after receiving the decree and said, "I have been married to Ren Huan for a long time, and we both came from humble origins. We have supported each other and achieved honor and official positions. Now, Ren Huan has many favored concubines. It is truly better to die." She drank the wine and fell asleep. However, the wine was not poison, and she woke up at midnight. The emperor said to Ren Huan, "Her nature is like this; I too should fear her." He then ordered the two maidens to be moved to a separate residence. [49]

    隋开皇中京兆韦衮有奴曰桃符每征讨将行有胆力衮至左卫中郎以桃符久从驱使乃放从良桃符家有黄防宰而献之因问衮乞姓衮曰止从我姓为韦氏符叩头曰不敢与郎君同姓衮曰汝但从之此有深意故至今为黄犊子韦即韦庶人其后也不许异姓者盖虑年代深逺子孙或与韦氏通婚此其意也
    　　则天后尝梦一鹦鹉羽毛甚伟两趐俱折以问宰臣羣公黙然内史狄仁杰曰鹉者陛下姓也两趐折陛下二子庐陵相王也陛下起此二子两趐全也武承嗣武三思连项皆赤后契丹围幽州檄朝廷曰还我庐陵相王来则天乃忆狄公之言曰卿曽为我占梦今乃应矣朕欲立太子何者为得仁杰曰陛下有贤子外有贤侄取舍详择断在圣衷则天曰我自有圣子承嗣三思是何疥癣承嗣等惧掩耳而走即降敕追庐陵立为太子充元帅初募兵无有应者闻太子行北印山头皆兵满无容人处贼自退散 [50]

    隋开皇年间，京兆韦衮有一奴仆名叫桃符。每次出征讨伐，桃符都表现出极大的勇气。韦衮官至左卫中郎，因桃符长期服役，便将其释放为良民。桃符家中有一头黄牛，他将其宰杀并献给韦衮。桃符请求韦衮赐予他一个姓氏，韦衮说：“你只随我姓韦即可。”桃符叩头说：“不敢与郎君同姓。”韦衮说：“你只管随我姓，这其中另有深意，因此至今仍被称为黄犊子。”韦衮即韦庶人，其后世禁止异姓者，大概是考虑到年代久远，子孙可能与韦氏通婚，这是他的用意。

    则天后曾梦见一只羽毛极其华丽的鹦鹉，但它的两翼都被折断了。她询问宰相们，群臣默然不语。内史狄仁杰说：“鹦鹉是陛下的姓氏，两翼折断意味着陛下的两位儿子——庐陵相王。陛下若能起用这两位儿子，他们的两翼便会完整。”武承嗣和武三思连脖子都红了。后来契丹围攻幽州，向朝廷发出檄文，要求归还庐陵相王。则天后想起狄仁杰的话，说：“你曾为我占卜梦境，如今应验了。朕想要立太子，谁最为合适？”狄仁杰说：“陛下有贤子，外有贤侄，取舍详加选择，断在圣心。”则天后说：“我自有圣子，承嗣和三思不过是疥癣之患。”承嗣等人害怕，掩耳而逃。则天后随即下诏追封庐陵为太子，充任元帅。起初招募士兵无人响应，但听说太子出行，北印山头皆布满士兵，无处容身，敌军自退散。 [50]

    薛季昶为荆州长史梦猫儿伏卧于堂限上头向外以问占者张猷猷曰猫儿者爪牙伏门限者阃外之事君必知军马之要未旬日除桂州都督岭南招讨使
    　　给事中陈安平子年满赴选与乡人李仙药卧夜梦十一月养仙药占曰十一月养冬丝也君必送东司数日果送吏部
    　　饶阳李瞿云勲官方满选夜梦一母猪极大李仙药占曰母猪防主也君必得屯主数日果如其言
    　　张鷟曽梦一大鸟紫色五彩成文飞下至庭前不去以告祖父云此吉祥也昔蔡衡云鳯之类有五其色赤者文章鳯也青者鸾也黄者鹓雏也白者鸿鹄也紫者鸑鷟也此鸟为鳯皇之左汝当为帝辅也遂以为名字焉鷟初举进士至懐州梦庆云覆其身其年对防考功员外骞味道以为天下第一又初为岐王属夜梦着绯乗驴睡中自怪我緑衣当乗马何为衣绯却乗驴其年应举及第授鸿胪丞未经考而授五品此其应也 [51]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [51]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 梦猫儿伏卧于堂限上头向外 - Dream of a cat lying down facing outward on the threshold of the hall.
    • 猫儿者爪牙伏门限者阃外之事 - The cat lying down on the threshold symbolizes subordinates or assistants dealing with military affairs outside the home.
    • 与乡人李仙药卧夜梦十一月养 - Dreamed while sleeping at night with a villager named Li Xianyao in November, raising something (possibly livestock).
    • 未旬日除桂州都督岭南招讨使 - Within ten days, appointed as Governor of Gui Prefecture and Commander of the Southern Lingnan Campaign.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 薛季昶 - Historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 荆州长史 - Official title, indicating a high-ranking administrative position in the state of Jingzhou.
    • 张猷 - Historical figure, likely an astrologer or fortune-teller.
    • 陈安平子 - Historical figure, likely a candidate for the imperial examination.
    • 李仙药 - Historical figure, likely a villager or acquaintance.
    • 饶阳 - Place name, likely a county or city in ancient China.
    • 李瞿云 - Historical figure, likely a candidate for the imperial examination.
    • 张鷟 - Historical figure, likely a scholar or official.
    • 蔡衡 - Historical figure, likely a scholar or philosopher.
    • 鳯 - Mythical bird, often associated with auspiciousness and nobility.
    • 鸾 - Mythical bird, often associated with auspiciousness and nobility.
    • 鹓雏 - Mythical bird, often associated with auspiciousness and nobility.
    • 鸿鹄 - Wild geese, often used metaphorically in literature.
    • 鸑鷟 - Mythical bird, often associated with auspiciousness and nobility.
    • 岐王 - King Qi, likely a historical ruler or noble.
    • 鸿胪丞 - Official title, indicating a high-ranking position in the imperial court.
    • 五品 - Rank in the imperial bureaucracy, indicating a mid-level official.

    Proper Nouns

    • 薛季昶 - Proper name of a historical figure.
    • 张猷 - Proper name of a historical figure.
    • 陈安平子 - Proper name of a historical figure.
    • 李仙药 - Proper name of a historical figure.
    • 饶阳 - Place name.
    • 李瞿云 - Proper name of a historical figure.
    • 张鷟 - Proper name of a historical figure.
    • 蔡衡 - Proper name of a historical figure.
    • 岐王 - Proper name of a historical figure.
    • 骞味道 - Proper name of a historical figure.

    薛季昶担任荆州长史时，梦见一只猫儿伏卧在堂限上，头朝外。他去问占卜者张猷，张猷说：“猫儿是爪牙，伏在门限上，这是阃外之事，您必定知道军马的重要性。”不到十天，薛季昶就被任命为桂州都督、岭南招讨使。

    给事中陈安平的儿子年满后参加科举考试，与同乡李仙药一起睡觉时梦见十一月养。李仙药解释说：“十一月养是冬丝，您必定会被送到东司。”几天后，陈安平的儿子果然被送到吏部。

    饶阳的李瞿云勋官刚刚完成科举考试，夜晚梦见一只极大的母猪。李仙药解释说：“母猪是防主，您必定会得到屯主的职位。”几天后，李瞿云果然如其所言得到了屯主的职位。

    张鷟曾经梦见一只紫色、五彩成文的大鸟飞到庭前，没有离去。他告诉祖父说：“这是吉祥的征兆。”从前蔡衡说过：“凤凰有五种，红色的是文章凤，青色的是鸾，黄色的是鹓雏，白色的是鸿鹄，紫色的是鸑鷟。”这只鸟在凤凰之左，您应当成为帝王的辅佐。于是张鷟以此为自己命名。张鷟初次参加科举考试到达怀州时，梦见祥云覆盖了他的身体。那一年，他在对防考功员外骞味道的考试中被认为是最好的。后来，他最初担任岐王的属官时，梦见自己穿着红色的衣服骑着驴子，睡梦中自问：“我应该穿着绿色的衣服骑马，为什么穿着红色的衣服却骑着驴子？”那一年，他参加科举考试及第，被授予鸿胪丞的职位，未经考试就被授予五品官职，这正是他的应验。 [51]

    河东裴元质初举进士明朝唱防夜梦一狗从窦出挽射之其箭遂撆以为不祥问曹良史曰吾往唱防之夜亦为此梦梦神为吾解之曰狗者第字头也第字身也箭者第竖也有撆为第也寻而唱第果如梦焉
    　　右丞相卢藏用中书令崔湜太平党被流岭南至荆州湜夜梦讲坐下听法而照镜问善占梦张猷谓卢右丞曰崔令公大恶梦座下听讲法从上来也镜字金傍竟也其竟于今日乎寻有御史陆遗勉赍敕令湜自尽
    　　洛州杜有牛一头甚怜之夜梦见其牛有两尾以问占者李仙药曰牛字有两尾失字也经数月果失之
    　　载初年中来俊臣罗织告故庶人贤二子夜遣巫祈祷星月呪咀不道栲楚酸痛奴婢妄证二子自诬并鞭杀之朝野伤痛浮休子张鷟曰下里庸人多信厌祷小儿妇女甚重符书蕴慝崇奸搆虚实土用血诚伊戾之故为掘地埋桐乃江充之擅造也
    　　韦庶人之全盛日好厌祷并将昏镜以照人令其速乱与崇仁坊邢俗师婆阿来专行厌魅平王诛之后往往于殿上掘得巫蛊皆逆韦之辈为之也 [52]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [52]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 初举进士 - The first time taking the imperial examination for the jinshi degree.
    • 明朝唱防 - The announcement of the exam results the next morning.
    • 从窦出 - Literally 'coming out from a hole,' but likely refers to a symbolic or metaphorical event.
    • 挽射之 - Pulling a bow and shooting an arrow at something.
    • 撆 - To hit or strike.
    • 唱第 - The announcement of the exam results.
    • 讲坐下听法 - Listening to a lecture or teaching.
    • 照镜 - To look in a mirror.
    • 座下听讲法从上来也 - Listening to the lecture from above or hearing the teaching from a higher place.
    • 镜字金傍竟也 - A play on words where '镜' (mirror) is broken down into '金' (metal) and '竟' (end), suggesting the end of something.
    • 经数月果失之 - After several months, it was indeed lost.
    • 载初年中 - Refers to the early years of a reign.
    • 来俊臣罗织告故庶人贤二子 - The act of framing or accusing someone.
    • 夜遣巫祈祷星月呪咀不道 - Sending a witch to pray to stars and the moon and curse someone.
    • 栲楚酸痛 - Severe physical punishment.
    • 自诬 - To falsely confess.
    • 鞭杀之 - To beat to death.
    • 浮休子张鷟 - Zhang Zuo, a famous writer and official.
    • 下里庸人多信厌祷 - Ordinary people believing in superstitious practices.
    • 小儿妇女甚重符书 - Children and women placing great importance on talismans and spells.
    • 蕴慝崇奸搆虚实土用血诚 - Harboring evil, promoting deceit, and using blood and sincerity to manipulate reality.
    • 伊戾之故为掘地埋桐乃江充之擅造也 - The practice of digging holes and burying trees, which was a method used by Jiang Zhong to frame people.
    • 韦庶人之全盛日 - The height of power of Empress Wei.
    • 好厌祷 - Engaging in superstitious practices.
    • 将昏镜以照人 - Using a mirror to curse someone.
    • 令其速乱 - To make them quickly fall into chaos.
    • 与崇仁坊邢俗师婆阿来专行厌魅 - A woman named A Lai who specialized in superstitious practices.
    • 平王诛之后 - After the death of King Ping.
    • 往往于殿上掘得巫蛊 - Finding evidence of witchcraft in the palace.
    • 皆逆韦之辈为之也 - All of these were done by those who opposed Empress Wei.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 河东裴元质 - Pei Yuanzhi, a historical figure from Hedong (modern-day Shanxi).
    • 右丞相卢藏用 - Lu Zangong, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 中书令崔湜 - Cui Shi, another high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 太平党 - A political faction during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 来俊臣 - Lai Junchen, a notorious official known for his cruel methods.
    • 庶人贤二子 - The sons of a commoner named Xian.
    • 韦庶人 - Empress Wei, a powerful figure during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 平王 - King Ping, a historical figure.
    • 崇仁坊 - A district in Chang'an (modern-day Xi'an).

    Proper Nouns

    • 河东裴元质 - Pei Yuanzhi from Hedong.
    • 右丞相卢藏用 - Lu Zangong, the Right Chancellor.
    • 中书令崔湜 - Cui Shi, the Grand Secretary.
    • 来俊臣 - Lai Junchen.
    • 庶人贤二子 - The sons of Xian.
    • 韦庶人 - Empress Wei.
    • 平王 - King Ping.
    • 崇仁坊 - A district in Chang'an.

    Pei Yuanzhi from Hedong first took the imperial examination for the jinshi degree. The next morning, when the results were announced, he dreamed of a dog coming out from a hole and pulling a bow to shoot at it. His arrow struck the dog, which he considered an ominous sign. He asked Cao Liangshi, a skilled dream interpreter, about the dream. Cao said, "The dog represents the character '第' (di), the head of the character. The bow represents the body of the character. The arrow represents the vertical stroke of the character. The striking of the dog means '第' (di), which is your rank." Soon after, when the results were announced, he indeed received the rank he had dreamed of.

    Right Chancellor Lu Zangong and Grand Secretary Cui Shi, who were part of the Tai Ping faction, were exiled to the south. They reached Jingzhou, where Cui had a dream of sitting and listening to a lecture while looking in a mirror. He asked Zhang You, a skilled dream interpreter, about the dream. Zhang said, "This is a very bad dream. Listening to the lecture from above and looking in the mirror suggests that your end is near. The character '镜' (jing) breaks down into '金' (jin) and '竟' (jing), which means the end. Is your end today?" Soon after, a censor named Lu Yiman brought an imperial decree ordering Cui to commit suicide.

    Du Xuan from Luo Prefecture had a cow that he deeply loved. One night, he dreamed that his cow had two tails. He asked Li Xianyao, a skilled dream interpreter, about the dream. Li said, "The character '牛' (niu) has two tails, which means '失' (shi), the character for loss." After several months, the cow was indeed lost.

    During the early years of the reign, Lai Junchen framed and accused the sons of a commoner named Xian. At night, he sent a witch to pray to the stars and the moon and curse the sons. They were subjected to severe physical punishment, and their servants falsely testified against them, leading the sons to falsely confess and be beaten to death. The people were deeply saddened. Zhang Zuo, a famous writer and official, criticized the situation, saying that ordinary people often believed in superstitious practices, and children and women placed great importance on talismans and spells. He condemned the practice of harboring evil, promoting deceit, and using blood and sincerity to manipulate reality, which was a method used by Jiang Zhong to frame people.

    During the height of Empress Wei's power, she engaged in superstitious practices and used mirrors to curse people, making them quickly fall into chaos. She also involved a woman named A Lai from Chongrenfang, who specialized in superstitious practices. After the death of King Ping, evidence of witchcraft was often found in the palace, all done by those who opposed Empress Wei. [52]

    韦庶人葬其父韦贞号酆王葬毕宫人路见师雍文智诈宣酆王教曰常作宫人甚大艰苦宜与赏着緑者与绯韦庶人悲恸欲依教与之未处分间有告文智诈受赂贿騐遂斩之
    　　中宗之时有见师彭君卿被御史所辱他日对百官总集诈宣孝和敕曰御史不检校去却巾带即去之曰有敕与一顿杖大使曰御史不奉正敕不合决杖君卿曰若不合有敕且放却御史裹头仍舞蹈拜谢而去观者骇之
    　　浮休子张鷟为德州平昌令大旱郡符下令以师婆师僧祈之二十余日无效浮休子乃推土龙倒其夜雨足江淮南好多邪俗病即祀之无医人浮休子曽于江南洪州停数日遂闻土人何婆善琵琶卜与同行人郭司法质焉其何婆士女填门饷遗满道颜色充恱心气殊髙郭再拜下钱问其品秩何婆乃调柱和声气曰个丈夫富贵今年得一品明年得二品后年得三品更后年得四品郭曰阿婆错品少者官髙品多者官小何婆曰今年减一品明年减二品后年减三品更后年减四品更得五六年总没品郭大骂而起 [53]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [53]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 葬畢 - Literally 'after burial,' used here to indicate the completion of a funeral.
    • 宮人路見 - Palace maids encountered, referring to palace servants who saw a ghost.
    • 師雍文智 - Ghost teacher Yong Wen Zhi, where '師' refers to a medium or someone who communicates with spirits.
    • 宣酆王教 - Proclaimed the orders of Prince Feng, indicating that Yong Wen Zhi claimed to be delivering messages from the deceased prince.
    • 緑者與绯 - Those wearing green should be given red, a reference to a color change in clothing, likely symbolizing a promotion or reward.
    • 悲恸欲依 - Mourned deeply and wished to follow, indicating the emotional response of the Wei Shuren (Empress Wei) to the ghost's message.
    • 处分间 - In the midst of making arrangements, referring to the time when Empress Wei was still considering the ghost's request.
    • 诈受赂贿 - Falsely accepted bribes, indicating that Yong Wen Zhi was accused of corruption.
    • 騐遂斩之 - Upon investigation, he was executed, showing the outcome of the investigation into Yong Wen Zhi's actions.
    • 中宗之时 - During the reign of Emperor Zhongzong, setting the historical context.
    • 对百官总集 - In front of all officials gathered, indicating a public assembly.
    • 诈宣孝和敕 - Falsely proclaimed the edict of Emperor Xuanhe, suggesting that Peng Junqing claimed to be delivering orders from the emperor.
    • 去却巾带 - Remove the hat and sash, a command that would have been a significant act of disrespect.
    • 一顿杖 - One beating, a form of punishment.
    • 大使曰 - The chief magistrate said, indicating a response from an official.
    • 御史不奉正敕 - The censor did not follow the proper edict, suggesting that the censor refused to carry out the false command.
    • 且放却御史裹头 - Let the censor put his hat back on, a gesture of restoration and respect.
    • 舞蹈拜谢而去 - Danced, bowed, and left, showing the dramatic and theatrical nature of Peng Junqing's actions.
    • 观者骇之 - The spectators were amazed, indicating the public reaction.
    • 浮休子 - Floating Leisure Man, a nickname or title for Zhang Yue, a scholar and official.
    • 德州平昌令 - Magistrate of Pingchang in Dezhou, indicating Zhang Yue's official position.
    • 大旱 - Severe drought, setting the environmental context.
    • 郡符下令 - The prefect issued an order, indicating a local government directive.
    • 师婆师僧 - Mediums and monks, referring to spiritual practitioners.
    • 二十余日无效 - After more than twenty days without effect, indicating the failure of the rituals.
    • 遂闻土人何婆 - He heard of a local woman named He Po, introducing a new character.
    • 善琵琶卜 - Skilled in playing the pipa and divination, indicating her abilities.
    • 填门 - Crowded the door, showing the popularity of He Po.
    • 饷遗满道 - Gifts and offerings filled the path, indicating the wealth of gifts she received.
    • 颜色充恱 - Her appearance was full of joy, describing her demeanor.
    • 心气殊髙 - Her spirit was unusually high, indicating her confidence and status.
    • 再拜下钱 - Bowed twice and placed money, indicating the ritual of divination.
    • 问其品秩 - Asked about her rank, indicating the inquiry into her social status.
    • 调柱 - Tuned the strings, a reference to adjusting the instrument.
    • 和声气 - Harmonized the sound and breath, indicating the musical and spiritual preparation.
    • 个丈夫富贵今年得一品 - This man will be wealthy and honored this year, achieving the first rank, a prediction.
    • 明年得二品 - Next year, he will achieve the second rank, another prediction.
    • 后年得三品 - The following year, he will achieve the third rank, a third prediction.
    • 更后年得四品 - Even further, he will achieve the fourth rank, a fourth prediction.
    • 阿婆错品少者官髙品多者官小 - Auntie, you're wrong. Those with fewer ranks are higher in rank, and those with more ranks are lower, a correction.
    • 今年减一品 - This year, he will lose one rank, a revised prediction.
    • 明年减二品 - Next year, he will lose two ranks, another revised prediction.
    • 后年减三品 - The following year, he will lose three ranks, a third revised prediction.
