    • 更后年减四品 - Even further, he will lose four ranks, a fourth revised prediction.
    • 更得五六年总没品 - After five or six years, he will have no rank at all, a final prediction.
    • 大骂而起 - He cursed and stood up, indicating a strong negative reaction.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 韦庶人 - Empress Wei, a historical figure who was the wife of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 酆王 - Prince Feng, a title given to a deceased prince.
    • 中宗 - Emperor Zhongzong, the ruler of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 孝和 - Emperor Xuanhe, another ruler of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 浮休子 - Floating Leisure Man, a nickname for Zhang Yue, a scholar and official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 德州平昌令 - Magistrate of Pingchang in Dezhou, indicating Zhang Yue's official position.
    • 琵琶 - Pipa, a traditional Chinese lute.
    • 卜 - Divination, a practice of predicting the future using various methods.

    Proper Nouns

    • 韦庶人 - Empress Wei
    • 酆王 - Prince Feng
    • 中宗 - Emperor Zhongzong
    • 孝和 - Emperor Xuanhe
    • 浮休子 - Floating Leisure Man
    • 张鷟 - Zhang Yue
    • 何婆 - He Po
    • 郭司法 - Deputy Judge Guo

    Empress Wei buried her father, Wei Zhen, who was posthumously known as Prince Feng. After the burial, palace maids encountered a ghost teacher named Yong Wen Zhi, who falsely proclaimed the orders of Prince Feng, saying, "Those who have been palace maids have endured great hardship; they should be rewarded by changing their green clothing to red." Empress Wei mourned deeply and wished to follow the ghost's instruction, but before she could make the arrangements, someone reported that Yong Wen Zhi had falsely accepted bribes. Upon investigation, he was executed.

    During the reign of Emperor Zhongzong, there was a ghost teacher named Peng Junqing who was humiliated by a censor. On another day, in front of all officials gathered, Peng Junqing falsely proclaimed the edict of Emperor Xuanhe, saying, "The censor did not check and remove his hat and sash; let him go." He then said, "There is an edict to give him one beating." The chief magistrate said, "The censor did not follow the proper edict; it is not appropriate to beat him." Peng Junqing replied, "If there is no proper edict, then let the censor put his hat back on and dance, bow, and leave." The spectators were amazed.

    Floating Leisure Man, Zhang Yue, was the magistrate of Pingchang in Dezhou. During a severe drought, the prefect issued an order to summon mediums and monks for rituals. After more than twenty days without effect, Zhang Yue pushed a clay dragon down, and that night it rained heavily. In Jiangnan, where ghosts were prevalent, the superstitious locals would worship them when sick, as there were no doctors. Zhang Yue once stayed in Hongzhou for several days and heard of a local woman named He Po, who was skilled in playing the pipa and divination. Deputies Judge Guo and his companions sought her out. Her door was crowded with people, and gifts and offerings filled the path. Her appearance was full of joy, and her spirit was unusually high. Guo bowed twice and placed money, asking about her rank. He Po tuned the strings and harmonized the sound and breath, saying, "This man will be wealthy and honored this year, achieving the first rank; next year, he will achieve the second rank; the following year, he will achieve the third rank; even further, he will achieve the fourth rank." Guo said, "Auntie, you're wrong. Those with fewer ranks are higher in rank, and those with more ranks are lower." He Po replied, "This year, he will lose one rank; next year, he will lose two ranks; the following year, he will lose three ranks; even further, he will lose four ranks; after five or six years, he will have no rank at all." Guo cursed and stood up. [53]

    崇仁方阿来婆弹琵琶卜朱紫填门浮休子张鷟曽往观之见一将军紫袍玉带甚伟下一疋细绫请一局卜来婆鸣柱烧香合眼而唱东告东方朔西告西方朔南告南方朔北告北方朔上告上方朔下告下方朔将军顶礼既告请甚多必望细看以决疑惑遂即随意支配
    　　咸亨中赵州祖珍俭有妖术悬水瓮于梁上以刀斫之防断而瓮不落又于空房内密闭门置一瓮水横刀其上人良久入看见俭支解五叚水瓮皆是血人去之后平复如初冬月极寒石臼水冻呪之防出卖卜于信都市日取百钱盖君平之法也后被人糺告引向市斩之颜色自若了无惧命纸笔作词精神不挠 [54]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [54]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 崇仁方阿來婆 - A proper name, likely referring to a woman known for her skills in divination using a lute.
    • 彈琵琶卜 - A form of divination using a lute, a practice that may have been common in certain regions or among certain groups.
    • 朱紫填門 - A colorful metaphor suggesting a crowded or bustling scene, possibly implying a busy or prosperous household.
    • 浮休子張鷟 - A proper name, likely referring to a scholar or writer.
    • 曽往觀之 - Indicates a past visit to witness the event.
    • 見一將軍紫袍玉帶 - A vivid description of a general dressed in purple robes and jade belts, symbolizing high rank and status.
    • 甚偉 - Emphasizes the grandeur of the scene.
    • 下一疋細綾 - Indicates a payment or offering.
    • 請一局卜 - Refers to a request for a reading.
    • 來婆鳴柱燒香 - Describes the diviner's actions.
    • 合眼而唱 - Indicates a trance-like state.
    • 東告東方朔 - A reference to the legendary figure Dongfang Shuo, a court jester and scholar.
    • 西告西方朔 - Similar to the previous phrase, but for the west.
    • 南告南方朔 - Similar to the previous phrases, but for the south.
    • 北告北方朔 - Similar to the previous phrases, but for the north.
    • 上告上方朔 - Similar to the previous phrases, but for the upper or celestial realm.
    • 下告下方朔 - Similar to the previous phrases, but for the lower or earthly realm.
    • 將軍頂禮 - Indicates a gesture of respect or reverence.
    • 既告請甚多 - Indicates many requests after the divination.
    • 必望細看 - Indicates the need for careful examination.
    • 以決疑惑 - Indicates the purpose of the divination.
    • 遂即隨意支配 - Indicates the diviner's decisions based on the readings.
    • 咸亨中 - Refers to the year 670 AD.
    • 趙州祖珍儉 - A proper name, likely a person known for his magical abilities.
    • 有妖術 - Indicates supernatural powers.
    • 懸水瓮於梁上 - Describes a specific magical act.
    • 以刀斫之 - Indicates a test of the magic.
    • 防斷而瓮不落 - Indicates the success of the magic.
    • 又於空房内密閉門 - Describes another magical act.
    • 置一瓮水橫刀其上 - Sets up a test.
    • 人良久入見 - Indicates a delayed observation.
    • 見珍儉支解五叚 - Indicates a gruesome scene.
    • 水瓮皆是血 - Indicates the result of the test.
    • 人去之後平復如初 - Indicates the temporary nature of the magical act.
    • 冬月極寒 - Indicates a specific time.
    • 石臼水凍 - Indicates a natural phenomenon.
    • 呪之防出 - Indicates a magical act.
    • 賣卜於信都市 - Indicates a profession.
    • 日取百錢 - Indicates a daily income.
    • 蓋君平之法也 - Refers to a famous diviner.
    • 後被人糾告 - Indicates a legal issue.
    • 引向市斬之 - Indicates the punishment.
    • 顏色自若 - Indicates composure.
    • 了無懼 - Indicates lack of fear.
    • 命紙筆作詞 - Indicates a final act.
    • 精神不撓 - Indicates determination.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 崇仁方阿來婆 - A proper name, possibly a diviner or fortune-teller.
    • 彈琵琶卜 - A form of divination using a lute, a practice that may have been common in certain regions or among certain groups.
    • 朱紫填門 - A colorful metaphor suggesting a busy or prosperous household.
    • 浮休子張鷟 - A proper name, likely a scholar or writer.
    • 曽往觀之 - Indicates a past visit to witness the event.
    • 見一將軍紫袍玉帶 - A vivid description of a high-ranking general.
    • 甚偉 - Emphasizes the grandeur of the scene.
    • 下一疋細綾 - Indicates a payment or offering.
    • 請一局卜 - Refers to a request for a reading.
    • 來婆鳴柱燒香 - Describes the diviner's actions.
    • 合眼而唱 - Indicates a trance-like state.
    • 東告東方朔 - A reference to the legendary figure Dongfang Shuo.
    • 西告西方朔 - Similar to the previous phrase, but for the west.
    • 南告南方朔 - Similar to the previous phrases, but for the south.
    • 北告北方朔 - Similar to the previous phrases, but for the north.
    • 上告上方朔 - Similar to the previous phrases, but for the upper or celestial realm.
    • 下告下方朔 - Similar to the previous phrases, but for the lower or earthly realm.
    • 將軍頂禮 - Indicates a gesture of respect.
    • 既告請甚多 - Indicates many requests after the divination.
    • 必望細看 - Indicates the need for careful examination.
    • 以決疑惑 - Indicates the purpose of the divination.
    • 遂即隨意支配 - Indicates the diviner's decisions based on the readings.
    • 咸亨中 - Refers to the year 670 AD.
    • 趙州祖珍儉 - A proper name, likely a person known for his magical abilities.
    • 有妖術 - Indicates supernatural powers.
    • 懸水瓮於梁上 - Describes a specific magical act.
    • 以刀斫之 - Indicates a test of the magic.
    • 防斷而瓮不落 - Indicates the success of the magic.
    • 又於空房内密閉門 - Describes another magical act.
    • 置一瓮水橫刀其上 - Sets up a test.
    • 人良久入見 - Indicates a delayed observation.
    • 見珍儉支解五叚 - Indicates a gruesome scene.
    • 水瓮皆是血 - Indicates the result of the test.
    • 人去之後平復如初 - Indicates the temporary nature of the magical act.
    • 冬月極寒 - Indicates a specific time.
    • 石臼水凍 - Indicates a natural phenomenon.
    • 呪之防出 - Indicates a magical act.
    • 賣卜於信都市 - Indicates a profession.
    • 日取百錢 - Indicates a daily income.
    • 蓋君平之法也 - Refers to a famous diviner.
    • 後被人糾告 - Indicates a legal issue.
    • 引向市斬之 - Indicates the punishment.
    • 顏色自若 - Indicates composure.
    • 了無懼 - Indicates lack of fear.
    • 命紙筆作詞 - Indicates a final act.
    • 精神不撓 - Indicates determination.

    In the city of Chongren, there was a woman named Fang Ailai, who was known for her skill in divination using a lute. Her house was often bustling with people, and the scholar Zhang Zhuo of Fuxiu once visited to observe her. He saw a general dressed in a purple robe and jade belt, a very impressive sight. The general placed a piece of fine silk and asked for a session of divination. The old woman plucked the strings of her lute, burned incense, and closed her eyes while singing. She revealed that the east was connected to Dongfang Shuo, the west to Xifang Shuo, the south to Nifang Shuo, the north to Beifang Shuo, the upper realm to Shangfang Shuo, and the lower realm to Xiafang Shuo. The general bowed respectfully. After the divination, many requests were made, and it was necessary to examine the results carefully to resolve doubts. The old woman then arranged the outcomes at will.

    During the year 670 AD, in Zhaozhou, there was a man named Zu Zhenjian who possessed magical abilities. He could hang a water jar on a beam and cut it with a knife without it breaking or falling. In an empty room, he would close the door secretly and place a jar of water with a knife on top. After a long time, someone would enter to see, and they would find Zu Zhenjian dismembered into five pieces, with the water jar filled with blood. However, everything returned to normal after the person left. In the coldest month of winter, the water in a stone mortar would freeze. Zu Zhenjian would curse it to unfreeze. He sold divination in the city of Xindu, making a hundred coins a day, using the method of Junping. Later, he was accused by someone and led to the market to be executed. Despite this, he remained calm and composed, completely without fear. Before his execution, he wrote a poem with paper and pen, his spirit unyielding. [54]

    陵空观叶道士呪刀尽力斩病人肚横桃栁于腹上桃栁断而内不伤后将双刀斫一女子应手两断血流遍地家人大哭道士取续之喷水而呪须臾平复如故
    　　河南府立徳坊及南市西坊皆有胡祅神庙每嵗商胡祈福烹猪羊琵琶鼓笛酣歌醉舞酹神之后募一胡为祅主看者施钱并与之其祅主取一横刀利同霜雪吹毛不过以刀刺腹刄出于背仍乱扰膓肚流血食顷喷水呪之平复如故此西域之幻法也
    　　凉州祅神祠至祈祷日祅主以铁钉从额上钉之直洞腋下即出门身轻若飞须臾数百里至西祅神前舞一曲即却至旧祅所乃防钉无所损卧十余日平复如故莫知其所以然也
    　　明崇俨有术法大帝试之为地窖遣妓奏乐引俨至谓曰此地常闻管是何祥也卿能止之乎俨曰诺遂书二桃符于其上钉之其声寂然上笑唤妓人问云见二龙头张口向上遂怖惧不敢奏乐也上大恱 [55]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [55]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 呪刀 - Magical knife; a knife used in rituals with spells.
    • 桃符 - Peach-wood charm; a magical object used to ward off evil spirits.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 胡祅神庙 - Temple dedicated to foreign gods; likely associated with Zoroastrianism or other foreign religions.
    • 凉州 - Ancient region of Gansu, known for its strategic importance and cultural diversity.
    • 明崇俨 - Magician or sorcerer named Ming Chongyan, known for his magical abilities.
    • 大帝 - High-ranking emperor or ruler, likely the Tang Dynasty emperor who tested Ming Chongyan's skills.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陵空观叶道士 - Taoist priest named Lingkong Guanye.
    • 明崇俨 - Magician or sorcerer named Ming Chongyan.
    • 大帝 - High-ranking emperor or ruler, likely the Tang Dynasty emperor.

    Lingkong Guanye, a Taoist priest, used a spell and a knife to cut through a patient's abdomen, severing peach and willow branches placed on the belly without causing internal damage. Later, he used two knives to cut a woman in half, causing her to split into two parts with blood flowing everywhere, and then magically reassembled her.

    In Henan Province, the Lide Lane and the West Lane of the Southern Market both have temples dedicated to foreign gods. Each year, foreign merchants worship these gods by sacrificing pigs and sheep, playing music, singing, and dancing, and then offering money to a chosen person (the "祅主"). The "祅主" takes a sharp knife, as cold as frost and snow, and cuts through their body, causing their intestines to spill out. After a short while, they sprinkle water and recite a spell, and the wounds heal instantly, just as they were before. This is a magical practice from the Western Regions.

    At the temple of the foreign god in Liangzhou, on the day of prayer, the "祅主" is nailed through the forehead with an iron nail, which passes through their armpit. They then leave the temple, flying like a bird, and travel hundreds of miles to the temple of the Western god, where they perform a dance. Afterward, they return to the old temple, and the nail causes no further harm. They lie down for over ten days and heal completely, but no one knows how this is possible.

    Ming Chongyan, a magician, was tested by the emperor, who ordered him to stop a sound in a dungeon by writing two peach-wood charms. The sound ceased immediately, and the emperor laughed and asked the musicians if they had seen two dragon heads opening their mouths upward. The musicians were terrified and dared not play their instruments. The emperor was very pleased. [55]

    罗县令刘静妻患疾正谏议大夫明崇俨诊之曰须得生龙肝食之必愈静以为不可得俨乃画符乗风放之上天须臾有龙下入瓮水中剔取食之而差大帝盛夏须雪及枇杷龙眼俨坐顷间往隂山取雪岭南取果子并到食之无别时四月未熟上思之俨索百钱将去须臾得一大云缑氏老人园内得之上追老人至问之云土埋一拟进适卖唯得百钱耳俨独坐堂中夜被刺死刀子仍在心上敕求贼甚急竟无踪绪或以为俨役劳苦被杀之孔子曰攻乎异端斯害也已信哉
    　　则天朝有鼎师者瀛州博野人有竒行大平公主进则天试之以银瓮盛酒三斗一举而饮尽又曰臣能食酱即令以银瓮盛酱一斗鼎师以匙抄之须臾即竭则天欲与官鼎曰情愿出家即与剃头后则天之复辟也鼎曰如来螺髻菩萨寳首若能修道何必剃除遂长发使张濳决一百不废行动亦无疮疾时人莫测 [56]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [56]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 患疾 - Suffers from illness.
    • 诊之 - Diagnosed.
    • 须得生龙肝食之 - Must obtain fresh dragon liver to eat.
    • 静以为不可得 - Liu Jing thought it was impossible to obtain.
    • 俨乃画符乘风放之 - Ming Chongyan drew a talisman and sent it with the wind.
    • 须臾有龙下入瓮水中 - Soon a dragon descended into the water in the jar.
    • 剔取食之而差 - The liver was extracted and eaten, and the illness was cured.
    • 大帝盛夏须雪及枇杷龙眼 - The emperor needed snow and loquat and longan fruits during the hot summer.
    • 俨坐顷间往阴山取雪岭南取果子 - Ming Chongyan sat for a moment and went to the Yin Mountains to fetch snow and to the south to fetch fruits.
    • 四月未熟上思之 - In April, the fruits were not yet ripe, but the emperor thought about them.
    • 俨索百钱将去 - Ming Chongyan asked for a hundred coins to leave.
    • 得一大果云缑氏老人园内得之 - He obtained a large fruit, saying it was from the garden of an old man in Gou City.
    • 敕求贼甚急竟无踪绪 - The imperial decree sought the thief urgently, but there was no trace of him.
    • 或以为俨役鬼劳苦被鬼杀之 - Some believed that Ming Chongyan, who had been working with spirits, was killed by a ghost due to exhaustion.
    • 攻乎异端斯害也已 - Attacking heterodox ideas is itself a harm.
    • 则天朝有鼎师者瀛州博野人 - During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, there was a Daoist named Ding Shi from Bo Ye in Ying Prefecture.
    • 有奇行 - Had extraordinary conduct.
    • 太平公主进则天试之以银瓮盛酒三斗一举而饮尽 - Princess Taiping introduced him to Empress Wu Zetian, who tested him by having him drink three dou of wine from a silver jar in one go.
    • 臣能食酱即令以银瓮盛酱一斗鼎师以匙抄之须臾即竭 - When asked if he could eat soy sauce, he agreed and finished a dou of soy sauce from a silver jar with a spoon in a short time.
    • 则天欲与官鼎曰情愿出家即与剃头 - Empress Wu Zetian wanted to appoint him to an official position, but Ding Shi preferred to become a monk and asked to be shaved.
    • 后则天之复辟也鼎曰如来螺髻菩萨宝首若能修道何必剃除遂长发 - After Empress Wu Zetian regained power, Ding Shi said that if one could practice the Way, why bother with shaving? He grew his hair long.
    • 使张濳决一百不废行动亦无疮疾时人莫测 - He was able to withstand one hundred lashes from a whip without any injury, and people could not understand how he did it.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 罗县令刘静妻 - The wife of Liu Jing, the magistrate of Luo County.
    • 正谏议大夫明崇俨 - Ming Chongyan, a high-ranking official known for his abilities in medicine and exorcism.
    • 银瓮 - A silver jar, often used for ceremonial or medicinal purposes.
    • 生龙肝 - Fresh dragon liver, a mythical ingredient believed to have curative properties.
    • 阴山 - The Yin Mountains, a range in northern China.
    • 岭南 - The southern regions of China, known for tropical fruits.
    • 大帝 - Refers to the emperor.
    • 四月未熟 - In April, the fruits were not yet ripe.
    • 百钱 - A hundred coins, a small amount of money.
    • 缑氏老人园内 - The garden of an old man in Gou City.
    • 敕求贼 - The imperial decree seeking the thief.
    • 役鬼 - Working with spirits.
    • 则天朝 - The reign of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 鼎师 - A Daoist practitioner named Ding Shi.
    • 瀛州博野 - Bo Ye in Ying Prefecture.
    • 太平公主 - Princess Taiping, a powerful figure during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 如来螺髻菩萨宝首 - The Buddha's coiled hair, a symbol of enlightenment.
    • 张濳决一百 - One hundred lashes from a whip.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The phrase '生龙肝' is ambiguous. It could mean 'fresh dragon liver' or 'live dragon liver.'
    • The phrase '乘风放之' is ambiguous. It could mean 'sent with the wind' or 'released with the wind.'
    • The phrase '须臾有龙下入瓮水中' is ambiguous. It could mean 'soon a dragon descended into the water in the jar' or 'soon a dragon entered the jar containing water.'
    • The phrase '剔取食之而差' is ambiguous. It could mean 'the liver was extracted and eaten, and the illness was cured' or 'the liver was taken out and eaten, and the illness was cured.'
    • The phrase '大帝盛夏须雪及枇杷龙眼' is ambiguous. It could mean 'the emperor needed snow and loquat and longan fruits during the hot summer' or 'the emperor needed snow and loquat and longan fruits in the summer.'
    • The phrase '俨坐顷间往阴山取雪岭南取果子' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Ming Chongyan sat for a moment and went to the Yin Mountains to fetch snow and to the south to fetch fruits' or 'Ming Chongyan sat for a moment and went to the Yin Mountains to fetch snow and to the south to fetch fruits.'
    • The phrase '四月未熟上思之' is ambiguous. It could mean 'In April, the fruits were not yet ripe, but the emperor thought about them' or 'In April, the fruits were not yet ripe, and the emperor thought about them.'
    • The phrase '俨索百钱将去' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Ming Chongyan asked for a hundred coins to leave' or 'Ming Chongyan asked for a hundred coins and left.'
    • The phrase '得一大果云缑氏老人园内得之' is ambiguous. It could mean 'He obtained a large fruit, saying it was from the garden of an old man in Gou City' or 'He obtained a large fruit, saying it came from the garden of an old man in Gou City.'
    • The phrase '敕求贼甚急竟无踪绪' is ambiguous. It could mean 'The imperial decree sought the thief urgently, but there was no trace of him' or 'The imperial decree sought the thief urgently, and there was no trace of him.'
    • The phrase '或以为俨役鬼劳苦被鬼杀之' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Some believed that Ming Chongyan, who had been working with spirits, was killed by a ghost due to exhaustion' or 'Some believed that Ming Chongyan, who had been working with spirits, was killed by a ghost because of exhaustion.'
    • The phrase '攻乎异端斯害也已' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Attacking heterodox ideas is itself a harm' or 'Attacking heterodox ideas is itself a danger.'

    Proper Nouns

    • 罗县令刘静 - The magistrate of Luo County, Liu Jing.
    • 正谏议大夫明崇俨 - Ming Chongyan, a high-ranking official.
    • 大帝 - The emperor.
    • 则天朝 - The reign of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 鼎师 - A Daoist practitioner named Ding Shi.
    • 太平公主 - Princess Taiping.
    • 如来螺髻菩萨宝首 - The Buddha's coiled hair.
    • 张濳 - A whip used for punishment.

    The wife of Liu Jing, the magistrate of Luo County, fell ill, and the official Ming Chongyan, who was known for his medical skills, diagnosed her and said that she would recover if she ate fresh dragon liver. Liu Jing thought it was impossible to obtain. Ming Chongyan then drew a talisman and sent it with the wind into the sky. Soon a dragon descended into the water in a jar. The liver was extracted and eaten, and the illness was cured.

    During the hot summer, the emperor needed snow and loquat and longan fruits. Ming Chongyan sat for a moment and went to the Yin Mountains to fetch snow and to the south to fetch fruits. They arrived and were eaten, and there was no difference. In April, the fruits were not yet ripe, but the emperor thought about them. Ming Chongyan asked for a hundred coins to leave. Soon he obtained a large fruit, saying it was from the garden of an old man in Gou City. The emperor went to the garden and caught up with the old man. He asked him, "How did you get this fruit?" The old man replied, "I buried a fruit in the ground, intending to present it, but I just sold it for a hundred coins." Ming Chongyan was sitting alone in the hall at night when he was stabbed to death. The knife was still in his heart. The imperial decree sought the thief urgently, but there was no trace of him. Some believed that Ming Chongyan, who had been working with spirits, was killed by a ghost due to exhaustion. Confucius said, "Attacking heterodox ideas is itself a harm." Indeed.

    During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, there was a Daoist named Ding Shi from Bo Ye in Ying Prefecture. He had extraordinary conduct. Princess Taiping introduced him to Empress Wu Zetian, who tested him by having him drink three dou of wine from a silver jar in one go. When asked if he could eat soy sauce, he agreed and finished a dou of soy sauce from a silver jar with a spoon in a short time. Empress Wu Zetian wanted to appoint him to an official position, but Ding Shi preferred to become a monk and asked to be shaved. After Empress Wu Zetian regained power, Ding Shi said that if one could practice the Way, why bother with shaving? He grew his hair long. He was able to withstand one hundred lashes from a whip without any injury, and people could not understand how he did it. [56]

    大足中李慈德有祅妄人李慈徳自云能行符书厌则天于内安置布豆成兵马画地为江河与给使相知削竹为枪纒被为甲三更于内反宫人扰乱相杀者十二三羽林将军杨基闻内里声呌领兵斩闗而入杀慈徳阉竖数十人惜哉慈德以厌为客以厌而丧
    　　孝和帝令内道埸僧与道士各述所能久而不决都观叶法善取胡桃二升并殻食之并尽僧仍不伏法善烧一铁鉢赫赤两手欲合老僧头上僧唱贼袈裟掩头而走孝和抚掌大笑
    　　道士罗公逺幼时不慧入梁山数年忽有异见言事皆中敕追入京先天中皇太子设斋逺从太子乞金银器物太子靳固不与逺曰少时自取太子自封署房门须臾开视器物一无所见东房先封闭往视之器物并在其中又借太子所乗马太子怒不与逺曰已取得来见于后园中放在太子急往枥上检看马在如故侍御史守一将食器数枚就罗公逺看年命奴擎衣襆在门外不觉须臾在逺公衣箱中诸人大惊莫知其然 [57]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [57]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 祅妄人 - Refers to a person who practices or claims to practice witchcraft or sorcery. '祅' (yāo) is often associated with evil or harmful practices, while '妄' (wàng) implies false or reckless behavior.
    • 行符书 - Means 'to perform talismans or spells,' referring to the act of writing magical characters or incantations.
    • 厌则天 - Literally 'to suppress or restrain Heaven,' but in this context, it likely means to attempt to control or influence the will of Heaven or the emperor.
    • 画地为江河 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to draw rivers on the ground,' symbolizing the creation of an imaginary or magical boundary.
    • 削竹为枪 - Means 'to carve bamboo into spears,' describing the act of making weapons.
    • 缠被为甲 - Means 'to wrap oneself in armor,' symbolizing the preparation for battle or defense.
    • 反宫人扰乱 - Means 'to cause chaos among the palace attendants,' indicating a disturbance or rebellion.
    • 羽林将军 - Refers to a high-ranking military official responsible for guarding the palace.
    • 阉竖数十人 - Refers to a group of eunuchs, likely palace guards or attendants.
    • 惜哉 - An expression of pity or regret.
    • 内道埸僧 - Refers to Buddhist monks within the palace or imperial court.
    • 道士 - Refers to Taoist priests or practitioners.
    • 述所能 - Means 'to describe their abilities' or 'to demonstrate their powers.'
    • 胡桃二升并壳食之 - Means 'to eat two liters of walnuts along with their shells,' a seemingly impossible feat that demonstrates the道士's supernatural abilities.
    • 赫赤两手 - Means 'to have hands glowing red,' describing a magical or supernatural transformation.
    • 老僧头上 - Refers to an elderly monk, likely a key figure in the story.
    • 唱贼袈裟掩头而走 - Means 'to shout and flee while covering his head with a monk's robe,' indicating a panicked reaction.
    • 抚掌大笑 - Means 'to clap hands and laugh,' showing approval or amusement.
    • 少时自取 - Means 'to take it later,' referring to the太子's promise to give the道士 the requested items.
    • 东房先封闭 - Means 'the eastern room was previously sealed,' indicating a hidden or secret location.
    • 借太子所乘马 - Means 'to borrow the太子's horse,' a request that was denied.
    • 年命奴擎衣袱 - Refers to a servant carrying the道士's clothes or belongings.
    • 侍御史将食器数枚 - Refers to a court official carrying several dishes.
    • 博学求之 - Means 'to seek knowledge broadly,' indicating a desire for learning from various sources.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 孝和帝 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (Li Longji), a prominent ruler of the Tang Dynasty known for his patronage of Buddhism and Taoism.
    • 内道埸 - Refers to a Buddhist temple or monastery within the imperial palace, indicating the presence of religious institutions in the imperial court.
    • 道士罗公远 - Refers to Luo Gongyuan, a legendary Taoist priest known for his supernatural abilities and close association with Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 太子 - Refers to the crown prince, the heir to the throne, who is a key figure in the story.
    • 羽林将军杨玄基 - Refers to Yang Xuanji, a high-ranking military official who intervenes in the palace disturbance.
    • 叶法善 - Refers to Ye Fashan, a famous Taoist priest who served as an advisor to Emperor Xuanzong and was known for his magical abilities.
    • 侍御史 - Refers to a high-ranking official responsible for overseeing the imperial court and ensuring proper conduct.

    Proper Nouns

    • 大足 - A year in the Tang Dynasty, 701 CE.
    • 李慈德 - The name of the妖妄人 who attempts to cause chaos in the palace.
    • 孝和帝 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (Li Longji).
    • 羽林将军杨玄基 - Yang Xuanji, a high-ranking military official.
    • 内道埸僧 - Buddhist monks within the imperial court.
    • 道士罗公远 - Luo Gongyuan, a legendary Taoist priest.
    • 太子 - The crown prince, heir to the throne.
    • 叶法善 - Ye Fashan, a famous Taoist priest.
    • 侍御史 - A high-ranking official responsible for overseeing the imperial court.

    During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong (701-710 CE), there was a man named Li Cide who claimed to be an expert in performing talismans and spells. He said he could suppress the will of Heaven and, within the palace, arrange beans to form an army and draw rivers on the ground. He also carved bamboo into spears and wrapped himself in armor. At the third watch of the night, he caused chaos among the palace attendants, leading to the deaths of several dozen of them. The General of the Feathered Forest, Yang Xuanji, heard the commotion inside the palace and led his troops to break in, killing Li Cide and several dozen eunuchs. Alas, Li Cide met his end through his own sorcery.

    Emperor Xuanzong then ordered the Buddhist monks and Taoist priests within the palace to demonstrate their abilities, but the contest dragged on without a clear resolution. Master Ye Fashan of the Xuan Dao Temple took two liters of walnuts, including their shells, and ate them all. Despite this, the monks still did not concede. Master Ye then burned an iron basin, causing his hands to glow red, as if he were about to strike the head of an elderly monk. The monk cried out, covered his head with his robe, and fled. Emperor Xuanzong clapped his hands and laughed heartily.

    The Taoist priest Luo Gongyuan, when he was young, was not particularly bright. He spent several years in Liang Mountain, where he suddenly gained extraordinary insight and foresight. He accurately predicted many events, and the emperor summoned him to the capital. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, the crown prince held a ritual feast. Luo Gongyuan asked the prince for some silver and gold vessels, but the prince refused, insisting that he would give them to him later. Luo then said he would take them himself in a short while. The prince sealed the door of his room, but shortly after, when he opened it, he found no trace of the vessels. However, in the previously sealed eastern room, the vessels were found inside. Luo then asked to borrow the prince’s horse, but the prince became angry and refused. Luo said he had already taken it and would return it later. The prince rushed to the stables to check, and the horse was still there as if nothing had happened. A court official named Wei Shouyi, carrying several dishes, approached Luo Gongyuan to see what was happening. Meanwhile, a servant carrying Luo’s clothes and belongings was outside, but soon after, the clothes were found in Luo’s chest. Everyone was greatly surprised and could not understand how this had happened. [57]

    欧阳通询之子善书瘦怯于父常自矜能书必以牙犀角为笔管狸毛为心覆以秋兎毫松烟为墨末以麝香纸必须坚薄白滑者乃书之盖自重其书薛纯陀亦效欧阳草伤于肥钝亦通之亚也
    　　孟知俭并州人少时病忽亡见衙府如平生时不知其死逢故人为吏谓曰因何得来具报之乃知是防途吏为检寻曰君平生无修福处何以得还俭曰一生诵多心经及髙王经虽不记数亦三四万遍重检获之遂还吏问欲知官乎曰甚要遂以簿示之云孟知俭合运出身为曹州参军转邓州司佥即掩却不许看遂至荒榛入一黑坑遂活不知运是何事寻有敕募运粮因放选授曹州参军乃悟曰此州吾不见小书耳满授邓州司佥去任又选唱晋州判司未过而卒
    　　贞观年中顿丘县有一贤者于黄河渚上拾菜得一树栽子大如指持归莳之三年乃结子五颗味状如柰又似林檎多汁异常酸美送县县上州以其味竒乃进之赐绫一十疋后树长成渐至三百颗每年进之号曰朱柰至今存德贝博等州取其枝接所在丰足人以为从西域来碍渚而往矣 [58]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [58]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 善書瘦怯于父 - Literally means 'good at writing but more slender and timid than his father.' This phrase describes the son's writing style and demeanor compared to his father's.
    • 必以牙犀角为笔管 - Means 'he must use ivory and rhinoceros horn as pen tubes,' indicating the high-quality materials he uses for writing.
    • 狸毛为心覆以秋兎毫 - Means 'use cat hair as the core and cover it with autumn rabbit hair,' describing the composition of the brush he uses.
    • 松烟为墨末以麝香 - Means 'use pine soot as ink and add musk,' indicating the quality and fragrance of the ink he uses.
    • 纸必须坚薄白滑者乃书之 - Means 'the paper must be firm, thin, white, and smooth before he writes,' emphasizing the quality of the paper he prefers.
    • 盖自重其书 - Means 'this shows his high regard for his own writing,' indicating his pride in his work.
    • 薛纯陀亦效欧阳草伤于肥钝亦通之亚也 - Means 'Xue Chuntuo also imitated Ouyang's cursive script, but it was too heavy and blunt, making it inferior to Ouyang's,' comparing the styles of the two writers.
    • 少时病忽亡见衙府如平生时不知其死 - Means 'he fell ill and suddenly died, seeing the official palace as he had in life, unaware of his death,' describing a ghostly experience.
    • 因何得来具报之乃知是防途吏为检寻 - Means 'why did you come here? Report it, and you will know that a boundary official is checking,' explaining the context of the ghost's appearance.
    • 君平生无修福处何以得还 - Means 'you have not done any good deeds in life, so how did you return?' questioning the ghost's ability to return.
    • 俭曰一生诵多心经及高王经虽不记数亦三四万遍重检获之遂还 - Means 'Jian said, 'I have recited the Heart Sutra and the High King Sutra many times, though I can't count them, perhaps thirty to forty thousand times. They were rechecked, and he returned,' explaining the reason for his return.
    • 吏问欲知官乎曰甚要遂以簿示之云孟知俭合运出身为曹州参军转邓州司佥即掩却不许看遂至荒榛入一黑坑遂活不知运是何事 - Means 'the official asked if he wanted to know his position, saying it was very important, and showed him a register, saying 'Meng Zjian is destined to be a magistrate in Caozhou, then transfer to a clerk in Dengzhou. He immediately covered it and did not allow him to see it, so he went to a desolate thicket and entered a black pit, and then he lived. He did not know what his destiny was,' describing the ghost's fate.
    • 寻有敕募运粮因放选授曹州参军乃悟曰此州吾不见小书耳 - Means 'soon there was an imperial decree to recruit for transporting grain, and he was appointed as a magistrate in Caozhou. He realized, 'This state I did not see a small book,' meaning he recognized the state mentioned in the register,' indicating his realization.
    • 满授邓州司佥去任又选唱晋州判司未过而卒 - Means 'he was fully appointed as a clerk in Dengzhou, and after leaving his post, he was selected and appointed as a judge in Jinzhou, but he died before he could take up the position,' describing his final career move.
    • 贞观年中顿丘县有一贤者于黄河渚上拾菜得一树栽子大如指持归莳之三年乃结子五颗味状如柰又似林檎多汁异常酸美送县县上州以其味奇乃进之赐绫一十疋后树长成渐至三百颗每年进之号曰朱柰至今存德贝博等州取其枝接所在丰足人以为从西域来碍渚而往矣 - Means 'During the reign of Emperor Taizong, in Duntou County, there was a virtuous person who picked a tree seed as large as a finger while gathering vegetables on the bank of the Yellow River. He took it home and planted it. After three years, it bore five fruits, which tasted like pears and apples, with a lot of juice and an exceptionally sour and delicious flavor. He sent them to the county, which then sent them to the state. The state, impressed by the flavor, presented them to the emperor, who rewarded him with ten bolts of silk. Later, the tree grew and bore up to three hundred fruits each year, and they were presented to the emperor annually, known as Zhu Nai. Today, the trees still exist in Debei and other states, where people graft their branches, making the fruit abundant. People believe the tree came from the Western Regions and was brought across the river,' describing the story of the tree and its fruit.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 欧阳通 - Refers to Ouyang Tong, a famous calligrapher of the Tang Dynasty, known for his cursive script.
    • 薛纯陀 - Refers to Xue Chuntuo, another calligrapher who imitated Ouyang Tong's style.
    • 孟知俭 - Refers to Meng Zjian, a person who had a ghostly experience and was later appointed to a government position.
    • 贞观 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Taizong of Tang, a period known for its cultural and political achievements.
    • 顿丘县 - Refers to Duntou County, a place in Henan Province during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 黄河 - Refers to the Yellow River, one of the major rivers in China.
    • 朱柰 - Refers to a type of fruit known for its unique flavor and appearance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 欧阳通 - Ouyang Tong, a famous calligrapher of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 薛纯陀 - Xue Chuntuo, another calligrapher who imitated Ouyang Tong's style.
    • 孟知俭 - Meng Zjian, a person who had a ghostly experience and was later appointed to a government position.
    • 贞观 - The reign of Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 顿丘县 - Duntou County, a place in Henan Province during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 黄河 - The Yellow River, one of the major rivers in China.
    • 朱柰 - A type of fruit known for its unique flavor and appearance.

    Ouyang Tong's son was skilled in calligraphy, but he was more slender and timid than his father. He often boasted of his ability to write, insisting on using ivory and rhinoceros horn for pen tubes, cat hair as the core, and autumn rabbit hair as the outer layer. He used pine soot as ink, adding musk, and required the paper to be firm, thin, white, and smooth before writing. This shows his high regard for his own writing. Xue Chuntuo also imitated Ouyang's cursive script, but it was too heavy and blunt, making it inferior to Ouyang's.

    Meng Zjian was from Pingzhou. When he was young, he fell ill and suddenly died, seeing the official palace as he had in life, unaware of his death. He met an old friend who was an official and asked, "Why did you come here?" Meng reported his experience, and the official realized that a boundary official was checking. The official asked, "You have not done any good deeds in life, so how did you return?" Meng replied, "I have recited the Heart Sutra and the High King Sutra many times, though I can't count them, perhaps thirty to forty thousand times. They were rechecked, and he returned." The official then asked if he wanted to know his position, saying it was very important, and showed him a register, saying, "Meng Zjian is destined to be a magistrate in Caozhou, then transfer to a clerk in Dengzhou." He immediately covered it and did not allow him to see it, so he went to a desolate thicket and entered a black pit, and then he lived. He did not know what his destiny was. Soon there was an imperial decree to recruit for transporting grain, and he was appointed as a magistrate in Caozhou. He realized, "This state I did not see a small book," meaning he recognized the state mentioned in the register. He was fully appointed as a clerk in Dengzhou, and after leaving his post, he was selected and appointed as a judge in Jinzhou, but he died before he could take up the position.

    During the reign of Emperor Taizong, in Duntou County, there was a virtuous person who picked a tree seed as large as a finger while gathering vegetables on the bank of the Yellow River. He took it home and planted it. After three years, it bore five fruits, which tasted like pears and apples, with a lot of juice and an exceptionally sour and delicious flavor. He sent them to the county, which then sent them to the state. The state, impressed by the flavor, presented them to the emperor, who rewarded him with ten bolts of silk. Later, the tree grew and bore up to three hundred fruits each year, and they were presented to the emperor annually, known as Zhu Nai. Today, the trees still exist in Debei and other states, where people graft their branches, making the fruit abundant. People believe the tree came from the Western Regions and was brought across the river. [58]

    西晋末有旌阳县令许逊者得道于豫章西山江中有蛟为患旌阳没水劔斩之后不知所在顷渔人网得一石甚鸣击之声闻数十里唐朝赵王为洪州刺史破之得劔一双视其铭一有许旌阳字一有万仭字遂有万仭师出焉
    　　上元年中令九品已上佩刀砺等袋彩帨为鱼形结帛作之取鱼之象强之兆也至天后朝乃絶景云之后又复前结白鱼为饼
    　　中宗令杨州造方丈镜铸铜为桂树金花银叶帝每骑马自照人马并在镜中专知官髙邮县令防临也
    　　睿宗先天二年正月十五十六夜于京师安福门外作灯轮髙二十丈衣以锦绮饰以金玉燃五万盏灯簇之如花树宫女千数衣罗绮曳锦绣耀珠翠施香粉一花冠一巾帔皆万钱装束一妓女皆至三百贯妙简长安万年少女妇千余人衣服花钗媚子亦称是于灯轮下踏歌三日夜欢乐之极未始有之 [59]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [59]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 得道 - Attained enlightenment or became a sage, referring to spiritual achievement.
    • 没水劔斩 - Sank underwater and cut with a sword, describing a dramatic action.
    • 不知所在 - Unknown whereabouts, indicating disappearance.
    • 网得一石甚鸣 - Caught a stone that made a loud sound, referring to a mysterious object.
    • 取鱼之象 - Taking the form of a fish, referring to symbolic representation.
    • 强之兆也 - A sign of strength, indicating auspiciousness.
    • 破之得劔一双 - Broke it open and found a pair of swords, describing a discovery.
    • 视其铭 - Look at its inscription, referring to examining the markings.
    • 遂有万仭师出焉 - Then there emerged a teacher named Wan Zhen, indicating the origin of a teacher.
    • 令九品已上佩刀砺等袋彩帨为鱼形结帛作之 - Ordered officials of the ninth rank and above to wear a bag shaped like a fish made of silk and tied with a ribbon, describing a specific dress code.
    • 景云之后又复前结白鱼为饼 - After the Jingyun era, they resumed the practice of tying a white fish-shaped ribbon, indicating a revival of tradition.
    • 中宗令扬州造方丈镜 - Emperor Zhongzong ordered the creation of a large mirror in Yangzhou, describing a specific imperial order.
    • 铸铜为桂树金花银叶 - Cast copper into a tree with golden flowers and silver leaves, describing the mirror's design.
    • 帝每骑马自照 - The emperor would ride his horse and look at himself in the mirror, describing a personal habit.
    • 专知官高邮县令防临也 - The official responsible was the magistrate of Gaoyou County, indicating the person in charge.
    • 睿宗先天二年正月十五十六夜 - In the second year of the Tianxian era of Emperor Ruizong, on the 15th and 16th nights of the first month, providing a specific historical context.
    • 于京师安福门外作灯轮 - In the city of Chang'an, outside the Anfu Gate, a lantern wheel was made, describing a specific location and event.
    • 高二十丈 - Twenty zhang high, indicating the size of the lantern wheel.
    • 衣以锦绮饰以金玉 - Covered with brocade and decorated with gold and jade, describing the decoration.
    • 燃五万盏灯 - Lit with fifty thousand lamps, indicating the scale of the event.
    • 簇之如花树 - Clustered together like a flower tree, describing the appearance.
    • 宫女千数 - Thousands of palace women, indicating the number of participants.
    • 衣罗绮曳锦绣耀珠翠施香粉 - Dressed in silk and brocade, adorned with pearls and jade, and powdered with fragrant powder, describing the attire.
    • 一花冠一巾帔皆万钱装束 - Each headdress and veil cost ten thousand coins, indicating the cost.
    • 一妓女皆至三百贯 - Each courtesan cost three hundred guan, indicating the cost.
    • 妙简长安万年少女妇千余人 - Carefully selected more than a thousand young women from Chang'an and Wanyuan, indicating the selection process.
    • 衣服花钗媚子亦称是 - Their clothing, hairpins, and accessories were equally fine, describing the quality of the attire.
    • 于灯轮下踏歌三日夜 - They danced and sang under the lantern wheel for three days and nights, describing the event.
    • 欢乐之极未始有之 - The joy was unprecedented, indicating the level of enjoyment.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 西晋末 - Refers to the end of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 豫章西山 - Refers to the western mountains of Yuzhang (modern-day Nanchang, Jiangxi).
    • 许逊 - A historical figure known for his spiritual achievements.
    • 旌阳县令 - Refers to the position of magistrate in Jingyang County.
    • 唐朝 - Refers to the Tang dynasty.
    • 赵王 - Refers to the Prince of Zhao.
    • 洪州 - Refers to the state of Hongzhou (modern-day Nanchang, Jiangxi).
    • 上元年中 - Refers to the middle of the Shangyuan era.
    • 九品已上 - Refers to officials of the ninth rank and above.
    • 景云 - Refers to the Jingyun era of Emperor Ruizong.
    • 中宗 - Refers to Emperor Zhongzong.
    • 扬州 - Refers to the state of Yangzhou.
    • 睿宗 - Refers to Emperor Ruizong.
    • 先天二年 - Refers to the second year of the Tianxian era.
    • 京师 - Refers to the capital city of Chang'an.
    • 安福门 - Refers to the Anfu Gate in Chang'an.
    • 长安 - Refers to the city of Chang'an.
    • 万年 - Refers to the county of Wanyuan.
    • 踏歌 - Refers to a form of dance and song.
    • 贯 - Refers to a unit of currency.

    Proper Nouns

    • 西晋 - The Western Jin dynasty.
    • 豫章 - The state of Yuzhang (modern-day Nanchang, Jiangxi).
    • 许逊 - A historical figure.
    • 旌阳 - The county of Jingyang.
    • 唐朝 - The Tang dynasty.
    • 赵王 - The Prince of Zhao.
    • 洪州 - The state of Hongzhou (modern-day Nanchang, Jiangxi).
    • 上元 - The Shangyuan era.
    • 景云 - The Jingyun era.
    • 中宗 - Emperor Zhongzong.
    • 扬州 - The state of Yangzhou.
    • 睿宗 - Emperor Ruizong.
    • 先天 - The Tianxian era.
    • 京师 - The capital city of Chang'an.
    • 安福门 - The Anfu Gate in Chang'an.
    • 长安 - The city of Chang'an.
    • 万年 - The county of Wanyuan.
    • 踏歌 - A form of dance and song.
    • 贯 - A unit of currency.

    At the end of the Western Jin dynasty, there was a magistrate named Xu Xun in Jingyang County who attained enlightenment in the western mountains of Yuzhang. There was a dragon in the river that caused trouble, and Xu Xun submerged underwater and cut it with a sword. Afterward, his whereabouts were unknown. Soon, a fisherman caught a stone that made a loud sound, and the sound could be heard for dozens of li. In the Tang dynasty, the Prince of Zhao, as the governor of Hongzhou, broke the stone open and found a pair of swords. Upon examining their inscriptions, one had the words "Xu Jingyang," and the other had the words "Wan Zhen." Thus, there emerged a teacher named Wan Zhen.

    In the middle of the Shangyuan era, officials of the ninth rank and above were ordered to wear a bag shaped like a fish made of silk and tied with a ribbon. This was taken as a symbol of strength, a sign of good fortune. This practice ceased during the reign of Empress Wu but was revived after the Jingyun era, when they resumed the practice of tying a white fish-shaped ribbon.

    Emperor Zhongzong ordered the creation of a large mirror in Yangzhou, casting copper into a tree with golden flowers and silver leaves. The emperor would ride his horse and look at himself in the mirror, and both he and his horse would appear in the mirror. The official responsible for this was the magistrate of Gaoyou County.

    In the second year of the Tianxian era of Emperor Ruizong, on the 15th and 16th nights of the first month, in the city of Chang'an, outside the Anfu Gate, a lantern wheel was made, twenty zhang high, covered with brocade and decorated with gold and jade, and lit with fifty thousand lamps, clustered together like a flower tree. Thousands of palace women, dressed in silk and brocade, adorned with pearls and jade, and powdered with fragrant powder, danced and sang under the lantern wheel for three days and nights. The joy was unprecedented. [59]

    张易之为母阿臧造七寳帐金银珠玉寳贝之类罔不毕萃旷古以来未曽闻见铺象牙牀织犀角簟貂之褥蛩蟁之氊汾晋之龙须河中之鳯翮以为席凤阁侍郎李逈秀通逼之也同饮以鸳盏一双取其常相逐逈秀畏其盛嫌其老乃荒饮无度昏醉是常频唤不觉出为恒州刺史易之败阿臧入官逈秀被坐降为衡州刺史
    　　宗楚客造一宅新成皆是文栢为梁沈香和红粉以泥壁开门则香气蓬勃磨文石为阶砌及地着告莫鞾者行则仰仆楚客被建昌王推得赃万余贯兄弟配流太平公主就其宅看叹曰看他行坐处我等虚生浪死一年追入为凤阁侍郎景龙中为中书令韦氏之败斩之
    　　洛州昭成佛寺有安乐公主造百寳香炉髙三尺开四门绛桥勾栏花草飞禽走兽诸天妓乐麒麟鸾鳯白鹤飞仙丝来线去出神入居钑镂窈窕便娟真珠玛瑙瑠璃琥珀玻瓈珊瑚璖琬琰一切寳贝用钱三万府库之物尽于是矣 [60]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [60]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 七寳帳 - A luxurious tent adorned with seven treasures, symbolizing wealth and grandeur.
    • 銀金珠玉寳貝 - Refers to precious metals and gems, indicating the opulence of the items.
    • 鋪象牙牀 - A bed made of ivory, symbolizing luxury and refinement.
    • 織犀角簟 - A mat woven from rhinoceros horn, indicating high quality and craftsmanship.
    • 貂之褥蛩蟁之氊 - A fur rug and a felt mat, both luxurious materials.
    • 汾晋之龙须河中之鳯翮 - Dragon whiskers from Fen and Jin, and phoenix feathers from the middle river, symbolic of rare and valuable materials.
    • 同飲以鸳盏一双 - Drinking from a pair of鸳 (yuān) cups, a symbol of love and intimacy.
    • 荒飲無度 - Drinking excessively and without restraint, indicating a lack of moderation.
    • 頻喚不覺 - Being unable to respond even when called frequently, suggesting a state of deep intoxication.
    • 出為恒州刺史 - Being appointed as the governor of Hengzhou, a high-ranking official position.
    • 阿臧入官 - Having Ao Zang (张易之的母亲) taken into custody by the government.
    • 被坐降為衡州刺史 - Being demoted to the position of governor of Hengzhou due to his involvement in corruption.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 張易之 - Zhang Yizhi, a prominent figure during the Tang Dynasty, known for his close relationship with Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 阿臧 - The mother of Zhang Yizhi, who was also involved in court affairs.
    • 李逈秀 - Li Jiaoxiu, a high-ranking official who was forced to drink excessively and ultimately demoted.
    • 宗楚客 - Zong Chuke, another high-ranking official known for his extravagant lifestyle.
    • 建昌王 - Jianchang Prince, a prince who exposed Zong Chuke's corruption.
    • 太平公主 - Princess Taiping, a powerful figure during the Tang Dynasty, known for her influence over the court.
    • 鳳閣侍郎 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, equivalent to a minister.
    • 中書令 - A high-ranking official in charge of the imperial secretariat.
    • 韋氏之敗 - The downfall of the Wei clan, a powerful family during the Tang Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 張易之 - Zhang Yizhi
    • 阿臧 - Ao Zang
    • 李逈秀 - Li Jiaoxiu
    • 宗楚客 - Zong Chuke
    • 建昌王 - Jianchang Prince
    • 太平公主 - Princess Taiping
    • 鳳閣侍郎 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty
    • 中書令 - A high-ranking official in charge of the imperial secretariat
    • 韋氏 - The Wei clan
    • 洛州昭成佛寺 - The Zhaosheng Temple in Luoyang
    • 安樂公主 - Princess Anle

    Zhang Yizhi built a seven-treasure tent for his mother Ao Zang, adorned with silver, gold, pearls, jade, and other precious items, all of which were unparalleled in history. The tent featured an ivory bed, a mat woven from rhinoceros horn, a fur rug, a felt mat, dragon whiskers from Fen and Jin, and phoenix feathers from the middle river, all used as cushions. The Vice Minister of the Secretariat, Li Jiaoxiu, was pressured by Zhang Yizhi to drink together from a pair of鸳 cups, symbolizing their close relationship. However, Li Jiaoxiu, fearing Zhang Yizhi's power and disliking his age, drank excessively and became frequently drunk. He was often unresponsive even when called, and was eventually appointed as the governor of Hengzhou. After Zhang Yizhi's downfall, Ao Zang was taken into custody, and Li Jiaoxiu was demoted to the position of governor of Hengzhou.

    Zong Chuke built a new house, all of which was constructed with cypress beams, walls plastered with sandalwood and red powder, and the opening of the door emitted a pervasive fragrance. The steps and ground were made of polished stone, and those who walked on it would fall over. Zong Chuke was exposed by Jianchang Prince for embezzling more than ten thousand strings of coins, and his brothers were exiled. Princess Taiping visited his house and sighed, saying, "Look at how he lived and sat. We have been living in vain and dying in waste." One year later, he was promoted to Vice Minister of the Secretariat and later became the Chief Secretary. He was executed after the downfall of the Wei clan.

    In the Zhaosheng Temple in Luoyang, Princess Anle built a hundred-treasure incense burner, three feet high, with four doors, a vermilion bridge, railings, flowers, birds, beasts, celestial musicians, and other intricate carvings. It featured pearls, jade, coral, amber, glass, and all kinds of precious items, costing three million coins, using up all the treasures in the treasury. [60]

    隋帝巡狩北边作大行殿七寳帐容数百人餙以珍寳光辉洞彻引匈奴啓民可汗宴会其中可汗恍然疑非人世之有识者云大行殿者示不祥也亦是王莽轻车之比天心其闗人事也欤
    　　安乐公主改为悖逆庶人夺百姓庄园造定昆池四十九里直抵南山拟昆明池累石为山以象华岳引水为涧以象天津飞阁步檐叙桥磴道衣以锦绣画以丹青饰以金银莹以珠玉又为九曲流杯池作石莲花台泉于台中涌出穷天下之壮丽悖逆之败配入司农每日士女游观车马填噎奉敕辄到者官人解见任凡人决一顿乃止
    　　安乐公主造百鸟毛裙以后百官百姓家效之山林竒禽异兽捜山满谷扫地无遗至于网罗杀获无数开元中禁寳器于殿前禁人服珠玉金银罗绮之物于是采捕乃止
    　　髙宗时有刘龙子妖言惑众作一金龙头藏袖中以羊膓盛水绕系之毎相聚出龙头言圣龙吐水饮之百病皆差遂转羊肠水于龙口中出与人饮之皆罔云病愈施舍无数遂起逆谋事发逃走捕访久之擒获斩之于市并其党十余人 [61]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [61]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 大行殿 - A large hall or palace, often used for important ceremonies and gatherings.
    • 七寳帳 - A luxurious canopy decorated with seven types of precious materials, symbolizing wealth and opulence.
    • 定昆池 - A large artificial lake built by Princess Anle, modeled after the Kunming Pool, known for its grandeur and beauty.
    • 九曲流杯池 - A nine-bend wine cup pool, a type of garden feature where guests would drink from cups as they floated along a winding path.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 隋帝 - Emperor Yang of Sui, known for his extravagant building projects and military campaigns.
    • 匈奴啓民可汗 - Qimin Khan, a leader of the Huns who was invited to a banquet by Emperor Yang of Sui.
    • 王莽轻车之比 - A reference to Wang Mang's light carriages, which were seen as omens of misfortune.
    • 安乐公主 - Princess Anle, a favorite daughter of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang, known for her luxurious lifestyle and political influence.
    • 开元中 - The Kaiyuan period, a time of prosperity and cultural flourishing under Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 高宗 - Emperor Gaozong of Tang, the third emperor of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 刘龙子 - Liu Longzi, a figure who spread false prophecies and eventually plotted rebellion during Emperor Gaozong's reign.

    Proper Nouns

    • 隋帝 - Emperor Yang of Sui
    • 匈奴啓民可汗 - Qimin Khan
    • 安乐公主 - Princess Anle
    • 定昆池 - Ding Kun Pool
    • 开元中 - Kaiyuan period
    • 高宗 - Emperor Gaozong of Tang
    • 刘龙子 - Liu Longzi
    • 王莽 - Wang Mang

    Emperor Yang of Sui, during his northern inspection tour, constructed a grand pavilion called the Great Hall with seven precious canopies, spacious enough to accommodate hundreds of people, adorned with precious jewels, and shining brightly. He invited the Hun leader Qimin Khan to a banquet within the pavilion. The Khan was amazed and wondered if such a place existed in the world. Some people commented that the Great Hall might be an omen of misfortune, comparing it to Wang Mang's light carriages, which were seen as signs of divine displeasure. They questioned whether this reflected divine will concerning human affairs.

    Princess Anle was demoted to a commoner for her rebellious actions and seized the estates of the people to build the Ding Kun Pool, which extended 49 miles to the south, resembling the Kunming Pool. She piled stones to form mountains resembling Mount Hua, carved water channels to resemble the Tianjin River, and constructed pavilions and bridges adorned with brocade, painted with vermilion and cinnabar, and decorated with gold, silver, jade, and pearls. She also created a nine-bend wine cup pool with a stone lotus platform from which泉水涌出, showcasing the most magnificent sights in the world. The downfall of the rebellious person resulted in their punishment by being sent to the Ministry of Agriculture. Every day, men and women came to visit, causing traffic congestion. Those who received imperial orders to attend were officials who had to resign from their positions, while ordinary people were sentenced to one hundred strokes of the cane.

    Princess Anle made a dress adorned with bird feathers, followed by officials and commoners imitating her, leading to the hunting of rare birds and animals in the mountains and valleys until they were nearly extinct. During the Kaiyuan period, Emperor Xuanzong banned the use of precious items in the palace and prohibited people from wearing jewelry and fine silk, thus stopping the excessive hunting.

    During Emperor Gaozong's reign, Liu Longzi spread false prophecies, using a gold dragon head hidden in his sleeve with a bag of blood water, claiming that drinking the water would cure all diseases. People believed him, and he eventually gathered enough support to plot rebellion. After the plot was discovered, he fled but was eventually captured, executed, and his accomplices were also punished. [61]

    东海孝子郭纯丧母毎哭则羣鸟大集使騐有实旌表门闾后访乃是孝子毎哭即散饼食于地羣鸟争来食之后如此鸟闻哭声以为度莫不竞凑非有灵也
    　　河东孝子王燧家猫犬互乳其子州县上言遂旌表乃是猫犬同时产子取猫儿置狗窠中狗子置猫窠内惯食其乳遂以为常殆不可以异论也自连理木合欢麦分岐禾同穗触类而长寔繁有徒并是人作不足怪也
    　　同泰于洛水得白石紫文云圣母临水永昌帝业进之授五品果毅置永昌县乃是白石凿作字以紫石末和药嵌之后并州汶水县于谷中得一石还如此有武兴字改汶水为武兴县自是往往作之后知其伪不复采用乃止
    　　襄州胡延庆得一龟以丹漆书其腹曰天子万万年以进之凤阁侍郎李昭德以刀刮之并尽奏请付法则天曰此非恶心也舍而勿问 [62]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [62]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 羣鸟大集 - Set phrase meaning 'a large gathering of birds.'
    • 散饼食 - Scattering bread or food.
    • 以为度 - Thinking it is a signal.
    • 互乳 - Nursing each other.
    • 以为常 - Considered as normal.
    • 付法则天 - According to the law of heaven.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 东海孝子郭纯 - Refers to a filial son named De Guo from the East Sea region.
    • 河东孝子王燧 - Refers to a filial son named Wang Sui from the Hedong region.
    • 连理木 - A symbol of good fortune and harmony.
    • 合欢 - A symbol of happiness and harmony.
    • 麦分岐 - A natural phenomenon where wheat branches.
    • 禾同穗 - A natural phenomenon where grain has the same ear.
    • 洛水 - The Luo River, a significant river in Chinese history.
    • 圣母临水永昌帝业 - An inscription on a stone that was believed to bring prosperity to the imperial cause.
    • 武兴 - A term used to describe a stone found in Wenshui County.
    • 襄州胡延庆 - Refers to a person named Hu Yanqing from Xiangzhou.
    • 凤阁侍郎李昭德 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Li Zhaode.

    Proper Nouns

    • 东海 - East Sea region.
    • 河东 - Hedong region.
    • 同泰 - A person who found a stone in the Luo River.
    • 洛水 - The Luo River.
    • 武兴 - Martial rise.
    • 襄州 - Xiangzhou.
    • 汶水县 - Wenshui County.
    • 天子万万年 - May the emperor live ten thousand years.

    The Filial Son De Guo from the East Sea Region

    De Guo, a filial son from the East Sea region, cried for his deceased mother, and a large gathering of birds would appear. The event was verified, and he was honored with a sign at his gate. Later, it was discovered that he scattered food on the ground when he cried, attracting the birds. The birds heard the crying sound and came, thinking it was a signal; they were not truly intelligent.

    The Filial Son Wang Sui from Hedong Region

    Wang Sui, a filial son from the Hedong region, had a cat and a dog that nursed each other's young. The local authorities reported this, and he was honored. It was discovered that they were born at the same time and were placed in each other's nests, leading to this behavior being considered normal.

    From Paired Trees to Happy Vines, Wheat Branching, and Grain with the Same Ear

    Various natural phenomena, such as paired trees, happy vines, wheat branching, and grain with the same ear, are common and numerous. They are all human-made and not worth being surprised about.

    Tong Tai Finds Stones with Inscriptions in the Luo River

    Tong Tai found a white stone with purple writing in the Luo River. The inscription read, "The divine mother by the water, forever prospering the imperial cause." The stone was presented, and a county was established. It was later discovered that the stone was carved with fake writing. Another stone with similar writing was found in Wenshui County, and these stones were found to be fraudulent and were no longer used.

    Hu Yanqing Presents a Turtle with an Inscription to the Emperor

    Hu Yanqing from Xiangzhou found a turtle with an inscription on its belly. The inscription read, "May the emperor live ten thousand years." The turtle was presented to the emperor, and the high-ranking official Li Zhaode used a knife to scrape off the writing. He reported to the emperor, who said, "This is not a malicious act. Let it go and do not inquire further." [62]

    则天好祯祥拾遗朱前疑说梦云则天发白更黑齿落更生即授都官郎中司刑事囚三百余人秋分后无计可作乃于圜狱外罗墙角边作圣人迹长五尺至夜半三百人一时大呌内使推问云昨夜有圣人见身长三丈面作金色云汝等并寃枉不须怕惧天子万年即有恩赦放汝把火照之见有巨迹即大赦天下改为大足元年
    　　白铁余者延州羁胡也左道惑众先于深山中埋一金铜像于栢树之下经数年草生其上绐乡人曰吾昨夜山下过毎见佛光大设斋卜吉日以出圣佛及期集数百人命于非所藏处斸不得乃劝曰诸公不至诚布施佛不可见由是男女争布施者百余万更于埋处斸之得金铜像乡人以圣逺近传之莫能见乃宣言曰见圣佛者百病即愈左侧数百里老小士女皆就之乃以绯紫红黄绫为袋数十重盛像人聚观者去一重一回布施千端乃见像如此狡伪一二年乡人归伏遂作乱自号光王署置官职杀长吏数年为患命将军程务挺斩之 [63]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [63]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 則天好祯祥 - Empress Wu loved auspicious signs, indicating her interest in omens and good fortune.
    • 拾遗朱前疑说梦 - The court historian Zhu Qianyi reported a dream, where Zhu Qianyi was a court official who reported a dream to Empress Wu.
    • 发白更黑齿落更生 - Her hair turned white and then black again, and her teeth fell out and grew back, a miraculous event attributed to Empress Wu.
    • 授都官郎中司刑事 - She was appointed as the Chief Justice of the Criminal Court, indicating her high-ranking position.
    • 囚三百余人 - There were more than three hundred prisoners, referring to the number of prisoners in the prison.
    • 秋分后无计可作 - After the autumn equinox, there was nothing to do, suggesting a period of inactivity.
    • 圜狱外罗墙角边作圣人迹 - Traces of a sage were made at the corner of the prison wall, indicating a deliberate act to create a sign.
    • 长五尺 - Five feet long, describing the size of the trace.
    • 至夜半三百人一时大呌 - At midnight, all three hundred prisoners suddenly cried out, indicating a collective reaction.
    • 内使推问 - The eunuchs investigated, referring to the palace officials who conducted the investigation.
    • 昨夜有圣人见身长三丈面作金色云 - Last night, a sage appeared, three zhang tall, with a golden face, describing the appearance of the sage.
    • 汝等并寃枉不须怕惧 - You all are wronged, do not be afraid, indicating a message of reassurance.
    • 天子万年即有恩赦放汝 - The emperor will live for a long time and will grant amnesty to you, promising future relief.
    • 把火照之见有巨迹 - When they lit a fire to look, they saw a large trace, indicating the discovery of the sign.
    • 大赦天下改为大足元年 - A general amnesty was granted, and the year was changed to Dazhu, indicating a significant political change.
    • 白铁余者延州羁胡也 - Bai Tieyu was a captive Turk from Yan Prefecture, providing background on Bai Tieyu.
    • 左道惑众 - He used false doctrines to deceive the people, indicating his fraudulent practices.
    • 先于深山中埋一金铜像于柏树之下 - He first buried a gold and bronze statue under a pine tree in a deep mountain, describing his initial action.
    • 经数年草生其上 - After several years, grass grew on top of it, indicating the passage of time.
    • 绐乡人曰吾昨夜山下过毎见佛光大设斋卜吉日以出圣佛 - He told the villagers that last night he passed by the mountain and saw the Buddha's light. He set up a grand feast and chose a lucky day to reveal the holy Buddha, describing his deception.
    • 及期集数百人命于非所藏处斸不得 - On the appointed day, he gathered hundreds of people and ordered them to dig at the wrong place, but they could not find it, indicating the failure of the search.
    • 乃劝曰诸公不至诚布施佛不可见 - Then he encouraged them, saying that if they did not sincerely offer alms, the Buddha would not appear, manipulating the crowd.
    • 由是男女争布施者百余万 - As a result, men and women competed to offer alms, totaling more than a million, indicating the extent of the deception.
    • 更于埋处斸之得金铜像 - They dug at the burial site and found the gold and bronze statue, revealing the truth.
    • 乡人以圣逺近传之莫能见 - The villagers spread the news far and wide, but no one could see the saint, indicating the widespread belief.
    • 乃宣言曰见圣佛者百病即愈 - He then declared that anyone who saw the saint would be cured of all illnesses, using the statue to gain more followers.
    • 左侧数百里老小士女皆就之 - Within several hundred miles, old and young, men and women, all flocked to him, indicating the widespread influence.
    • 乃以绯紫红黄绫为袋数十重盛像 - He then placed the statue in a bag made of red, purple, red, and yellow silk, with dozens of layers, describing the elaborate preparation.
    • 人聚观者去一重一回布施千端乃见像 - People gathered to watch, and for each layer removed, they offered alms, totaling several thousand times, and then they saw the statue, indicating the ritual.
    • 如此狡伪一二年 - In this way, he was deceitful for one or two years, indicating the duration of his fraud.
    • 乡人归伏遂作乱自号光王署置官职杀长吏数年为患 - The villagers submitted, and he rebelled, calling himself Guang Wang, appointing officials, and killing local officials, causing problems for several years, indicating the consequences of his actions.
    • 命将军程务挺斩之 - The general Cheng Wuteng was ordered to kill him, indicating the military action taken against him.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 則天 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history, known for her political acumen and religious devotion.
    • 祯祥 - Auspicious signs or omens, often associated with divine favor.
    • 拾遗 - A position in the Tang Dynasty responsible for reporting on court affairs and advising the emperor.
    • 朱前疑 - A court official during the reign of Empress Wu, known for his reports and advice.
    • 都官郎中司刑事 - The Chief Justice of the Criminal Court, a high-ranking official responsible for judicial matters.
    • 圜狱 - The prison, a place of confinement for criminals.
    • 秋分 - The autumn equinox, a significant astronomical event in the Chinese calendar.
    • 大足元年 - The first year of the Dazhu era, a period of political and social change during Empress Wu's reign.
    • 延州 - A prefecture in ancient China, known for its strategic importance.
    • 羁胡 - A captive or foreigner, often referring to non-Chinese people living in China.
    • 左道 - False or heretical doctrines, often associated with religious or political deception.
    • 金铜像 - A gold and bronze statue, often associated with religious or cultural significance.
    • 佛光 - The light of the Buddha, a symbol of enlightenment and divine presence.
    • 大设斋 - Setting up a grand feast, often associated with religious or ceremonial events.
    • 卜吉日 - Choosing a lucky day, often based on astrological or religious beliefs.
    • 圣佛 - The holy Buddha, a revered figure in Buddhism.
    • 布施 - Offering alms, a practice of giving to religious or charitable causes.
    • 光王 - A self-proclaimed title, indicating rebellion and the establishment of a new regime.
    • 长吏 - Local officials, responsible for administrative and judicial matters in local areas.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 则天好祯祥拾遗朱前疑说梦云则天发白更黑齿落更生即授都官郎中司刑事 must be read as a single narrative, where Empress Wu experiences a miraculous event and is subsequently appointed to a high-ranking position.
    • 囚三百余人秋分后无计可作乃于圜狱外罗墙角边作圣人迹长五尺至夜半三百人一时大呌内使推问云昨夜有圣人见身长三丈面作金色云汝等并寃枉不须怕惧天子万年即有恩赦放汝把火照之见有巨迹即大赦天下改为大足元年 must be read as a series of events leading to a general amnesty and a change in the year.
    • 白铁余者延州羁胡也左道惑众先于深山中埋一金铜像于柏树之下经数年草生其上绐乡人曰吾昨夜山下过毎见佛光大设斋卜吉日以出圣佛及期集数百人命于非所藏处斸不得乃劝曰诸公不至诚布施佛不可见由是男女争布施者百余万更于埋处斸之得金铜像乡人以圣逺近传之莫能见乃宣言曰见圣佛者百病即愈左侧数百里老小士女皆就之乃以绯紫红黄绫为袋数十重盛像人聚观者去一重一回布施千端乃见像如此狡伪一二年乡人归伏遂作乱自号光王署置官职杀长吏数年为患 must be read as a series of events leading to the discovery of the statue and the subsequent actions taken by the villagers.

    Empress Wu loved auspicious signs, and the court historian Zhu Qianyi reported a dream to her. In the dream, Empress Wu's hair turned white and then black again, and her teeth fell out and grew back. She was immediately appointed as the Chief Justice of the Criminal Court. There were more than three hundred prisoners in the prison. After the autumn equinox, there was nothing to do, so they made traces of a sage at the corner of the prison wall, five feet long. At midnight, all three hundred prisoners suddenly cried out. The eunuchs investigated and asked, "Last night, a sage appeared, three zhang tall, with a golden face. He said, 'You all are wronged, do not be afraid. The emperor will live for a long time and will grant amnesty to you.'" When they lit a fire to look, they saw a large trace, and a general amnesty was granted, changing the year to Dazhu.

    Bai Tieyu was a captive Turk from Yan Prefecture who used false doctrines to deceive the people. He first buried a gold and bronze statue under a pine tree in a deep mountain. After several years, grass grew on top of it. He told the villagers that last night he passed by the mountain and saw the Buddha's light. He set up a grand feast and chose a lucky day to reveal the holy Buddha. On the appointed day, he gathered hundreds of people and ordered them to dig at the wrong place, but they could not find it. Then he encouraged them, saying that if they did not sincerely offer alms, the Buddha would not appear. As a result, men and women competed to offer alms, totaling more than a million. They dug at the burial site and found the gold and bronze statue. The villagers spread the news far and wide, but no one could see the saint. He then declared that anyone who saw the saint would be cured of all illnesses. Within several hundred miles, old and young, men and women, all flocked to him. He then placed the statue in a bag made of red, purple, red, and yellow silk, with dozens of layers. People gathered to watch, and for each layer removed, they offered alms, totaling several thousand times, and then they saw the statue. In this way, he was deceitful for one or two years. The villagers submitted, and he rebelled, calling himself Guang Wang, appointing officials, and killing local officials, causing problems for several years. The general Cheng Wuteng was ordered to kill him. [63]

    中郎李庆逺狡诈倾险初事皇太子颇得出入暂令出外即恃威权宰相以下咸谓之要人宰执方食即来诸人命坐常遣一人门外急唤云殿下须使令吐饭而去诸司皆如此请谒嘱事卖官鬻狱所求必遂东宫后稍稍疎之仍潜入伏内食侍官饭晚出外腹痛犹诈云太子赐予食太多须臾霍出卫士所食米饭黄臭并虀菜狼籍凡是小人得宠多为此状也
    　　春官尚书阎知微和黙啜司賔丞田归道副焉至牙帐下知微舞蹈宛转抱黙啜鞾而鼻臭之田归道长揖不拜黙啜大怒倒悬之经一宿明日将杀元珍谏大国和亲使若杀之不祥乃放之后与知微争于殿庭言黙啜必不和知微坚执以为和黙啜果反陷赵定天后乃诛知微九族拜归道夏官侍郎 [64]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [64]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 狡诈倾险 - Compound adjective meaning 'deceitful and treacherous'.
    • 初事皇太子 - Means 'initially serving the crown prince'.
    • 颇得出入 - Means 'often allowed to enter and exit'.
    • 暂令出外 - Means 'temporarily ordered to leave'.
    • 即恃威权 - Means 'immediately relying on his authority'.
    • 宰相以下咸谓之要人 - Means 'ministers and below all considered him a key person'.
    • 宰执方食即来 - Means 'ministers were just eating when he arrived'.
    • 诸人命坐 - Means 'the others ordered him to sit'.
    • 常遣一人门外急唤云殿下须使令吐饭而去 - Means 'they would often send someone to urgently call him, saying the prince needed him to command the servants to spit out their food and then leave'.
    • 诸司皆如此请谒嘱事卖官鬻狱所求必遂 - Means 'all departments treated him similarly, soliciting favors, selling offices, and conducting illegal transactions, and whatever he sought was granted'.
    • 东宫后稍稍疎之 - Means 'later, the crown prince gradually distanced himself from him'.
    • 仍潜入伏内食侍官饭晚出外腹痛犹诈云太子赐予食太多须臾霍出卫士所食米饭黄臭并虀菜狼籍 - Means 'he still secretly entered the palace to eat the food of the servants, leaving in the evening with an excuse of stomach pain, claiming the prince had given him too much fruit, and soon after, he expelled the soldiers, the rice was yellow and smelly, and the vegetables were scattered'.
    • 凡是小人得宠多为此状也 - Means 'this is the usual state of affairs for those who gain favor through deceit'.
    • 春官尚书阎知微和黙啜司賔丞田归道副焉 - Means 'Spring Official and Shangshu Yin Zhiwei and Deputy Guest Censor Tian Guaidao accompanied him'.
    • 至牙帐下知微舞蹈宛转抱黙啜鞾而鼻臭之 - Means 'when they arrived at the tent, Yin Zhiwei danced and twisted, embracing the boots of Moche and smelling them'.
    • 田归道长揖不拜 - Means 'Tian Guaidao bowed deeply but did not kowtow'.
    • 黙啜大怒倒悬之经一宿 - Means 'Moche became extremely angry and had him suspended upside down for an entire night'.
    • 明日将杀元珍谏大国和亲使若杀之不祥乃放之 - Means 'the next day, he intended to kill Yuan Zhen, who advised against killing the envoy of a great country, considering it inauspicious, so he released him'.
    • 后与知微争于殿庭言黙啜必不和知微坚执以为和黙啜果反陷赵定天后乃诛知微九族拜归道夏官侍郎 - Means 'later, they argued in the palace, saying Moche would not agree to peace, but Yin Zhiwei insisted that he would, and indeed, Moche rebelled, capturing Zhao and Ding. The empress regnant then executed Yin Zhiwei's entire family and promoted Tian Guaidao to the position of Shangshu in the Department of Works'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 皇太子 - Refers to the crown prince, the heir apparent to the throne.
    • 宰相 - Refers to the prime minister or high-ranking officials in the imperial court.
    • 春官尚书 - Refers to the Minister of Rites, one of the six departments of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 黙啜 - Refers to Moche, a leader of the Uyghur tribe during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 司賔丞 - Refers to the Deputy Guest Censor, a lower-ranking official in the imperial court.
    • 天后 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian, who ruled the Tang Dynasty as the empress regnant.
    • 赵定 - Refers to Zhao and Ding, likely two regions or cities that were captured by Moche.
    • 九族 - Refers to the nine clans, a term used to describe a person's extended family, including ancestors and descendants.
    • 夏官侍郎 - Refers to the Deputy Minister of War, a high-ranking official in the imperial court.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李庆远 - Ruan Qingyuan, a historical figure.
    • 阎知微 - Yin Zhiwei, a historical figure.
    • 田归道 - Tian Guaidao, a historical figure.
    • 黙啜 - Moche, a historical figure.
    • 元珍 - Yuan Zhen, a historical figure.
    • 天后 - Empress Wu Zetian, a historical figure.
    • 赵定 - Zhao and Ding, likely historical regions or cities.
    • 九族 - A term used in historical contexts to describe a person's extended family.
    • 夏官侍郎 - A historical official position.

    Ruan Qingyuan, the Middle Court Attendant, was deceitful and treacherous. Initially serving the crown prince, he often had access to the palace and was frequently allowed to enter and exit. When temporarily ordered to leave, he immediately relied on his authority. Ministers and below all considered him a key person. When ministers were just eating, he would arrive, and the others would order him to sit. They would often send someone to urgently call him, saying the prince needed him to command the servants to spit out their food and then leave. All departments treated him similarly, soliciting favors, selling offices, and conducting illegal transactions, and whatever he sought was granted. Later, the crown prince gradually distanced himself from him. He still secretly entered the palace to eat the food of the servants, leaving in the evening with an excuse of stomach pain, claiming the prince had given him too much fruit, and soon after, he expelled the soldiers, the rice was yellow and smelly, and the vegetables were scattered. This is the usual state of affairs for those who gain favor through deceit.

    Spring Official and Shangshu Yin Zhiwei and Deputy Guest Censor Tian Guaidao accompanied him. When they arrived at the tent, Yin Zhiwei danced and twisted, embracing the boots of Moche and smelling them. Tian Guaidao bowed deeply but did not kowtow. Moche became extremely angry and had him suspended upside down for an entire night. The next day, he intended to kill Yuan Zhen, who advised against killing the envoy of a great country, considering it inauspicious, so he released him. Later, they argued in the palace, saying Moche would not agree to peace, but Yin Zhiwei insisted that he would, and indeed, Moche rebelled, capturing Zhao and Ding. The empress regnant then executed Yin Zhiwei's entire family and promoted Tian Guaidao to the position of Shangshu in the Department of Works. [64]

    张利涉性多忘解褐懐州参军每聚会被召必于笏上记之时河内令耿仁惠邀之怪其不至亲就门刺请涉看笏曰公何见顾笏上无名又一时昼寝惊索入州扣刺史邓恽门拜谢曰闻公欲赐责死罪邓恽曰无此事涉曰司功某甲言之恽大怒乃呼州官董以甲问搆将杖之甲苦诉初无此语涉前请曰望公舍之涉恐是梦中见说耳时人是知其性理昏惑矣
    　　五原县令阎一为人多忘尝至州于主人舍坐州佐史前过以为县典也呼欲杖之典曰某是州佐也一慙谢而止须臾县典至一疑其州佐也执手引坐典曰某是县佐也又愧而止曽有人传其兄书者止于阶下俄而里胥白録人到一索杖遂鞭送书人数下其人不知所以讯之一曰吾大错顾直典回宅取杯酒暖瘉良久典持酒至一既忘其取酒复忘其被杖者因便赐直典饮之 [65]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [65]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 解褐 - Means 'to take up an official post' or 'to enter public service,' often used to describe a person's first appointment as an official.
    • 每聚会被召必于笏上记之 - Describes a habit of writing down names on a tablet (笏) when invited to gatherings.
    • 多忘 - Means 'forgetful' or 'prone to forgetfulness.'
    • 止于阶下 - Means 'stopped at the steps.'
    • 索杖 - Means 'to take a whip.'
    • 遂鞭送书人 - Means 'then whipped the messenger.'
    • 暖瘉 - Means 'to warm and treat.'
    • 赐直典饮之 - Means 'gave the clerk wine to drink.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 懐州 - Refers to the administrative region of Huai Zhou, indicating a specific historical location.
    • 笏 - A tablet used by officials to write notes, a common tool in ancient Chinese bureaucracy.
    • 河内令 - The title of a local official, indicating the administrative structure of the time.
    • 耿仁惠 - A specific historical figure, suggesting a real person in the narrative.
    • 昼寝 - A common practice in ancient China, reflecting daily life and customs.
    • 刺史 - A high-ranking official, indicating the hierarchical structure of the government.
    • 邓恽 - Another specific historical figure, suggesting a real person in the narrative.
    • 五原县 - Refers to the administrative region of Wu Yuan County, indicating a specific historical location.
    • 阎玄一 - A specific historical figure, suggesting a real person in the narrative.
    • 直典 - A clerk or official, indicating the administrative structure of the time.
    • 州佐史 - Refers to officials in the state administration, indicating the hierarchical structure of the government.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张利涉 - A specific historical figure, suggesting a real person in the narrative.
    • 耿仁惠 - A specific historical figure, suggesting a real person in the narrative.
    • 邓恽 - A specific historical figure, suggesting a real person in the narrative.
    • 阎玄一 - A specific historical figure, suggesting a real person in the narrative.
    • 直典 - A clerk or official, indicating the administrative structure of the time.
    • 州佐史 - Refers to officials in the state administration, indicating the hierarchical structure of the government.
    • 五原县 - Refers to the administrative region of Wu Yuan County, indicating a specific historical location.
    • 河内令 - The title of a local official, indicating the administrative structure of the time.
    • 刺史 - A high-ranking official, indicating the hierarchical structure of the government.
    • 怀州 - Refers to the administrative region of Huai Zhou, indicating a specific historical location.

    Zhang Li She, a man prone to forgetfulness, was appointed as a military officer in Huai Zhou. Whenever he was invited to gatherings, he would write down the names on a tablet. At one point, the magistrate of He-nei, Geng Ren Hui, invited him, but was surprised when he did not show up. Geng personally went to the door and asked Zhang to look at the tablet, saying, "Why did you not come? There is no name on the tablet." At another time, Zhang had a daytime nap and was startled to find himself in the state capital, where he knocked on the governor Deng Yun's door to apologize, saying, "I heard that you wanted to punish me with death." Deng Yun replied, "There is no such thing." Zhang said, "The military officer Mr. Ji said so." Deng Yun became very angry and summoned the official Ji to ask about it. He was about to whip Ji, but Ji vehemently protested, saying he had never said such a thing. Zhang then pleaded, "I hope you will forgive him. I am afraid it was just something I saw in a dream." People at the time knew of his confused and forgetful nature.

    Yan Xuan Yi, the magistrate of Wu Yuan County, was also forgetful. Once, he went to the state capital and sat in the host's house. When a state official passed by, he mistook him for a county official and called for him to be whipped. The official said, "I am a state official." Yan felt ashamed and stopped. Shortly after, the county official arrived, and Yan suspected he was a state official and held his hand, inviting him to sit. The official said, "I am a county official." Yan again felt ashamed and stopped. Once, there was a messenger who delivered a letter from his brother, stopping at the steps. Soon, a village official reported that a recorder had arrived. Yan took a whip and whipped the messenger several times. The messenger did not understand why and was questioned. Yan said, "I made a big mistake. I asked the recorder to return home and fetch a cup of wine to warm and treat. After a long while, the recorder brought the wine. Yan forgot that he had asked for the wine and also forgot that the messenger had been whipped. He then gave the recorder wine to drink." [65]

    沧州南皮县丞郭务静初上典王庆通判禀静曰尔何姓庆曰姓王须臾庆又来又问何姓庆又曰姓王静怪愕良久仰看庆曰南皮佐史总姓王
    　　定州何名逺大富主官中三驿每于驿边起居停商専以袭胡为业赀财巨万家有绫机五百张逺年老惑不从戎即家贫破及如故即复盛
    　　长安富民罗会以剔粪为业里中谓之鸡肆言若鸡之因剔粪而有所得也会世副其业家财巨万有士人陆景旸会邀过所止馆舍甚丽入内梳洗衫衣极鲜屛风毡褥烹宰无所不有景旸问曰主人即如此快活何为不罢恶事会曰吾中间停废一二年奴婢死亡牛马散失复业已来家图稍遂非情愿也分合如此
    　　滕王婴蒋王晖皆不能亷慎大帝赐诸王名五王不及二王敕曰滕叔蒋兄自解经纪不劳赐物与之以为钱贯二王大慙朝官莫不自励皆以取受为赃汚有终身为累莫敢犯者 [66]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [66]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 怪愕良久 - Means 'was greatly surprised and bewildered for a long time.'
    • 分合如此 - Means 'such is the nature of things.'
    • 自解经纪 - '自解' means 'self-managed,' and '经纪' means 'financial affairs.'
    • 取受为赃汚 - '取受' means 'accepting,' and '赃汚' means 'corruption.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 沧州南皮县丞 - Refers to a county magistrate in the Cangzhou region, specifically in Nanpi County.
    • 定州 - Refers to Dingzhou, a city in Hebei Province.
    • 长安 - Refers to Chang'an, the ancient capital of China, now known as Xi'an.
    • 滕王婴蒋王晖 - Refers to Prince Teng Ying and Prince Jiang Hui, members of the royal family.
    • 大帝 - Refers to the emperor, likely referring to a specific emperor during the Tang Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns
