    • 沧州 - Cangzhou, a city in Hebei Province.
    • 南皮县 - Nanpi County, a county in Cangzhou.
    • 定州 - Dingzhou, a city in Hebei Province.
    • 长安 - Chang'an, the ancient capital of China, now known as Xi'an.
    • 滕王婴 - Prince Teng Ying.
    • 蒋王晖 - Prince Jiang Hui.
    • 大帝 - The emperor, likely referring to a specific emperor during the Tang Dynasty.

    Deputy County Magistrate Guo Wu Jing of Nanpi, Cangzhou, first reported to the governor Wang Qing, who asked, "What is your surname?" Guo replied, "Wang." Soon after, Wang Qing returned and asked again, "What is your surname?" Guo again replied, "Wang." Wang Qing was greatly surprised and bewildered for a long time, looking up at Guo and saying, "All the assistants and clerks in Nanpi are surnamed Wang."

    In Dingzhou, a wealthy man named He Yuan managed three official stations. He always stayed and conducted business near the stations, devoting himself to trade with the nomadic tribes. He was immensely wealthy, owning five hundred silk looms. As he grew older, he became bewildered and did not join the military, leading to his family's financial ruin. However, when he returned to his former business, his family's fortunes gradually recovered, though not by his will. Such is the nature of things.

    In Chang'an, a wealthy man named Luo Hui made his living by cleaning latrines. The people in the neighborhood called him "Chicken Shop," saying that he gained something from his work, just as chickens gain from their feed. Luo Hui's family had immense wealth, and he was visited by a scholar named Lu Jingyang. Luo invited Lu to his residence, which was very beautiful. Inside, the scholar found the house well-furnished, with fresh clothes and luxurious bedding. Lu asked, "How can you be so content if you continue this occupation?" Luo replied, "I suspended my business for a couple of years, and my servants and animals died or were lost. Since I resumed my business, my family's plans have gradually been achieved, though not by my will. Such is the nature of things."

    Princes Teng Ying and Jiang Hui were both unable to be upright and cautious. The emperor bestowed names on the princes, and the five princes did not match the two princes. The emperor decreed, "Teng and Jiang, you manage your financial affairs yourselves and do not need bestowed goods. Give them as strings of coins." The two princes were deeply ashamed, and officials in the court were all self-motivated. They all considered accepting bribes as corruption, which would be a burden for life, and no one dared to commit such acts. [66]

    瀛州饶阳县令窦知范贪汚有一里正死范令门内一人为里正造像各出钱一贯范自纳之谓曰里正有过罪先须急捄范先造得一像且以与之结钱二千百【阙】五寸半其贪皆类此范惟一男放鹰马惊桑枝打破其脑百姓快之皆曰千金之子易一兎之命
    　　益州新昌县令夏侯彪之初下车问里正曰鸡卵一钱几颗曰三颗彪之乃遣取十千钱买三万颗谓里正曰未须要且寄母鸡抱之遂成三万头鸡经数月长成令县吏与我卖一鸡三十钱半年之间成三十万又问竹笋一钱几茎曰五茎又取十千钱付之买得五万茎谓里正曰吾未须要笋且向林中养之至秋竹成一茎十钱成五十万其贪鄙不道皆类此
    　　汴州刺史王志愔饮食精细对賔下脱粟饭商客有一骡日行三百里曽三十年不卖市人报价云十四千愔曰四千金少更増一千又令买单丝罗疋至三千愔问用几两丝对曰五两愔令竖子取五两丝来每两别与十钱手功之直 [67]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [67]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 贪汚 - Corrupt or greedy, describing the magistrate's behavior.
    • 脱粟饭 - Plain, unpolished rice, often associated with frugality or simplicity.
    • 千金之子易一兎之命 - Proverb meaning 'a wealthy person's child easily exchanges one rabbit's life,' suggesting that the wealthy are more likely to forgive minor offenses.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 瀛州 - Refers to Ying Prefecture, an administrative region during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 饶阳县 - Refers to Rao County, a specific county within Ying Prefecture.
    • 窦知范 - The corrupt magistrate of Rao County.
    • 夏侯彪之 - The corrupt magistrate of Xinchang County.
    • 益州 - Refers to Yi Prefecture, another administrative region during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 新昌县 - Refers to Xinchang County, a specific county within Yi Prefecture.
    • 汴州 - Refers to Bian Prefecture, another administrative region during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 王志愔 - The magistrate of Bian Prefecture known for his greed and luxury.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence '贪汚有一里正死范令门内一人为里正造像各出钱一贯范自纳之谓曰里正有过罪先须急捄范先造得一像且以与之结钱二千百【阙】五寸半' is complex and elliptical, describing a series of events involving the magistrate's corruption and the creation of a statue.
    • The sentence '益州新昌县令夏侯彪之初下车问里正曰鸡卵一钱几颗曰三颗彪之乃遣取十千钱买三万颗谓里正曰未须要且寄母鸡抱之遂成三万头鸡经数月长成令县吏与我卖一鸡三十钱半年之间成三十万' describes the magistrate's cunning scheme to acquire chickens by buying eggs.
    • The sentence '汴州刺史王志愔饮食精细对賔下脱粟饭商客有一骡日行三百里曽三十年不卖市人报价云十四千愔曰四千金少更増一千又令买单丝罗疋至三千愔问用几两丝对曰五两愔令竖子取五两丝来每两别与十钱手功之直' describes the magistrate's luxurious lifestyle and his dealings with a merchant.

    Proper Nouns

    • 窦知范 - The corrupt magistrate of Rao County.
    • 夏侯彪之 - The corrupt magistrate of Xinchang County.
    • 王志愔 - The magistrate of Bian Prefecture known for his greed and luxury.

    Ying Prefecture's Rao County magistrate Dou Zhifan was corrupt. When one village head died, Dou ordered a villager to create an image for him, each contributing one string of coins. Dou took the money for himself and said, "If the village head has committed a crime, we must rescue him urgently." Dou first created an image and gave it to the villager, settling the money at two thousand and five hundred strings, half of which he kept for himself. His greed was similar to this. Dou had only one son. When Dou was releasing hawks, his horse startled a mulberry branch, breaking someone's head. The people were pleased and said, "A thousand gold pieces can easily exchange for a rabbit's life."

    In Yi Prefecture's Xinchang County, the newly appointed magistrate Xiahou Biao asked a village head, "How many eggs can be bought for one string of coins?" The village head replied, "Three." Xiahou then sent someone to fetch ten thousand strings of coins to buy thirty thousand eggs, telling the village head, "We don't need them yet; just have the hens incubate them." After a few months, they grew into thirty thousand chickens. Xiahou ordered the county officials to sell them for thirty coins each, earning thirty thousand strings of coins in half a year. He then asked, "How many bamboo shoots can be bought for one string of coins?" The village head replied, "Five." Xiahou then took ten thousand strings of coins and bought fifty thousand shoots, telling the village head, "I don't need them yet; just plant them in the forest." By autumn, the bamboo grew, and each shoot was worth ten coins, totaling fifty thousand strings of coins. His greed and lack of morality were similar to this.

    In Bian Prefecture, the governor Wang Zhi'an had a refined diet. When he served guests, he served plain rice. A merchant had a horse that could travel three hundred miles a day and had not been sold for thirty years. The market valued it at fourteen thousand strings of coins. Wang said, "Four thousand is too little; increase it by a thousand more." He then ordered the merchant to buy a piece of fine silk for three thousand strings of coins. Wang asked, "How many taels of silk are needed?" The merchant replied, "Five." Wang ordered a servant to fetch five taels of silk, giving ten coins for each tael as payment for manual labor. [67]

    深州刺史段崇简性贪暴到任令里正括客云不得称无上戸每取两人下戸取一人以刑脇之人惧皆妄通通讫简云不用唤客来但须见主人主人到处分每客索绢一疋约一月之内纳三十车罢任发至鹿城县有一车装绢未满载欠六百疋即唤里正令满之里正许无所出遂于县令丞尉家一倍举送至都拜栁州刺史
    　　安南都防崔信命女婿裴惟岳摄受州刺史贪暴取金银财物向万贯有首领取妇裴郎要障车绫索一千疋得八百疋仍不肯放捉新妇归戏之三日乃放还首领更不复纳裴即领物至州安南及问至擒之物并纳官裴亦锁项至安南以谢百姓及海口会赦而免
    　　洛州司佥严升期摄侍御史于江南巡察性嗜牛肉所至州县烹宰极多事无大小入金则弭凡到处金银为之踊贵故江南人谓为金牛刺史 [68]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [68]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 深州刺史 - Governor of Shen Prefecture, a specific title and position in the Tang Dynasty administrative system.
    • 性貪暴 - Describes someone with a greedy and violent nature.
    • 到任令里正括客 - Upon taking office, he ordered the village head to conduct a census of guests.
    • 不得稱無上戶 - No one can claim to be without an upper household.
    • 每取兩人下戸取一人 - He would take two from the upper households and one from the lower households.
    • 以刑脅之 - Using threats of punishment to coerce them.
    • 人懼皆妄通 - People, fearing punishment, falsely reported their status.
    • 通讫简云不用唤客来但须见主人 - After completing the census, he said he did not need to call the guests; he only needed to see the hosts.
    • 主人到处分 - The hosts would be everywhere distributing the burden.
    • 每客索绢一疋约一月之内纳三十车 - Each guest was required to contribute one bolt of silk, amounting to thirty carts within a month.
    • 罢任发至鹿城县有一车装绢未满载欠六百疋 - Upon leaving office, he was sent to Lucheng County, where one cart of silk was not fully loaded, short by six hundred bolts.
    • 即唤里正令满之 - He immediately called the village head to make it up.
    • 里正许无所出遂于县令丞尉家一倍举送至都 - The village head had nothing to offer, so he doubled the amount from the county magistrate, deputy magistrate, and military commander, sending it to the capital.
    • 拜栁州刺史 - He was appointed governor of Liuzhou.
    • 安南都防崔信命女婿裴惟岳摄受州刺史 - Feng Xianxin, the military governor of Annan, ordered his son-in-law Pei Weiye to temporarily serve as governor of the state.
    • 贪暴取金银财物向万贯 - He was greedy and violent, seizing gold, silver, and property worth tens of thousands of strings of cash.
    • 有首领取妇裴郎要障车绫索一千疋 - A leader brought Pei Lang, his wife, and demanded one thousand bolts of silk for a bridal procession.
    • 得八百疋仍不肯放捉新妇归戏之三日乃放还首领更不复纳 - He obtained eight hundred bolts but still refused to let go, taking the new bride back and playing with her for three days before releasing her, and the leader never paid again.
    • 裴即领物至州安南及问至擒之物并纳官 - Pei then took the goods to Yangzhou and Annan, and when questioned, he surrendered the seized items to the authorities.
    • 裴亦锁项至安南以谢百姓及海口会赦而免 - Pei was also shackled and sent to Annan to apologize to the people, and he was eventually pardoned at the time of a general amnesty.
    • 洛州司佥严升期摄侍御史于江南巡察 - Yan Shengqi, an official from Luo Prefecture, was appointed as a temporary censor to inspect the Jiangnan region.
    • 性嗜牛肉所至州县烹宰极多 - He had a strong liking for beef, and wherever he went, there was a lot of slaughtering of cattle.
    • 事无大小入金则弭 - No matter the size of the matter, if gold was offered, it would be resolved.
    • 凡到处金银为之踊贵 - Everywhere he went, gold and silver became extremely expensive.
    • 故江南人谓为金牛刺史 - Therefore, people in Jiangnan called him the 'Gold Cattle Governor.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 深州 - A prefecture in the Tang Dynasty, located in present-day Hebei Province.
    • 安南都防 - The military governor of Annan, a region in southern China.
    • 崔信 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official or military governor.
    • 裴惟岳 - A historical figure, likely a relative of a prominent family.
    • 栁州 - A prefecture in the Tang Dynasty, located in present-day Guangxi Province.
    • 安南 - A region in southern China, now part of Vietnam.
    • 洛州 - A prefecture in the Tang Dynasty, located in present-day Henan Province.
    • 司佥 - A lower-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty bureaucracy.
    • 侍御史 - A censor or inspector in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 江南 - A region in southern China, encompassing parts of modern-day Jiangsu, Zhejiang, and Anhui provinces.

    Proper Nouns

    • 深州 - A prefecture in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 崔信 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official or military governor.
    • 裴惟岳 - A historical figure, likely a relative of a prominent family.
    • 栁州 - A prefecture in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 安南 - A region in southern China, now part of Vietnam.
    • 洛州 - A prefecture in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 严升期 - An official from Luo Prefecture.
    • 江南 - A region in southern China, encompassing parts of modern-day Jiangsu, Zhejiang, and Anhui provinces.

    Governor Duan Chongjian of Shen Prefecture was greedy and violent. Upon taking office, he ordered the village head to conduct a census of guests, saying no one could claim to be without an upper household. He would take two from the upper households and one from the lower households, using threats of punishment to coerce them. Fearing punishment, people falsely reported their status. After completing the census, he said he did not need to call the guests; he only needed to see the hosts. The hosts would be everywhere distributing the burden. Each guest was required to contribute one bolt of silk, amounting to thirty carts within a month. Upon leaving office, he was sent to Lucheng County, where one cart of silk was not fully loaded, short by six hundred bolts. He immediately called the village head to make it up. The village head had nothing to offer, so he doubled the amount from the county magistrate, deputy magistrate, and military commander, sending it to the capital. He was then appointed governor of Liuzhou.

    Feng Xianxin, the military governor of Annan, ordered his son-in-law Pei Weiye to temporarily serve as governor of the state. Pei was greedy and violent, seizing gold, silver, and property worth tens of thousands of strings of cash. A leader brought Pei Lang, his wife, and demanded one thousand bolts of silk for a bridal procession. He obtained eight hundred bolts but still refused to let go, taking the new bride back and playing with her for three days before releasing her, and the leader never paid again. Pei then took the goods to Yangzhou and Annan, and when questioned, he surrendered the seized items to the authorities. Pei was also shackled and sent to Annan to apologize to the people, and he was eventually pardoned at the time of a general amnesty.

    Yan Shengqi, an official from Luo Prefecture, was appointed as a temporary censor to inspect the Jiangnan region. He had a strong liking for beef, and wherever he went, there was a lot of slaughtering of cattle. No matter the size of the matter, if gold was offered, it would be resolved. Everywhere he went, gold and silver became extremely expensive. Therefore, people in Jiangnan called him the "Gold Cattle Governor." [68]

    张昌仪为洛阳令借易之权势属官无不允者风声鼔动有一人姓薛赍金五十两遮而奉之仪领金受其状至朝堂付天官侍郎张锡数日失状以问仪仪曰我亦不记得有姓薛者即与锡检业内姓薛者六十余人并令与官其蠧政也如此

    　　朝野佥载卷三
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　朝野佥载卷四
    　　唐　张鷟　撰
    　　隋辛亶为吏部侍郎选人为之牓略曰州枉抑县屈滞乡不申里衔恨先生问隋吏部侍郎辛亶曰当今天子圣明羣僚用令外拓四方内齐七政而子位处权衡职当水镜居进退之首握褒贬之柄理应识是识非知滞知微使无才者泥伏有用者云飞柰何尸禄素餐滥处上官黜陟失所选补伤残小人在位君子駮弹莫不代子战灼而子独何以安辛亶曰百姓之子万国之人不可皆识谁厚谁亲为桀赏者不可不喜被尧责者宁有不嗔得官者见喜失官者见疾细而论之非亶之失先生曰是何疾欤是何疾欤不识何不访其名 [69]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [69]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 張昌儀為洛阳令 - Zhang Changyi served as the magistrate of Luoyang, a title indicating his administrative role.
    • 借易之权势 - Borrowed the power and influence of the easy-going or lenient style, possibly referring to a person or a period of governance characterized by leniency.
    • 属官无不允者 - All subordinate officials were willing to comply, indicating the influence Zhang Changyi had over his subordinates.
    • 风声鼓动 - The news spread like a drumbeat, suggesting the rapid dissemination of information.
    • 赍金五十两 - Carried gold worth fifty taels, a significant amount of money in ancient China.
    • 遮而奉之 - Blocked his path and presented the gold, a gesture of bribery.
    • 领金受其状 - Received the gold and the complaint, indicating acceptance of the bribe.
    • 付天官侍郎张锡 - Handed over the case to Zhang Xi, a high-ranking official in charge of personnel affairs.
    • 数日失状 - Lost the complaint after several days, a suspicious omission.
    • 我亦不记得有姓薛者 - I do not remember anyone named Xue, a denial of knowledge.
    • 即与锡检业内姓薛者六十余人 - Immediately asked Zhang Xi to check the records for over sixty people surnamed Xue, an attempt to cover up the bribery.
    • 并令与官 - All of them were appointed to official positions, indicating corruption.
    • 其蠧政也如此 - Corruption in government was like this, a general statement about the extent of corruption.
    • 朝野佥载卷三 - Volume 3 of *Chao Ye Qian Zai*, a collection of anecdotes and gossip.
    • 钦定四库全书 - The Imperially Approved Complete Library, a massive collection of Chinese literature.
    • 朝野佥载卷四 - Volume 4 of *Chao Ye Qian Zai*.
    • 唐张鷟撰 - Compiled by Zhang Zhe during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 隋辛亶为吏部侍郎 - Xin Dan served as the Minister of Personnel during the Sui Dynasty.
    • 选人为之牓 - The list of candidates for selection.
    • 略曰 - Briefly stated, introducing the content of the list.
    • 州枉抑县屈滞乡不申里衔恨 - Prefectures oppress, counties delay, towns do not report, and villages harbor resentment, describing the grievances of the lower levels of society.
    • 先生问隋吏部侍郎辛亶 - A learned person asked Xin Dan, setting up a dialogue.
    • 当今天子圣明 - The current emperor is wise and enlightened, a common praise.
    • 群僚用令 - Officials follow orders, indicating obedience.
    • 外拓四方 - Expanding the territory outward, referring to territorial expansion.
    • 内齐七政 - Governing the seven major aspects of state affairs internally, a reference to good governance.
    • 子位处权衡 - You hold a position of balance, indicating a central and influential role.
    • 职当水镜 - Your duty is to be like a mirror, reflecting truth and fairness.
    • 居进退之首 - You are at the forefront of promotions and demotions, indicating a key role in personnel decisions.
    • 握褒贬之柄 - You hold the power of praise and criticism, indicating significant influence over evaluations.
    • 理应识是识非知滞知微 - You should know what is right and wrong, what is stuck and what is subtle, indicating the need for discernment.
    • 使无才者泥伏 - Make the unqualified ones stay in their place, indicating the need to keep incompetent people out of power.
    • 有用者云飞 - Allow the talented to rise, indicating the need to promote capable people.
    • 奈何尸禄素餐 - Why do you sit idly and eat without working, a criticism of laziness and incompetence.
    • 滥处上官 - Hold a high position unworthily, indicating abuse of power.
    • 黜陟失所 - Promotions and demotions are misplaced, indicating poor judgment.
    • 选补伤残 - Selections cause harm, indicating the negative consequences of poor selection.
    • 小人在位 - Petty people are in power, indicating corruption.
    • 君子驳弹 - Virtuous people criticize, indicating opposition to corruption.
    • 莫不代子战灼 - No one does not fight on your behalf, indicating widespread support or criticism.
    • 而子独何以安 - But how can you be at peace, a rhetorical question challenging the official's actions.
    • 百姓之子万国之人 - The children of the common people and people from all over the country, indicating a broad audience.
    • 不可皆识 - It is impossible to know everyone, indicating the limitations of human knowledge.
    • 谁厚谁亲 - Who is favored and who is close, indicating favoritism.
    • 为桀赏者不可不喜 - Those praised by Jie (a tyrant) cannot help but be happy, a reference to the corrupt system.
    • 被尧责者宁有不嗔 - Those criticized by Yao (a virtuous ruler) cannot help but be angry, a reference to the virtuous system.
    • 得官者见喜失官者见疾 - Those who get positions are happy, and those who lose them are angry, indicating the emotional impact of promotions and demotions.
    • 细而论之非亶之失 - If we look at it in detail, it is not Xin Dan's fault, a defense of the official.
    • 是何疾欤是何疾欤 - What is the illness? What is the illness? A rhetorical question emphasizing the problem.
    • 不识何不访其名 - If you do not recognize them, why not ask for their names, a suggestion for better identification.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 張昌儀 - A historical figure who served as the magistrate of Luoyang during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 洛阳 - Luoyang, a major city in China, known for its historical significance.
    • 隋辛亶 - Xin Dan, a high-ranking official during the Sui Dynasty.
    • 吏部侍郎 - A high-ranking official in charge of personnel affairs.
    • 选人 - Candidates for official positions.
    • 牓 - A list or register.
    • 州、县、乡、里 - Administrative divisions in ancient China, from the highest to the lowest level.
    • 天子 - The emperor, the supreme ruler.
    • 群僚 - Officials, referring to the government bureaucracy.
    • 外拓四方 - Territorial expansion, a key aspect of state policy.
    • 内齐七政 - Internal governance, referring to the management of state affairs.
    • 权衡 - Balance, indicating a central and influential role.
    • 水镜 - Mirror, symbolizing truth and fairness.
    • 进退之首 - At the forefront of promotions and demotions, indicating a key role in personnel decisions.
    • 褒贬之柄 - Power of praise and criticism, indicating significant influence over evaluations.
    • 识是识非知滞知微 - Knowing what is right and wrong, what is stuck and what is subtle, indicating the need for discernment.
    • 尸禄素餐 - Sitting idly and eating without working, a criticism of laziness and incompetence.
    • 滥处上官 - Holding a high position unworthily, indicating abuse of power.
    • 黜陟失所 - Promotions and demotions are misplaced, indicating poor judgment.
    • 选补伤残 - Selections cause harm, indicating the negative consequences of poor selection.
    • 小人在位 - Petty people are in power, indicating corruption.
    • 君子驳弹 - Virtuous people criticize, indicating opposition to corruption.
    • 代子战灼 - Fighting on your behalf, indicating widespread support or criticism.
    • 百姓之子万国之人 - The children of the common people and people from all over the country, indicating a broad audience.
    • 不可皆识 - It is impossible to know everyone, indicating the limitations of human knowledge.
    • 谁厚谁亲 - Who is favored and who is close, indicating favoritism.
    • 为桀赏者不可不喜 - Those praised by Jie (a tyrant) cannot help but be happy, a reference to the corrupt system.
    • 被尧责者宁有不嗔 - Those criticized by Yao (a virtuous ruler) cannot help but be angry, a reference to the virtuous system.
    • 得官者见喜失官者见疾 - Those who get positions are happy, and those who lose them are angry, indicating the emotional impact of promotions and demotions.
    • 细而论之非亶之失 - If we look at it in detail, it is not Xin Dan's fault, a defense of the official.
    • 是何疾欤是何疾欤 - What is the illness? What is the illness? A rhetorical question emphasizing the problem.
    • 不识何不访其名 - If you do not recognize them, why not ask for their names, a suggestion for better identification.

    Zhang Changyi, serving as the magistrate of Luoyang, borrowed the power and influence of the easy-going style, and all his subordinate officials were willing to comply. The news spread like a drumbeat, and one day, a man surnamed Xue carried gold worth fifty taels, blocked his path, and presented it to him. Zhang Changyi received the gold and the complaint, and handed it over to Zhang Xi, the Director of Personnel, at court. After several days, he lost the complaint and asked Zhang Changyi about it. Zhang Changyi said, "I do not remember anyone named Xue." He immediately asked Zhang Xi to check the records for over sixty people surnamed Xue and appointed them all to official positions. Corruption in government was like this.

    Volume 3 of Chao Ye Qian Zai
    The Imperially Approved Complete Library
    Volume 4 of Chao Ye Qian Zai
    Compiled by Zhang Zhe during the Tang Dynasty

    Xin Dan served as the Minister of Personnel during the Sui Dynasty. The list of candidates for selection briefly stated: "Prefectures oppress, counties delay, towns do not report, and villages harbor resentment." A learned person asked Xin Dan: "The current emperor is wise and enlightened, and officials follow orders. The territory is expanding outward, and internal governance is orderly. You hold a position of balance, your duty is to be like a mirror, at the forefront of promotions and demotions, and you hold the power of praise and criticism. You should know what is right and wrong, what is stuck and what is subtle, make the unqualified ones stay in their place, and allow the talented to rise. Why do you sit idly and eat without working, hold a high position unworthily, and have promotions and demotions misplaced? Selections cause harm, petty people are in power, and virtuous people criticize. No one does not fight on your behalf, but how can you be at peace?" Xin Dan replied, "The children of the common people and people from all over the country are too many to know everyone. Who is favored and who is close? Those praised by Jie (a tyrant) cannot help but be happy, and those criticized by Yao (a virtuous ruler) cannot help but be angry. Those who get positions are happy, and those who lose them are angry. If we look at it in detail, it is not my fault. What is the illness? What is the illness? If you do not recognize them, why not ask for their names?" [69]

    官少何不简其精细寻状迹足识法家细寻判騐足识文华宁不知石中出玉黄金出沙量子之才度子之智秪可投之四裔以御魑魅怨嗟不少实伤和气辛亶再拜而谢曰辛先生见责实觉多违谨当刮肌贯骨改过惩非请先生纵亶自修舍亶之罚如更有违甘从斧钺先生曰如子之辈车载斗量朝廷多少立须相代那得久旷天官侍子自作急去急去不得久住唤取师巫却行无处亶掩泣而言曰罪过自招自灭自消岂敢更将面目来汚圣朝先生曵杖而歌曰辛亶去吏部开贤路遇太平今年定知不可得后歳依期更入京 [70]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [70]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 官少何不简其精细寻状迹足识法家细寻判騐足识文华 - Complex phrase suggesting thorough investigation and careful judgment when there are fewer officials.
    • 怨嗟不少实伤和气 - Complaints and grievances are not few and actually harm harmony and peace.
    • 辛亶再拜而谢曰 - Formal expression of deep respect and gratitude, indicating a bow and thank you.
    • 辛先生见责实觉多违谨当刮肌贯骨改过惩非 - Acknowledgment of criticism and promise to deeply reflect and correct mistakes.
    • 请先生纵亶自修舍亶之罚如更有违甘从斧钺 - Request to waive punishment and willingness to accept severe punishment for further mistakes.
    • 如子之辈车载斗量朝廷多少立须相代那得久旷天官侍子自作急去急去不得久住唤取师巫却行无处 - Metaphorical expression suggesting numerous and easily replaceable individuals in the government.
    • 掩泣而言曰罪过自招自灭自消岂敢更将面目来汚圣朝 - Covering face with tears and acknowledging self-caused downfall, not daring to show face again to the holy court.
    • 先生曵杖而歌曰辛亶去吏部开贤路遇太平今年定知不可得后歳依期更入京 - Teacher pulls out walking stick and sings, suggesting leaving to open the way for talented people and returning to the capital next year.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 辛亶 - Historical figure, likely a scholar or official.
    • 法家 - Refers to the Legalist school of thought in ancient China.
    • 文华 - Refers to literary talent and refinement.
    • 斧钺 - Metaphor for severe punishment.
    • 师巫 - Refers to spiritual or religious figures.
    • 吏部 - The Ministry of Personnel, responsible for appointments and promotions.
    • 圣朝 - Refers to the current or a revered dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 辛亶 - Historical figure, likely a scholar or official.
    • 法家 - Refers to the Legalist school of thought in ancient China.
    • 文华 - Refers to literary talent and refinement.
    • 斧钺 - Metaphor for severe punishment.
    • 师巫 - Refers to spiritual or religious figures.
    • 吏部 - The Ministry of Personnel.
    • 圣朝 - Refers to the current or a revered dynasty.

    If there are fewer officials, why not carefully examine the details and traces to truly understand the nature of the law and its application? Do you not know that precious gems can be found in stones and gold in sand? With your talents and wisdom, you are only suitable to be sent to the remote regions to guard against evil spirits. Complaints and grievances are not few and actually harm the harmony and peace. Xin Dan bowed twice and thanked, saying, "Mr. Xin Dan, you have criticized me, and I feel that I have committed many violations. I will deeply reflect on my actions and correct my mistakes. Please allow me to self-improve and waive my punishment. If I make more mistakes, I am willing to accept severe punishment." The teacher replied, "People like you are as numerous as the stars in the sky and are easily replaceable in the government. How can you delay and stay long? You must leave immediately, as there is no place for you to stay." Xin Dan covered his face with tears and said, "My faults have brought about my own downfall. I dare not show my face again to the holy court." The teacher pulled out his walking stick and sang, "Xin Dan, go. Open the way for talented people at the Ministry of Personnel. You will not meet peace this year, but you will definitely return to the capital next year." [70]

    隋牛为吏部侍郎有选人马敞者形貌最陋轻之侧卧食果子嘲敞曰尝闻扶风马谓言天上下今见扶风马得驴亦不假敞应声曰当闻陇西牛千石不用軥今见陇西牛卧地打草头惊起遂与官
    　　陈朝尝令人聘隋不知其使机辩深浅乃宻令侯白变形貌着故衣为贱人供承客谓是微贱甚轻之乃傍卧放气与之言白心颇不平问白曰汝国马价贵贱报云马有数等贵贱不同若从伎俩筋脚好形容不恶堪得乗骑者直二十千已上若形容麤壮虽无伎俩堪防物直四五千已上弥【音十浩反】尾燥蹄絶无伎俩傍卧放气一钱不直使者大惊问其姓名知是侯白方始愧谢 [71]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [71]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 侧卧食果子 - Means 'lying on one's side eating fruit,' indicating a casual and informal posture.
    • 卧地打草头 - Means 'lying on the ground eating grass,' another casual and informal posture.
    • 一钱不直 - Means 'not worth even a single coin,' indicating extreme low value.
    • 方始愧谢 - Means 'only then did he feel ashamed and apologize,' indicating a realization of one's mistake.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 隋 - Refers to the Sui dynasty (581-618 AD), a short-lived but significant Chinese dynasty.
    • 牛弘 - A prominent official during the Sui dynasty, known for his role as a high-ranking minister.
    • 吏部侍郎 - A high-ranking official in charge of personnel matters in the imperial bureaucracy.
    • 陈朝 - Refers to the Chen dynasty (557-589 AD), a Southern Chinese dynasty that was eventually conquered by the Sui dynasty.
    • 聘隋 - Refers to a diplomatic mission from the Chen dynasty to the Sui dynasty.
    • 侯白 - A famous scholar and writer of the Sui dynasty, known for his wit and literary works.
    • 陇西牛 - A reference to the high-quality oxen from the region of Longxi, known for their strength and value.
    • 軥 - A yoke used to harness oxen.
    • 千石 - An ancient unit of weight, often used to measure the value of goods.

    Proper Nouns

    • Niuhong - A high-ranking official in the Sui dynasty.
    • Ma Chang - A candidate with the ugliest appearance in the Sui dynasty.
    • Fufeng - A region known for its fine horses.
    • Longxi - A region known for its high-quality oxen.
    • Hou Bai - A famous scholar and writer of the Sui dynasty, known for his wit and literary works.

    During the Sui dynasty, Niuhong served as the Deputy Minister of Personnel. There was a candidate named Ma Chang who had the ugliest appearance. Niuhong looked down on him and, while lying on his side eating fruit, mocked him by saying, "I have heard that the horses of Fufeng are so fine that even if they come down from heaven, they would not be inferior. Now I see the horses of Fufeng, and they are not even as good as a donkey." Ma Chang responded immediately, "I have heard that the oxen of Longxi are so valuable that even if they are worth a thousand stones, they do not need a yoke. Now I see the oxen of Longxi, and they are lying on the ground eating grass." Niuhong was startled and got up, and thus gave him an official position.

    In the Chen dynasty, someone was sent to visit the Sui dynasty to test the depth of the envoy's wit and eloquence. They secretly ordered Hou Bai to change his appearance, wear old, ragged clothes, and serve as a lowly servant. The guest treated him as a lowly person and mocked him. Hou Bai replied, "The horses of my country have different prices. If they have good skills, a good appearance, and are suitable for riding, they are worth at least twenty thousand coins. If they are only strong and can carry loads, they are worth at least four or five thousand coins. If they are dry-tailed, have rough hooves, and have no skills, they are lying on the ground eating grass and are not worth even a single coin." The envoy was greatly surprised and asked his name, realizing that he was Hou Bai, and only then did he feel ashamed and apologize. [71]

    唐髙士亷选其人齿髙有选人自云解嘲谑士亷时着木履令嘲之应声云刺鼻何曾踏面不知瞠髙生两个齿自谓得胜人士亷笑而引之
    　　周则天朝蕃人上封事多加官赏有为右台御史者因则天尝问郎中张元一曰在外有何可笑事元一曰朱前疑着緑逯仁杰着朱阎知微骑马马吉甫骑驴将名作姓李千里将姓作名呉栖梧左台胡御史右台御史胡胡御史胡元礼也御史胡蕃人为御史者寻改他官周革命举人贝州赵廓眇小起家监察御史时人谓之台秽李昭德詈之为中霜谷束元一目为枭坐鹰架时同州鲁孔丘为拾遗有武夫气时人谓之外军主帅元一目为鹙入凤池苏味道才学识度物望攸归王方庆体质鄙陋言词鲁钝智不逾俗才不出凡俱为凤阁侍郎或问元一曰苏王孰贤答曰苏九月得霜鹰王十月被 [72]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [72]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 唐髙士亷 - Refers to Tang Dynasty scholar-official Gao Shilin, known for his wit and integrity.
    • 選其人齒髙 - Means 'selecting people of high status or age.'
    • 解嘲 - Means 'to explain or justify oneself humorously,' often in response to criticism.
    • 應聲 - Means 'in response' or 'immediately.'
    • 刺鼻何曾踏面不知瞠 - A humorous and somewhat obscure line, likely a play on words or a pun, which could be loosely translated as 'How could you poke your nose into something that isn't your business, or step on someone's face without knowing it?'
    • 自謂得勝人 - Means 'believes himself to be superior to others.'
    • 笑而引之 - Means 'laughed and led him away.'
    • 蕃人上封事多加官賞 - Foreigners submitting memorials were often promoted and rewarded.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周則天朝 - The era of Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 右台御史 - High-ranking officials in the imperial inspection system.
    • 郎中張元一 - Court official known for his wit and humor.
    • 朱前疑 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 逯仁杰 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 閻知微 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 騎馬馬吉甫 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 李千里 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 吳棲梧 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 左台胡御史 - High-ranking official in the imperial inspection system.
    • 胡元禮 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 周革命 - Period of political upheaval during Empress Wu Zetian's reign.
    • 舉人 - Scholars who have passed the imperial examinations.
    • 貝州趙廓 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 監察御史 - High-ranking officials in the imperial inspection system.
    • 台穢 - A stain on the court.
    • 李昭德 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 中霜谷束 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 目為梟 - To call someone a crow (a derogatory term).
    • 坐鷹架 - To sit on an eagle perch (a metaphor for being in a position of power).
    • 同州魯孔丘 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 拾遺 - Low-ranking officials in the imperial bureaucracy.
    • 武夫氣 - Martial spirit or warrior-like demeanor.
    • 外軍主帥 - Commander of the external army.
    • 目為鷲 - To call someone a buzzard (a derogatory term).
    • 入鳳池 - To enter the Phoenix Pool (a metaphor for entering a high position in the imperial court).
    • 蘇味道 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 才學識度 - Talent, learning, and wisdom.
    • 物望攸歸 - Respected and looked up to by others.
    • 王方慶 - A person's name, possibly a reference to a specific individual.
    • 體質鄙陋 - Ugly and unrefined in appearance.
    • 言詞魯鈍 - Slow-witted and unrefined in speech.
    • 智不逾俗 - Not more intelligent than the average person.
    • 才不出凡 - Not more talented than the average person.
    • 俱為鳳閣侍郎 - Both served as侍郎in the Phoenix Pavilion (a high-ranking position in the imperial bureaucracy).

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 選其人齒髙 - Selecting people of high status or age.
    • 解嘲 - Explaining or justifying oneself humorously.
    • 應聲 - In response or immediately.
    • 刺鼻何曾踏面不知瞠 - A humorous and somewhat obscure line, likely a play on words or a pun, which could be loosely translated as 'How could you poke your nose into something that isn't your business, or step on someone's face without knowing it?'
    • 自謂得勝人 - Believes himself to be superior to others.
    • 笑而引之 - Laughed and led him away.
    • 蕃人上封事多加官賞 - Foreigners submitting memorials were often promoted and rewarded.

    During the Tang Dynasty, Gao Shilin, a renowned scholar-official, was known for his wit and integrity. He once selected a person of high status who claimed to be skilled in humor and jest. Gao, in a playful mood, wore wooden shoes and challenged him to a humorous exchange. The man responded with a line that was both clever and obscure, something like, "How could you poke your nose into something that isn't your business, or step on someone's face without knowing it?" Gao, finding the exchange amusing, laughed and led him away.

    In the era of Empress Wu Zetian, foreigners were often allowed to submit memorials to the court, and many were promoted and rewarded for their contributions. One such foreigner became a high-ranking official known as a Right Platform Censor. On one occasion, Empress Wu asked Zhang Yuanyi, a court official, what amusing things had happened outside the palace. Zhang replied with a series of humorous observations about the eccentricities of his colleagues. He mentioned Zhu Qianyi, who wore green; Lu Renjie, who wore red; Yan Zhimou, who rode a horse named Ma Jifu; and Li Qianli, who used his name as a surname. He also noted Wu Qiwu, a Left Platform Censor, and the Right Platform Censor, both of whom were known as "Hu" (a term that could be interpreted as either "foreign" or "black"). Zhang then pointed out that a foreigner who had served as a Right Platform Censor was later reassigned to another position. During the period of political upheaval known as the Zhou Revolution, a man from Beizhou named Zhao Kuo, who was of humble background, rose to the position of Inspector of Judicial Affairs. He was often referred to as a "stain on the court." Li Zhaode, another official, once called him a "crow," a derogatory term. Zhang, in turn, referred to him as a "buzzard" and noted that he was once in a position of power, like sitting on an eagle perch. At the same time, Lu Kongqiu from Tongzhou, who served as a Remonstrant Censor, had a martial spirit and was often called the "commander of the external army." Zhang, however, referred to him as a "buzzard" and noted that he had entered the Phoenix Pool, a metaphor for a high position in the imperial court. Su Weidao, a man of great talent, learning, and wisdom, was highly respected and looked up to by others. In contrast, Wang Fangqing, who was considered ugly and unrefined in appearance and speech, was no more intelligent or talented than the average person. Both men served as侍郎in the Phoenix Pavilion, a high-ranking position in the imperial bureaucracy. When asked by Zhang which of the two was more talented, Zhang replied that Su was like a frosty eagle in September, while Wang was like a bird in October, implying that Su was more formidable and impressive. [72]

    冻蝇或问其故答曰得霜鹰伎防被冻蝇顽怯时人谓能体物也契丹之孙万荣之寇幽河内王武懿宗为元帅引兵至赵州闻敌骆务整从此数千骑来王乃弃兵甲南走荆州军资器械遗于道路闻敌已退方更向前军回至都置酒髙会元一于御前嘲懿宗曰长短度箭蜀马临堦骗去敌七百里隈墙独自战甲仗纵抛却骑猪正南掾上曰懿宗有马何因骑猪对曰骑猪豕走也上大笑懿宗曰元一宿搆不是卒辞上曰尔叶韵与之懿宗曰请以菶韵元一应声曰裹头极草草掠不菶菶未见桃花面皮漫作杏子眼孔则天大悦王极有慙色懿宗形貌短丑故曰长短度箭周静乐县主河内王懿 [73]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [73]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 冻蝇 - Metaphor for a weak or sluggish person, likely referring to someone who is easily frozen or immobilized, symbolizing lack of vigor or resilience.
    • 得霜鹰伎防 - Phrase referring to a falcon that has lost its sharpness or skill due to frost, symbolizing a decline in ability or effectiveness.
    • 体物 - Literally means 'to embody or represent something,' but in this context, it likely refers to understanding or empathizing with the nature of something, possibly the falcon's condition.
    • 长短度箭蜀马 - Phrase that is difficult to interpret literally. It may refer to a long and short measurement of an arrow and a horse from Shu, possibly symbolizing inconsistency or lack of precision.
    • 临堦骗去敌七百里 - Means 'approaching the steps, he tricked the enemy into retreating seven hundred miles,' indicating a clever military maneuver.
    • 甲仗纵抛却 - Means 'armor and weapons were carelessly discarded,' indicating a lack of discipline.
    • 骑猪正南掾 - Phrase that is difficult to interpret literally. It may refer to 'riding a pig and serving as a southern official,' possibly symbolizing incompetence or absurdity.
    • 裹头极草草 - Means 'wrapping the head very carelessly,' indicating a lack of attention to detail.
    • 掠不菶菶 - Means 'sweeping and not neatly,' indicating a lack of care or precision.
    • 未见桃花面皮漫作杏子眼 - Means 'not having seen the face of peach blossoms, the skin is carelessly made into apricot eyes,' possibly symbolizing a lack of refinement or attention to appearance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 契丹 - Refers to the Khitan people, a nomadic group that established the Liao Dynasty in northern China.
    • 孙万荣 - A historical figure, likely a Khitan leader, who led raids against Chinese territories.
    • 幽河内 - Refers to the regions of You and He, which were important areas in northern China during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 赵州 - A prefecture in northern China, likely the location of a military engagement.
    • 骆务整 - A historical figure, likely a military commander, who led a force against the Chinese.
    • 荆州 - A prefecture in southern China, likely the location of the king's retreat.
    • 元一 - A historical figure, likely a court poet or scholar, who mocked Prince Yizong.
    • 懿宗 - Refers to Prince Yizong, likely a member of the Tang imperial family.
    • 蜀马 - Refers to horses from Shu, a region in southwestern China, possibly symbolizing a specific type of horse.
    • 七百里 - A measure of distance, likely used to emphasize the extent of the enemy's retreat.

    Proper Nouns

    • 契丹 - The Khitan people.
    • 孙万荣 - A historical figure, likely a Khitan leader.
    • 幽河内 - The regions of You and He.
    • 赵州 - A prefecture in northern China.
    • 骆务整 - A historical figure, likely a military commander.
    • 荆州 - A prefecture in southern China.
    • 元一 - A historical figure, likely a court poet or scholar.
    • 懿宗 - Refers to Prince Yizong, likely a member of the Tang imperial family.
    • 蜀马 - Horses from Shu, a region in southwestern China.
    • 七百里 - A measure of distance.

    A weak person once asked about the cause of a falcon's decline, and the response was that the falcon's skill had been dulled by frost, and people believed it embodied something. During the reign of the Tang Dynasty, the descendant of a Khitan leader named Wanrong launched a raid against the regions of You and He. Prince Yizong, the commander-in-chief, led his army to Zhaozhou and heard that the enemy, Lu Wu Zheng, was approaching with several thousand cavalry. Fearing the enemy's strength, Prince Yizong abandoned his weapons and armor and fled south to Jingzhou, leaving military supplies and equipment scattered along the road. When he heard that the enemy had retreated, he advanced again. Upon returning to the capital, the army held a grand banquet. At the banquet, Yuan Yi mocked Prince Yizong in front of the emperor, saying, "With a long and short measurement of an arrow and a horse from Shu, he tricked the enemy into retreating seven hundred miles, and alone he fought against the enemy, carelessly discarding his armor and weapons, riding a pig as a southern official." The emperor laughed greatly and asked Prince Yizong, "Do you have a horse, and why are you riding a pig?" Prince Yizong replied, "I am riding a pig because it is running away." The emperor laughed even more. Prince Yizong then said, "Yuan Yi had prepared this in advance and did not improvise." The emperor said, "You must match the rhyme with him." Prince Yizong said, "Please use the 'beng' rhyme." Yuan Yi responded, "Wrapping the head very carelessly, sweeping and not neatly, not having seen the face of peach blossoms, the skin is carelessly made into apricot eyes." The emperor was greatly pleased, but Prince Yizong had an extremely ashamed look, as he had a short and ugly appearance. This is why Yuan Yi said, "Long and short measurement of an arrow," referring to Prince Yizong's inconsistent and absurd actions. The emperor was delighted, and the king was deeply embarrassed. [73]

    宗妹短丑武氏最长时号大歌县主与则天并马行命元一咏曰马带桃花锦裙拖緑草罗定知纱帽底形容似大歌则天大笑县主极慙纳言娄师德长大而黒一足蹇元一目为行辙方相亦号为卫灵公言防灵柩方相也天官侍郎吉顼长大好昻头行视髙而望逺目为望栁骆驼殿中侍御史元本竦体伛身黑而且瘦目为领南考典驾部郎中朱前疑粗黑肥短身体垢腻目为光禄掌膳东方虬身长衫短骨面麤眉目为外军校尉唐波若矮短目为鬰屈蜀马目李昭德卒【子锐反】岁猢狲修文学士马吉甫眇一目为端箭师郎中长孺子视望阳目为呷醋汉泥水令苏徴举止轻薄目为失孔老鼠
    　　周张元一腹麤而脚短项缩而眼跌吉顼目为逆流虾蟆
    　　周韶州曲江令朱随侯女夫李逖游客尔朱九并姿相少媚广州人号为三樵【七肖反】人歌曰奉敕追三樵随侯傍道走回头语李郎唤取尔朱九张鷟目随侯为臛乱土袅 [74]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [74]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宗妹短丑武氏最长时号大歌县主 - Describes a short and unattractive sister of the Wu clan, who was the eldest and was called 'Dagē Xiànzhǔ' (大歌县主). '大歌' likely refers to a title or nickname.
    • 与则天并马行命元一咏曰 - Describes a situation where the sister and Wu Zetian (武则天) were riding together, and Wu Zetian ordered Yuan Yi (元一) to compose a poem.
    • 马带桃花锦裙拖緑草罗定知纱帽底形容似大歌 - A poem composed by Yuan Yi, describing the sister's appearance. '马带桃花锦裙拖緑草罗定知纱帽底形容似大歌' can be interpreted as 'The horse wears a peach blossom brocade skirt, dragging green grass, and the robe clearly shows the hat, resembling Dagē.'
    • 则天大笑县主极慙 - Wu Zetian laughed, and the sister felt extremely ashamed.
    • 纳言娄师德长大而黒一足蹇 - Describes a tall and dark official named Lou Shide (娄师德) who had a limp in one foot.
    • 元一目为行辙方相亦号为卫灵公 - Yuan Yi nicknamed Lou Shide 'Xingzhe Fangxiang' (行辙方相) and also called him 'Wei Linggong' (卫灵公). '方相' refers to a ritual figure, and '卫灵公' is a historical figure.
    • 天官侍郎吉顼长大好昻头行视髙而望逺目为望栁骆驼 - Describes a tall official named Ji Xu (吉顼) who liked to look up and far, and was nicknamed 'Wangliu Luotuo' (望栁骆驼).
    • 殿中侍御史元本竦体伛身黑而且瘦目为领南考典 - Describes a short and thin official named Yuan Ben (元本) who was nicknamed 'Lingnan Kaodian' (领南考典).
    • 驾部郎中朱前疑粗黑肥短身体垢腻目为光禄掌膳 - Describes a short and fat official named Zhu Qianyi (朱前疑) who was nicknamed 'Guanglu Zhaishan' (光禄掌膳).
    • 东方虬身长衫短骨面麤眉目为外军校尉 - Describes a tall official named Dongfang Qiu (东方虬) who was nicknamed 'Waijun Xiaowei' (外军校尉).
    • 唐波若矮短目为鬰屈蜀马目 - Describes a short official named Tang Bo'uo (唐波若) who was nicknamed 'Yuqu Shuma' (鬰屈蜀马).
    • 李昭德卒【子锐反】岁猢狲修文学士 - Describes a scholar named Li Zhaode (李昭德) who died, and was nicknamed 'Suihusun Xiuwen' (岁猢狲修文学士).
    • 马吉甫眇一目为端箭师 - Describes an official named Ma Jifu (马吉甫) who was one-eyed and was nicknamed 'Duanyanshi' (端箭师).
    • 郎中长孺子视望阳目为呷醋汉 - Describes an official named Zhang Ruzi (郎中长孺子) who was nicknamed 'Xiacu Han' (呷醋汉).
    • 泥水令苏徴举止轻薄目为失孔老鼠 - Describes an official named Su Zheng (苏徴) who was nicknamed 'Shikong Laozu' (失孔老鼠).
    • 周张元一腹麤而脚短项缩而眼跌吉顼目为逆流虾蟆 - Describes an official named Zhang Yuan Yi (张元一) who was nicknamed 'Niliu Xieha' (逆流虾蟆) by Ji Xu (吉顼).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宗妹短丑武氏 - Refers to a sister of the Wu clan, a prominent family in Chinese history.
    • 则天 - Refers to Wu Zetian (武则天), the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 纳言娄师德 - Refers to Lou Shide (娄师德), a famous official during the Tang Dynasty known for his wisdom and tolerance.
    • 天官侍郎吉顼 - Refers to Ji Xu (吉顼), a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 殿中侍御史元本 - Refers to Yuan Ben (元本), an official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 驾部郎中朱前疑 - Refers to Zhu Qianyi (朱前疑), an official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 东方虬 - Refers to Dongfang Qiu (东方虬), a scholar and official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 唐波若 - Refers to Tang Bo'uo (唐波若), an official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 李昭德 - Refers to Li Zhaode (李昭德), a scholar and official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 马吉甫 - Refers to Ma Jifu (马吉甫), an official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 郎中长孺子 - Refers to Zhang Ruzi (郎中长孺子), an official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 泥水令苏徴 - Refers to Su Zheng (苏徴), an official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 周张元一 - Refers to Zhang Yuan Yi (张元一), an official during the Tang Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 武氏 - The Wu clan, a prominent family in Chinese history.
    • 则天 - Wu Zetian (武则天), the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 元一 - Yuan Yi (元一), a scholar and poet during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 娄师德 - Lou Shide (娄师德), a famous official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 吉顼 - Ji Xu (吉顼), a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 元本 - Yuan Ben (元本), an official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 朱前疑 - Zhu Qianyi (朱前疑), an official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 东方虬 - Dongfang Qiu (东方虬), a scholar and official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 唐波若 - Tang Bo'uo (唐波若), an official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 李昭德 - Li Zhaode (李昭德), a scholar and official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 马吉甫 - Ma Jifu (马吉甫), an official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 郎中长孺子 - Zhang Ruzi (郎中长孺子), an official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 泥水令苏徴 - Su Zheng (苏徴), an official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 周张元一 - Zhang Yuan Yi (张元一), an official during the Tang Dynasty.

    The Short and Ugly Sister of the Wu Clan, the Eldest and Known as Dagē Xiànzhǔ, was riding with Wu Zetian. Wu Zetian ordered Yuan Yi to compose a poem, which read: "The horse wears a peach blossom brocade skirt, dragging green grass, and the robe clearly shows the hat, resembling Dagē." Wu Zetian laughed, and the sister felt extremely ashamed. The official Lou Shide, who was tall, dark, and had a limp in one foot, was nicknamed "Xingzhe Fangxiang" (行辙方相) and also called "Wei Linggong" (卫灵公). The official Ji Xu, who was tall and liked to look up and far, was nicknamed "Wangliu Luotuo" (望栁骆驼). The official Yuan Ben, who was short and thin, was nicknamed "Lingnan Kaodian" (领南考典). The official Zhu Qianyi, who was short and fat, was nicknamed "Guanglu Zhaishan" (光禄掌膳). The official Dongfang Qiu, who was tall, was nicknamed "Waijun Xiaowei" (外军校尉). The official Tang Bo'uo, who was short, was nicknamed "Yuqu Shuma" (鬰屈蜀马). The scholar Li Zhaode, who died, was nicknamed "Suihusun Xiuwen" (岁猢狲修文学士). The official Ma Jifu, who was one-eyed, was nicknamed "Duanyanshi" (端箭师). The official Zhang Ruzi, who liked to look up and far, was nicknamed "Xiacu Han" (呷醋汉). The official Su Zheng, who was light and thin, was nicknamed "Shikong Laozu" (失孔老鼠). The official Zhang Yuan Yi, who was short and had a small neck and eyes, was nicknamed "Niliu Xieha" (逆流虾蟆) by Ji Xu (吉顼).

    In Zhou, the magistrate of Qujiang County, Zhu Suihou, had a wife named Li Ti, who was a traveler. Erzhu Jiu was a young man with a charming appearance, and the people of Guangzhou called him "Sanqiao" (三樵). A song was sung: "Imperial decree to pursue Sanqiao, Suihou walked beside the road. He turned around and said to Li Lang, 'Call Erzhu Jiu.'" Zhang Zhuo (张鷟) called Suihou "Huanluntu Niao" (臛乱土袅). [74]

    周李详河内人气侠刚劲初为梓州监示尉主书考曰刺史问平已否详独曰不平刺史曰不平君把笔考详曰请考使君即下笔曰怯断大事好勾小稽自不清疑人总浊考中下刺史黙然而罢
    　　则天革命举人不试皆与官起家至御史评事拾遗补阙者不可胜数张鷟谓谣曰补阙连车载拾遗平斗量把推侍御史腕脱校书郎时有沈全交者傲诞自纵露才己髙巾子长布衫南院吟之续四句曰评事不读律博士不寻章面糊存抚使眯目圣神皇遂被把推御史纪先知捉向左台对仗弹劾以为谤朝政败国风请于朝堂决杖然后付法则天笑曰但使卿等不滥何虑天下人语不须与罪即宜放却先知于是乎面无色
    　　唐豫章令贺若瑾眼皮急项辕麤鷟号为饱乳犊子唐郑愔曽骂选人为痴汉选人曰仆是呉痴汉即是公愔令咏痴呉人曰榆儿复榆妇造屋兼造车十七八九夜还书复借书愔本姓鄚改姓郑时人号为鄚郑 [75]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [75]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 氣侠刚劲 - Compound term meaning 'valiant, resolute, and vigorous.'
    • 起家至御史评事拾遗补阙 - Phrase indicating rapid rise in official rank, meaning 'from a humble beginning to become a censor, a deputy censor, and a secretary who corrects omissions.'
    • 不平君把笔考 - Colloquial expression meaning 'if you think it's unfair, you can take the pen and examine it.'
    • 怯断大事好勾小稽 - Phrase criticizing someone for being timid in handling major matters but meticulous in minor details, meaning 'timid in making major decisions, yet meticulous in minor investigations.'
    • 面糊存抚使 - Derogatory term for a corrupt official, meaning 'a governor who is as insubstantial as paste.'
    • 眯目圣神皇 - Sarcastic phrase, meaning 'blinding the Holy and Divine Emperor.'
    • 傲诞自纵露才扬己 - Phrase describing a person who is arrogant, frivolous, and self-promoting, meaning 'arrogant and frivolous, openly displaying one's talents.'
    • 饱乳犊子 - Derogatory term for a young man who is pampered and spoiled, meaning 'a well-fed calf.'
    • 痴汉 - Colloquial term for a foolish or ignorant person, meaning 'foolish man.'
    • 榆儿复榆妇 - Mocking phrase, meaning 'a son of elm and a wife of elm,' suggesting a lack of intelligence or distinction.
    • 造屋兼造车 - Phrase indicating inefficiency, meaning 'building a house and making a cart at the same time.'
    • 十七八九夜 - Phrase referring to a specific time, meaning 'around the seventh, eighth, or ninth night.'
    • 还书复借书 - Phrase indicating a cycle of borrowing and returning books, meaning 'returning a book and then borrowing another one.'
    • 把推侍御史腕脱 - Phrase describing the severity of punishment, meaning 'the wrist of the censor being twisted off.'
    • 面无色 - Phrase describing a person's expression, meaning 'face pale with fear.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周李详河内人 - Refers to Li Xiang, a historical figure from Hedong (modern-day Shanxi Province).
    • 梓州监示尉 - Local official position in the Tang Dynasty, indicating a lower-ranking government post.
    • 则天革命 - Refers to the reign of Wu Zetian, who established the Zhou Dynasty and is known for her political reforms.
    • 御史评事拾遗补阙 - High-ranking official positions in the Tang Dynasty, indicating a rapid rise in the bureaucracy.
    • 张鷟 - Prominent literary figure of the Tang Dynasty, known for his works on poetry and prose.
    • 沈全交 - Historical figure known for his arrogance and self-promotion.
    • 唐豫章令贺若瑾 - Refers to He Rujin, a Tang Dynasty official from Yuzhang (modern-day Nanchang, Jiangxi Province).
    • 眼皮急项辕麤 - Derogatory description of He Rujin's appearance, meaning 'with twitching eyelids and a thick neck.'
    • 饱乳犊子 - Derogatory term for a pampered young man, often used to criticize officials who are overly privileged.
    • 唐郑愔 - Tang Dynasty official known for his wit and sarcasm.
    • 痴汉 - Colloquial term for a foolish or ignorant person, often used in a derogatory manner.
    • 鄚郑 - Pun on the surname '郑' (Zheng), which Zheng Yin changed from '鄚' (Mo) to '郑' (Zheng), leading to the nickname '鄚郑.'

    Proper Nouns

    • 周李详 - Li Xiang, a historical figure from Hedong.
    • 梓州 - Region in modern-day Sichuan Province.
    • 则天 - Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 张鷟 - Prominent literary figure of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 沈全交 - Historical figure known for his arrogance and self-promotion.
    • 唐豫章令贺若瑾 - He Rujin, a Tang Dynasty official from Yuzhang.
    • 唐郑愔 - Zheng Yin, a Tang Dynasty official known for his wit and sarcasm.
    • 鄚郑 - Nickname for Zheng Yin due to his surname change.

    Li Xiang, a man from Hedong, was valiant, resolute, and vigorous. He initially served as a minor official in Zizhou, where he was responsible for examining documents. The governor asked if the documents were fair, and Li Xiang replied that they were not. The governor said, "If you think it's unfair, you can take the pen and examine it." Li Xiang responded, "Please let me examine it, Your Excellency." The governor immediately wrote, "He is timid in making major decisions, yet meticulous in minor investigations; he is as insubstantial as paste and blinds the Holy and Divine Emperor." The examination resulted in a failing grade, and the governor remained silent and dismissed him.

    During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, candidates for official positions did not need to take exams and were directly appointed to office. Many rose from humble beginnings to become censors, deputy censors, and secretaries who corrected omissions. Zhang Zhe once remarked, "There are so many censors that they could fill carts, and so many deputy censors that they could be measured by the bushel." He further added, "The wrists of the censors are twisted off, and the hands of the secretaries are broken." At the time, there was a man named Shen Quanjiao who was arrogant, frivolous, and self-promoting. He wore a tall hat and a long robe and would recite poetry in the southern courtyard. Someone added four lines to his poem: "Deputies do not study the law, and doctors do not seek the classics. A governor as insubstantial as paste blinds the Holy and Divine Emperor." Shen Quanjiao was subsequently brought before the Left Censor, Ji Xianzhi, who accused him of slandering the court and harming the state. Ji Xianzhi demanded that he be punished by flogging in the court, but Empress Wu laughed and said, "As long as you do not abuse your power, what do I care about what others say? There is no need to punish him." Ji Xianzhi then turned pale with fear.

    In the Tang Dynasty, He Rujin, the magistrate of Yuzhang, was known for his twitching eyelids and thick neck. Zhang Zhe called him "a well-fed calf." Once, Zheng Yin, a Tang official, insulted a candidate for office, calling him a "foolish man." The candidate replied, "I am a foolish man from Wu, and you are a foolish man from Wu." Zheng Yin then asked him to compose a poem about a "foolish Wu man," and he wrote: "A son of elm and a wife of elm, building a house and making a cart at the same time. Around the seventh, eighth, or ninth night, returning a book and then borrowing another one." Zheng Yin originally had the surname Mo, but he changed it to Zheng, and people began to call him "Mo Zheng." [75]

    唐中书令李敬为元帅讨吐蕃至树墩城闻刘尚书没蕃着鞾不得狼狈而走时将军王杲副总管曹懐舜等惊退遗却麦饭及首尾千里地上尺余时军中謡曰姚河李阿婆鄯州王伯母见贼不能闘总由曹新妇
    　　唐礼部尚书祝钦明颇涉经史不关时务博硕肥腯顽滞多疑台中小吏号之为媪媪者肉块无七窍秦穆公时野人得之
    　　唐先天中姜师度于长安城中穿渠绕朝堂坊市无所不至上登西楼望之师度堰水陇柴栰而下遂授司农卿于后水涨则奔突水缩则竭涸又前开黄河引水向棣州费亿兆功百姓苦其淹渍又役夫塞河开元六年水泛溢河口堰破棣州百姓一槩没尽师度以为功官品益进又有傅孝忠为太史令自言明象専行矫谲京中语曰姜师度一心看地傅孝忠两眼相天神武即位知其矫并斩之
    　　唐姜晦为吏部侍郎眼不识字手不解书滥掌铨衡曽无分别选人歌曰今年选数恰相当都由座主无文章案后一腔冻猪肉所以名为姜侍郎 [76]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [76]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元帅 - Field marshal; military leader.
    • 讨 - To campaign against; to attack.
    • 没蕃 - Captured by the Tibetans.
    • 着鞾不得 - Unable to wear his boots.
    • 惊退 - Fled in panic.
    • 遗却 - Left behind.
    • 謡 - Ballad or song.
    • 阿婆 - Granny; an affectionate term for an elderly woman.
    • 伯母 - Aunt; a respectful term for an elder woman.
    • 颇涉 - Somewhat knowledgeable.
    • 不关时务 - Not concerned with current affairs.
    • 博硕肥腯 - Broad and fat; describes a person's physical appearance.
    • 顽滞多疑 - Stubborn and suspicious.
    • 媪媪者肉块无七窍 - A meat block with no seven orifices; a derogatory term.
    • 穿渠绕朝堂坊市 - Dug canals around the imperial court and markets.
    • 堰水陇柴栰而下 - Dammed water with logs and planks.
    • 泛溢 - Overflow.
    • 専行矫谲 - Practiced deceit and trickery.
    • 两眼相天 - Looked at the sky with two eyes; a metaphorical expression.
    • 眼不识字手不解书 - Unable to read or write.
    • 滥掌铨衡 - Mismanaged the selection process.
    • 冻猪肉 - Frozen pork; a derogatory term.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐中书令李敬 - Tang Dynasty official Li Jingxuan.
    • 吐蕃 - Tibetan Empire.
    • 树墩城 - Shudun City.
    • 刘尚书 - Official Liu.
    • 王杲 - General Wang.
    • 曹懐舜 - Vice Commander Cao.
    • 姚河 - Yaohe.
    • 鄯州 - Shanzhou.
    • 祝钦明 - Zhu Qinming.
    • 秦穆公 - King Mu of Qin.
    • 姜师度 - Jiang Shidu.
    • 开元六年 - The sixth year of the Kaiyuan era.
    • 傅孝忠 - Fu Xiaozhong.
    • 神武 - Shenwu.
    • 姜晦 - Jiang Hui.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐中书令李敬 - Tang Dynasty official Li Jingxuan.
    • 吐蕃 - Tibetan Empire.
    • 树墩城 - Shudun City.
    • 刘尚书 - Official Liu.
    • 王杲 - General Wang.
    • 曹懐舜 - Vice Commander Cao.
    • 姚河 - Yaohe.
    • 鄯州 - Shanzhou.
    • 祝钦明 - Zhu Qinming.
    • 秦穆公 - King Mu of Qin.
    • 姜师度 - Jiang Shidu.
    • 傅孝忠 - Fu Xiaozhong.
    • 神武 - Shenwu.
    • 姜晦 - Jiang Hui.

    During the Tang Dynasty, Li Jingxuan, the Chief Secretary of the Secretariat, served as the field marshal and campaigned against the Tibetans, reaching Shudun City. Upon hearing that Official Liu had been captured by the Tibetans and was unable to wear his boots, he fled in a panic. At that time, General Wang Gao and Vice Commander Cao Huishun, among others, also fled in panic, leaving behind their meal and leaving a trace on the ground for a thousand miles. At that time, a folk song circulated among the troops: "Yaohe Granny, Shanzhou Aunt, see the enemy but cannot fight; all due to Cao's new wife."

    Zhu Qinming, the Minister of the Ministry of Rites, was somewhat knowledgeable in the classics and histories but not concerned with current affairs. He was broad and fat, stubborn and suspicious. The small clerks in the office called him "Old Lady," meaning a meat block with no seven orifices, a reference to a story from the time of King Mu of Qin when a commoner found such a creature.

    During the Tianbao era, Jiang Shidu dug canals around the imperial court and markets in Chang'an, extending them to the highest point, where he climbed the Western Tower to look down. Jiang built a dam with logs and planks, and later was appointed as the Minister of Agriculture. Afterward, when the water rose, it surged and overflowed, and when it receded, it dried up. He also opened the Yellow River to divert water to Dezhou, costing millions of laborers, and the people suffered from flooding. He then ordered the laborers to block the river. In the sixth year of the Kaiyuan era, the water overflowed at the river mouth, breaking the dam and submerging Dezhou, causing all the people to drown. Jiang considered this a great achievement and his official rank was promoted. Another official, Fu Xiaozhong, served as the Grand Historian and claimed to be skilled in astronomy, practicing deceit and trickery. The people of the capital said, "Jiang Shidu looks at the ground with one eye, while Fu Xiaozhong looks at the sky with two eyes." When Emperor Shenwu ascended the throne, he discovered their deceit and had them both executed.

    Jiang Hui served as the Minister of the Ministry of Personnel, unable to read or write, and mismanaged the selection process, showing no discrimination. The selected officials sang, "This year's selection is just right; all due to the chief examiner's lack of talent. Behind the case, a lump of frozen pork." Hence, he was called Jiang the Secretary. [76]

    唐兵部尚书姚元崇长大行急魏光乗目为赶蛇鹳鹊黄门侍郎卢懐慎好视地目为观鼠猫儿殿中监姜皎肥而黒目为饱椹母猪紫微舍人倪若水黒而无须目为醉部落精舍人齐处冲好眇目视目为暗烛防覔虱老母舍人吕延嗣长大少发目为日本国使人又有舍人郑勉为醉髙丽目拾遗蔡孚小州医博士诈谙药性又有殿中侍御史短而丑黒目为烟熏地木目御史张孝嵩为小村方相目舍人杨伸嗣为熟上猢狲目补阙辉为王门下弹琴博士目员外郎魏恬为祈雨婆罗门目李全交为品官给使目黄门侍郎李广为饱水虾蟇由是坐此品题朝士自左拾遗贬新州新与县尉
    　　唐贞观中桂杨令阮嵩妻阎氏极妬嵩在防防客饮召女奴歌阎披发跣足袒臂拔刀至席诸客惊散嵩伏牀下女奴狼狈而奔刺史崔邈为嵩作考词云妇强夫弱内刚外柔一妻不能禁止百姓如何整肃妻既礼教不修夫又精神何在考下省符解见任 [77]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [77]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 长大行急 - Describes a person who is tall and quick-tempered.
    • 目为赶蛇鹳鹊 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 好视地 - Describes a person who looks down frequently.
    • 目为观鼠猫儿 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 肥而黑 - Describes a person who is fat and dark-skinned.
    • 目为饱椹母猪 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 黑而无须 - Describes a person who is dark-skinned and has no beard.
    • 目为醉部落精 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 好眇目视 - Describes a person who looks with a squinting eye.
    • 目为暗烛防觅虱 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 长大少发 - Describes a person who is tall and has sparse hair.
    • 目为日本国使人 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 诈谙药性 - Describes a person who pretends to be knowledgeable about medicine.
    • 短而丑黑 - Describes a person who is short, ugly, and dark-skinned.
    • 目为烟熏地木 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为小村方相 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 熟上猢狲 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为王门下弹琴博士 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为祈雨婆罗门 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为小村方相 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为熟上猢狲 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为王门下弹琴博士 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为祈雨婆罗门 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为小村方相 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为熟上猢狲 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为王门下弹琴博士 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为祈雨婆罗门 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为小村方相 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为熟上猢狲 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为王门下弹琴博士 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.
    • 为祈雨婆罗门 - Metaphorical expression used to describe someone's appearance or behavior.

    Proper Nouns

    • 姚元崇 - Yao Yuanzong, a prominent statesman of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 魏光乘 - Wei Guangcheng, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 卢怀慎 - Lu Huishen, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 姜皎 - Jiang Jiao, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 倪若水 - Ni Ruoshui, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 齐处冲 - Qi Chuchong, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 吕延嗣 - Lü Yanshi, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 郑勉 - Zheng Min, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 蔡孚 - Cai Fu, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 杨伸嗣 - Yang Shenshi, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 辉 - Xing Hui, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 魏恬 - Wei Tian, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 李全交 - Li Quan Jiao, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 李广 - Li Guang, a contemporary of Yao Yuanzong.
    • 阮嵩 - Yuan Song, a local official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 阎氏 - Yan Shi, the wife of Yuan Song.
    • 崔邈 - Cui Miao, a local official in the Tang Dynasty.

    During the Tang Dynasty, the Minister of War Yao Yuanzong was tall and quick-tempered. Wei Guangcheng called him "a swift heron and crane chasing snakes." Lu Huishen, the Grand Secretary of the Yellow Gate, often looked down. Wei Guangcheng referred to him as "a cat watching mice." Jiang Jiao, the Grand Master of the Imperial Court, was fat and dark-skinned. Wei Guangcheng called him "a pig well-fed on mulberries." Ni Ruoshui, the Palace Attendant, was dark-skinned and had no beard. Wei Guangcheng referred to him as "a drunken tribal leader." Qi Chuchong, another Palace Attendant, had a squinting eye. Wei Guangcheng called him "a dim candle searching for lice." Lü Yanshi, the Palace Attendant, was tall with sparse hair. Wei Guangcheng referred to him as "an envoy from Japan." Zheng Min, another Palace Attendant, was called "a drunken Korean" by Wei Guangcheng. Cai Fu, the Imperial Historian, was a medical doctor in a small state who pretended to be knowledgeable about medicine. Zhang Xiaosong, the Imperial Censor, was short, ugly, and dark-skinned. Wei Guangcheng called him "a piece of smoky wood." Yang Shenshi, the Palace Attendant, was referred to as "a monkey on a high branch" by Wei Guangcheng. Xing Hui, the Imperial Historian, was called "a court musician" by Wei Guangcheng. Wei Tian, the Vice Minister, was referred to as "a Brahmin praying for rain" by Wei Guangcheng. Li Guang, the Palace Attendant, was called "a water-filled frog" by Wei Guangcheng. Because of these descriptions, these officials were criticized and ridiculed for their appearance or behavior. They were demoted from their positions as Imperial Historians to the new state of Xinzhou and became county magistrates.

    During the reign of Emperor Taizong in the Tang Dynasty, Yuan Song, the magistrate of Guiyang, had a wife named Yan Shi who was extremely jealous. When Yuan Song was drinking with guests, he summoned a female servant to sing. Yan Shi arrived at the scene with her hair loose, barefoot, and holding a knife. The guests were shocked and scattered, and Yuan Song hid under the bed. The female servant ran away in a panic. Cui Miao, the governor, wrote an evaluation of Yuan Song, stating that the wife was strong and the husband was weak, with an inner strength but an outward gentleness. He questioned how a single wife could control the people and how the government could maintain order. The evaluation was submitted to the central government for review and decision. [77]

    唐郝象贤侍郎处俊之孙顿丘令南容之子也弱冠诸友生为之字曰宠之每于父前称字父绐之曰汝朋友极贤吾为汝设馔可命之也翼日象贤因邀致十数人南容引生与之饮谓曰谚云三公后出死狗小儿诚愚劳诸君制字损南容之身尚可岂可波及侍中也因涕泣众慙而退宠之者反语为痴种也
    　　朱前疑浅钝无识容貌极丑上书云臣梦见陛下八百歳即授拾遗俄迁郎中出使回又上书云闻嵩山唱万嵗声即赐绯鱼袋未入五品于緑衫上带之朝野莫不怪笑后契丹反有敕京官出马一匹供军者即酧五品前疑买马纳讫表索绯上怒批其状即放归丘园愤恚而卒
    　　唐王及善才行庸猥风神钝浊为内史时人号为鸠集凤池俄迁文昌右相无他政但不许令史双驴入台终日迫逐无时蹔舎时人号为驱驴宰相
    　　周有逯仁杰河阳人自地官令史出尚书改天下帐式颇甚繁细法令滋章每邨立社官仍置平甚老三员掌簿案设鏁钥十羊九牧人皆逃散而宰相浅识以为万代可行授仁杰地官郎中数年百姓苦之其法遂寝 [78]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [78]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 弱冠 - Refers to a man who is 20 years old, having just undergone the capping ceremony, which marks the transition to adulthood.
    • 字曰 - Means 'to be given a courtesy name,' a practice in ancient China where a person was given a second name at adulthood.
    • 詐之 - Means 'to deceive,' but in this context, it implies a playful or gentle deception rather than malice.
    • 誠愚勞諸君制字損南容之身尚可豈可波及侍中也 - Translates roughly to 'Indeed, it is foolish to trouble you all with naming, which harms my son's dignity, but how much more so to affect the dignity of a chancellor?' The phrase '誠愚' (indeed foolish) and '損南容之身' (harm my son's dignity) are key idiomatic expressions.
    • 反語 - Means 'reversal of words,' often used to create a pun or a play on words.
    • 酧五品 - Means 'to be granted the rank of fifth grade official,' which is a formal recognition of service.
    • 緑衫 - Refers to the official uniform worn by lower-ranking officials.
    • 驅驢宰相 - A derogatory term meaning 'a chancellor who drives donkeys,' implying incompetence or triviality in a high position.
    • 波及 - Means 'to affect' or 'to extend to,' often used in a negative sense.

    Translation of the Source Text

    Part 1:

    唐郝象贤侍郎处俊之孙顿丘令南容之子也弱冠诸友生为之字曰宠之每于父前称字父绐之曰汝朋友极贤吾为汝设馔可命之也翼日象贤因邀致十数人南容引生与之饮谓曰谚云三公后出死狗小儿诚愚劳诸君制字损南容之身尚可岂可波及侍中也因涕泣众慙而退宠之者反语为痴种也

    Translation:
    Hao Xiangxian, a Langya of the Tang Dynasty, was the grandson of Cujun, a Langya, and the son of Nanrong, the governor of Duntou. When he was twenty (weak crown), his friends gave him the courtesy name "Chongzhi." Whenever he addressed his father by this name, his father would deceive him, saying, "Your friends are extremely virtuous; I will prepare a feast for you and invite them." The next day, Hao Xiangxian invited about a dozen people. Nanrong led them to a banquet and said, "There is a saying: 'After the Three Dukes, there come dead dog children.' Indeed, it is foolish to trouble you all with naming, which harms my son's dignity, but how much more so to affect the dignity of a chancellor?" He then wept, and the guests felt ashamed and left. "Chongzhi" is a reversal of words meaning "foolish offspring."

    Part 2:

    朱前疑浅钝无识容貌极丑上书云臣梦见陛下八百歳即授拾遗俄迁郎中出使回又上书云闻嵩山唱万嵗声即赐绯鱼袋未入五品于緑衫上带之朝野莫不怪笑后契丹反有敕京官出马一匹供军者即酧五品前疑买马纳讫表索绯上怒批其状即放归丘园愤恚而卒

    Translation:
    Zhu Qianyi was dull-witted and ignorant, with an extremely ugly appearance. He wrote to the emperor, saying, "I dreamed that Your Majesty would live for eight hundred years and would appoint me as a Pick-up (a low-ranking official). Soon after, I was promoted to Langzhong (a mid-ranking official) and returned from a mission. I wrote again, saying, 'I heard that Mount Song is singing 'Long Live the Emperor,' and I was granted a red fish bag (a symbol of higher rank), though I was not yet a fifth-grade official, and wore it on my green official robe.' The court and the common people found this absurd and laughed. Later, when the Khitans rebelled, the emperor decreed that officials who provided a horse for the army would be granted the rank of fifth grade. Zhu Qianyi bought a horse and submitted a report to claim the red fish bag. The emperor became angry and dismissed him, sending him back to his estate, where he died in frustration.

    Part 3:

    唐王及善才行庸猥风神钝浊为内史时人号为鸠集凤池俄迁文昌右相无他政但不许令史双驴入台终日迫逐无时蹔舎时人号为驱驴宰相

    Translation:
    Wang Jishan of the Tang Dynasty was mediocre in talent and appearance, with a dull and turbid demeanor. When he was an Inner Historian, people called him "Gathering of Crows in the Phoenix Pool." Soon, he was promoted to the Right Chancellor of the Wenchang Office. He had no other policies but prohibited officials from bringing two donkeys into the office, forcing them to chase the donkeys all day without rest. People called him the "Donkey-Chasing Chancellor."

    Part 4:

    周有逯仁杰河阳人自地官令史出尚书改天下帐式颇甚繁细法令滋章每邨立社官仍置平甚老三员掌簿案设鏁钥十羊九牧人皆逃散而宰相浅识以为万代可行授仁杰地官郎中数年百姓苦之其法遂寝

    Translation:
    During the Zhou Dynasty, there was Lu Renjie of Heyang, who started as a clerk in the Earth Official Office and later became a minister, changing the national accounting system. The new system was very complex and detailed, with laws becoming increasingly elaborate. Each village had to set up a local office with three very old officials to manage records and lockboxes. Ten sheep were guarded by nine shepherds, and people fled in droves. The chancellor, with shallow understanding, believed this system could last for generations and appointed Lu Renjie as an Earth Official Langzhong. After several years, the people suffered greatly, and the system was eventually abandoned. [78]

    周考功令史袁琰国忌众人聚防充録事勾当遂判曰曹司繁閙无时蹔闻不因国忌之辰无以展其欢笑合坐嗤之
    　　周夏官侍郎侯知一年老敕放致仕上表不伏于朝堂踊跃驰走以示轻便张悰丁忧自请起复吏部主事高筠母丧亲戚为举哀筠曰我不能作孝员外郎张栖贞被讼诈遭母忧不肯起对时台中为之语曰侯知一不伏致仕张琮自请起复高筠不肯作孝张栖贞情愿遭忧皆非名教中人并是王化外物兽心人靣不其然乎
    　　周天官选人沈子荣诵判二百道试日不下笔人问之荣曰无非命也今日诵判无一相当有一道颇同人名又别至来年选判水硙又不下笔人问之曰我诵水硙乃是蓝田今问富平如何下笔闻者莫不抚掌焉
    　　周则天内宴甚乐河内王懿宗忽然起奏曰臣告君子告父则天大惊引问之对曰臣封物承前府家自征近敕州县征送太有损折则天大怒仰观屋椽良久曰朕诸亲饮正乐汝是亲王为三二百户封防惊杀我不堪作王令曳下懿宗免冠拜伏诸王救之曰懿宗愚钝无意之失上乃释之 [79]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [79]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 周考功令史袁琰國忌眾人聚防充録事勾當遂判曰 - Describes a clerk named Yuan Yan, during a national mourning day, was tasked with managing a gathering and subsequently made a judgment. '國忌' refers to a day of national mourning, and '聚防充録事勾當' suggests organizing and recording the event.
    • 曹司繁閙無時蹔聞不因國忌之辰無以展其歡笑 - Indicates that the office was usually busy, and without the distraction of a national mourning day, people could not express their joy. '無時蹔聞' means 'always noisy,' and '無以展其歡笑' means 'unable to show their joy.'
    • 合坐嗤之 - Means that everyone present laughed at the situation.
    • 周夏官侍郎侯知一年老敕放致仕上表不伏於朝堂躍躍驅走以示輕便 - Describes a scenario where a high-ranking official, Hou Zhi, was ordered to retire due to old age but refused and showed off his agility in the court. '躍躍驅走' means 'jumping and running around.'
    • 張悰丁憂自請起復吏部主事高筠母喪親戚為舉哀筠曰我不能作孝員外郎 - Describes Zhang Cong, who requested to resume his duties after a period of mourning for his mother, while Gao Yun, whose mother had died, refused to perform the mourning rituals. '丁憂' means 'mourning for a parent,' and '起復' means 'resume duties.'
    • 張栖贞被讼诈遭母忧不肯起对时台中为之语曰侯知一不伏致仕张琮自请起复高筠不肯作孝张栖贞情愿遭忧皆非名教中人并是王化外物兽心人面不其然乎 - Describes Zhang Qi Zhen, who was accused of falsely claiming a mother's death to avoid work, and the court's reaction. '被讼' means 'accused,' and '情愿遭忧' means 'willingly endure mourning.'
    • 周天官选人沈子荣诵判二百道试日不下笔人问之荣曰无非命也今日诵判无一相当有一道颇同人名又别至来年选判水硙又不下笔人问之曰我诵水硙乃是蓝田今问富平如何下笔闻者莫不抚掌焉 - Describes Shen Zi Rong, who recited 200 judgments but failed to write them on the day of the exam. He explained that it was not his fate, and the next year, when asked about watermills, he again failed to write. '诵判' means 'recite judgments,' and '水硙' means 'watermill.'
    • 周则天内宴甚乐河内王懿宗忽然起奏曰臣告君子告父则天大惊引问之对曰臣封物承前府家自征近敕州县征送太有损折则天大怒仰观屋椽良久曰朕诸亲饮正乐汝是亲王为三二百户封防惊杀我不堪作王令曳下懿宗免冠拜伏诸王救之曰懿宗愚钝无意之失上乃释之 - Describes a scenario where Emperor Wu of Zhou, during an inner banquet, was surprised by a sudden report from Prince Yi Zong, who explained that he was ordered to collect taxes and it caused significant damage. '内宴' means 'inner banquet,' and '封物承前府家自征' means 'collecting taxes from the previous household.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周考功令史 - Refers to a clerk in the Ministry of Personnel during the Zhou Dynasty.
    • 國忌 - A day of national mourning, typically observed for the death of a ruler or a significant figure.
    • 周夏官侍郎 - A high-ranking official in the Ministry of War during the Zhou Dynasty.
    • 丁憂 - A traditional period of mourning for a parent, during which one is expected to refrain from official duties.
    • 起復 - Resuming official duties after a period of mourning.
    • 周天官选人 - A candidate for a position in the Ministry of Personnel during the Zhou Dynasty.
    • 周则天 - Refers to Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history, who ruled during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 内宴 - An inner banquet, typically held in the imperial palace.
    • 封物承前府家自征 - Collecting taxes from the previous household, indicating a change in administrative practices.

    Proper Nouns

    • 袁琰 - Yuan Yan, a clerk in the Ministry of Personnel.
    • 侯知一 - Hou Zhi, a high-ranking official in the Ministry of War.
    • 张悰 - Zhang Cong, a high-ranking official in the Ministry of Personnel.
    • 高筠 - Gao Yun, a high-ranking official in the Ministry of Personnel.
    • 张栖贞 - Zhang Qi Zhen, a high-ranking official in the Ministry of Personnel.

    During a national mourning day, Yuan Yan, a clerk in the Ministry of Personnel, was tasked with managing a gathering and subsequently made a judgment, stating, "The office is always noisy; without the distraction of a national mourning day, people could not express their joy." Everyone present laughed at the situation.

    In the Ministry of War, Hou Zhi, a high-ranking official, was ordered to retire due to old age but refused and showed off his agility in the court. Zhang Cong, who was mourning for his mother, requested to resume his duties, while Gao Yun, whose mother had died, refused to perform the mourning rituals and said, "I cannot act as a filial official." Zhang Qi Zhen was accused of falsely claiming a mother's death to avoid work, and the court said, "Hou Zhi refused to retire, Zhang Cong requested to resume duties, Gao Yun refused to perform the mourning rituals, and Zhang Qi Zhen willingly endured mourning. All of them are not people of the moral code and are outside the realm of civilization, with hearts of beasts and faces of humans."

    Shen Zi Rong, a candidate for a position in the Ministry of Personnel, recited 200 judgments but failed to write them on the day of the exam. When asked, he said, "It is not my fate." Today, he recited judgments but none matched. There was one that was somewhat similar to a person's name. The next year, when asked about watermills, he again failed to write. When asked, he said, "I recited watermills in Lantian, but now they are asking about Fufeng. How can I write?" Those who heard this could not help but laugh.

    During an inner banquet, Emperor Wu was very happy, but Prince Yi Zong suddenly stood up and reported, "I have a report to make." Wu was surprised and asked, "What is it?" Yi Zong replied, "I was ordered to collect taxes from the previous household, and recently, the state and counties were ordered to send them, causing significant damage." Wu was angry and looked at the rafters for a long time, saying, "My relatives are drinking and enjoying themselves, but you, as a prince, are responsible for collecting taxes for three or two hundred households. This is a shock. I cannot bear to be a king. I order you to be dragged out." Yi Zong removed his hat and knelt down. The other princes saved him, saying, "Yi Zong is dull and made an unintentional mistake." Wu then released him. [79]

    周张衡令史出身位至四品加一阶合入三品已团甲因退朝路旁见蒸餠新熟遂市其一马上食之被御史弹奏则天降敕流外出身不许入三品遂落甲
    　　周右拾遗李良弼自矜唇颊好谈理请使北蕃说骨笃禄匈奴以木盘盛粪饲之临以白刄弼惧食一盘并尽乃放还人讥之曰李拾遗能拾突厥之遗出为真源令秩满还瀛州遇契丹之孙万荣使何阿小取沧瀛粪具良弼谓鹿城令李怀璧曰孙者胡孙即是狝猴难可当也万字者有草即是草中藏劝怀璧降何阿小授怀璧五品将军阿小败怀璧及良弼父子四人并为河内王武懿宗斩之
