    　　周春官尚书阎知微庸琐驽怯使入蕃受黙啜封为汉可汗贼入怕定遣知防先往赵州招慰将军陈令英等守城西面知微谓令英曰陈将军何不早降下可汗兵到然后降者剪土无遗令英不荅知微城下连手歌称万嵗乐令英曰尚书国家八座受委非轻翻为敌踏歌无惭也知防乃唱曰万歳乐万歳年不自由万嵗乐时人鄙之 [80]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [80]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 位至四品 - Reaching the fourth rank in the official hierarchy.
    • 加一阶合入三品 - Adding one rank would bring him into the third rank.
    • 被御史弹奏 - Was impeached by the censor.
    • 流外出身不许入三品 - Was demoted to an outer office and barred from entering the third rank.
    • 落甲 - A term related to the imperial examination system, possibly referring to the cancellation of examination eligibility or a failure in the examination process.
    • 自矜唇颊好谈玄理 - Boasting about his eloquence and love for discussing metaphysical principles.
    • 请使北蕃说骨笃禄匈奴 - Requested to be sent to the northern frontier to persuade the Khitan tribe.
    • 以木盘盛粪饲之 - Fed him with a wooden plate full of excrement.
    • 临以白刃 - Threatened him with a white blade.
    • 陈令英等守城西面 - Chen Lingying and others were guarding the western part of the city.
    • 剪土无遗 - Will not leave a single inch of land.
    • 拾遗 - A low-ranking official position in the Tang dynasty, often used as a stepping stone for future advancement.
    • 授怀璧五品将军 - Was granted the title of Fifth-Rank General.
    • 武懿宗 - A prominent figure in the Tang dynasty, known for his role in suppressing rebellions.
    • 万岁乐 - A term that could refer to a type of music or a song, but in this context, it seems to be a sarcastic reference to the act of singing 'long live the emperor.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周张衡 - Zhang Heng, a famous astronomer, mathematician, and inventor of the seismoscope during the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 令史出身 - Refers to a low-ranking official position, often the starting point for a bureaucratic career.
    • 周右拾遗李良弼 - Li Liangbi, a low-ranking official known for his eloquence and philosophical discussions.
    • 骨笃禄匈奴 - Refers to the Khitan tribe, a nomadic group that posed a threat to the Tang dynasty.
    • 孙万荣 - A leader of a rebellion against the Tang dynasty.
    • 何阿小 - A subordinate of Sun Wanyong, who led a rebellion.
    • 鹿城令李怀璧 - Li Huaibi, a local official in Luocheng.
    • 真源令 - A county magistrate, a lower-ranking official.
    • 瀛州 - A prefecture in the Tang dynasty, located in present-day Hebei province.
    • 契丹 - A nomadic people who later founded the Liao dynasty.
    • 河内王武懿宗 - A high-ranking official and military leader in the Tang dynasty, known for his harsh methods in suppressing rebellions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张衡 - Zhang Heng, a famous astronomer, mathematician, and inventor.
    • 李良弼 - Li Liangbi, a low-ranking official known for his eloquence and philosophical discussions.
    • 骨笃禄匈奴 - The Khitan tribe, a nomadic group that posed a threat to the Tang dynasty.
    • 孙万荣 - A leader of a rebellion against the Tang dynasty.
    • 何阿小 - A subordinate of Sun Wanyong, who led a rebellion.
    • 李怀璧 - Li Huaibi, a local official in Luocheng.
    • 武懿宗 - A high-ranking official and military leader in the Tang dynasty.

    Zhang Heng, who began his career as a low-ranking official, reached the fourth rank and, with an additional rank, would have entered the third rank. However, after dismissing court, he saw freshly steamed buns by the roadside and bought one to eat on horseback. He was subsequently impeached by the censor. Empress Wu Zetian issued an edict demoting him to an outer office and barring him from entering the third rank, thus he lost his examination eligibility.

    Li Liangbi, a Right Remonstrant of the Zhou dynasty, boasted about his eloquence and love for discussing metaphysical principles. He requested to be sent to the northern frontier to persuade the Khitan tribe. They fed him with a wooden plate full of excrement and threatened him with a white blade. Li Liangbi, fearing for his life, ate the entire plate of excrement and was then released. People mocked him, saying, "Li Remonstrant can pick up the leftovers of the Turks." He was later appointed as the magistrate of Zhenyuan County. After completing his term, he returned to Yingzhou, where he encountered Sun Wanyong, a leader of a rebellion, and his subordinate, He Aoxiao, who were gathering supplies from Cang and Ying. Li Liangbi told Li Huaibi, the magistrate of Luocheng, "Sun is a monkey, and monkeys are hard to deal with. The character '万' with grass in it means 'hidden in the grass.' I advise you to surrender." He Aoxiao granted Li Huaibi the title of Fifth-Rank General. When He Aoxiao was defeated, Li Huaibi and Li Liangbi, along with their four sons, were all executed by Hei Wuyi, the Duke of Heinei.

    Yan Zhiwei, a Spring Official and Grand Secretary of the Zhou dynasty, was an incompetent and timid official. He was sent to the frontier and received the title of Han Khan by the Khitan leader. When the Khitans invaded, he was sent ahead to Zhaozhou to persuade the locals. The general Chen Lingying and others were guarding the western part of the city. Yan Zhiwei told Chen Lingying, "Why didn't you surrender earlier? Once the Khan's troops arrive, those who do not surrender will not leave a single inch of land." Chen Lingying did not respond. Yan Zhiwei sang "Long Live the Emperor" below the city walls. Chen Lingying replied, "As a high-ranking official in the state, you have been entrusted with important duties. To sing for the enemy is shameful." Yan Zhiwei then sang, "Long Live the Emperor, Long Live the Year, Not Free." People at the time looked down on him for this. [80]

    唐崔湜为吏部侍郎贪纵兄凭弟力父挟子威咸受嘱求汚狼籍父挹为司业受选人钱湜不知之也长名放之其人诉曰公亲将赂去何为不与官湜曰所亲为谁吾捉取鞭杀曰鞭即遭忧湜大慙主上以湜父年老初熟赐一颗湜以瓜遗妾不及其父朝野讥之时崔岑郑愔并为吏部京中謡曰岑羲獠子俊崔湜令公孙三人相比接莫贺咄最浑
    　　唐左卫将军权龙襄性褊常自矜能诗通天年中为沧州刺史初到乃为诗呈州官曰遥看沧州城杨栁郁青青中央一羣汉聚坐打杯觥诸公谢曰公有逸才襄曰不敢趁韵而已又秋日述怀曰檐前飞七百雪白后园彊饱食房里侧家粪集野蜋参军不晓请释襄曰鹞子檐前飞直七百文洗衫挂笈园干白如雪饱食房中侧卧家粪便转集得野泽蜋谈者嗤之皇太子宴夏日赋诗严霜白浩浩明月赤团团太子援笔为讃曰龙襄才子秦州人士明月昼耀严霜夏起如此诗章趁韵而已襄以张易之事出为容山府折冲神龙中追入乃上诗曰无事向容山今日向东都陛下敕 [81]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [81]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 兄凭弟力父挟子威咸受嘱求汚狼籍 - Complex phrase describing widespread corruption involving family members.
    • 鞭即遭忧 - Indicates that Cui Shi faced a period of mourning due to his actions.
    • 崔岑郑愔并为吏部京中謡曰 - Indicates that Cui Shi, Cui Cen, and Zheng Yin were all serving in the Ministry of Personnel, and there was a popular song about them.
    • 岑羲獠子俊崔湜令公孙三人相比接莫贺咄最浑 - Cryptic phrase that needs careful interpretation, possibly referring to a comparison of their characters or actions.
    • 檐前飞七百雪白后园彊饱食房里侧家粪集野蜋 - Poem with symbolic meanings, where '七百' could be a play on words or a metaphor.
    • 无事向容山今日向东都陛下敕 - Poem written after being recalled from exile, showing his response to the emperor's order.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐 - The Tang Dynasty, a period of significant cultural and literary development.
    • 崔湜 - A prominent official during the Tang Dynasty known for his corruption.
    • 吏部 - The Ministry of Personnel, responsible for civil service appointments.
    • 通天年中 - A specific year during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 沧州 - A prefecture in modern-day Hebei Province.
    • 严霜白浩浩明月赤团团 - A poetic description that may have deeper symbolic meanings.
    • 皇太子 - The crown prince, indicating the high status of the person being addressed.
    • 神龙中 - A specific year during the reign of Emperor Ruizong of Tang.
    • 容山府 - A specific location where Quan Longxiang was exiled.
    • 张易之 - A powerful official during the Tang Dynasty, whose fall led to Quan Longxiang's recall.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐 - Tang Dynasty.
    • 崔湜 - Cui Shi.
    • 吏部 - Ministry of Personnel.
    • 通天年中 - Specific year during Emperor Zhongzong's reign.
    • 沧州 - Cangzhou Prefecture.
    • 严霜白浩浩明月赤团团 - Poetic description.
    • 皇太子 - Crown Prince.
    • 神龙中 - Specific year during Emperor Ruizong's reign.
    • 容山府 - Location of Quan Longxiang's exile.
    • 张易之 - Powerful official during the Tang Dynasty.

    During the Tang Dynasty, Cui Shi served as the Minister of Personnel and was notorious for his corruption and arrogance. He and his family members, including his brother, father, and son, were all involved in accepting bribes and engaging in various corrupt practices, leading to a state of chaos and disorder. His father, Cui Yi, was the Director of the Imperial Academy and accepted money from candidates for admission, which Cui Shi was unaware of. When a candidate was admitted, they complained, saying, "Why didn't you give me an official position?" Cui Shi replied, "Who is your relative? I will catch and whip him to death." However, he was immediately summoned for mourning duties, which greatly embarrassed him. The emperor, seeing that Cui Shi's father was old, granted him a special favor by giving him a ripe melon. Cui Shi gave the melon to his concubine but not to his father, which was widely criticized by the court and the public. At the time, Cui Cen and Zheng Yin were also serving in the Ministry of Personnel, and there was a popular song in the capital that said, "Cen Xi, the son of a barbarian, is a fine man; Cui Shi, the grandson of a noble, is a fine man. The three of them are closely related, but none of them are as good as Mo He Du, who is the most refined."

    In the Tang Dynasty, General Quan Longxiang of the Left Guard was known for his narrow-mindedness and often boasted about his poetry. During the Tongtian period, he became the governor of Cangzhou and, upon his arrival, presented a poem to the local officials: "From afar, the city of Cangzhou looks green with willows; in the center, a group of men sit together drinking wine." The officials praised him, saying, "You have a rare talent." Quan Longxiang replied, "I dare not say so; I just followed the rhyme." He then composed another poem about his autumn thoughts: "Seven hundred birds fly by the eaves, white as snow; in the backyard, I eat heartily; in the room, I lie down on my side, and the excrement gathers around me; in the wild, I catch a cicada." The officials did not understand the poem and asked for an explanation. Quan Longxiang said, "The birds flying by the eaves are hawks, worth seven hundred coins; the white object is a shirt hung on the garden pole, as white as snow; after eating, I lie down on my side in the room, and the excrement gathers around me; I catch a cicada in the wild." The officials laughed at his explanations.

    The crown prince held a summer banquet and asked the participants to compose poems. One participant wrote, "The frost is white and vast, the moon is red and round." The crown prince took up the pen and wrote a commentary: "Quan Longxiang, the talented man of Qin Prefecture, the bright moon shines during the day, and the frost rises in the summer." Quan Longxiang, having been exiled to Rongshan Commandery due to the fall of Zhang Yizhi, was recalled to the capital during the Shendong period. He then presented a poem to the emperor: "I have no business in Rongshan; today I return to the capital. Your Majesty has granted me a special favor." [81]

    进来今作右金吾又为喜雨诗曰暗去也没雨明来也没云日头赫赤赤地上丝氲氲为瀛州刺史日新过嵗京中数人附书曰改年多感敬想同之正新唤官人集云有诏改年号为多感元年将书呈判司已下众人大笑龙襄复侧聴怪赦书来迟高阳博野两县竞地陈牒龙襄乃判曰两县竞地非州不裁既是两县于理无妨付司权龙襄示典曰比来长官判事皆不着姓龙襄曰余人不解若不着姓知我是谁家浪驴也龙襄不知忌日谓府史曰何名私忌对曰父母忌日请假独坐房中不出襄至日于房中静坐有青狗突入龙襄大怒曰冲破我忌更陈牒改作明日好作忌日谈者笑之
    　　李宜得本贱人背主逃匿当宗起义兵与王毛仲等立功宜得官至武卫将军旧主遇诸途趋而避之不敢仰视宜得令左右命之主甚惶惧至宅舎请居上坐宜得自捧酒食旧主流汗辞之流连数日遂奏云臣国恩荣禄过分臣旧主卑曾无寸禄臣请割半俸解官以荣之愿陛下遂臣愚欵上嘉其志擢主为郎将宜得复其秩朝廷以此多之 [82]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [82]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 進來今作右金吾 - Indicates a change in official position, becoming the Right Gold-Standard Commander.
    • 又為喜雨詩 - Composing a poem celebrating rain, often done during droughts to invoke rainfall.
    • 暗去也没雨明来也没云 - Poetic way of saying it neither rains nor clouds up, indicating a lack of precipitation.
    • 日头赫赤赤地上丝氲氲 - Describes the sun as intensely hot and the ground as steamy or humid.
    • 改年多感敬想同之 - Suggests a change in the year's name, possibly a new era name, and expresses respect and gratitude.
    • 高阳博野两县竞地陈牒 - Indicates a dispute between two counties over land, with formal documents being presented.
    • 龙襄复侧聴怪赦书来迟 - Suggests that Long Xiang was surprised and waited anxiously for an edict or decree.
    • 陈牒改作明日好作忌日 - Changing the date of the document to the next day to avoid a taboo date.
    • 李宜得本贱人背主逃匿 - Describes Li Yide as a lowly person who betrayed his master and went into hiding.
    • 玄宗起义兵与王毛仲等立功 - Indicates that Emperor Xuanzong raised an army and achieved success with the help of Wang Maozhong and others.
    • 旧主遇诸途趋而避之 - Means that the former master, when encountered on the road, would be avoided and not looked at directly.
    • 旧主流汗辞之 - Suggests that the former master, upon being offered a seat, would sweat and decline.
    • 遂奏云臣国恩荣禄过分 - This means that Li Yide then presented a petition stating that he had received too much honor and wealth from the state.
    • 臣旧主卑曾无寸禄 - Indicates that his former master was of low rank and had not received any rewards.
    • 臣请割半俸解官以荣之 - Suggests that Li Yide offered to give half of his salary and resign his position to honor his former master.
    • 愿陛下遂臣愚欵 - This means that he wished the emperor to grant his humble request.
    • 上嘉其志擢主为郎将 - Indicates that the emperor praised his intentions and promoted his former master to the position of Langjiang (a military officer).
    • 朝廷以此多之 - Suggests that the court praised this action.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 右金吾 - A high-ranking military officer in ancient China.
    • 喜雨诗 - Poems written to celebrate rain, often during droughts.
    • 瀛州 - An ancient place name in China.
    • 改年 - Changing the era name, a common practice in imperial China to mark significant events or changes.
    • 高阳博野 - Two counties in ancient China.
    • 龙襄 - A person's name, likely a local official.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a famous emperor known for his cultural and military achievements.
    • 王毛仲 - A general who served under Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 忌日 - A day considered unlucky or taboo, often associated with the death anniversary of a person.
    • 郎将 - A military rank in ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 右金吾 - A high-ranking military officer.
    • 瀛州 - An ancient place name.
    • 高阳博野 - Two ancient counties.
    • 龙襄 - A person's name.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 王毛仲 - A general.
    • 郎将 - A military rank.

    In recent times, I have been appointed as the Right Gold-Standard Commander and composed a poem celebrating rain, which says, "When it disappears, there is no rain; when it arrives, there is no cloud. The sun is scorching, and the ground is steamy." As the governor of Yingzhou, on New Year's Day, several people in the capital attached a letter saying, "Change the year to the Year of Deep Gratitude. I respectfully think you should join in." The new officials were gathered and informed that there was an imperial edict changing the year to the Year of Deep Gratitude. When the letter was presented to the lower officials, everyone laughed. Long Xiang, upon hearing this, listened attentively and was surprised that the edict had arrived late. The counties of Gaoyang and Boye were disputing land and presented formal documents. Long Xiang then ruled, "The two counties are disputing land, which is not within the jurisdiction of the state. Since it is between two counties, it is reasonable to hand it over to the local authorities." Long Xiang showed a legal precedent and said, "Recently, when officials make judgments, they do not sign their names." Long Xiang replied, "Others do not understand. If I do not sign my name, how would they know who I am? I am just a stray donkey." Long Xiang did not know it was a taboo day and asked the court historian, "What is called a private taboo?" The historian replied, "It is the day of one's parents' death anniversary. One should ask for leave and sit alone in the room without coming out." On that day, Long Xiang sat quietly in the room, and a black dog suddenly entered. Long Xiang became very angry and said, "It has disrupted my taboo." He then changed the date of the document to the next day to avoid the taboo day, which made people laugh.

    Li Yide, originally a lowly person, betrayed his master and went into hiding. When Emperor Xuanzong raised an army and achieved success with the help of Wang Maozhong and others, Li Yide became a General of the Guard. When his former master met him on the road, he would avoid him and not look at him directly. Li Yide ordered his attendants to invite his former master, who was very frightened and came to his house. He asked to sit in the upper seat, and Li Yide personally served him wine and food. The former master, sweating profusely, declined. They stayed for several days, and Li Yide then presented a petition saying, "I have received too much honor and wealth from the state. My former master was of low rank and had not received any rewards. I offer to give half of my salary and resign my position to honor him. I wish the emperor to grant my humble request." The emperor praised his intentions and promoted his former master to the position of Langjiang (a military officer). Li Yide resumed his rank, and the court praised this action. [82]

    苏颋年五嵗裴谈过其父颋方在乃试诵庾信枯树赋将及终篇避谈字因易其韵曰昔年移树依依汉隂今看揺落凄凄江浔树犹如此人何以任谈骇叹久之知其他日必主文章也
    　　唐娄师徳荥阳人也为纳言客问浮休子曰娄纳言何如答曰纳言而温寛而栗外愚而内敏表晦而里明万顷之波浑而不浊百炼之质磨而不磷可谓淑人君子近代之名公者焉客曰狄仁杰为纳言何如浮休子曰粗览经史薄阅文华箴规切諌有古人之风剪伐淫词有烈士之操心神耿直湼而不缁胆气坚刚明而能断晚途钱癖和峤之徒与客曰凤阁侍郎李昭徳可谓名相乎答曰李昭徳志大而器小气高而智薄假权制物扼险小人刚愎有余而恭寛不足非谋身之道也俄伏法焉又问洛阳令来俊臣雍容美 [83]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [83]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 年五嵗 - Indicates the age of the subject, literally 'five years old.'
    • 依依漢隂 - A poetic expression meaning 'lovingly by the Han River,' often used to evoke a sense of nostalgia or attachment to a place.
    • 揺落凄凄江浔 - A poetic expression meaning 'shaking off and falling, desolate by the riverbank,' often used to evoke a sense of melancholy or desolation.
    • 樹猶如此人何以任 - A rhetorical question meaning 'if the tree is already like this, how much more so for a person,' emphasizing the fragility of human life.
    • 粗覽經史 - Means 'to skim through the classics and histories,' indicating a superficial knowledge of these texts.
    • 薄閱文華 - Means 'to have a shallow acquaintance with literary elegance,' indicating a limited understanding of literary refinement.
    • 箴規切諌 - Means 'to give earnest advice and sharp rebuke,' indicating a style of governance that is both advisory and corrective.
    • 剪伐淫詞 - Means 'to cut down excessive or vulgar words,' indicating a commitment to purifying language.
    • 心神耿直 - Means 'mind and spirit are upright and honest,' indicating a moral character.
    • 湼而不缁 - A metaphor meaning 'not to be stained black,' indicating the preservation of purity despite external influences.
    • 胆气坚刚 - Means 'courage and spirit are firm and strong,' indicating a resolute character.
    • 明而能断 - Means 'clear-sighted and able to make decisions,' indicating a wise and decisive nature.
    • 晚途钱癖 - Means 'a late-life obsession with money,' indicating a flaw in character.
    • 和峤之徒 - Refers to a group of people similar to He Jiao, a historical figure known for his greed.
    • 凤阁侍郎 - A title meaning 'Cabinet Secretary,' indicating a high-ranking official.
    • 名相 - Means 'famous prime minister,' indicating a respected political figure.
    • 志大而器小 - Means 'ambitious but lacking in capacity,' indicating a mismatch between aspirations and abilities.
    • 气高而智薄 - Means 'high-spirited but lacking in wisdom,' indicating a lack of practical judgment.
    • 假权制物 - Means 'to wield power to control others,' indicating a manipulative nature.
    • 扼险小人 - Means 'to grasp the dangers posed by small people,' indicating a recognition of threats from less influential individuals.
    • 刚愎有余而恭宽不足 - Means 'overly stubborn and lacking in humility and tolerance,' indicating a flawed character.
    • 非谋身之道也 - Means 'not a way to plan for one's own safety,' indicating a lack of self-preservation.
    • 伏法焉 - Means 'to be punished by law,' indicating a legal consequence.
    • 洛阳令 - A title meaning 'Magistrate of Luoyang,' indicating a local official.
    • 雍容美 - Means 'graceful and beautiful,' indicating a dignified and attractive demeanor.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 苏颋 - A historical figure, likely a prominent statesman or scholar.
    • 裴谈 - A historical figure, likely a contemporary or predecessor of苏颋.
    • 庾信 - A famous poet and writer from the Liang Dynasty, known for his contributions to Chinese literature.
    • 枯树赋 - A famous literary work by庾信, often cited as an example of classical Chinese literature.
    • 狄仁杰 - A famous statesman and judge from the Tang Dynasty, known for his integrity and wisdom.
    • 李昭德 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 来俊臣 - A historical figure, likely a controversial official known for his harsh methods.
    • 凤阁 - A term referring to the central government office in the Tang Dynasty, equivalent to the modern cabinet.
    • 名相 - Refers to a respected political figure, often a prime minister.
    • 晚途钱癖 - Refers to a late-life obsession with money, a common theme in Chinese literature and history.
    • 和峤 - A historical figure known for his greed, often used as a cautionary example.
    • 洛阳 - The capital of the Tang Dynasty, a significant city in Chinese history.
    • 雍容美 - A term used to describe a dignified and attractive demeanor, often associated with high-ranking officials.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This long sentence is a complex narrative that describes a scene where裴谈 visits苏颋's father and苏颋 recites庾信's枯树赋, changing the rhyme to avoid mentioning裴谈's name. The sentence is elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of events.
    • A question asking about狄仁杰's performance as a纳言 (a high-ranking official).
    • A question asking whether李昭德 can be considered a名相 (a respected prime minister).
    • A statement describing来俊臣's dignified and attractive demeanor.

    Proper Nouns

    • 苏颋 - A historical figure, likely a prominent statesman or scholar.
    • 裴谈 - A historical figure, likely a contemporary or predecessor of苏颋.
    • 庾信 - A famous poet and writer from the Liang Dynasty.
    • 枯树赋 - A famous literary work by庾信.
    • 狄仁杰 - A famous statesman and judge from the Tang Dynasty.
    • 李昭德 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 来俊臣 - A historical figure, likely a controversial official known for his harsh methods.
    • 凤阁 - A term referring to the central government office in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 名相 - Refers to a respected political figure, often a prime minister.
    • 和峤 - A historical figure known for his greed.
    • 洛阳 - The capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 雍容美 - A term used to describe a dignified and attractive demeanor.

    苏颋五岁时，裴谈拜访他的父亲，当时苏颋正在场，于是试着背诵庾信的《枯树赋》，即将背完时，为了避免提到裴谈的名字，他改了韵脚，说道：“昔年移树依依汉阴，今看摇落凄凄江浔。树犹如此，人何以任。”裴谈听后惊叹不已，长时间赞赏，由此知道苏颋日后必定成为文章大家。

    唐娄师德，荥阳人，担任纳言。有人问浮休子：“娄纳言如何？”浮休子回答说：“娄纳言外表温和而内心谨慎，外显愚钝而内藏聪敏，表面晦暗而内心光明。他如万顷波涛，浑然不浊；如百炼之质，磨而不磷。可以说他是当代的贤人君子，名公之一。”那人又问：“狄仁杰担任纳言如何？”浮休子回答说：“狄仁杰粗略地浏览过经史，对文学有浅薄的了解，他有古人之风，给予诚恳的劝谏，剪除浮华之词，有烈士之操守。他的心神耿直，不为外物所染，胆气刚强，明察秋毫，能做出果断的决策。然而，晚年沉迷于金钱，与和峤之流为伍。”那人又问：“凤阁侍郎李昭德可谓名相吗？”浮休子回答说：“李昭德志向远大但器量狭小，气度高傲但智慧不足，他假借权势控制他人，对小人虽有警惕但过于刚愎自用，缺乏谦恭和宽容，这不是保全自己的方法。”不久，李昭德被依法惩处。那人又问：“洛阳令来俊臣如何？”浮休子回答说：“来俊臣雍容华贵，举止优雅。” [83]

    貌忠赤之士乎答曰俊臣靣柔心狠行险徳薄巧辨似智巧谀似忠倾覆家诬陷良善其江充之徒欤蜂虿害人终为人所害无何为太仆卿戮于西市又问武三思可谓名王哉答曰三思慿借国亲位超衮职貌象恭敬心极残忍外示公直内结隂谋弄王法以复讐假朝权而害物晚封为徳静王乃鼎贼也不可以夀终竟为节愍太子所杀又问中书令魏元忠耿耿正直近代之名臣也答曰元忠文武双阙名实两空外示贞刚内怀趋附面折张食其之党勇若熊罴谄事武士开之俦怯同驽犬首鼠之士进退两端虺蜥之夫曾 [84]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [84]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 貌忠赤之士乎 - Rhetorical question asking about those who appear loyal and devoted.
    • 靣柔心狠 - Describes someone who appears gentle on the surface but is cruel inside.
    • 行險德薄 - Refers to someone who takes dangerous actions and has shallow virtue.
    • 巧辨似智 - Means 'clever arguments that seem wise.'
    • 巧谀似忠 - Means 'flattery that seems loyal.'
    • 倾覆家国 - Refers to the act of overthrowing a family or country.
    • 诬陷良善 - Means 'to falsely accuse the innocent.'
    • 其江充之徒欤 - Rhetorical question asking if they are like Jiang Chong, a notorious figure known for his treachery.
    • 蜂虿害人终为人所害 - Proverb meaning 'a scorpion or wasp harms others but eventually suffers the same fate.'
    • 无何为太仆卿戮于西市 - Means 'and soon became the Grand Steward, only to be executed in the western market.'
    • 武三思可谓名王哉 - Rhetorical question asking if Wu Sanzhi can be called a famous prince.
    • 慫借国亲位超衮职 - Means 'relying on his royal connection, he rose above the highest rank.'
    • 貌象恭敬心极残忍 - Describes someone who appears respectful but is extremely cruel.
    • 外示公直内结阴谋 - Means 'appearing fair and upright on the outside while plotting secretly.'
    • 弄王法以复雠 - Means 'using the king's laws to take revenge.'
    • 假朝权而害物 - Means 'using official power to harm others.'
    • 晚封为德静王乃鼎贼也 - Means 'later enfeoffed as the Prince of Dejing, he was a great traitor.'
    • 不可以寿终 - Means 'he did not die a natural death.'
    • 竟为节愍太子所杀 - Means 'and was eventually killed by the Prince of Jieming.'
    • 中书令魏元忠耿耿正直近代之名臣也 - Describes Wei Yuanzhong as a loyal and upright minister in recent times.
    • 元忠文武双阙名实两空 - Means 'Wei Yuanzhong lacks both literary and martial skills, and his reputation and reality are both empty.'
    • 外示贞刚内怀趋附 - Describes someone who appears steadfast and strong but is actually fawning.
    • 面折张食其之党勇若熊罴谄事武士开之俦怯同驽犬 - Means 'facing Zhang Shiqi's faction head-on with the courage of a bear, yet flattering Wu Shi Kai's group with the timidity of a weak dog.'
    • 首鼠之士进退两端 - Describes someone who wavers between two choices.
    • 虺蜥之夫曾 - Means 'a man like a snake or lizard.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 江充 - A historical figure known for his treachery during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 武三思 - A powerful official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his political machinations and eventual downfall.
    • 魏元忠 - A loyal minister during the Tang Dynasty, known for his integrity and resistance to corrupt officials.
    • 张食其 - A historical figure, possibly a high-ranking official or military leader.
    • 武士开 - Another historical figure, possibly a high-ranking official or military leader.
    • 节愍太子 - The Prince of Jieming, a historical figure who played a significant role in the political turmoil of the Tang Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 江充 - Jiang Chong, a historical figure known for his treachery.
    • 武三思 - Wu Sanzhi, a powerful official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 魏元忠 - Wei Yuanzhong, a loyal minister during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 张食其 - Zhang Shiqi, a historical figure.
    • 武士开 - Wu Shi Kai, a historical figure.
    • 节愍太子 - The Prince of Jieming, a historical figure.

    Is the person described as "貌忠赤之士" (appearing loyal and devoted) truly loyal, or is he like Jiang Chong, a notorious traitor? Answer: Junchen appears gentle on the surface but is actually cruel and treacherous, taking dangerous actions and having shallow virtue. His clever arguments seem wise, and his flattery seems loyal, but he overthrows families and countries and falsely accuses the innocent. Is he like Jiang Chong? A scorpion or wasp that harms others will eventually suffer the same fate. He soon became the Grand Steward, only to be executed in the western market.

    Is Wu Sanzhi a famous prince? Answer: Sanzhi relied on his royal connections to rise above the highest rank, appearing respectful but being extremely cruel. He appears fair and upright on the outside while plotting secretly, using the king's laws to take revenge and official power to harm others. Later enfeoffed as the Prince of Dejing, he was a great traitor and did not die a natural death, eventually being killed by the Prince of Jieming.

    Is Wei Yuanzhong a loyal and upright minister in recent times? Answer: Yuanzhong lacks both literary and martial skills, and his reputation and reality are both empty. He appears steadfast and strong but is actually fawning, facing Zhang Shiqi's faction head-on with the courage of a bear, yet flattering Wu Shi Kai's group with the timidity of a weak dog. He wavers between two choices, like a snake or lizard. [84]

    无一志乱朝败政莫非斯人附三思之徒斥五王之族以吾熟察终不得其死然果坐事长流思州忧恚而卒又问中书令李峤何如答曰李公有三戾性好荣迁憎人升进性好文章憎人才笔性好贪浊憎人受赂亦如古者有女君性嗜肥鲜禁人食肉性爱绮罗防人衣锦性好淫纵憎人畜声色此亦李公之徒也又问司刑卿徐有功何如荅曰有功耿直之士也明而有胆刚而能防处陵夷之运不偷媚以取容居板荡之朝不逊辞以茍免来俊臣罗织者有功出之袁智锻錬者有功寛之蹑虎尾而不惊触龙鳞而不惧凤跱 [85]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [85]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 无一志乱朝败政 - A negative statement meaning 'no single intention to disrupt the court or ruin the government.'
    • 附三思之徒 - Refers to those who followed or supported Zhang San Si (张三思), a historical figure.
    • 斥五王之族 - Refers to the act of denouncing or expelling the families of the Five Princes.
    • 终不得其死 - Means 'ultimately did not die a natural death,' implying an unnatural or untimely death.
    • 坐事长流思州 - Means 'was exiled to Sizhou due to some offense,' where '坐事' indicates being punished for a crime.
    • 忧恚而卒 - Means 'died of worry and anger,' indicating a state of emotional distress leading to death.
    • 何如 - A question asking for an opinion or evaluation.
    • 李公有三戾 - Refers to Li Qiang (李峤) having three vices or flaws.
    • 性好荣迁 - Means 'likes promotion and advancement.'
    • 憎人升进 - Means 'hates others' promotion and advancement.'
    • 性好文章 - Means 'likes literature and writing.'
    • 憎人才笔 - Means 'hates others' literary talent.'
    • 性好贪浊 - Means 'likes corruption and greed.'
    • 憎人受赂 - Means 'hates others receiving bribes.'
    • 女君 - Refers to a woman in a position of authority or a female ruler.
    • 嗜肥鲜 - Means 'loves fatty and fresh food.'
    • 禁人食肉 - Means 'forbids others from eating meat.'
    • 爱绮罗 - Means 'loves silk and brocade.'
    • 防人衣锦 - Means 'prevents others from wearing fine clothes.'
    • 好淫纵 - Means 'likes indulgence and excess.'
    • 憎人畜声色 - Means 'hates others enjoying music and entertainment.'
    • 李公之徒也 - Means 'is a follower of Li Qiang.'
    • 司刑卿徐有功何如 - Asks for an evaluation of Xu Yougong (徐有功), the Chief Justice.
    • 耿直之士也 - Means 'an upright and honest person.'
    • 明而有胆 - Means 'clear-sighted and brave.'
    • 刚而能防 - Means 'firm and able to prevent wrongdoing.'
    • 处陵夷之运 - Means 'in times of decline and hardship.'
    • 不偷媚以取容 - Means 'does not flatter to gain favor.'
    • 居板荡之朝 - Means 'in a chaotic and unstable court.'
    • 不逊辞以茍免 - Means 'does not use humble words to avoid blame.'
    • 来俊臣罗织者 - Refers to Lai Junchen, who was known for fabricating charges.
    • 有功出之 - Means 'Xu Yougong cleared them.'
    • 袁智锻錬者 - Refers to Yuan Zhihong, who was known for fabricating charges.
    • 有功寛之 - Means 'Xu Yougong treated them leniently.'
    • 蹑虎尾而不惊 - Means 'treads on the tail of a tiger without being startled,' indicating courage and composure.
    • 触龙鳞而不惧 - Means 'touches the scales of a dragon without fear,' indicating boldness and fearlessness.
    • 凤跱 - Means 'perches like a phoenix,' indicating a dignified and noble stance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张三思 - A historical figure during the Tang Dynasty, known for his political influence.
    • 五王 - Refers to the Five Princes of the Tang Dynasty, who were influential figures.
    • 李峤 - A prominent official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his literary and political achievements.
    • 徐有功 - A famous judge during the Tang Dynasty, known for his fairness and integrity.
    • 来俊臣 - A notorious official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his cruelty and fabrication of charges.
    • 袁智 - Another official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his involvement in political purges.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张三思 - A historical figure during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 五王 - Refers to the Five Princes of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 李峤 - A prominent official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 徐有功 - A famous judge during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 来俊臣 - A notorious official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 袁智 - Another official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 思州 - A place name, likely referring to a region in China.

    No intention to disrupt the court or ruin the government was not this person who followed Zhang San Si and denounced the families of the Five Princes. After careful observation, he did not die a natural death. However, he was exiled to Sizhou due to some offense and died of worry and anger.

    What is Xu Yougong, the Chief Justice, like? He is an upright and honest person, clear-sighted and brave, firm and able to prevent wrongdoing. In times of decline and hardship, he does not flatter to gain favor. In a chaotic and unstable court, he does not use humble words to avoid blame. When Lai Junchen fabricated charges, Xu Yougong cleared them. When Yuan Zhihong fabricated charges, Xu Yougong treated them leniently. He treads on the tail of a tiger without being startled, touches the scales of a dragon without fear, and perches like a phoenix. [85]

    防枭之内直以全身豹变豺狼之间忠以逺害值清平之代则张释之于定国岂可同年而语哉又问司农卿赵履温何如答曰履温心不涉学眼不识文貌恭而性狠智小而谋大趦趄狗盗突忽猪贪晨羊诱外不觉其死夜蛾覆烛不觉其毙头寄于项其能久乎后从事韦氏为逆夷其三族又问郑愔为选部侍郎何如答曰愔猖獗小子狡猾庸人浅学浮词轻才薄徳狐蹲贵介雉伏权门前托俊臣后附张易折支徳静之室防痔安乐之庭鸋鳷栖于苇苕魦鲿游于沸鼎既无雅量终是凡材以此求荣得死为幸果谋反伏诛 [86]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [86]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 防枭之内直以全身 - 直译为“在枭鸟的巢穴中保持正直以保全自身”，可能指在险恶环境中保持正直以求自保。
    • 豹变豺狼之间忠以逺害 - 直译为“在豹变豺狼之间忠诚以远离祸害”，可能指在复杂多变的环境中保持忠诚以避免灾祸。
    • 清平之代 - 指太平盛世。
    • 张释之于定国岂可同年而语哉 - 张释之和于定国是汉代著名官员，此句意为“张释之和于定国怎能相提并论呢？”
    • 心不涉学眼不识文 - 直译为“心中不涉学问，眼中不识文字”，形容人缺乏学识。
    • 貌恭而性狠 - 外表恭敬而内心狠毒。
    • 智小而谋大 - 智谋小而野心大。
    • 趦趄狗盗 - 形容行为犹豫不决，像狗偷东西一样。
    • 突忽猪贪 - 形容行为突然，像猪一样贪婪。
    • 晨羊诱外 - 直译为“早晨的羊被引诱到外面”，可能指被外物诱惑。
    • 夜蛾覆烛 - 直译为“夜晚的蛾子扑向烛火”，比喻自取灭亡。
    • 头寄于项 - 直译为“头寄于颈项”，比喻地位不稳固。
    • 司农卿 - 古代官职，掌管国家财政。
    • 郑愔 - 人名，此处指郑愔。
    • 选部侍郎 - 古代官职，负责选拔官员。
    • 猖獗小子 - 形容人行为猖狂。
    • 狡猾庸人 - 形容人狡猾而平庸。
    • 浅学浮词 - 学识浅薄，言辞浮夸。
    • 轻才薄德 - 才能轻薄，品德浅薄。
    • 狐蹲贵介 - 形容人像狐狸一样蹲在贵人的位置上。
    • 雉伏权门 - 形容人像野鸡一样伏在权贵的门前。
    • 前托俊臣后附张易 - 形容人前后依附不同的人。
    • 折支德静之室 - 直译为“折断支德静的房间”，可能指破坏他人的安宁。
    • 防痔安乐之庭 - 直译为“防止痔疮的安乐庭院”，可能指在安乐中防备祸患。
    • 鸋鳷栖于苇苕 - 鸋鳷是一种鸟，苇苕是芦苇的顶端，形容鸟栖息在芦苇顶端。
    • 魦鲿游于沸鼎 - 魦鲿是一种鱼，沸鼎是沸腾的锅，形容鱼在沸腾的锅中游动，比喻身处险境。
    • 无雅量 - 没有雅量，指缺乏宽广的胸怀。
    • 凡材 - 平凡的材料，指平庸的人。
    • 得死为幸 - 得到死亡是幸运的，指命运悲惨。
    • 谋反伏诛 - 因谋反而被处死。

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张释之 - 汉代著名官员，以正直著称。
    • 于定国 - 汉代著名官员，以忠诚著称。
    • 司农卿 - 古代官职，掌管国家财政。
    • 郑愔 - 唐代官员，曾担任选部侍郎。
    • 选部侍郎 - 古代官职，负责选拔官员。
    • 俊臣 - 指有才能的大臣。
    • 张易 - 唐代官员，曾任宰相。
    • 折支德静 - 唐代官员，曾任宰相。
    • 魦鲿 - 古代鱼类，常用于比喻。
    • 鸋鳷 - 古代鸟类，常用于比喻。

    Proper Nouns

    • 张释之 - 汉代著名官员。
    • 于定国 - 汉代著名官员。
    • 司农卿 - 古代官职，掌管国家财政。
    • 赵履温 - 人名，此处指司农卿赵履温。
    • 郑愔 - 唐代官员。
    • 选部侍郎 - 古代官职，负责选拔官员。
    • 俊臣 - 指有才能的大臣。
    • 张易 - 唐代官员，曾任宰相。
    • 折支德静 - 唐代官员，曾任宰相。
    • 魦鲿 - 古代鱼类。
    • 鸋鳷 - 古代鸟类。

    In the midst of danger, one must remain upright to preserve oneself, and in the midst of chaos, one must remain loyal to avoid harm. In times of peace, how can Zhang Shizhi and Yu Dingguo be compared? When asked about the agricultural minister Zhao Lutang, the response was: Zhao Lutang has no interest in learning, does not recognize the written word, appears respectful but is cruel, has small intelligence but great ambitions, hesitates like a dog thief, acts suddenly like a greedy pig, is lured outward by the morning sheep, unaware of its death, and is like a moth that flies into the candle flame, unaware of its demise. His head is placed on his neck, how long can he last? Later, he was involved in the rebellion of Wei Shi, and his three clans were destroyed. When asked about Zheng Yin as the director of the personnel department, the response was: Zheng Yin is a reckless and cunning man of little learning and empty words, lacking in talent and virtue. He crouches like a fox in a noble position, crouches like a pheasant at the gate of power, relying on the talents of others, first on Jun Chen and then on Zhang Yi, disrupting the tranquility of the court and the peace of the household. Like a bird perched on the tip of a reed, or a fish swimming in a boiling pot, he lacks the grace of a gentleman and is ultimately just an ordinary man. Seeking honor in this way, he finds death to be a fortunate outcome. Indeed, he was executed for plotting rebellion. [86]

    贞观末南康景逸居于空青山常有鹊巢其侧每饭食以喂之后隣近失布者诬景逸盗之系南康狱月余劾不承欲讯之其鹊止于狱楼向景逸欢喜似传语之状其日传有赦官司诘其来云路逢衣素衿人所说三日而赦至景逸还山乃知衣素衿者鹊之所传也
    　　汝州刺史张昌期易之弟也恃宠骄贵酷暴羣僚梁县有人白云有白鹊见昌期令司户杨楚玉捕之部人有鹞子七十笼令以蜡涂爪至林见白鹊有羣鹊随之见鹞迸散惟白者存焉鹞竦身取之一无损伤而笼送之昌期笑曰此鹊赎君命也玉叩头曰此天活玉不然投河赴海不敢见公拜谢而去 [87]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [87]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 常有鹊巢其侧 - Indicates that there was often a magpie's nest near his dwelling.
    • 欢喜似传语之状 - Indicates that the magpie seemed to be conveying a message.
    • 路逢玄衣素衿人 - Describes encountering a person wearing black clothes and a white collar.
    • 所说三日而赦至 - Indicates that the person said the pardon would come in three days, and it did.
    • 此天活玉不然投河赴海不敢见公拜谢而去 - Indicates that Yang Chuyu bowed and said, 'This is a sign from heaven that saved me; otherwise, I would have thrown myself into the river or the sea and not dared to see you again.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 贞观 - The reign title of Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 南康 - A prefecture in Jiangxi province.
    • 汝州 - A prefecture in Henan province.
    • 张易之 - A eunuch and favorite of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 张昌期 - The younger brother of Zhang Yi, known for his arrogance and cruelty.
    • 玄衣素衿 - A description of the person encountered by the officials.

    Proper Nouns

    • 贞观 - The reign title of Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 南康 - A prefecture in Jiangxi province.
    • 汝州 - A prefecture in Henan province.
    • 张易之 - A eunuch and favorite of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 张昌期 - The younger brother of Zhang Yi.
    • 景逸 - The name of the person living in the mountain.
    • 杨楚玉 - The name of the head of the household affairs department.
    • 梁县 - A county in Henan province.

    At the end of the Zhenguan era, Jingyi lived in an empty, green mountain. There was often a magpie's nest near his dwelling, and he would regularly feed the magpie with his meals. A neighbor who lost cloth falsely accused Jingyi of stealing it, and he was imprisoned in Nan'gang. After more than a month, Jingyi did not confess to the crime, and they wanted to interrogate him. One day, the magpie perched on the prison tower and faced Jingyi, seeming to convey a message. On that day, it was announced that there would be a pardon. The officials asked the magpie about the message, and it described encountering a person wearing black clothes and a white collar, who said the pardon would come in three days. The next day, the pardon was announced, and Jingyi returned to the mountain. He realized that the person in black and white was the message conveyed by the magpie.

    In Ruzhou, Zhang Changqi, the younger brother of Zhang Yi, was arrogant and proud due to his favor. He was cruel and oppressive to his subordinates. Someone from Liang county reported that a white magpie had been seen by Zhang Changqi. Zhang Changqi ordered Yang Chuyu, the head of the household affairs department, to capture the magpie. There were seventy cages containing falcons, and he ordered the falcons' claws to be coated with wax. When the falcons encountered the white magpie and a group of other magpies, the magpies scattered upon seeing the falcons. Only the white magpie remained, and the falcon leaped up to catch it without any injury. The magpie was captured and sent in a cage. Zhang Changqi laughed and said, "This magpie has redeemed your life." Yang Chuyu bowed and said, "This is a sign from heaven that saved me; otherwise, I would have thrown myself into the river or the sea and not dared to see you again." [87]

    渤海高嶷巨富忽患月余日帖然而卒心上仍暖经日而苏云有一白衣人眇目把牒防司讼杀其妻子嶷对元不识此老人防官云君命未尽且放归遂悟白衣人乃是家中老瞎麻鸡也令射杀魅遂絶
    　　文明以后天下诸州进雌鸡变为雄者多或半已化半未化乃则天正位之兆
    　　卫镐为县官下乡至里人王幸在家方假寐梦一乌衣妇人引十数小儿着黄衣咸言乞命叩头再三斯须又至镐甚恶其事遂催食欲前适所亲有报曰王幸在家穷无物设馔有一鸡见抱儿已得十余日将欲杀之镐方悟鸟衣妇人果乌鸡也遂市解放是夜复梦咸欣然而去
    　　久视年中越州有祖録事不得名早出见担鵞向市中者鵞见録事频顾而鸣祖乃以钱赎之至僧寺令放为长生鵞竟不肯入寺但走逐祖后经坊歴市稠人广众之处一步不放祖收养之左丞张锡亲见说也 [88]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [88]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 渤海高嶷巨富 - Refers to a wealthy man named Gao Yi from Bohai.
    • 忽患月余日帖然而卒 - Describes an abrupt illness lasting over a month, followed by death.
    • 心上仍暖经日而苏 - The heart remained warm for several days before he revived.
    • 云有一白衣人眇目把牒防司讼杀其妻子 - He claimed that a blind man with a scroll was preventing his wife and children from being killed.
    • 君命未尽且放归 - The blind man said that his life was not yet finished and he would be returned.
    • 遂悟白衣人乃是家中老瞎麻鸡也 - He realized that the blind man was actually an old blind rooster from his home.
    • 令射杀魅遂绝 - He ordered the rooster to be shot, which ended the haunting.
    • 文明以后天下诸州进雌鸡变为雄者多或半已化半未化乃则天正位之兆 - After the reign of Emperor Zetian, many hens across various states turned into roosters, either partially or fully, which was seen as an omen of her rise to power.
    • 卫镐为县官下乡至里人王幸在家方假寐 - Wei Hao, a county official, visited a villager named Wang Xing who was just dozing off.
    • 梦一乌衣妇人引十数小儿着黄衣咸言乞命叩头再三 - In a dream, he saw a woman in black clothing leading several children in yellow clothing who were pleading for their lives.
    • 斯须又至镐甚恶其事遂催食欲前适所亲 - Shortly after, Wei Hao hurriedly ate and went to visit a friend.
    • 有报曰王幸在家穷无物设馔有一鸡见抱儿已得十余日将欲杀之 - He was informed that Wang Xing was poor and had nothing to offer, but there was a chicken that had been carrying a child for over ten days and was about to be killed.
    • 祖録事不得名早出见担鵞向市中者鵞见録事频顾而鸣 - An unnamed official named Zu saw a goose being carried to the market, and the goose kept looking at him and calling.
    • 祖乃以钱赎之至僧寺令放为长生鵞竟不肯入寺但走逐祖 - Zu bought the goose and took it to a temple to release it as a long-lived goose, but the goose refused to enter the temple and followed him instead.
    • 后经坊歴市稠人广众之处一步不放 - The goose followed him through the streets and markets, never letting go.
    • 左丞张锡亲见说也 - Left Chancellor Zhang Xi personally saw this happen.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 渤海 - A region in ancient China.
    • 文明 - The reign title of Emperor Zetian of Tang.
    • 则天正位 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian's rise to power.
    • 卫镐 - A historical figure, a county official.
    • 王幸 - A historical figure, a villager.
    • 张锡 - A historical figure, a Left Chancellor.
    • 长生鹅 - A term for a goose that is released to live a long life, often associated with religious or spiritual practices.

    Proper Nouns

    • 渤海高嶷 - A wealthy man from Bohai.
    • 文明 - The reign title of Emperor Zetian of Tang.
    • 则天正位 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian's rise to power.
    • 卫镐 - A historical figure, a county official.
    • 王幸 - A historical figure, a villager.
    • 张锡 - A historical figure, a Left Chancellor.
    • 祖録事 - An unnamed official named Zu.

    渤海高嶷，一位巨富，突然患病，持续了一个多月后突然去世，但他的心脏仍然温暖，几天后他苏醒过来。他说有一个白衣人，眼睛失明，拿着文书，阻止他的妻子和孩子们被杀害。高嶷原本不认识这位老人，防官说他的生命尚未终结，可以暂时放他回去。他恍然大悟，那白衣人其实是他家的老瞎麻鸡。于是他命令射杀这只麻鸡，从此不再受其困扰。

    文明年间以后，天下各州出现了许多雌鸡变为雄鸡的现象，有的已经完全变性，有的则部分变性，这被认为是武则天登基的预兆。

    卫镐是县官，他下乡到村里，见村民王幸正准备小睡。王幸梦见一个穿着黑衣的女人，带着十几个穿黄衣的孩子，都在乞求饶命，不停地磕头。不久后，卫镐非常厌恶这件事，便匆匆吃完饭去拜访朋友。有人告诉他，王幸家里很穷，没有东西招待，但有一只鸡已经抱了孩子十多天，正准备被杀。卫镐这才明白，那个黑衣女人其实是只乌鸡。于是他买下这只鸡，带到寺庙放生为长生鹅，但那只鹅不肯进寺庙，反而跟着卫镐走。后来，这只鹅跟着卫镐穿过街市，无论人多的地方都不肯离开。卫镐收养了这只鹅，左丞张锡亲眼见过这件事。

    久视年间，越州有一位名叫祖録事的官员，他早上出门时看到一只鹅被带到市场，那只鹅一直看着他并叫唤。祖録事便用钱买下这只鹅，带到寺庙放生为长生鹅，但那只鹅不肯进寺庙，反而跟着他走。后来，这只鹅跟着祖録事穿过街市，无论人多的地方都不肯离开。祖録事收养了这只鹅，左丞张锡亲眼见过这件事。 [88]

    汉时鄢县南门两扇忽开忽一声称鸳一声称央晨夕开闭声闻京师汉末恶之令毁其门两扇化为鸳鸯相随飞去后改鄢县为晏城县
    　　天后时左卫兵曹刘景阳使岭南得秦吉了鸟雌雄各一解人语至都进之留其雌者雄者烦然不食则天问曰何无聊也鸟为言曰其配为使者所得今颇思之乃呼景阳曰卿何故藏一鸟不进景阳叩头谢罪乃进之则天不罪也
    　　峯州有一道水从吐蕃中来夏冷如冰雪有鱼长一二寸来去有时并水上如粥人取烹之而食千万家取不可尽不知所从来
    　　通州界内多獭各有主养之并在河侧岸间獭若入穴插雉尾于獭穴前獭即不敢出去却尾即出取得鱼必须上岸人便夺之取得多然后放令自吃吃饱即鸣杖以驱之还插雉尾更不敢出
    　　有人见竖子在洛水中洗马顷之见一物如白练带极光晶缴竖子项三两匝即落水死凢是水中及湾泊之所皆有之人澡浴洗马死者皆谓鼋所引非也此名白特宜慎防之蛟之类也 [89]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [89]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 鄢縣南門兩扇忽開忽閉 - Describes the sudden and repeated opening and closing of the two doors of the southern gate of Yan County.
    • 聲稱 - In this context, it means 'calling out' or 'announcing.'
    • 晨夕開閉 - Refers to the doors opening and closing both in the morning and evening.
    • 聲聞京師 - Means 'the sound was heard in the capital city.'
    • 惡之 - Indicates disapproval or dislike.
    • 令毀其門 - Means 'ordered the gate to be destroyed.'
    • 化為鴛鴦 - Describes a transformation into a pair of mandarin ducks.
    • 相隨飛去 - Means 'flew away together.'
    • 改鄢縣為晏城县 - Refers to the renaming of Yan County to Yancheng County.
    • 天后時 - Refers to the period of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 左衛兵曹 - A military officer position.
    • 秦吉了鳥 - Refers to a type of bird, possibly the Asian koel.
    • 雌雄各一 - Means 'one male and one female.'
    • 解人語 - Means 'understand human language.'
    • 不食 - Refers to refusing to eat.
    • 何无聊也 - Means 'what is wrong?'
    • 配為使者所得 - Means 'the female bird was taken by the envoy.'
    • 思之 - Means 'misses it.'
    • 叩頭謝罪 - Means 'knocked his head to the ground in apology.'
    • 峯州有一道水 - Refers to a river in Fengzhou.
    • 從吐蕃中來 - Means 'comes from Tibet.'
    • 夏冷如冰雪 - Describes the water being cold like ice and snow in summer.
    • 長一二寸 - Means 'about one or two inches long.'
    • 來去時 - Means 'when they come and go.'
    • 並水上如粥 - Describes the fish moving on the surface like porridge.
    • 取烹之而食 - Means 'caught and cooked for food.'
    • 不可盡 - Means 'cannot be exhausted.'
    • 通州界内多獭 - Refers to many otters in Tongzhou.
    • 各有主養之 - Means 'each has an owner who raises them.'
    • 插雉尾于獭穴前 - Means 'inserting a pheasant's tail in front of the otter's den.'
    • 取魚 - Means 'catching fish.'
    • 上岸 - Means 'going ashore.'
    • 奪之 - Means 'taking them.'
    • 放令自吃 - Means 'letting them eat on their own.'
    • 飽即鳴杖以驅之 - Means 'when full, they beat a stick to drive them away.'
    • 有人見竖子在洛水中洗馬 - Describes someone seeing a child washing a horse in the Luo River.
    • 見一物如白練帶 - Refers to seeing something like a white silk ribbon.
    • 極光晶 - Means 'extremely bright and shiny.'
    • 繳 - Means 'to wrap around.'
    • 項三兩匝 - Means 'wrapped around the neck three or four times.'
    • 落水死 - Means 'fell into the water and died.'
    • 凡是水中及湾泊之所皆有之 - Refers to places where such incidents occur.
    • 人澡浴洗馬死者皆謂鼋所引 - Means 'people who bathe or wash horses and die are said to be led by a turtle.'
    • 非也 - Means 'not so.'
    • 此名白特 - Refers to this being called 'White Te.'
    • 宜慎防之 - Means 'should be cautious.'
    • 蛟類也 - Means 'a type of dragon.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 漢時 - Refers to the Han Dynasty.
    • 漢末 - Refers to the end of the Han Dynasty.
    • 天后 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 左衛兵曹 - A military officer position during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 秦吉了鳥 - An ancient Chinese name for the Asian koel, a type of cuckoo bird known for its ability to mimic human speech.
    • 峯州 - A historical region in southern China.
    • 吐蕃 - An ancient kingdom that corresponds to the Tibetan Plateau.
    • 通州 - A historical region in southern China.
    • 洛水 - The Luo River, a major river in Henan Province.
    • 洛陽 - The city of Luoyang, a significant cultural and political center in ancient China.
    • 白特 - A mythical creature in Chinese folklore, often associated with water and danger.

    During the Han Dynasty, the two doors of the southern gate of Yan County would suddenly open and close, with one calling out "yuanyuan" and the other "yongyang." This phenomenon occurred both in the morning and evening, and the sound could be heard in the capital city. By the end of the Han Dynasty, this was considered an ominous sign, and the gate was ordered to be destroyed. Afterward, the two doors transformed into a pair of mandarin ducks and flew away together. Later, Yan County was renamed Yancheng County.

    During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, Liu Jingyang, a military officer of the Left Guard, traveled to the south and obtained a pair of Asian koels, one male and one female, that could understand human language. When he arrived in the capital, he presented the female bird to the empress, but the male bird refused to eat. Wu Zetian asked, "What is wrong?" The bird replied, "My mate was taken by the envoy and I miss her." The empress then called out to Liu Jingyang, "Why did you hide one of the birds from me?" Liu Jingyang knelt and apologized, and the bird was then presented to the empress, who did not punish him.

    In Fengzhou, there is a river that flows from Tibet. In the summer, the water is as cold as ice and snow. There are fish that are about one or two inches long, which come and go at certain times, moving on the surface like porridge. People catch and cook them for food, but they cannot be exhausted. The origin of these fish is unknown.

    In Tongzhou, there are many otters, each with an owner who raises them. The otters live along the riverside. When an otter enters its den, a pheasant's tail is inserted in front of the den, and the otter will not come out. When the tail is removed, the otter comes out and catches fish, but it must go ashore to do so. People then take the fish. Once the otter has caught enough fish, it is allowed to eat on its own. When the otter is full, a stick is beaten to drive it away, and the pheasant's tail is reinserted, preventing the otter from coming out again.

    Once, a child was washing a horse in the Luo River when he saw something like a white silk ribbon, extremely bright and shiny, wrapping around his neck three or four times. The child fell into the water and died. Such incidents occur in all places where there is water or where people bathe or wash horses. People who die in such circumstances are said to be led by a turtle, but this is not true. This creature is called "White Te" and should be cautiously guarded against, as it is a type of dragon. [89]

    齐州有万顷陂鱼鳖水族无所不有咸亨中忽一僧持鉢乞食邨人长者施以蔬供食讫而去于时渔人得一鱼长六七尺丝鳞镂甲锦质宝章特异常鱼赍赴州饷遗至邨而死众共剖而分之于腹中得长者所施蔬食俨然并在邨人遂于陂中设斋超度自是陂中无水族至今犹然
    　　杭州富阳县韩珣庄掘井才深五六尺土中得鱼数十头土有防润
    　　贞观中卫州板桥店主张迪妻归寜有卫州三卫杨贞等三人投店宿五更早发夜有人取三卫刀杀张迪其刀却内鞘中贞等不知之至明店人趋贞等拔刀血狼藉囚禁拷讯贞等苦毒遂自诬上疑之差御史蒋恒覆推至总追店人十五以上集为人不足且散惟留一老婆年八十已上晩放出令狱典密觇之曰婆出当有一人与婆语者即记取姓名勿令漏泄果有一人共语者即记之明日复尔其人又问婆使人作何推勘如是者二日并是此人恒总追集男女三百余人就中唤与老婆语者一人出余并放散问之具伏云与迪妻奸杀有实奏之敕赐帛二百段除侍御史 [90]

    In Qi State, there was a vast lake called Wanqin Pool, which was home to a variety of fish and aquatic creatures. During the Xianhe period of Emperor Gaozong's reign, a monk suddenly appeared to beg for alms. A village elder offered him a meal of vegetables, which he finished and then left. At that time, a fisherman caught a fish that was six or seven feet long, with shimmering scales and a shiny shell, marked with precious patterns. It was unusually different from ordinary fish. The fisherman brought it to the city as a gift, but by the time it reached the village, it had died. The villagers cut it open and found the vegetables that the elder had offered the monk still inside. The villagers then held a ritual in the lake to appease the spirits of the fish. After that, the lake was no longer home to any aquatic creatures, and this state has continued to the present day.

    In Fuyang County, Hangzhou, there was a man named Han Xun who dug a well. When the well was only five or six feet deep, he found dozens of fish in the soil, which had a protective moisture.

    During the Zhenguan period of Emperor Taizong's reign, in Weizhou, there was an inn called Banqiao Inn, owned by Zhang Di. One night, three military officials from Weizhou, including Yang Zhen, came to stay at the inn. They left early the next morning, but at night, someone took one of the officials' knives and killed Zhang Di. The knife was returned to its sheath, and the officials did not know what had happened. The next morning, the innkeeper noticed bloodstains and reported the incident. The officials were questioned and tortured, and under duress, they falsely confessed to the murder. The emperor suspected their innocence and ordered the Censor-General Jiang Heng to re-investigate the case. The governor of Weizhou summoned all men over the age of fifteen, but there were not enough people, so they were released except for an elderly woman over eighty years old. She was released late at night, and the jailer was instructed to secretly observe her. The jailer reported that when the woman left, one person spoke to her, and the name was recorded. The next day, the same person was seen speaking to the woman again, and the name was recorded once more. Over the next two days, this person was consistently observed. The governor then summoned 300 men and women, and the person who spoke to the woman was identified and brought forward. The rest were released. When questioned, the person confessed to having an affair with Zhang Di's wife and killing him. The case was reported to the emperor, who ordered the granting of 200 bolts of silk as a reward for Jiang Heng and appointed him as a Censor. [90]

    朝野佥载卷四
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,朝野佥载>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　朝野佥载卷五
    　　唐　张鷟　撰
    　　贞观中左丞李行亷弟行诠前妻子忠烝其后母遂私将潜藏亡勅追入内行亷不知乃进状问奉敕推诘极其后母诈以领巾勒项卧街中长安县诘之云有人诈宣勅唤去一紫袍人见留宿不知姓名勒项送至街中忠惶恐私就卜问被不良人疑之执送县县尉王璥引就房内推问不允璥先令一人于案褥下伏聴令一人走报长使唤璥鏁房门而去子母相谓曰必不得承并私密之语璥至开门案下之人亦起母子大惊并具承伏法云 [91]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [91]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 烝其后母 - A euphemism for 'incest' or 'having sexual relations with one's stepmother.'
    • 私将潜藏 - Means 'secretly taking and hiding.'
    • 亡勅追入内 - Means 'a false imperial edict was issued to bring him into the palace.'
    • 进状问 - Means 'submitting a report to inquire.'
    • 奉敕推诘 - Means 'being ordered to investigate.'
    • 极 - Means 'to be severely interrogated.'
    • 诈以领巾勒项卧街中 - Means 'faking a suicide by hanging with a scarf around the neck and lying on the street.'
    • 卜问 - Means 'consulting an oracle or fortune-teller.'
    • 长使唤 - Means 'being called by a superior.'
    • 锁房门而去 - Means 'locking the door and leaving.'
    • 伏听 - Means 'lying in hiding to listen.'
    • 走报 - Means 'running to report.'
    • 具承伏法 - Means 'admitting guilt and accepting punishment.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 朝野佥载 - A historical text that records anecdotes and gossip from the Tang Dynasty, providing insights into the social and political life of the time.
    • 贞观 - The era name of Emperor Taizong of Tang, known for its prosperity and cultural achievements.
    • 左丞 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, responsible for various administrative duties.
    • 长安县 - Refers to Chang'an County, the capital city of the Tang Dynasty, which was a center of political and cultural activity.
    • 紫袍人 - A person wearing a purple robe, indicating a high-ranking official or someone of significant status.
    • 不良人 - A term used for low-ranking officials or constables who were often involved in law enforcement and public order.

    Proper Nouns

    • 朝野佥载 - The title of the historical text.
    • 贞观 - The era name of Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 左丞李行亷 - Li Xinglian, a high-ranking official.
    • 弟行诠 - Li Xingquan, the younger brother of Li Xinglian.
    • 前妻子忠 - Li Zhong, the son of Li Xingquan from his previous marriage.
    • 长安县 - Chang'an County, the capital city of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 紫袍人 - A person wearing a purple robe, likely an official.
    • 不良人 - A low-ranking official or constable.

    Records from the Court and the Countryside, Volume Four
    <Zi Department, Miscellaneous Stories, Miscellaneous Affairs, Records from the Court and the Countryside>
    　　Imperially Approved Four Treasuries of Literature
    Records from the Court and the Countryside, Volume Five
    Tang Dynasty Zhang Zhe Compiled
    During the Zhenguan era, Left Chancellor Li Xinglian's younger brother, Li Xingquan, had a son named Li Zhong from his previous marriage. Li Zhong had sexual relations with his stepmother. He then secretly took and hid something, and a false imperial edict was issued to bring him into the palace. Li Xinglian did not know about this and submitted a report to inquire. Being ordered to investigate, Li Zhong was severely interrogated. His stepmother faked a suicide by hanging with a scarf around her neck and lying on the street. The magistrate of Chang'an County interrogated her and she claimed that someone had falsely announced an imperial edict and called her away. A person in a purple robe was seen and kept her overnight, but his name was unknown. He forced her to hang herself and sent her to the street. Li Zhong, in fear, privately consulted an oracle, which led to suspicion. He was arrested and brought to the county. The county magistrate Wang Jing led him into a room for questioning, but he refused to confess. Wang Jing first ordered someone to hide under the table to listen, and another to run and report that he was called by a superior. Wang Jing locked the door and left. The mother and son said to each other, "We must not confess, and we must keep our private words secret." When Wang Jing returned and opened the door, the person under the table also got up. The mother and son were greatly alarmed and confessed everything, accepting punishment. [91]

    李杰为河南尹有寡妇告其子不孝其子不能自理但云得罪于母死所甘分杰察其状非不孝子谓寡妇曰汝寡居惟有一子今告之罪至死得无悔乎寡妇曰子无赖不顺母寜复惜乎杰曰审如此可买棺木来取儿尸因使人觇其后寡妇既出谓一道士曰事了矣俄而棺至杰尚冀有悔再三喻之寡妇执意如初道士立于门外密令擒之一问承伏某与寡妇私尝苦儿所制故欲除之杰放其子杖杀道士及寡妇便同棺盛之
    　　卫州新乡县令裴子云好竒防部人王敬戍边留防牛六头于舅李进处养五年产犊三十头例十贯已上敬还索牛两头已死只还四头老牛余并非汝牛生总不肯还敬忿之经县陈牒子云令送敬府狱禁教追盗牛贼李进进惶怖至县叱之曰贼引汝同盗牛三十头藏于汝家唤贼共对乃以布衫笼敬头立南墙下进乃吐欵云三十头牛总是外甥犊牛所生实非盗得云遣去布衫进见是敬曰此是外甥也云曰若是即还他牛进黙然云曰五年养牛辛苦与数头余并与敬一县服其精察 [92]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [92]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 察其状 - Observing his demeanor, used to assess someone's character based on their behavior.
    • 甘分 - Content to die, suggests resignation or acceptance of a dire fate.
    • 事了矣 - The matter is settled, indicates that a situation has been resolved.
    • 再三喻之 - Repeatedly tried to persuade him, emphasizes the persistence of the persuasion.
    • 密令擒之 - Secretly ordered him to be captured, suggests a covert action.
    • 博学求之 - Broadly seek knowledge from them, emphasizes the value of learning from various sources.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 河南尹 - Administrative head of Henan Prefecture during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 不孝 - Lack of filial piety, a serious moral failing in Confucian ethics.
    • 道士 - Taoist priest or practitioner.
    • 卫州新乡县令 - Magistrate of Xinxiang County in Weizhou Prefecture.
    • 裴子云 - Historical figure, likely a magistrate or official.
    • 王敬 - Historical figure, likely a soldier or traveler.
    • 戍边 - Military service on the frontier.
    • 部人 - People from a specific region or tribe.
    • 舅 - Uncle by marriage.
    • 盗牛贼 - Cattle thief.
    • 布衫 - Simple cloth garment, often worn by common people.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李杰 - Historical figure, likely a magistrate or official.
    • 裴子云 - Historical figure, likely a magistrate or official.
    • 王敬 - Historical figure, likely a soldier or traveler.
    • 李进 - Historical figure, likely a relative or acquaintance.

    Translation:

    Li Jie, the Prefect of Henan, received a complaint from a widow accusing her son of being unfilial. The son could not defend himself, merely saying that he had offended his mother and was content to die. Li Jie observed his demeanor and concluded that he was not an unfilial son. He told the widow, "You are a widow with only one son. Now that you have accused him of a crime that could lead to his death, have you no regrets?" The widow replied, "My son is a troublemaker who does not obey his mother. What do I have to be sorry about?" Li Jie said, "If that is truly the case, you can buy a coffin to retrieve his body." He then sent someone to follow the widow. After she left, she told a Taoist priest, "The matter is settled." Soon after, the coffin arrived. Li Jie still hoped she might have second thoughts and tried to persuade her repeatedly, but she remained firm in her decision. The Taoist priest stood outside the door, and Li Jie secretly ordered him to be captured. Upon questioning, the priest confessed, "I had an affair with the widow and was once troubled by her son, so I wanted to get rid of him." Li Jie released the son and had the Taoist priest and the widow buried together in the same coffin.

    In the state of Wei, the magistrate of Xinxiang County, Pei Ziyun, was known for his sharp discernment. A man named Wang Jing, who was戍边 (on military service on the frontier), left six cows with his uncle Li Jin to guard. After five years, the cows produced thirty calves, each worth ten guan or more. When Wang Jing returned to claim the cows, Li Jin only gave him two dead cows and four old ones, refusing to return the rest, claiming they were not his calves. Wang Jing was furious and filed a complaint with the county. Pei Ziyun sent Wang Jing to the county jail and ordered the pursuit of the cattle thief, Li Jin. Li Jin was terrified and went to the county, where Pei Ziyun shouted at him, "Thief, bring your accomplice to face the thirty stolen cows hidden in your house." Li Jin was then made to wear a cloth shirt and stand against the south wall. Under pressure, Li Jin confessed, "The thirty cows were all calves born by my nephew, not stolen. Please remove the cloth shirt." Pei Ziyun saw that it was Wang Jing and said, "If it is your nephew, return the cows to him." Li Jin remained silent, and Pei Ziyun said, "You have worked hard for five years to raise these cows. Give him several of them, and the rest can be returned to you." The entire county admired Pei Ziyun's keen judgment. [92]

    中书舎人郭正一破平襄得一高丽婢名玉素极姝艳令专知财物库正一夜须浆水粥非玉素煮之不可玉素乃毒之而进正一曰此婢药我索土浆甘草服解之良久乃止覔婢不得幷失金银器物余十事録奏勅令长安万年捉不良脊烂求贼鼎沸三日不获不良王帅魏昶有防畧取舎人家奴选年少端正者三人布衫笼头至卫缚卫士四人问十日内已来何人覔舎人家卫士云有投高丽留书遣付舎人捉马奴书见在检云金城坊中有一空宅更无语不良往金城坊空宅并搜之至一宅封鏁正密打鏁破开之婢及高丽并在其中拷问乃是投化高丽共捉马奴藏之奉敕斩于东市 [93]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [93]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 極姝艳 - Describes exceptional beauty; rendered as 'exceptionally beautiful'.
    • 奉敕斩于东市 - Means 'executed by imperial decree in the eastern market'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 中书舎人 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty's imperial bureaucracy.
    • 高丽 - Refers to the ancient Korean kingdom of Goryeo.
    • 不良 - Refers to the unsavory people or the police force during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 东市 - Refers to the eastern market, a common execution site in ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 郭正一 - The name of the official, transliterated as 'Guo Zhengyi'.
    • 玉素 - The name of the slave girl, transliterated as 'Yusu'.
    • 魏昶 - A historical figure, transliterated as 'Wei Chang'.

    The Middle Secretariat, Guo Zhengyi, after the conquest of Pingshang, obtained a Korean slave girl named Yusu, who was exceptionally beautiful. He ordered her to be in charge of the treasury. Whenever he needed rice porridge, it had to be prepared by Yusu. One night, he needed rice porridge, and only Yusu could prepare it. Yusu poisoned the rice porridge and served it to Guo Zhengyi. Guo Zhengyi said, "This girl poisoned me. I took earth water and licorice to counteract the poison. It took a long time to recover." When he looked for Yusu, she was missing, and ten valuable items were also lost. He recorded the incident and reported it to the emperor. The emperor ordered the unsavory people to whip and force the search for the thief. After three days of intense search, they still could not find the thief. The commander of the unsavory people, Wei Chang, had a plan. He took a servant from Guo Zhengyi's household, dressed him in a plain shirt and hooded, and took four guards to the prison. The unsavory people were whipped and forced to search for the thief. They searched a vacant house in Jin Cheng Fang and found Yusu and the Korean man hiding there. They were caught and executed by imperial decree in the eastern market. [93]

    垂拱年则天监国罗织事起湖州佐史江琛取刺史裴光判书割字合成文理诈为徐敬业反书以告差使推光欵书是光书欵语非光语前后三使推不能决敕令差能推事人勘当取实佥曰张楚金可乃使之楚金忧闷仰卧西窻日高向看之字似补作平看则不觉向日则见之令唤州官集索一瓮水令琛投书于水中字一一解散琛叩头伏罪敕令决一百然后斩之赏楚金绢百疋
    　　怀州河内县董行成能防贼有一人从河阳长店盗行人驴一头幷皮袋天欲晓至怀州行成至街中见嗤之曰个贼住即下驴来即承伏人问何以知之行成曰此驴行急而汗非长行也见人则引驴逺过怯也以此知之捉送县有顷驴主踪至皆如其言 [94]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [94]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 垂拱年 - Year during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian (690–705 CE), when she ruled as empress regnant.
    • 則天監國 - Empress Wu Zetian’s regency, specifically her rule as empress regnant.
    • 羅織事起 - Practice of fabricating charges began, referring to the political persecution known as the 'Luo Zhi' or 'fabrication of crimes.'
    • 割字合成文理 - Cutting characters and combining them to form a coherent text, referring to the manipulation of written text.
    • 欵書 - Confession or admission of guilt.
    • 欵語 - Words used in a confession.
    • 差使推 - To send officials to investigate.
    • 敕令 - Imperial decree or royal order.
    • 能推事人 - Officials skilled in investigating cases.
    • 佥曰 - All agreed or unanimously said.
    • 捉送县 - To capture and send to the county.
    • 踪至 - To follow the trail or to arrive.
    • 如其言 - As he said or as expected.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 垂拱年 - Year during Empress Wu Zetian’s reign, a period marked by political intrigue and the persecution of officials.
    • 則天監國 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history, ruled during this period.
    • 羅織事 - Political persecution known as 'Luo Zhi,' where officials fabricated charges against political opponents.
    • 湖州 - A prefecture in Zhejiang province, where the events described took place.
    • 裴光 - A local official (刺史) who was falsely accused of treason.
    • 徐敬业 - A historical figure who led a rebellion against the Tang dynasty, often used as a symbol of treason.
    • 董行成 - A local official in Henan who was skilled in detecting thieves.
    • 河内县 - A county in Henan province, where the events involving董行成 took place.

    Proper Nouns

    • 垂拱 - The era name of Empress Wu Zetian’s reign.
    • 则天 - A title for Empress Wu Zetian, meaning 'the ruler of the world.'
    • 徐敬业 - A historical figure who led a rebellion against the Tang dynasty.
    • 湖州 - A prefecture in Zhejiang province.
    • 裴光 - A local official (刺史) falsely accused of treason.
    • 董行成 - A local official in Henan skilled in detecting thieves.
    • 河内县 - A county in Henan province.

    During the year of Chui Gong, when Wu Zetian was regent and the practice of fabricating charges began, a deputy historian named Jiang Chen from Huzhou took the governor's judgment book, cut and rearranged the characters to form a coherent text, and forged a letter from Xu Jingye, who had rebelled against the state. He reported this to the authorities, who sent officials to investigate. The governor, Pei Guang, confessed to the charges, but the words in his confession were not his own. Three officials were sent to investigate, but they could not reach a conclusion. The emperor then issued an imperial decree, ordering skilled officials to investigate and bring the truth to light. All agreed that Zhang Chu Jin was the right person for the task, so he was sent to handle the case. Zhang Chu Jin was worried and lay down, looking out the west window. When the sun rose, he noticed that the characters seemed to be patched together. When viewed from the side, they appeared normal, but when viewed directly, they were clearly altered. He ordered the state officials to gather and brought a large pot of water. He instructed Jiang Chen to throw the letter into the water, and the characters dispersed one by one. Jiang Chen knelt and confessed his crime. The emperor ordered him to be punished with a hundred strokes of the cane and then executed. Zhang Chu Jin was rewarded with a hundred bolts of silk.

    In Huai Prefecture's He Nei County, Dong Xingcheng was skilled at catching thieves. One day, a man stole a donkey and its leather bag from a traveler on the road from Heyang to Changdian. As dawn approached, he arrived in Huai Prefecture. Dong Xingcheng saw him in the street and mocked him, saying, "This thief, stop right there. Get off your donkey." The man immediately confessed. Someone asked how he knew. Dong Xingcheng replied, "This donkey was running fast and sweating, which is not typical of a long journey. When he saw people, he led the donkey far away, showing his fear. That is how I knew." He took the thief to the county. Soon after, the donkey's owner arrived, and everything happened exactly as Dong Xingcheng had said. [94]

    张鷟为阳县尉日有称架人吕元伪作仓督冯忱书盗粜仓粮粟忱不认书元乃坚执不能定鷟取吕元告牒括两头唯留一字问是汝书即注是以字押不是即注非亦以字押元乃注曰非去括即是元牒且决五下括诈冯忱书上一字以问之注曰是去括乃诈书也元连项赤叩头伏罪又有一客驴缰防幷鞍失三日访不获经县告鷟推勘夜放驴出而藏其鞍可直五千已来鷟曰此可知也令将笼头放之驴向旧馁处鷟令搜其家其鞍于草积下得人伏其计
    　　张松夀为长安令时昆明池侧有劫杀奉敕十日内须获贼如违所由科罪夀至行刼处寻踪迹见一老婆树下卖食至以从骑防来入县供以酒食经三日还以马送旧坐处令一腹心人看有人共婆语即捉来须臾一人来问明府若为推逐即披布衫笼头送县一问具承幷贼并获时人以为神明 [95]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [95]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 稱架人 - Refers to a person who falsely accuses others, likely a term for a false accuser or a litigant who makes baseless claims.
    • 偽作 - Means 'to forge' or 'to counterfeit.'
    • 堅執 - Means 'to insist firmly' or 'to hold fast.'
    • 決五下 - Refers to a form of corporal punishment, where '決' means to beat or punish, and '五下' means five strokes.
    • 括兩頭 - Means 'to wrap or tie both ends,' likely referring to a method of concealing or altering a document.
    • 注是以字押 - Means 'to write 'yes' with the character stamped,' indicating a formal acknowledgment.
    • 連項赤 - Means 'to blush all over,' indicating embarrassment or shame.
    • 伏罪 - Means 'to confess guilt.'
    • 奉敕 - Means 'under imperial order' or 'by imperial decree.'
    • 如違所由科罪 - Means 'if the case is not solved within the time limit, the responsible officials will be punished.'
    • 披布衫 - Means 'to wear a loose robe,' likely indicating a casual or relaxed state.
    • 籠頭 - Refers to a bridle or halter for a horse, used here metaphorically to control or guide.
    • 一腹心人 - Means 'a trusted person' or 'a close confidant.'
    • 須臾 - Means 'in a short while' or 'soon.'
    • 明府 - A respectful term for a local official, often used by subordinates.
    • 若為推逐 - Means 'how to investigate' or 'how to pursue.'
    • 披布衫籠頭送縣 - Means 'to send the person wearing a loose robe and leading a horse to the county.'
    • 一問具承 - Means 'to confess everything after one question.'
    • 時人以為神明 - Means 'the people of the time considered him a deity or a sage.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 張鷟 - Zhang Zuo, a prominent Tang Dynasty official and literary figure known for his administrative skills and literary works.
    • 陽縣尉 - Refers to Zhang Zuo's position as the magistrate of Yang County.
    • 昆明池 - A large artificial lake in Chang'an (modern-day Xi'an), famous for its scenic beauty and historical significance.
    • 劫殺 - Refers to robbery and murder, a serious crime in ancient China.
    • 奉敕 - Indicates that the investigation was conducted under imperial order, highlighting the gravity of the case.
    • 科罪 - Refers to the punishment of officials for failing to solve a case within the prescribed time limit.
    • 披布衫籠頭 - The act of dressing someone in a loose robe and leading them by a bridle is a symbolic way of guiding or controlling them, often used in interrogations.
    • 明府 - A term of respect used by subordinates to address a local official, emphasizing the hierarchical nature of ancient Chinese society.
    • 若為推逐 - A question about the method of investigation, reflecting the importance of proper procedure in solving crimes.

    Proper Nouns

    • 張鷟 - Zhang Zuo, a Tang Dynasty official.
    • 呂元 - Lu Yuan, the false accuser.
    • 馮忱 - Feng Chen, the warehouse supervisor.
    • 昆明池 - A large artificial lake in Chang'an.
    • 劫殺 - Robbery and murder.
    • 奉敕 - Imperial order.
    • 科罪 - Punishment for officials.
    • 披布衫籠頭 - A method of guiding or controlling someone.
    • 明府 - A term of respect for a local official.
    • 若為推逐 - How to investigate.

    Zhang Zuo, while serving as the magistrate of Yang County, encountered a case where a false accuser named Lu Yuan forged a document attributed to the warehouse supervisor Feng Chen, stealing grain from the warehouse. Feng Chen denied the document, but Lu Yuan insisted on its authenticity, making it impossible to resolve the case. Zhang Zuo took Lu Yuan's accusation and wrapped both ends of the document, leaving only one character visible. He asked Lu Yuan if the document was his, and if so, to write "yes" with the character stamped; if not, to write "no" with the character stamped. Lu Yuan wrote "no," and when the wrapping was removed, it revealed that the document was indeed Lu Yuan's. Lu Yuan then blushed all over and confessed his guilt.

    Another case involved a traveler who lost his horse's bridle and saddle after three days of searching without success. He reported the theft to Zhang Zuo, who investigated the matter. That night, Zhang Zuo released the horse and let it go, but kept the saddle hidden. The next morning, Zhang Zuo said, "This is easy to solve." He ordered the horse to be led out with a bridle and sent it back to the traveler's old location. He then instructed a trusted person to watch for anyone talking to an old woman. Soon, a man came and asked the magistrate how he was investigating the case. Zhang Zuo had the man wear a loose robe and lead the horse to the county, and after one question, the man confessed everything, and the thief was caught. The people of the time considered Zhang Zuo a deity or a sage.

    During Zhang Songshou's tenure as the magistrate of Chang'an, there was a case of robbery and murder near the Kunming Pool. The emperor ordered that the case be solved within ten days, or the responsible officials would be punished. Zhang Songshou arrived at the scene of the crime and followed the trail, finding an old woman selling food under a tree. He sent his horse to the county, where he provided the woman with food and drink for three days. The next morning, he returned the horse to its old location and instructed a trusted person to watch for anyone talking to the old woman. Soon, a man came and asked the magistrate how he was investigating the case. Zhang Songshou had the man wear a loose robe and lead the horse to the county, and after one question, the man confessed everything, and the thief was caught. The people of the time considered Zhang Songshou a deity or a sage. [95]

    元嘉少聪俊左手画员右手画方口诵经史目数羣羊兼成四十字诗一时而就足书五言一絶六事齐举代号神仙童子
    　　幷州人毛俊诞一男四嵗则天召入内试字千字文皆能暗书赐衣裳放还人皆以为精魅所托其后不知所终
    　　纳言娄师徳郑州人为兵部尚书使幷州接境诸县令随之日高至驿恐人烦扰驿家令就防同食尚书饭白而细诸人饭黑而麤呼驿长嗔之曰饭何为两种者驿客将恐对曰邂逅淅米不得死罪尚书曰卒客无卒主人亦复何损遂换取麤饭食之检校营田往梁州先有乡人姓娄者为屯官犯赃都督许钦明欲决杀令众乡人谒尚书欲救之尚书曰犯国法师徳当家儿子亦不能舍何况渠明日宴防都督与尚书俱坐尚书曰闻有一人犯国法云是师徳乡里师徳实不识但与其 [96]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [96]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元嘉少聪俊 - Young and wise, talented, describing the individual's early intelligence and aptitude.
    • 左手画员右手画方 - Metaphor for exceptional skill and multitasking.
    • 口诵经史 - Reciting the classics and histories orally, indicating a deep knowledge of literature and history.
    • 目数羣羊 - Counting a flock of sheep with one's eyes, a metaphor for quick mental calculation or attention to detail.
    • 兼成四十字诗一时而就 - Simultaneously composing a forty-character poem in one go, showcasing the ability to produce complex work quickly.
    • 足书五言一絶六事齐举 - Writing a five-character quatrain and six other tasks simultaneously, emphasizing multitasking and efficiency.
    • 代号神仙童子 - Known as the divine child, a title given to the individual for their extraordinary abilities.
    • 诞一男四嵗则天召入内试字千字文皆能暗书 - Born with a son who, at four years old, was summoned by the Empress Wu to test his ability to write the Thousand Character Classic, which he could recite from memory, highlighting the child's prodigious memory and skill.
    • 赐衣裳放还人皆以为精魅所托 - Bestowed with clothes and sent back, people believed he was possessed by a spirit, indicating the child's mysterious and supernatural abilities.
    • 纳言娄师徳郑州人为兵部尚书使幷州接境诸县令随之日高至驿恐人烦扰驿家令就防同食 - Liu Shide, a man from Zhengzhou and a minister of war, was sent to Bingzhou. Along the way, he was accompanied by the magistrates of neighboring counties. As the day grew hot, they reached an inn, fearing to disturb the innkeeper, they ordered food to be served, showing Liu's consideration for others.
    • 尚书饭白而细诸人饭黑而麤 - The minister's rice was white and fine, while the others' rice was black and coarse, highlighting the difference in quality and treatment.
    • 检校营田往梁州先有乡人姓娄者为屯官犯赃都督许钦明欲决杀 - Inspecting the fields and heading to Liangzhou, there was a man named Lou who was a local official and had committed embezzlement. The military governor Xǔ Qīnmíng wanted to execute him, indicating a serious legal issue.
    • 令众乡人谒尚书欲救之 - Ordering the locals to petition the minister to save him, showing the community's effort to seek help.
    • 犯国法师徳当家儿子亦不能舍何况渠 - If even a minister's own son cannot be spared for breaking the law, how much less so for someone else, emphasizing the principle of impartiality.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 则天 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 千字文 - A classic Chinese educational text consisting of a thousand characters, often used to teach children to read.
    • 纳言 - A high-ranking official position in the Tang Dynasty, equivalent to a minister of state.
    • 兵部尚书 - The head of the Ministry of War in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 幷州 - An ancient Chinese state located in what is now Shanxi Province.
    • 许钦明 - A historical figure, likely a military governor or official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 检校 - A term used to describe an inspection or examination of a particular area or task.

    Proper Nouns

    • 则天 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 千字文 - A classic Chinese educational text consisting of a thousand characters.
    • 纳言 - A high-ranking official position in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 兵部尚书 - The head of the Ministry of War in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 幷州 - An ancient Chinese state located in what is now Shanxi Province.
    • 许钦明 - A historical figure, likely a military governor or official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 检校 - A term used to describe an inspection or examination of a particular area or task.

    Young and wise, talented, with exceptional skill and multitasking ability, he could draw circles with his left hand and squares with his right, recite the classics and histories orally, and count a flock of sheep with his eyes. Simultaneously, he composed a forty-character poem in one go, wrote a five-character quatrain, and completed six other tasks at once, earning him the title of "divine child."

    A man from Bingzhou named Mao Jun gave birth to a son at the age of four. Empress Wu summoned him to test his ability to write the Thousand Character Classic, which he could recite from memory. She bestowed upon him clothes and sent him back, and people believed he was possessed by a spirit. Afterward, his whereabouts were unknown.

    Liu Shide, a man from Zhengzhou and a minister of war, was sent to Bingzhou. Along the way, he was accompanied by the magistrates of neighboring counties. As the day grew hot, they reached an inn, fearing to disturb the innkeeper, they ordered food to be served. The minister's rice was white and fine, while the others' rice was black and coarse. He called the innkeeper and scolded him, saying, "Why is there such a difference in the quality of the rice?"
