The innkeeper, fearing to offend, replied, "We happened to mill the rice carelessly, and I am at fault." Liu Shide said, "If the guests are hurried, the host is also hurried, and what harm is there?" He then exchanged the coarse rice for the fine rice and ate it.

    Inspecting the fields and heading to Liangzhou, there was a man named Lou who was a local official and had committed embezzlement. The military governor Xǔ Qīnmíng wanted to execute him. Liu Shide ordered the locals to petition him to save him. Liu Shide said, "If even a minister's own son cannot be spared for breaking the law, how much less so for someone else?" The next day, at a banquet, the military governor and Liu Shide sat together. Liu Shide said, "I heard that someone has broken the law, and it is said to be a man from my hometown. I do not know him, but I will deal with him accordingly." [96]

    父为小儿时共牧牛耳都督莫以师徳寛国家法都督遽令脱枷至尚书切责之曰汝辞父娘求覔官职不能谨洁知复奈何将一楪槌饼与之曰噇却作个饱死去都督从此舍之后为纳言平章事又检校屯田行有日矣谘执事早出娄先足疾待马未来于光政门外横木上坐须臾有一县令不知其纳言也因诉身名遂与之并坐令有一子逺觇之走告曰纳言也令大惊起曰死罪纳言曰人有不相识法有何死罪令因诉云有左嶷以其年老眼暗奏解某夜书表状亦得眼实不暗纳
    言曰道是夜书表状何故白日里不识宰相令大惭曰愿纳言莫说向宰相纳言南元佛不说公左右皆笑使至灵州果驿上食讫索马判官谘意家浆水亦索不得全不抵承纳言曰师徳已上马与公料理往呼驿长责曰判官与纳言何到不与供给索杖来驿长惶怖拜伏纳言曰我欲打汝一顿大使打驿将纳碎事徒涴却名声若你州县道你即不存生命且放却驿将跪拜流汗狼狈而走娄目送之谓判官曰与公踬顿之矣众皆怪叹其行事皆此类浮休子曰司马徽刘寛无以加也 [97]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [97]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 父为小儿时共牧牛耳 - Colloquial expression meaning 'when my father was a child, he herded cattle with me.'
    • 都督莫以师徳寛国家法 - Formal statement meaning 'the prefect should not be lenient with national laws due to his virtue.'
    • 都督遽令脱枷至尚书切责之 - Means 'the prefect quickly ordered him to remove his枷 (a type of punishment device) and severely rebuked him in the presence of the chancellor.'
    • 汝辞父娘求覔官职不能谨洁知复奈何 - Means 'you left your parents to seek an official position but could not be clean and upright, what else can you do?'
    • 将一楪槌饼与之曰噇却作个饱死去 - Means 'he gave him a plate of pounded cakes and said, 'eat them and become a well-fed ghost.''
    • 都督从此舍之后为纳言平章事 - Means 'the prefect then forgave him, and later he became a chancellor.'
    • 检校屯田行有日矣 - Means 'there was a day when he was inspecting the屯田 (military farming) operation.'
    • 谘执事早出娄先足疾待马未来于光政门外横木上坐 - Means 'he consulted the officials and went out early, but due to a foot ailment, he waited for his horse at the横木 (horizontal beam) outside the光政门 (Guangzheng Gate).'
    • 有一县令不知其纳言也因诉身名遂与之并坐 - Means 'a county magistrate, not knowing he was the chancellor, told him his name and they sat together.'
    • 令有一子远觇之走告曰纳言也 - Means 'the magistrate's son saw from afar and ran to tell him, 'it's the chancellor.'
    • 纳言曰人有不相识法有何死罪 - Means 'the chancellor said, 'people don't know each other, what death crime is there?'
    • 令因诉云有左嶷以其年老眼暗奏解某夜书表状亦得眼实不暗 - Means 'the magistrate then complained, 'there was a person named Zuo Yi who, because of old age and poor eyesight, was relieved of his duties. He wrote a memorial at night, but his eyes were actually not dim.'
    • 纳言曰道是夜书表状何故白日里不识宰相 - Means 'the chancellor said, 'if he wrote a memorial at night, why didn't he recognize the chancellor during the day?'
    • 令大惭曰愿纳言莫说向宰相 - Means 'the magistrate was greatly ashamed and said, 'please, chancellor, don't tell the emperor.'
    • 众皆笑使至灵州果驿上食讫索马判官谘意家浆水亦索不得全不抵承 - Means 'everyone laughed, and when he was sent to Lingzhou, he indeed stayed at an inn and had food, but when he asked for a horse and water, he couldn't get them at all, and he didn't dare to blame anyone.'
    • 纳言曰师徳已上马与公料理往呼驿长责曰判官与纳言何到不与供给索杖来驿长惶怖拜伏 - Means 'the chancellor said, 'Chancellor Shide has already mounted his horse, let me handle it for you. Go and call the驿长 (postmaster) and scold him, 'why didn't you provide for the chancellor and the judge?' and ask for a stick.' The postmaster was terrified and knelt down.'
    • 纳言曰我欲打汝一顿大使打驿将纳碎事徒涴却名声若你州县道你即不存生命且放却驿将跪拜流汗狼狈而走 - Means 'the chancellor said, 'I wanted to beat you, but the大使 (ambassador) would beat the驿将 (postmaster) to death. It would only soil your reputation. If you are a prefect or county magistrate, then your life is not worth saving, but let's just let the postmaster go. He knelt down, sweating profusely, and ran away in a mess.'
    • 娄目送之谓判官曰与公踬顿之矣 - Means 'Liu looked after him and said to the judge, 'I've tripped you up.'
    • 众皆怪叹其行事皆此类浮休子曰司马徽刘寛无以加也 - Means 'everyone was surprised and praised his actions, which were all like this. The浮休子 (Floating Leisure Scholar) said, 'there is no one better than Sima Hui and Liu Kuan.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 都督 - Refers to a prefect or military governor, a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 纳言 - Refers to a chancellor, a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 平章事 - Refers to a chancellor, a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 检校屯田 - Refers to the inspection of military farming operations, a common duty of high-ranking officials.
    • 光政门 - Refers to the Guangzheng Gate, a gate in the imperial city of Chang'an.
    • 左嶷 - Refers to a person named Zuo Yi, likely a historical figure.
    • 灵州 - Refers to Lingzhou, a prefecture in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 驿 - Refers to an inn or post station, a common place for travelers to stay.
    • 大使 - Refers to an ambassador or high-ranking official.
    • 驿将 - Refers to a postmaster, a low-ranking official responsible for the post station.
    • 浮休子 - Refers to a scholar or philosopher, likely a historical figure.
    • 司马徽 - Refers to Sima Hui, a historical figure known for his wisdom and virtue.
    • 刘寛 - Refers to Liu Kuan, a historical figure known for his kindness and virtue.

    When my father was a child, we herded cattle together. The prefect should not be lenient with national laws due to his virtue. The prefect quickly ordered him to remove his枷 (a type of punishment device) and severely rebuked him in the presence of the chancellor. He said, "You left your parents to seek an official position but could not be clean and upright, what else can you do?" He gave him a plate of pounded cakes and said, "Eat them and become a well-fed ghost." The prefect then forgave him, and later he became a chancellor. There was a day when he was inspecting the屯田 (military farming) operation. He consulted the officials and went out early, but due to a foot ailment, he waited for his horse at the横木 (horizontal beam) outside the光政门 (Guangzheng Gate). A county magistrate, not knowing he was the chancellor, told him his name and they sat together. The magistrate's son saw from afar and ran to tell him, "It's the chancellor." The chancellor said, "People don't know each other, what death crime is there?" The magistrate then complained, "There was a person named Zuo Yi who, because of old age and poor eyesight, was relieved of his duties. He wrote a memorial at night, but his eyes were actually not dim." The chancellor said, "If he wrote a memorial at night, why didn't he recognize the chancellor during the day?" The magistrate was greatly ashamed and said, "Please, chancellor, don't tell the emperor." Everyone laughed, and when he was sent to Lingzhou, he indeed stayed at an inn and had food, but when he asked for a horse and water, he couldn't get them at all, and he didn't dare to blame anyone. The chancellor said, "Chancellor Shide has already mounted his horse, let me handle it for you. Go and call the驿长 (postmaster) and scold him, 'why didn't you provide for the chancellor and the judge?' and ask for a stick." The postmaster was terrified and knelt down. The chancellor said, "I wanted to beat you, but the大使 (ambassador) would beat the驿将 (postmaster) to death. It would only soil your reputation. If you are a prefect or county magistrate, then your life is not worth saving, but let's just let the postmaster go. He knelt down, sweating profusely, and ran away in a mess. Liu looked after him and said to the judge, "I've tripped you up." Everyone was surprised and praised his actions, which were all like this. The浮休子 (Floating Leisure Scholar) said, "There is no one better than Sima Hui and Liu Kuan." [97]

    英公李勣为司空知政事有一畨官者叅选被放来辞英公公曰明日早向朝堂见我来及期而至郎中并在傍畨官至辞英公频眉谓之曰汝长生不知事尚书侍郎我老翁不识日知嗔吃李日知杖你亦不是人妻子亦不礼汝遂放之自是令史无敢犯者设有稽失众共讁之
    　　兵部郎中朱前疑貌丑其妻有美色天后时洛中殖业坊西门酒家有婢蓬头垢面伛眉皤腹寝恶之状举世所无而前疑大悦之殆忘寝食乃知前世言宿瘤爱信不虚也夫人世嗜欲一何殊性前闻文王嗜昌楚王嗜芹葅屈到嗜芰曾晢嗜羊枣宋刘雍嗜疮痂本传曰雍诣前吴兴太守孟休灵休脱袜粘炙疮痂坠地雍俯而取之飡焉宋明帝嗜蜜渍蛦每啖数升是知海上逐臭之谈陈君爱丑之说何足怪欤夫亦其癖也 [98]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [98]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 司空知政事 - 司空 is an official title, and 知政事 means to oversee government affairs. Together, it refers to a high-ranking official in charge of governance.
    • 叅选被放 - 叅选 means to participate in an examination, and 被放 means to be dismissed or rejected. This phrase refers to a candidate who failed an examination.
    • 频眉 - A colloquial expression meaning to frown or to look displeased.
    • 汝长生不知事尚书侍郎 - A rhetorical statement meaning You, who are long-lived, do not even know how to serve as a Shangshu or a Shilang (high-ranking officials). This is a way of criticizing the candidate's incompetence.
    • 老翁不识日 - A metaphorical expression meaning an old man who doesn’t know how to tell time, implying foolishness or lack of wisdom.
    • 遂放之 - Means and so he was let go, referring to the decision to dismiss the candidate.
    • 令史无敢犯者 - Means no officials dared to defy him, indicating the fear and respect instilled by the official.
    • 稽失 - Means delay or failure, referring to administrative inefficiency.
    • 讁之 - Means to criticize or blame, indicating collective criticism.
    • 朱前疑貌丑 - A straightforward description of the official's unattractive appearance.
    • 宿瘤爱信不虚也 - A reference to a historical anecdote about a woman with a hump who was loved by King Xuan of Qi, suggesting that true love transcends physical appearance.
    • 嗜欲 - Means desires or cravings, referring to the various tastes and preferences of historical figures.
    • 举世所无 - Means something that no one in the world has, emphasizing the extreme ugliness of the servant girl.
    • 殆忘寝食 - Means almost forgetting sleep and food, indicating intense obsession.
    • 前世言 - Means stories from the past, referring to historical or legendary accounts.
    • 海上逐臭之谈 - A metaphorical expression meaning talk about chasing foul smells by the sea, implying absurd or irrational behavior.
    • 陈君爱丑之说 - Refers to a historical anecdote about a man who loved a servant girl despite her ugliness.
    • 癖 - Means a strong, often irrational preference or habit.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 英公李勣 - Refers to Li Jing (594–669), a prominent general and statesman of the Tang Dynasty, who served under several emperors and was known for his administrative and military prowess.
    • 司空 - An ancient Chinese official title, equivalent to a high-ranking minister or prime minister.
    • 尚书侍郎 - Refers to high-ranking officials in the central government, with 尚书 (Shangshu) being a senior minister and 侍郎 (Shilang) being a junior minister.
    • 宿瘤 - Refers to a historical figure, a woman with a hump who was loved by King Xuan of Qi, symbolizing the idea that true love transcends physical appearance.
    • 文王嗜昌 - Refers to King Wen of Zhou, who was said to have a particular fondness for a type of vegetable, symbolizing his unique tastes.
    • 楚王嗜芹葅 - Refers to a king of Chu who enjoyed a type of pickled vegetable, symbolizing his personal preferences.
    • 屈到嗜芰 - Refers to Qu Dao, a historical figure who enjoyed eating lotus root, symbolizing his refined tastes.
    • 曾晢嗜羊枣 - Refers to Zeng Xi, a disciple of Confucius, who was said to have a particular fondness for a type of fruit, symbolizing his personal preferences.
    • 宋刘雍嗜疮痂 - Refers to Liu Yong, a figure from the Song Dynasty, who was said to have a peculiar taste for dried scabs, symbolizing his eccentricity.
    • 海上逐臭之谈 - A metaphorical expression referring to absurd or irrational behavior, often used to criticize excessive or unusual preferences.
    • 陈君爱丑之说 - Refers to a historical anecdote about a man who loved a servant girl despite her extreme ugliness, symbolizing the idea that true love transcends physical appearance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 英公李勣 - Li Jing, a prominent general and statesman of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 司空 - An ancient Chinese official title, equivalent to a high-ranking minister or prime minister.
    • 尚书侍郎 - High-ranking officials in the central government, with 尚书 (Shangshu) being a senior minister and 侍郎 (Shilang) being a junior minister.
    • 宿瘤 - A historical figure, a woman with a hump who was loved by King Xuan of Qi.
    • 文王 - King Wen of Zhou, a legendary ruler of the Zhou Dynasty.
    • 楚王 - A king of Chu, a state during the Warring States period.
    • 屈到 - Qu Dao, a historical figure from the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 曾晢 - Zeng Xi, a disciple of Confucius.
    • 宋刘雍 - Liu Yong, a figure from the Song Dynasty.
    • 宋明帝 - Emperor Mingdi of the Song Dynasty.
    • 天后 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 洛中殖业坊西门酒家 - A specific location in Luoyang during the Tang Dynasty.

    Li Jing, the Duke of Ying, served as the Grand Administrator overseeing government affairs. One official who had failed an examination came to bid farewell to Li Jing. Li Jing said, "Come to the court tomorrow morning to see me." When the time came, the official arrived, and the Langzhong (a junior minister) was also present. The official bowed to Li Jing, who frowned and said, "You, who are long-lived, do not even know how to serve as a Shangshu or a Shilang. I, an old man, don’t even know how to tell time. I will be angry and beat you, and you are not even a human being. I will not respect your wife, and so he was let go." From then on, no officials dared to defy him, and if there was any delay or failure, the officials would collectively criticize him.

    Zhu Qianyi, the Langzhong of the Ministry of War, was unattractive, but his wife was beautiful. During the reign of Empress Wu, in the Xizhi Fang of Luoyang, there was a servant girl who was unkempt, with a hump and a large belly, and her ugliness was unmatched in the world. Yet Zhu Qianyi was greatly pleased with her and almost forgot sleep and food. Thus, he knew that the stories from the past about the love of the humped woman were not false.

    People in the world have such different tastes and preferences. I have heard that King Wen of Zhou loved a type of vegetable, the king of Chu loved a type of pickled vegetable, Qu Dao loved lotus root, Zeng Xi loved a type of fruit, and Liu Yong of the Song Dynasty loved dried scabs. According to historical records, Liu Yong went to see the governor of Wu Prefecture, Meng Rui, who had removed his shoes and stuck dried scabs to the ground. Liu Yong bent down and ate them. The Song Emperor Mingdi loved to eat candied cicadas and would eat several bowls at a time. Thus, we know that the talk about chasing foul smells by the sea and the story of the man who loved the ugly girl are not strange. It is simply a matter of personal preference. [98]

    太宗时西国进一胡善弹琵琶作一曲琵琶拨倍麄上每不欲畨人胜中国乃置酒高防使罗黑黑隔帷聴之一遍而得谓胡人曰此曲吾宫人能之取大琵琶遂于帷下令黒黑弹之不遗一字胡人谓是宫女也惊叹辞去西国闻之降者数十国
    　　王沂者平生不解管忽旦睡至夜乃寤索琵琶之成数曲一名雀啅蛇一名胡王调一名葫苑人不识闻聴之者莫不流泪其妹请学之乃教数声须臾总忘不复成曲
    　　周有婆罗门僧恵范奸矫狐魅挟邪作蛊咨趄鼠左道弄权则天以为圣僧赏赉甚重太平以为梵王接纳弥优生其羽翼长其光价孝和临朝常乗官马往还宫掖太上登极从以给使出入禁门每入即赐绫罗金银器物气岸甚高风神傲诞内府珍宝积在僧家矫说祅祥妄陈祸福神武斩之京师称快 [99]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [99]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 作一曲 - Composed a piece or played a piece.
    • 拨倍麄 - Describes the strings and plectrum of the pipa, indicating they were thicker than usual.
    • 畨人胜中国 - Foreigners surpassing the Chinese.
    • 置酒高防 - Setting up a banquet for a high-ranking official or grand gathering.
    • 隔帷聴之一遍而得 - Heard it once through a curtain and memorized it.
    • 此曲吾宫人能之 - This piece can be played by our palace musicians.
    • 不遗一字 - Not missing a single note.
    • 西国闻之降者数十国 - Several dozen Western countries submitted after hearing this.
    • 管 - Stringed instruments.
    • 忽旦睡至夜乃寤 - Suddenly fell asleep in the morning and woke up at night.
    • 索琵琶之成数曲 - Requested a large pipa and composed several pieces.
    • 一名雀啅蛇 - A specific name for one of the pieces, which can be left untranslated.
    • 一名胡王调 - Another specific name for a piece, which can be left untranslated.
    • 一名葫苑人不识 - People in the garden of hawthorns did not recognize it.
    • 闻聴之者莫不流泪 - Those who heard it all shed tears.
    • 其妹请学之乃教数声 - His sister asked to learn it, and he taught her a few sounds.
    • 须臾总忘不复成曲 - In a short while, she completely forgot and could not form a complete piece.
    • 挟邪作蛊 - Holding onto evil and creating witchcraft.
    • 咨趄鼠 - Being indecisive like a mouse.
    • 左道弄权 - Using heretical ways to manipulate power.
    • 生其羽翼长其光价 - Grew his influence and increased his fame and value.
    • 孝和临朝常乗官马往还宫掖 - Emperor Shang, when he was in power, often rode an official horse back and forth in the palace quarters.
    • 太上登极从以给使出入禁门 - When Emperor Ruizong ascended the throne, he followed with servants in and out of the palace gates.
    • 每入即赐绫罗金银器物 - Every time he entered, he was given silk, gauze, gold, and silver objects.
    • 气岸甚高风神傲诞 - Had a very high bearing and an arrogant spirit.
    • 内府珍宝积在僧家 - The treasures of the imperial treasury were accumulated in the monk's household.
    • 矫说祅祥妄陈祸福 - Falsely claiming omens and recklessly predicting calamities and blessings.
    • 神武斩之京师称快 - Emperor Xuanzong executed him, and the capital cheered.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太宗 - Refers to Emperor Taizong of Tang, the second emperor of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 西国 - Refers to Western countries, likely neighboring states or distant lands.
    • 胡人 - Refers to foreigners, particularly those from the West.
    • 罗黑黑 - A name of a person, possibly a palace musician or official.
    • 王沂 - A name of a person, likely a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 则天 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 太平 - Refers to Princess Taiping, a powerful figure during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 孝和 - Refers to Emperor Shang of Tang, the fourth emperor of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 太上 - Refers to Emperor Ruizong, the fifth emperor of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 神武 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong, the seventh emperor of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 婆罗门僧 - Refers to a Brahmin monk, indicating the presence of Indian religious figures in China.
    • 左道 - Refers to heretical or unorthodox practices, often associated with magic or witchcraft.
    • 内府 - Refers to the imperial treasury, the repository of the state's wealth.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 西国 - Western countries.
    • 胡人 - Foreigners from the West.
    • 罗黑黑 - A palace musician or official.
    • 王沂 - A historical figure or fictional character.
    • 则天 - Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 太平 - Princess Taiping.
    • 孝和 - Emperor Shang of Tang.
    • 太上 - Emperor Ruizong.
    • 神武 - Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 婆罗门僧 - A Brahmin monk.
    • 左道 - Heretical practices.
    • 内府 - Imperial treasury.

    During the reign of Emperor Taizong, a foreigner from the West presented a piece of music played on the pipa. The strings and plectrum of the pipa were thicker than usual. The emperor always did not want foreigners to surpass the Chinese in musical skill. Therefore, he set up a banquet with high-ranking officials and ordered a palace musician named Luo Heihei to listen to the piece through a curtain. Luo Heihei heard it once and memorized it perfectly. The emperor then told the foreigner, "This piece can be played by our palace musicians." He took a large pipa and ordered Luo Heihei to play it through the curtain. Luo Heihei played the piece without missing a single note. The foreigner was amazed and thought that the player was a palace girl, and he was astonished and left. Several dozen Western countries submitted after hearing this.

    Wang Yi, who never understood stringed instruments, suddenly fell asleep in the morning and woke up at night. He then requested to play the pipa and composed several pieces. One of them was called "Quail Chattering Snake," another was called "Tribal King Tune," and another was called "Hawthorn Garden." People in the Hawthorn Garden did not recognize it. Those who heard it all shed tears. His sister asked to learn it, and he taught her a few sounds. In a short while, she completely forgot and could not form a complete piece.

    During the Zhou Dynasty, there was a Brahmin monk named Huifan. He was cunning and deceitful, holding onto evil and creating witchcraft. He was indecisive like a mouse and used heretical ways to manipulate power. Empress Wu Zetian considered him a holy monk and gave him heavy rewards. Princess Taiping considered him a Buddhist king and treated him even better. He grew his influence and increased his fame and value. When Emperor Shang was in power, he often rode an official horse back and forth in the palace quarters. When Emperor Ruizong ascended the throne, he followed with servants in and out of the palace gates. Every time he entered, he was given silk, gauze, gold, and silver objects. He had a very high bearing and an arrogant spirit. The treasures of the imperial treasury were accumulated in the monk's household. He falsely claimed omens and recklessly predicted calamities and blessings. Emperor Xuanzong executed him, and the capital cheered. [99]

    道士史崇懐州河内县缝靴人也后度为道士侨假人也附太平为太清观主金仙玉真出俗立为尊师每入内奏请赏赐甚厚无物不赐授鸿胪卿衣紫罗裙帔握象笏佩鱼符出入禁闱公私避路神武斩之京中士女相贺
    　　岭南风俗家有人病先杀鸡鵞等以祀之将为修福若不差即次杀猪狗以祈之不差即次杀太牢以祷之更不差即是命不复更祈死则打鼓鸣钟于堂比至讫初死且走大呌而哭
    　　景云中有长发贺景自称五戒贤者同为妖者十余人于陆浑山中结草舎幻惑愚人子女倾家产事之绐云至心求者必得成佛景为金簿袈裟独坐暗室令愚者窃视云佛放光众皆摄伏縁于悬崖下烧火遣数人于半崖间披红碧纱为仙衣随风习飏令众观之诳曰此仙也各令着仙衣以飞就之即得成道尅日设斋饮中置莨菪子与众餐之女子好发者截取为剃头串仙衣临崖下视眼时时似衍一时时烧杀没取资财事败官司来检灰中得焦拳尸柩数百余人敕决杀景县官左降 [100]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [100]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 道士史崇懐州河内县缝靴人也 - Formal introduction of a Taoist named Shi Chongxuan, originally from Huai Prefecture's Heine County, who was a cobbler.
    • 后度为道士侨假人也 - Indicates that he later became a Taoist, implying a transformation in his life.
    • 附太平为太清观主 - Suggests that he aligned himself with the 'Great Peace' (太平) and became the head of the Taqing Temple.
    • 金仙玉真出俗立为尊师 - Refers to the deities Gold Immortal and Jade Truth, who are mentioned in the context of him being elevated to the status of a revered teacher.
    • 每入内奏请赏赐甚厚无物不赐 - Describes the generous rewards he received for his services.
    • 授鸿胪卿衣紫罗裙帔握象笏佩鱼符 - Details the official attire and symbols of his high rank.
    • 出入禁闱公私避路 - Indicates the respect and deference shown to him by both officials and commoners.
    • 神武斩之京中士女相贺 - Describes the execution of a person named Shenwu and the public celebration that followed.
    • 岭南风俗家有人病先杀鸡鵞等以祀之 - Describes a local custom in southern China where people sacrifice chickens, geese, and other animals to appease illness.
    • 将为修福若不差即次杀猪狗以祈之 - Continues the description of the sacrificial rituals, moving from chickens and geese to pigs and dogs.
    • 不差即次杀太牢以祷之 - Further escalation of the sacrifices, involving the most prestigious offering, the 'Tai Lao' (a bull, sheep, and pig).
    • 更不差即是命不复更祈死则打鼓鸣钟于堂 - Indicates that if the sacrifices do not work, the person is considered to be beyond help and is subjected to a ritualistic death by drumming and ringing of bells.
    • 比至讫初死且走大呌而哭 - Describes the funeral rites and the initial mourning process.
    • 景云中有长发贺景自称五戒贤者 - Introduces a figure named He Xuanjing, who claims to be a 'Five Precepts Sage' during the Jingyun era.
    • 同为妖者十余人于陆浑山中结草舎幻惑愚人子女 - Describes a group of about ten people, including He Xuanjing, who set up a makeshift shelter in Lu Hun Mountain to deceive simple-minded people and their children.
    • 倾家产事之 - Indicates that people spent all their wealth to follow these deceivers.
    • 绐云至心求者必得成佛 - Describes the deception that those who sincerely seek will achieve Buddhahood.
    • 景为金簿袈裟独坐暗室 - Describes He Xuanjing wearing a golden robe and sitting alone in a dark room.
    • 令愚者窃视云佛放光众皆摄伏 - Describes how he tricked the simple-minded into believing that he was emitting divine light.
    • 縁于悬崖下烧火遣数人于半崖间披红碧纱为仙衣随风习飏 - Describes a ritual where fire is lit at the edge of a cliff, and several people wear red and green silk cloths to appear as flying deities.
    • 令众观之诳曰此仙也各令着仙衣以飞就之即得成道 - Describes how the spectators are deceived into thinking they can fly and achieve enlightenment by wearing the 'flying' clothes.
    • 克日设斋饮中置莨菪子与众餐之 - Describes a ritual meal where a poisonous plant (莨菪子) is added to the food.
    • 女子好发者截取为剃头串仙衣 - Describes how women with long hair had their hair cut and used to make 'flying' clothes.
    • 临崖下视眼时时似衍一时时烧杀没取资财 - Describes the final stages of the ritual, where people are pushed to the edge of the cliff and killed, with their belongings seized.
    • 事败官司来检灰中得焦拳尸柩数百余人 - Describes the aftermath, where officials found hundreds of charred bodies in the ashes.
    • 敕决杀景县官左降 - Describes the punishment for He Xuanjing and the officials involved.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 道士 - Refers to Taoist practitioners.
    • 金仙玉真 - Deities in Taoist belief.
    • 金簿袈裟 - A golden robe, often associated with high-ranking religious figures.
    • 鸿胪卿 - A high-ranking official in the imperial court.
    • 禁闱 - Refers to the inner palace or imperial court.
    • 神武 - A person's name, possibly a government official or military leader.
    • 岭南 - Southern China, known for its distinct customs and beliefs.
    • 景云 - A reign title of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 五戒 - The Five Precepts in Buddhism, which are basic moral guidelines.
    • 太牢 - The most prestigious offering in ancient Chinese rituals, consisting of a bull, sheep, and pig.
    • 莨菪子 - A poisonous plant, likely used in the ritual to cause death.

    Proper Nouns

    • 史崇 - The name of the Taoist.
    • 太平 - A political or religious movement.
    • 太清观 - The name of the Taoist temple.
    • 金仙玉真 - Deities in Taoist belief.
    • 神武 - A person's name, possibly a government official or military leader.
    • 景云 - A reign title of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 贺景 - The name of the 'Five Precepts Sage'.
    • 陆浑山 - The location of the makeshift shelter.
    • 莨菪子 - A poisonous plant.
    • 左降 - A punishment involving demotion.

    The Taoist Shi Chongxuan, originally from Heine County in Huai Prefecture, was a cobbler before becoming a Taoist. He aligned himself with the "Great Peace" and became the head of the Taqing Temple, where he was elevated to the status of a revered teacher. He was frequently admitted to the imperial court to present requests and received generous rewards, with no object being denied him. He was granted the rank of Honglu卿, dressed in a purple robe and a silk sash, and held an elephant-shaped tablet and a fish-shaped emblem. Both officials and commoners showed him great respect, clearing the way for him wherever he went. Shenwu was executed, and the people of the capital celebrated.

    In southern China, there was a local custom where if someone fell ill, they would first sacrifice chickens, geese, and other animals to appease the illness. If this did not work, they would then sacrifice pigs and dogs. If still unsuccessful, they would offer the most prestigious sacrifice, the "Tai Lao" (a bull, sheep, and pig). If the sacrifices did not work, the person was considered beyond help and would be subjected to a ritualistic death by drumming and ringing of bells in the hall. After the funeral rites, the initial mourning process involved running and crying loudly.

    During the Jingyun era, there was a man named He Xuanjing with long hair who claimed to be a "Five Precepts Sage." He and about ten others set up a makeshift shelter in Lu Hun Mountain to deceive simple-minded people and their children. People spent all their wealth to follow these deceivers, believing that those who sincerely sought would achieve Buddhahood. He Xuanjing wore a golden robe and sat alone in a dark room, tricking the simple-minded into believing that he was emitting divine light. He arranged for fire to be lit at the edge of a cliff and had several people wear red and green silk cloths to appear as flying deities, blowing in the wind. He led the spectators to believe that they could fly and achieve enlightenment by wearing the "flying" clothes. On a fixed day, he arranged a ritual meal where a poisonous plant (莨菪子) was added to the food. Women with long hair had their hair cut and used to make "flying" clothes. People were pushed to the edge of the cliff and killed, with their belongings seized. When the officials came to investigate, they found hundreds of charred bodies in the ashes. He Xuanjing and the officials involved were punished with execution and demotion. [100]

    景龙中瀛州进一妇人身上起浮图塔庙诸佛形像按察使进之授五品其女妇留内道塲逆韦死后不知去处
    　　周证圣元年薛师名怀义造功徳堂一千尺于明堂北其中大像高九百尺鼻如千斛船中容数十人并坐夹纻以漆之五月十五起无遮大防于朝堂掘地深五丈以乱防为宫殿台阁屈竹为胎张施为桢葢又为大像金刚并坑中引上诈称从地涌出又刺牛血画作大像头头高二百尺诳言薛师膝上血作之观者填城溢郭士女云防内载钱抛之更相踏籍老少死者非 [101]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [101]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 授五品 - Bestowed the rank of Fifth Class Official, indicating a promotion in the imperial bureaucracy.
    • 无遮大会 - A grand religious gathering in Buddhism, often involving large-scale offerings and rituals.
    • 朝堂掘地 - Digging the ground in front of the imperial palace, likely for some ritual or symbolic purpose.
    • 乱防为宫殿台阁 - Using chaotic defenses as palaces and towers, suggesting a metaphorical or symbolic construction.
    • 屈竹为胎 - Bending bamboo to form the framework, referring to a construction technique.
    • 张施为桢盖 - Stretching out to form the frame and cover, describing a building process.
    • 大像金刚 - Large statues of the Buddha and bodhisattvas, often associated with Buddhist temples.
    • 刺牛血画作大像头 - Painting the head of a large statue with cow's blood, a ritualistic or artistic practice.
    • 观者填城溢郭 - The crowd filled the city and overflowed the city walls, indicating a massive gathering.
    • 士女云防 - Men and women gathered like clouds, emphasizing the large number of attendees.
    • 内载钱抛之 - People threw money inside, likely as offerings or donations.
    • 更相踏籍 - Trampling on each other, indicating chaos and disorder in the crowd.
    • 老少死者非 - The old and the young who died were not few, indicating a tragic outcome.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 景龙中 - The reign period of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang (707-710 AD).
    • 瀛州 - A region in ancient China, known for its strategic importance.
    • 逆韦 - An anachronistic reference to Wei Zhongxian, a eunuch during the late Ming dynasty, which is not historically accurate for the Tang period.
    • 周证圣元年 - The reign period of Empress Wu Zetian (696 AD), indicating the time period of the events described.
    • 薛师名怀义 - The name of a person involved in the events.
    • 明堂 - The imperial palace.

    Proper Nouns

    • 景龙中 - The reign period of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 瀛州 - A region in ancient China.
    • 逆韦 - An anachronistic reference to Wei Zhongxian, a eunuch during the late Ming dynasty.
    • 周证圣元年 - The reign period of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 薛师名怀义 - The name of a person involved in the events.
    • 明堂 - The imperial palace.

    During the Jinglong period, a woman from Yingzhou presented herself to the court, her body adorned with images of pagodas, temples, and various Buddhas. The governor-general submitted her to the emperor, and she was granted the rank of Fifth Class Official. Her daughter was kept within the inner court, but after the death of the notorious eunuch Wei Zhongxian, her whereabouts became unknown.

    In the first year of Zhengsheng during the Zhou dynasty, a monk named Huaiyi built a Hall of Merit and Virtue, one thousand feet tall, to the north of the imperial palace. Within this hall, there was a colossal statue of the Buddha, nine hundred feet high, with a nose as large as a thousand-capacity ship, capable of accommodating dozens of people sitting side by side. The statue was made of lacquered wood. On the fifteenth day of the fifth month, a grand assembly known as the "Unobstructed Great Gathering" was held in front of the imperial palace. The ground was dug to a depth of five zhang, and chaotic defenses were used as palaces and towers, with bamboo bent to form the framework and stretched out to create the frame and cover. Large statues of the Buddha and bodhisattvas were also erected and brought up from the pit, falsely claimed to have emerged from the earth. Cow's blood was used to paint the head of a large statue, which was two hundred feet high, with the claim that it was made from the blood of the monk Huaiyi's knees. The crowd filled the city and overflowed the city walls, with men and women gathered like clouds, throwing money inside and trampling on each other, resulting in the deaths of many old and young people. [101]

    一至十六日张像于天津桥南设斋二更功徳堂火起延及明堂飞焰冲天洛城光如昼日其堂作仍未半已高七十余尺又延烧金银库鐡汁流液平地尺余人不知错入者便即焦烂其堂煨烬尺木无遗至晓乃更设防暴风欻起裂血像为数百段浮休子曰梁武帝舍身同泰寺百官倾库物以赎之其夜欻电霹风雨晦防寺浮圗佛殿一时荡尽非理之事岂如来本意哉
    　　景云中西京霖雨六十余日有一胡僧名宝严自云有术法能止雨设坛塲诵经咒其时禁屠宰宝严用羊二十口马两匹以祭祈请经五十余日其雨更盛于是斩逐胡僧其雨遂止
    　　周圣厯年中洪州有胡超僧出家学道白山防有法术自云数百嵗则天使合长生药所费巨万三年乃成自进药于三阳宫则天服之以为神妙望与彭祖同夀改元为久视元年放超还山赏赐甚厚服药之后三年而则天崩 [102]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [102]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 一至十六日 - Indicates a specific time period, from the first to the sixteenth day.
    • 张像 - Refers to a religious image or statue, possibly of a deity.
    • 设斋 - Means to set up a vegetarian meal or fasting period, often for religious purposes.
    • 二更 - Refers to the second watch of the night, typically around midnight.
    • 功德堂 - A hall dedicated to merit-making or religious practices.
    • 火起延及 - Indicates that a fire started and spread.
    • 明堂 - A large, important hall, often used for religious or ceremonial purposes.
    • 飞焰冲天 - Describes flames shooting up into the sky.
    • 洛城 - Refers to Luoyang, a significant city in Chinese history.
    • 光如昼日 - Means the light was as bright as daylight.
    • 其堂作仍未半 - Indicates that the construction of the hall was not yet halfway completed.
    • 已高七十余尺 - States that the structure was already over seventy feet tall.
    • 又延烧 - Means the fire spread further.
    • 金银库 - Refers to a treasury or storage for gold and silver.
    • 铁汁流液 - Describes molten iron flowing like liquid.
    • 平地尺余 - Indicates that the molten iron spread across the ground, covering more than a foot.
    • 不知错入者便即焦烂 - Means that anyone who accidentally entered the area would be burned to a crisp.
    • 其堂煨烬尺木无遗 - Indicates that the hall was completely destroyed, with not a single piece of wood left.
    • 至晓乃更设防 - Means that measures were taken to prevent further damage until dawn.
    • 暴风欻起 - Describes a sudden, violent wind.
    • 裂血像为数百段 - Means that the bloodied image was torn into hundreds of pieces.
    • 浮休子曰 - Indicates a quote from a person named Fuhuizi.
    • 梁武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Liang, a ruler of the Southern Dynasties.
    • 舍身同泰寺 - Means that the emperor offered himself to the Tongtai Temple.
    • 百官倾库物以赎之 - Indicates that officials emptied their treasuries to redeem the emperor.
    • 其夜欻电霹雳风雨晦防 - Describes a night filled with lightning, thunder, and heavy rain, making it dark and stormy.
    • 寺浮图佛殿一时荡尽 - Means that the temple's pagoda and Buddha hall were completely destroyed.
    • 非理之事岂如来本意哉 - Questions whether such irrational events align with the Buddha's original intentions.
    • 景云中 - Refers to the Jingyun period, a reign title of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 西京 - Refers to Chang'an, the western capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 霖雨六十余日 - Means that it rained continuously for more than sixty days.
    • 胡僧 - Refers to a foreign monk.
    • 宝严 - The name of the monk.
    • 有术法能止雨 - Means that the monk claimed to have a method to stop the rain.
    • 设坛场诵经咒 - Indicates that the monk set up an altar and chanted scriptures and incantations.
    • 禁屠宰 - Means that slaughtering animals was forbidden.
    • 宝严用羊二十口马两匹以祭祈请 - Indicates that the monk used twenty sheep and two horses for sacrifice and prayer.
    • 经五十余日 - Means that the ritual lasted for more than fifty days.
    • 其雨更盛 - Indicates that the rain became even heavier.
    • 于是斩逐胡僧 - Means that the monk was executed and driven away.
    • 其雨遂止 - Indicates that the rain finally stopped.
    • 周圣历年中 - Refers to the Shengli period, a reign title of Emperor Zhongzong of Zhou.
    • 洪州 - Refers to a region in southern China.
    • 胡超僧 - Refers to a foreign monk named Chao.
    • 出家学道 - Means that the monk left home to study the Dharma.
    • 白鹿山 - Refers to a mountain in Jiangxi Province.
    • 有法术 - Indicates that the monk claimed to have magical powers.
    • 自云数百岁 - Means that the monk claimed to be hundreds of years old.
    • 天使合长生药 - Indicates that the monk claimed to have a recipe for an elixir of immortality.
    • 所费巨万 - Means that the cost was enormous.
    • 三年乃成 - Indicates that it took three years to complete.
    • 自进药于三阳宫 - Means that the monk presented the elixir to the Sanyang Palace.
    • 则天服之 - Indicates that Empress Wu Zetian took the elixir.
    • 以为神妙 - Means that she believed it to be miraculous.
    • 望与彭祖同寿 - Indicates that she hoped to live as long as Peng Zu, a legendary figure known for longevity.
    • 改元为久视元年 - Means that she changed the era name to 'Jiushi' (Long Vision) in the first year.
    • 放超还山 - Indicates that the monk was allowed to return to the mountains.
    • 赏赐甚厚 - Means that he received generous rewards.
    • 服药之后三年而则天崩 - Indicates that Empress Wu Zetian died three years after taking the elixir.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张像 - Likely refers to a religious image, possibly of a deity or a revered figure.
    • 设斋 - A common practice in Chinese Buddhism and Taoism, involving fasting and vegetarian meals for religious purification.
    • 功德堂 - A hall where religious merit is accumulated through various practices.
    • 明堂 - A significant architectural structure used for religious and ceremonial purposes.
    • 洛城 - Luoyang, a major city in Chinese history, particularly during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 梁武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liang, a ruler known for his devotion to Buddhism.
    • 舍身同泰寺 - A famous incident where Emperor Wu of Liang offered himself to the Tongtai Temple.
    • 百官倾库物以赎之 - A historical event where officials spent their wealth to redeem the emperor.
    • 寺浮图佛殿一时荡尽 - A dramatic event where a temple's pagoda and Buddha hall were destroyed.
    • 景云中 - The Jingyun period, a reign title of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 西京 - Chang'an, the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 霖雨六十余日 - A significant weather event that caused widespread disruption.
    • 胡僧 - Foreign monks, often associated with Indian or Central Asian traditions.
    • 宝严 - The name of a specific monk.
    • 设坛场诵经咒 - A common ritual in Chinese Buddhism involving setting up an altar and chanting scriptures.
    • 禁屠宰 - A temporary ban on slaughtering animals, often imposed during religious observances.
    • 周圣历年中 - The Shengli period, a reign title of Emperor Zhongzong of Zhou.
    • 洪州 - A region in southern China, known for its cultural and religious significance.
    • 胡超僧 - A foreign monk named Chao.
    • 白鹿山 - A mountain in Jiangxi Province, known for its religious and cultural importance.
    • 则天 - Empress Wu Zetian, a powerful and influential ruler of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 三阳宫 - A palace where the empress resided.
    • 彭祖 - A legendary figure known for his extraordinary longevity.
    • 久视元年 - The first year of the 'Jiushi' era, a reign title of Empress Wu Zetian.

    From the first to the sixteenth day, an image was set up for a vegetarian meal at the southern end of Tianjin Bridge, and during the second watch, a fire broke out in the Hall of Merit, spreading to the Bright Hall. The flames shot up into the sky, illuminating the city of Luoyang as brightly as daylight. The hall's construction was not yet halfway completed, yet it had already reached a height of over seventy feet. The fire then spread to the treasury of gold and silver, and molten iron flowed like liquid across the ground, covering more than a foot. Anyone who accidentally entered the area would be burned to a crisp. The hall was completely destroyed, with not a single piece of wood left. Measures were taken to prevent further damage until dawn, but a sudden, violent wind arose, tearing the bloodied image into hundreds of pieces. Fuhuizi said, "Emperor Wu of Liang offered himself to the Tongtai Temple, and officials emptied their treasuries to redeem him. That night, lightning struck, thunder roared, and heavy rain made it dark and stormy, destroying the temple's pagoda and Buddha hall in an instant. Such irrational events, how could they align with the Buddha's original intentions?"

    During the Jingyun period, in the western capital, it rained continuously for more than sixty days. A foreign monk named Bao Yan claimed to have a method to stop the rain. He set up an altar and chanted scriptures and incantations, and at that time, slaughtering animals was forbidden. Bao Yan used twenty sheep and two horses for sacrifice and prayer. After more than fifty days, the rain became even heavier, and he was executed and driven away. The rain finally stopped.

    During the Shengli period of the Zhou Dynasty, in the region of Hongzhou, a foreign monk named Chao left home to study the Dharma at White Deer Mountain. He claimed to have magical powers and said he was hundreds of years old. He claimed to have a recipe for an elixir of immortality, which cost an enormous amount. It took three years to complete, and he presented the elixir to the Sanyang Palace. Empress Wu Zetian took the elixir, believing it to be miraculous, and hoped to live as long as Peng Zu. She changed the era name to "Jiushi" (Long Vision) in the first year, allowed Chao to return to the mountains, and rewarded him generously. Three years after taking the elixir, Empress Wu Zetian died. [102]

    则天时调猫儿与鹦鹉同器食命御史彭先觉监遍示百官及天下考使传看未遍猫儿饥遂齩杀鹦武以餐之则天甚愧武者国姓殆不祥之征也
    　　裴炎为中书令时徐敬业欲反令骆宾王画计取裴炎同起事宾王足踏壁静思食顷乃为謡曰一片火两片火绯衣小儿当殿坐教炎庄上小儿诵之幷都下童子皆唱炎乃访学者令解之召宾王至数啖以宝物锦绮皆不言又赂以音乐女妓骏马亦不语乃对古忠臣烈士圗共观之见司马宣王宾王歘然起曰此英雄丈夫也即说自古大臣执政多移社稷炎大喜宾王曰但不知謡防何如耳炎以謡言片火绯衣之事白宾王即下北靣而拜曰此真人矣遂与敬业等合谋杨州兵起炎从内应书与敬业等合谋惟有青鵞人【阙】有告者朝廷莫之能则天曰此青字者十二月鵞字者我自与也遂诛炎敬业等寻败
    　　逆韦之妹妈太和之妻号七姨信邪见豹头枕以辟邪白泽枕以去魅作伏熊枕以为宜男太和死嗣虢王娶之韦之败也虢王斫七姨头送朝堂则知辟邪之枕无効矣 [103]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [103]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 則天時 - Refers to the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 調猫兒與鹦鹉同器食 - A literal description of Empress Wu Zetian's unusual act of feeding a cat and a parrot together in the same container.
    • 命御史彭先覺監遍示百官及天下考使传看 - Empress Wu Zetian ordered the censor Peng Xianjue to supervise the display of the cat and parrot to all officials and imperial examiners across the empire.
    • 未遍猫儿饥遂齩杀鹦武以餐之 - The cat, not yet fed, bit and killed the parrot, eating it.
    • 則天甚愧 - Empress Wu Zetian felt great shame.
    • 武者国姓殆不祥之征也 - "Wu" is the imperial surname, and this event is a sign of bad omen.
    • 裴炎為中書令時 - Refers to the time when Pei Yan was the Chief Councillor of State.
    • 徐敬业欲反 - Xu Jingye intended to rebel.
    • 令骆宾王画计取裴炎同起事 - Xu Jingye instructed Luo Binwang to devise a plan to recruit Pei Yan to join the rebellion.
    • 宾王足踏壁静思食顷乃为謡曰 - Luo Binwang stood against the wall, deep in thought, and after a meal's time composed a rhyme.
    • 一片火两片火绯衣小儿当殿坐 - A rhyme about a red-clothed child sitting on the throne, symbolizing the emperor.
    • 教炎庄上小儿诵之幷都下童子皆唱 - Pei Yan's children and children in the capital city were taught to recite the rhyme.
    • 访学者令解之 - Pei Yan sought scholars to interpret the rhyme.
    • 召宾王至数啖以宝物锦绮皆不言 - Pei Yan summoned Luo Binwang, offering him valuable gifts and silks, but Luo remained silent.
    • 赂以音乐女妓骏马亦不语 - Pei Yan offered Luo music, beautiful women, and fine horses, but Luo still said nothing.
    • 对古忠臣烈士圗共观之 - They looked at pictures of ancient loyal ministers and martyrs together.
    • 见司马宣王宾王歘然起曰 - Seeing Sima Xuanwang (Sima Yi), Luo suddenly stood up and said.
    • 但不知謡防何如耳 - Luo only wondered what the rhyme meant.
    • 此青字者十二月鵞字者我自与也 - "Qing" (青) refers to the eleventh month, and "e" (鵞) refers to the emperor himself.
    • 遂诛炎 - Pei Yan was executed.
    • 敬业等寻败 - Xu Jingye and others were soon defeated.
    • 逆韦之妹妈太和之妻号七姨 - The sister of Wu Zetian, married to Taihe, was known as "Qiyi."
    • 信邪见豹头枕以辟邪白泽枕以去魅作伏熊枕以为宜男 - She believed in superstitions, using a leopard-head pillow to ward off evil, a Bai Ze pillow to remove spirits, and a bear pillow to ensure the birth of a son.
    • 太和死嗣虢王娶之 - After Taihe's death, his son, the Prince of Guo, married her.
    • 韦之败也虢王斫七姨头送朝堂 - When the Wu clan fell, the Prince of Guo cut off Qiyi's head and sent it to the court.
    • 则知辟邪之枕无効矣 - Thus, it was known that the evil-warding pillows were ineffective.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 则天 - Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history, known for her political acumen and controversial reign.
    • 裴炎 - Pei Yan, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty, who was involved in political intrigues.
    • 徐敬业 - Xu Jingye, a Tang general who led a rebellion against Wu Zetian.
    • 骆宾王 - Luo Binwang, a renowned poet and scholar who wrote the "Inscription of the Lament of the Chrysanthemum Pavilion" and was involved in the rebellion against Wu Zetian.
    • 司马宣王 - Sima Xuanwang (Sima Yi), a famous general and statesman of the Three Kingdoms period, known for his strategic brilliance.
    • 豹头枕 - A pillow shaped like a leopard's head, believed to ward off evil.
    • 白泽枕 - A pillow named after the Bai Ze, a mythical creature that could speak to humans and reveal the nature of all spirits.
    • 伏熊枕 - A pillow shaped like a bear, believed to ensure the birth of a male child.

    Proper Nouns

    • 则天 - Wu Zetian.
    • 裴炎 - Pei Yan.
    • 徐敬业 - Xu Jingye.
    • 骆宾王 - Luo Binwang.
    • 司马宣王 - Sima Xuanwang (Sima Yi).
    • 太和 - Taihe.
    • 嗣虢王 - The Prince of Guo.
    • 七姨 - Qiyi.
    • 豹头枕 - Leopard-head pillow.
    • 白泽枕 - Bai Ze pillow.
    • 伏熊枕 - Bear pillow.

    During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, she ordered a cat and a parrot to be fed together in the same container, and commanded the censor Peng Xianjue to supervise and display this to all officials and imperial examiners across the empire. Before the display was complete, the cat, feeling hungry, bit and killed the parrot, eating it. Empress Wu Zetian felt great shame, for "Wu" was her imperial surname, and this event was a sign of bad omen.

    At the time when Pei Yan was the Chief Councillor of State, Xu Jingye intended to rebel and instructed Luo Binwang to devise a plan to recruit Pei Yan to join the rebellion. Luo Binwang stood against the wall, deep in thought, and after a meal's time composed a rhyme: "One piece of fire, two pieces of fire, a red-clothed child sits on the throne." He taught Pei Yan's children and children in the capital city to recite the rhyme. Pei Yan then sought scholars to interpret the rhyme. He summoned Luo Binwang, offering him valuable gifts and silks, but Luo remained silent. Pei Yan also offered Luo music, beautiful women, and fine horses, but Luo still said nothing. They then looked at pictures of ancient loyal ministers and martyrs together. Seeing Sima Xuanwang (Sima Yi), Luo suddenly stood up and said, "This is a true hero!" He explained that throughout history, many ministers had changed the fate of the nation. Pei Yan was greatly pleased. Luo Binwang said, "But I do not know what the rhyme means." Pei Yan explained the rhyme's meaning to Luo Binwang, who then knelt and bowed, saying, "This is truly a real person!" Pei Yan and Xu Jingye then joined in the conspiracy. When the rebellion in Yangzhou began, Pei Yan acted as an insider, writing to Xu Jingye and others to join the plot, but only the word "qing" (青) and "e" (鵞) were mentioned. Someone reported this to the court, but no one could understand it. Empress Wu Zetian said, "The character 'qing' refers to the eleventh month, and 'e' refers to myself." She then executed Pei Yan, and Xu Jingye and others were soon defeated.

    The sister of Wu Zetian, married to Taihe, was known as "Qiyi." She believed in superstitions, using a leopard-head pillow to ward off evil, a Bai Ze pillow to remove spirits, and a bear pillow to ensure the birth of a male child. After Taihe's death, his son, the Prince of Guo, married her. When the Wu clan fell, the Prince of Guo cut off Qiyi's head and sent it to the court. Thus, it was known that the evil-warding pillows were ineffective. [103]

    后魏高流之为除州刺史决滹沱河水绕城破一古墓得铭曰吾死后三百年背底生流泉赖逢高流之迁吾上高原流为造棺椁衣物取其柩而改之
    　　东都丰都市在长夀市之东北初筑市垣掘得古冢土藏无砧甓棺木陈朽触之便散尸上着平上帻朱衣得铭云筮道居朝言近市五百年间于斯见矣当时达者参验是魏黄初二年所也
    　　寇天师谦之后魏时得道者也常刻石为记藏于嵩山上元初有洛州郜城县民因采药于山得之以献县令樊文言于州州以上闻高宣皇帝诏藏于内府其铭记又甚多奥不可解畧曰木子当天下又曰止戈龙又曰李代代不移宗又曰中鼎显真容又曰基千万歳所谓木子当天下者葢言唐氏受命也止戈龙者言太后临朝也止戈为武武天后氏也李代代不移宗者谓中宗中兴再新天地中鼎显真容者实中宗之庙讳真为濬圣之征諡得不信乎基千万嵗者基宗名也千万嵗葢厯数久长也后中宗御位樊文男钦贲以石记本上献上命编于国史 [104]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [104]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 决滹沱河水 - Diverting the Hu River, a tributary of the Yellow River.
    • 赖逢高流之迁吾上高原 - Relocating to a higher ground by the official Gao Liu.
    • 取其柩而改之 - Taking the coffin and re-burying it.
    • 筮道居朝言近市 - Divination methods associated with the tomb located near the market.
    • 五百年间于斯见矣 - The tomb was seen five hundred years later.
    • 达者参验是魏黄初二年所也 - Verified that the tomb was buried in the second year of Huangchu of the Wei Dynasty.
    • 寇天师谦之后魏时得道者也 - Taoist Master Kou Qian who attained the Dao during the Later Wei Dynasty.
    • 常刻石为记藏于嵩山 - Often carved records on stone and hid them in Songshan Mountain.
    • 上元初有洛州郜城县民因采药于山得之以献县令樊文言于州 - People from Gaocounty found the stone and presented it to the county magistrate.
    • 高宣皇帝诏藏于内府 - Emperor Gaoxuan ordered the stone to be stored in the imperial treasury.
    • 其铭记又甚多奥不可解 - The inscription contains many deep and mysterious meanings that cannot be easily understood.
    • 略曰木子当天下 - Briefly says 'wood son rules the world.'
    • 止戈龙 - Stop the sword dragon.
    • 李代代不移宗 - The Li clan will remain unchanged generation after generation.
    • 中鼎显真容 - The true form will be revealed.
    • 基千万岁 - The foundation will last for ten thousand years.
    • 木子当天下者葢言唐氏受命也 - 'Wood son rules the world' refers to the Tang dynasty receiving the mandate.
    • 止戈龙者言太后临朝也 - 'Stop the sword dragon' refers to the empress dowager reigning.
    • 李代代不移宗者谓中宗中兴再新天地 - The Li clan will remain unchanged generation after generation, referring to Emperor Zhongzong reviving the world.
    • 中鼎显真容者实中宗之庙讳真为濬圣之征諡得不信乎 - The true form will be revealed, actually referring to Emperor Zhongzong's temple name and is evidence of his deep wisdom.
    • 基千万歳者基宗名也千万歳葢厯数久长也 - The foundation will last for ten thousand years, referring to Emperor Xuanzong's name and is a sign of long-lasting.
    • 后中宗御位樊文男钦贲以石记本上献上命编于国史 - After Emperor Zhongzong ascended the throne, the person presented the stone record to the emperor, who ordered it to be compiled into the national history.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 后魏 - Refers to the Northern Wei Dynasty.
    • 高流之 - A name, likely a high-ranking official.
    • 滹沱河 - A river in northern China.
    • 除州 - A place name, likely a city or region.
    • 刺史 - A high-ranking administrative official.
    • 铭 - An inscription on a tomb or monument.
    • 寇天师 - A Taoist master named Kou Qian.
    • 嵩山 - A famous mountain in Henan Province, known for its cultural and religious significance.
    • 上元 - A reign title, likely referring to the Tang Dynasty.
    • 洛州 - A place name, likely a city or region.
    • 郜城县 - A place name, likely a city or region.
    • 县令 - A county magistrate.

    During the Later Wei Dynasty, Gao Liu served as the governor of Chuzhou. He diverted the waters of the Hu River around the city, which broke open an ancient tomb. Inside, they found an inscription that read: "After my death, three hundred years later, a spring will flow from my back. Thanks to Gao Liu, I was relocated to a high plateau, and he built a coffin and prepared burial clothes for me."

    In the eastern capital, the Fengdu Market was located in the northeast of the Changshou Market. When the city walls were first constructed, they unearthed an ancient tomb. The tomb was buried in the ground without any bricks or tiles. The coffin and wooden structure were old and decayed, and upon touching them, they crumbled. The corpse was wearing a flat cap and a red robe, and another inscription was found, which said: "Divination methods are practiced in the court, and tortoise shell divination is near the market. In five hundred years, it will be seen here." At that time, those who understood verified that the tomb was buried in the second year of Huangchu of the Wei Dynasty.

    Taoist Master Kou Qian, who attained the Dao during the Later Wei Dynasty, often carved records on stone and hid them in Songshan Mountain. At the beginning of the Shangyuan era, people from Gaocounty in Luozhou were collecting herbs in the mountains and found one of these stones. They presented it to the county magistrate, Fan Wenyan, who then forwarded it to the prefecture. The prefecture reported it to Emperor Gaoxuan, who ordered it to be stored in the imperial treasury. The inscription on the stone contained many deep and mysterious meanings that could not be easily understood. It briefly said: "Wood son rules the world," "stop the sword dragon," "Li clan generation after generation unchanged," "central tripod reveals true form," and "foundation ten thousand years."

    "Wood son rules the world" refers to the Tang dynasty receiving the mandate. "Stop the sword dragon" refers to the empress dowager reigning, as "stop the sword" means "peace" and "dragon" symbolizes the empress dowager. "Li clan generation after generation unchanged" refers to Emperor Zhongzong reviving the world, as "Li" is the surname of the Tang imperial family, and "generation after generation unchanged" means the Li clan will remain unchanged. "Central tripod reveals true form" actually refers to Emperor Zhongzong's temple name, as "central tripod" symbolizes the emperor, and "true form" refers to his true identity. "Foundation ten thousand years" refers to Emperor Xuanzong's name, as "ten thousand years" symbolizes long-lasting.

    After Emperor Zhongzong ascended the throne, Fan Wenyan's son, Qinben, presented the original stone record to the emperor, who ordered it to be compiled into the national history. [104]

    辰州东有三山鼎足直上各数千丈古老传曰邓夸父与日竞走至此煮饭此三山者夸父支鼎之石也
    　　宝厯元年乙巳嵗资州资阳县清弓邨山有大石可三间屋大从此山下忽然吼踊下山越涧却上坡可百步其石走时有锄禾人见之各手把锄趂至所止其石高二丈
    　　赵州石桥甚工磨礲密致如削焉望之如初日出云长虹饮涧上有勾栏皆石也勾栏并有石狮子龙朔年中高丽谍者盗二狮子去后复募匠修之莫能相类者至天后大足年黙啜破赵定州复欲南过至石桥马跪地不进但见一青龙卧桥上奋迅而怒已乃遁去
    　　永昌年太州敷水店南西坡白日飞四五里直塞赤水坡上桑畦麦陇依然仍旧
    　　邹骆驼长安人先贫常以小车推蒸饼卖之每胜业坊角有伏砖车触之即翻尘土涴其饼驼苦之乃将镢劚去十余砖下有瓷瓮容五斛许开看有金数斗于是巨富其子昉与萧佺交厚时人语曰萧佺驸马子邹昉骆驼儿非闗道徳合只为钱相知 [105]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [105]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 辰州东有三山鼎足直上各数千丈 - Describes three mountains in Chenzhou rising like tripod legs, each thousands of feet high.
    • 与日竞走 - Literally 'competing with the sun,' this idiom suggests a race or contest against the sun.
    • 煮饭此三山者夸父支鼎之石也 - Reference to the mythological story of the race between the sun and the sun god's grandson, where the three mountains are said to be the tripod legs that the sun god's grandson used to support the cauldron for cooking.
    • 宝厯元年乙巳嵗 - Refers to the first year of the Baoli era, which was the second year of the Jia Si cycle in the Chinese calendar system.
    • 资州资阳县清弓邨山有大石可三间屋大 - Describes a large stone in Qinggong Village, Ziyang County, Zizhou, that is as big as three rooms.
    • 忽然吼踊下山越涧却上坡可百步 - Describes the stone suddenly roaring and leaping down the mountain, crossing a ravine, and then climbing back up a slope for about a hundred steps.
    • 其石走时有锄禾人见之各手把锄趂至所止 - Describes how the stone was moving and how the farmers were chasing it with their hoes.
    • 赵州石桥甚工磨礲密致如削焉 - Describes the Zhaozhou Bridge as being very well crafted, finely polished, and closely fitted, like it was cut out.
    • 望之如初日出云长虹饮涧 - Describes how the bridge looks like a rainbow drinking from a ravine when viewed from a distance.
    • 上有勾栏皆石也 - Describes the railings on the bridge as all being made of stone.
    • 龙朔年中高丽谍者盗二狮子去后复募匠修之莫能相类者 - Describes how in the Longshuo era, a spy from Goguryeo stole two stone lions, and later, when they were replaced, no one could make them look the same.
    • 天后大足年默啜破赵定州复欲南过至石桥马跪地不进 - Describes how in the Dazhu year of Empress Wu Zetian's reign, the Turkic leader Mochuo broke through Zhaozhou and tried to go south, but his horse knelt down and refused to move when they reached the stone bridge.
    • 但见一青龙卧桥上奋迅而怒已乃遁去 - Describes how a green dragon appeared on the bridge, flaring up in anger before disappearing.
    • 永昌年太州敷水店南西坡白日飞四五里直塞赤水坡上桑畦麦陇依然仍旧 - Describes how in the Yongchang year, a white object flew several miles from the south-west slope of Fushui Inn in Taizhou, blocking the red water slope, where the mulberry fields and wheat ridges remained the same.
    • 邹骆驼长安人先贫常以小车推蒸饼卖之每胜业坊角有伏砖车触之即翻尘土涴其饼驼苦之乃将镢劚去十余砖下有瓷瓮容五斛许开看有金数斗于是巨富其子昉与萧佺交厚时人语曰萧佺驸马子邹昉骆驼儿非闗道徳合只为钱相知 - Describes how Zou Camel, a poor man from Chang'an, used to sell steamed buns with a small cart, and how he became wealthy after discovering a large amount of gold hidden under a brick wall. His son, Zou Fang, became friends with Xiao Quan, and people would say that Xiao Quan was a prince's son and Zou Fang was a camel boy, implying that their friendship was based on wealth rather than virtue.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 辰州 - Refers to Chenzhou, a city in Hunan Province.
    • 夸父 - Refers to the mythological figure Kuafu, who raced against the sun.
    • 宝厯元年 - Refers to the first year of the Baoli era, which was 825 AD.
    • 资州 - Refers to Zizhou, a prefecture in Sichuan Province.
    • 赵州 - Refers to Zhaozhou, a prefecture in Hebei Province.
    • 龙朔年 - Refers to the Longshuo era, which was 661-663 AD.
    • 天后大足年 - Refers to the Dazhu year of Empress Wu Zetian's reign, which was 695 AD.
    • 默啜 - Refers to Mochuo, a Turkic leader who invaded China during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 永昌年 - Refers to the Yongchang era, which was 689 AD.
    • 太州 - Refers to Taizhou, a prefecture in Henan Province.
    • 邹骆驼 - Refers to Zou Camel, a historical figure from Chang'an.
    • 萧佺 - Refers to Xiao Quan, a historical figure who was a prince's son.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This category contains notes related to syntactic ambiguities in the source text, but no specific source-language expressions are identified.

    In the east of Chenzhou, there are three mountains standing upright like tripod legs, each thousands of feet high. According to ancient legends, Kuafu raced against the sun and reached this place to cook rice; these three mountains are said to be the tripod legs that Kuafu used to support the cauldron.

    In the first year of the Baoli era (825 AD), in Qinggong Village, Ziyang County, Zizhou, there was a large stone as big as three rooms. It suddenly roared and leaped down the mountain, crossed a ravine, and then climbed back up a slope for about a hundred steps. When the stone was moving, farmers saw it and chased it with their hoes. The stone was two zhang (about 6.6 meters) high.

    The Zhaozhou Bridge is very well crafted, finely polished, and closely fitted, like it was cut out. From a distance, it looks like a rainbow drinking from a ravine. The railings on the bridge are all made of stone, and there are stone lions on the railings. In the Longshuo era (661-663 AD), a spy from Goguryeo stole two stone lions. Later, when they were replaced, no one could make them look the same. In the Dazhu year of Empress Wu Zetian's reign (695 AD), the Turkic leader Mochuo broke through Zhaozhou and tried to go south. When they reached the stone bridge, his horse knelt down and refused to move. A green dragon appeared on the bridge, flaring up in anger before disappearing.

    In the Yongchang year (689 AD), a white object flew several miles from the south-west slope of Fushui Inn in Taizhou, blocking the red water slope, where the mulberry fields and wheat ridges remained the same.

    Zou Camel, a poor man from Chang'an, used to sell steamed buns with a small cart. Every time he reached the corner of Shengye Lane, a hidden brick wall would overturn his cart, scattering dust and dirt on his buns. He was very distressed by this and decided to dig away the bricks with a hoe. After removing more than ten bricks, he found a large ceramic jar that could hold five dou (about 75 liters). Inside, he discovered several dou of gold, and he became very wealthy. His son, Zou Fang, became friends with Xiao Quan, and people would say that Xiao Quan was a prince's son and Zou Fang was a camel boy, implying that their friendship was based on wealth rather than virtue. [105]

    先天年洛下人牵一牛奔腋下有一人手长尺余巡坊而乞
    　　隋文皇帝时大宛国献千里马曵地号曰师子骢上置之马羣陆梁人莫能制上令幷羣驱来谓左右曰谁能驭之郎将裴仁基曰臣能制之遂攘袂向前去十余步踊身腾上一手撮耳一手抠目马战不敢动乃鞴乗之朝发西京暮至东洛后隋末不知所在唐文武圣皇帝勅天下访之同州刺史宇文士及访得其马老于朝邑市麺家挠硙尾焦秃皮内穿穴及见之悲泣帝自出长乐坡马到新丰向西鸣跃帝得之甚喜齿口并平饲以钟乳仍生五驹皆千里足也后不知所在矣
    　　徳州刺史张纳之一白马其色如练父雄为荆州刺史常乗雄薨子敬之为考功郎中改夀州刺史又乗此马敬之薨弟讷之从给事中相府司马改徳州刺史入为国子祭酒出为常州刺史至今犹在计八十余年极肥徤行骤脚不散 [106]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [106]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 先天年 - Era name 'Tianxian' during the Tang Dynasty; rendered as 'in the Tianxian era.'
    • 洛下人 - Refers to a person from Luoyang; translated as 'a person from Luoyang.'
    • 牵一牛奔腋下 - Describes a scene where a person is pulling a cow and running alongside it; translated as 'pulling a cow and running alongside it.'
    • 巡坊而乞 - Means 'patrolling the streets and begging'; translated as 'patrolling the streets and begging.'
    • 隋文皇帝时 - Refers to the time of Emperor Wen of Sui; translated as 'during the reign of Emperor Wen of Sui.'
    • 大宛国献千里马 - Describes a gift of a thousand-mile horse from the Da Yuan (Fergana) kingdom; translated as 'a thousand-mile horse was presented by the Da Yuan kingdom.'
    • 陆梁人莫能制 - Means 'no one could control the wild and unruly horse'; translated as 'no one could control the wild and unruly horse.'
    • 攘袂向前 - Means 'raising the sleeves and moving forward'; translated as 'raising his sleeves and moving forward.'
    • 去十余步 - Means 'after walking more than ten steps'; translated as 'after walking more than ten steps.'
    • 踊身腾上 - Means 'jumping up and mounting the horse'; translated as 'jumping up and mounting the horse.'
    • 一手撮耳一手抠目 - Describes a physical action where one hand grabs the ear and the other hand grabs the eye; translated as 'one hand grabbing the ear and the other hand grabbing the eye.'
    • 马战不敢动 - Means 'the horse trembled and dared not move'; translated as 'the horse trembled and dared not move.'
    • 鞴乗之 - Means 'harnessing and riding it'; translated as 'harnessing and riding it.'
    • 朝发西京暮至东洛 - Means 'leaving the western capital in the morning and arriving at the eastern capital in the evening'; translated as 'leaving the western capital in the morning and arriving at the eastern capital in the evening.'
    • 齿口并平 - Means 'its teeth and mouth were even and healthy'; translated as 'its teeth and mouth were even and healthy.'
    • 饲以钟乳 - Means 'fed with cinnabar'; translated as 'fed with cinnabar.'
    • 仍生五驹 - Means 'and gave birth to five foals'; translated as 'and gave birth to five foals.'
    • 皆千里足也 - Means 'all of which were thousand-mile horses'; translated as 'all of which were thousand-mile horses.'
    • 至今犹在 - Means 'it is still here today'; translated as 'it is still here today.'
    • 极肥健 - Means 'extremely fat and strong'; translated as 'extremely fat and strong.'
    • 行骤脚不散 - Means 'it runs quickly and its hooves do not scatter'; translated as 'it runs quickly and its hooves do not scatter.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 先天年 - The era name 'Tianxian' was used during the Tang Dynasty, specifically during the reign of Emperor Ruizong.
    • 洛下人 - Luoyang was a major city in Chinese history, known for its cultural and political significance.
    • 隋文皇帝 - Emperor Wen of Sui was the founder of the Sui Dynasty, known for his administrative reforms and military conquests.
    • 大宛国 - The Da Yuan (Fergana) kingdom was located in Central Asia and was known for its horses.
    • 千里马 - A thousand-mile horse is a legendary horse known for its speed and endurance.
    • 唐文武圣皇帝 - This refers to Emperor Taizong of Tang, known for his military prowess and governance.
    • 同州刺史宇文士及 - Yuwen Shi was a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 朝邑市麺家 - A reference to a specific location and profession, indicating a place where noodles were sold.
    • 挠硙 - This term refers to a type of mill used for grinding grain.
    • 尾焦秃皮内穿穴 - Describes the physical condition of the horse, indicating it was old and worn out.
    • 张纳之 - Zhang Na was a historical figure, possibly a military leader or official.
    • 白马 - A white horse, often symbolizing purity and nobility in Chinese culture.
    • 饭牛牧羊 - These terms refer to the occupations of feeding cattle and herding sheep, indicating humble labor.

    Proper Nouns

    • 先天年 - The era name 'Tianxian' (先天).
    • 洛下人 - A person from Luoyang.
    • 隋文皇帝 - Emperor Wen of Sui.
    • 大宛国 - The Da Yuan (Fergana) kingdom.
    • 千里马 - A thousand-mile horse.
    • 唐文武圣皇帝 - Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 同州刺史宇文士及 - Yuwen Shi, a Tang Dynasty official.
    • 朝邑市麺家 - A specific location and profession.
    • 张纳之 - Zhang Na, a historical figure.
    • 白马 - A white horse.

    In the Tianxian era, a person from Luoyang was pulling a cow and running alongside it, with another person having unusually long hands who was patrolling the streets and begging.

    During the reign of Emperor Wen of Sui, a thousand-mile horse was presented by the Da Yuan kingdom. It dragged its feet on the ground and was called the Lion-colored Pony. The emperor placed it among other horses, but no one could control the wild and unruly horse. The emperor ordered all the horses to be driven together and asked his attendants, "Who can control this horse?" The general Pei Renji replied, "I can control it." He then raised his sleeves and moved forward, after walking more than ten steps, he jumped up and mounted the horse. One hand grabbed the ear and the other hand grabbed the eye, the horse trembled and dared not move. He then harnessed and rode it, leaving the western capital in the morning and arriving at the eastern capital in the evening. After the end of the Sui Dynasty, its whereabouts were unknown. Emperor Taizong of Tang issued an edict to search for it, and the Tangzhou governor Yuwen Shi found the horse, which was old and worn out, living with a noodle seller in Chao County. The horse had a burnt tail and a bald skin with holes inside. When he saw it, he wept. The emperor himself went out to Changle Slope, where the horse arrived at Xin Feng, facing west, it neighed and leaped. The emperor was very pleased, its teeth and mouth were even and healthy, it was fed with cinnabar, and it gave birth to five foals, all of which were thousand-mile horses. Later, its whereabouts were unknown.

    Zhang Na, the governor of Dezhou, had a white horse whose color was like silk. His father, Zhang Xiong, was the governor of Jingzhou and often rode this horse. After Zhang Xiong's death, his son Zhang Jingzhi became a Langzhong and later the governor of Shouzhou, also riding this horse. After Zhang Jingzhi's death, his brother Zhang Naji, who was a Shangshu and later the governor of Dezhou, took over. Zhang Naji then served as the Guozijiu and later the governor of Changzhou. The horse is still here today, having lived for over eighty years, extremely fat and strong, running quickly and its hooves do not scatter. [106]

    广平宋察娶同郡游昌女察先代胡人也归汉三世矣忽生一子深目而高鼻疑其非嗣将不举须臾赤草马生一白驹察悟曰我家先有白马种絶已二十五年今又复生吾曾祖貌胡今此子复其先也遂养之故曰白马活胡儿此其谓也
    　　东海有蛇丘地险多渐洳众蛇居之无人民蛇或有人头而蛇身
    　　岭南有报寃蛇人触之即三五里随身即至若打杀一蛇则百蛇相集将蜈蚣自防乃免
    　　顾渚山頳石洞有緑蛇长可三尺余大类小指好栖树杪视之若鞶带纒于柯叶间无螫毒见人则空中飞
    　　山南五溪黔中皆有毒蛇乌而反鼻蟠于草中其牙倒勾去人数步直来疾如缴箭螫人立死中手即断手中足即断足不然则全身肿烂百无一活谓蝮蛇也有黄喉蛇好在舎上无毒不害人唯善食毒蛇食饱垂头直下滴沬地喷起变为沙虱中人为疾额上有大王字众蛇之长常食蝮蛇 [107]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [107]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 忽生一子深目而高鼻 - Literal description of a child with deep-set eyes and a high nose, indicating a different racial or ethnic background.
    • 疑其非嗣将不举 - Phrase expressing doubt about the legitimacy of the child and the intention to abandon him.
    • 赤草马生一白驹 - Metaphorical expression where a red-grass horse gives birth to a white colt, symbolizing an unexpected or miraculous event.
    • 吾曾祖貌胡 - Reference to the speaker's great-grandfather having a 'Hun' appearance, indicating a mixed heritage.
    • 遂养之故曰白马活胡儿 - Phrase explaining that the child was adopted, hence the name 'White Horse Saves the Hun Child.'
    • 蛇丘地险多渐洳 - Description of a dangerous and swampy area where snakes live.
    • 蛇或有人头而蛇身 - Description of a snake with a human head and a snake body, indicating a mythical creature.
    • 岭南有报寃蛇 - Reference to a snake in the southern regions that can cause harm if provoked.
    • 将蜈蚣自防乃免 - Suggestion that carrying a centipede can protect against the snake's attack.
    • 顾渚山頳石洞有緑蛇 - Description of a green snake living in a red stone cave in Gu Zhu Mountain.
    • 好栖树杪 - Phrase indicating that the snake likes to rest at the tips of trees.
    • 视之若鞶带纒于柯叶间 - Metaphorical description of the snake's appearance, likening it to a belt wrapped around branches and leaves.
    • 无螫毒见人则空中飞 - Description of a snake that is not venomous and flies away when it sees people.
    • 山南五溪黔中皆有毒蛇 - Statement about the presence of venomous snakes in the southern regions and the five streams of Qian.
    • 乌而反鼻 - Description of a snake with a black color and a reversed snout.
    • 蟠于草中 - Phrase indicating that the snake coils in the grass.
    • 其牙倒勾 - Description of the snake's teeth, which are curved backward.
    • 去人数步直来疾如缴箭 - Phrase describing the snake's sudden and rapid movement towards people.
    • 螫人立死 - Statement about the snake's deadly bite.
    • 中手即断手中足即断足 - Description of the snake's venom causing immediate amputation of limbs.
    • 不然则全身肿烂 - Phrase indicating that if the snake's bite does not cause immediate death, it leads to severe swelling and decay.
    • 百无一活 - Statement about the high mortality rate of those bitten by the snake.
    • 谓蝮蛇也 - Phrase identifying the snake as a venomous one.
    • 黄喉蛇好在舎上 - Description of a yellow-throated snake that likes to live on rooftops.
    • 无毒不害人 - Statement about the snake being non-venomous and harmless.
    • 唯善食毒蛇 - Phrase indicating that the snake primarily feeds on other venomous snakes.
    • 食饱垂头直下滴沬地喷起变为沙虱 - Description of the snake's behavior after eating, where it drools and transforms into sand fleas.
    • 中人为疾额上有大王字 - Description of the symptoms of being bitten by the snake, including a large 'king' character on the forehead.
    • 众蛇之长常食蝮蛇 - Statement about the snake being the leader among snakes and often preying on venomous ones.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 广平宋察 - A person from Guangping who married a woman from the same region.
    • 胡人 - Refers to people of non-Han Chinese ethnicity, often associated with the Xiongnu or other nomadic groups.
    • 归汉三世矣 - Indicates that the family has been living in Han territory for three generations.
    • 蛇丘 - A place name referring to a hill or area known for its snakes.
    • 岭南 - Refers to the southern regions of China, particularly the area south of the Five Ridges.
    • 顾渚山 - A mountain in Zhejiang Province known for its tea and scenic beauty.
    • 五溪黔中 - Refers to the five streams and the region of Qian, known for its dangerous environment and venomous snakes.
    • 黄喉蛇 - A specific type of snake known for its yellow throat and non-venomous nature.
    • 沙虱 - Refers to sand fleas, small parasitic insects that can cause skin irritation.
    • 众蛇之长 - A term indicating the dominant or leader snake among a group of snakes.

    Proper Nouns

    • 广平 - A place name in Hebei Province.
    • 宋察 - A person's name.
    • 游昌 - A person's name.
    • 胡人 - A term referring to people of non-Han Chinese ethnicity.
    • 蛇丘 - A place name.
    • 岭南 - A region in southern China.
    • 顾渚山 - A mountain in Zhejiang Province.
    • 五溪黔中 - A region in southern China.
    • 黄喉蛇 - A specific type of snake.
    • 沙虱 - A term for sand fleas.
    • 众蛇之长 - A term for the dominant snake.

    A man from Guangping named Song Cha married a woman from his own county, You Chang. His ancestors were of non-Han Chinese ethnicity, but they had lived in Han territory for three generations. Suddenly, he gave birth to a son with deep-set eyes and a high nose. He doubted that the child was legitimate and considered abandoning him. Shortly after, a red-grass horse gave birth to a white colt. Song Cha realized, "My great-grandfather had a 'Hun' appearance, and now this child resembles our ancestors." He decided to raise the child, hence the name "White Horse Saves the Hun Child."

    In the East Sea, there is a place called Snake Hill, which is dangerous and swampy, inhabited by many snakes. There are no people living there, and some of the snakes have human heads and snake bodies.

    In the southern regions, there is a snake known as the "Vindictive Snake." If someone provokes it, it will follow them for several miles and attack. If one kills a snake, a hundred snakes will gather. Carrying a centipede can protect against its attack.

    In Gu Zhu Mountain, there is a red stone cave where a green snake lives. The snake is about three feet long, similar in size to a small finger, and likes to rest at the tips of trees. When seen, it appears as if a belt is wrapped around branches and leaves. It is not venomous and flies away when it sees people.

    In the southern regions and the five streams of Qian, there are venomous snakes with black color and a reversed snout. They coil in the grass, and their teeth are curved backward. They can move rapidly towards people, and their bite is deadly. If they bite a person, the hand or foot will be amputated immediately. If not, the person will suffer severe swelling and decay. The mortality rate is extremely high, and they are known as venomous snakes. There is also a yellow-throated snake that likes to live on rooftops. It is non-venomous and harmless, but it primarily feeds on other venomous snakes. After eating, it drools and transforms into sand fleas, which can cause skin irritation. The symptoms of being bitten by this snake include a large "king" character on the forehead. This snake is considered the leader among snakes and often preys on venomous ones. [107]

    种黍来蛇烧羖羊角及头发则蛇不敢来
    　　隋绛州夏县树提家新造宅欲移之忽有蛇无数从室中流出门外其稠如箔上葢地皆遍时有行客云解符镇取桃枝四枝书符绕宅四面钉之蛇渐退符亦移就之蛇入堂中心有一孔大如盆口蛇入并尽令煎汤一百斛濯之经宿以鍫掘之深尺得古铜钱二十万贯因陈破铸新钱遂巨富蛇乃是古铜之精
    　　开元四年六月郴州马岭山侧有白蛇长六七尺黑蛇长丈余须臾二蛇鬭白者吞黑蛇到粗处口两嗌皆裂血流滂沛黑蛇头入啮白蛇肋上作孔头出二尺余俄而两蛇并死后十余日大雨山水暴涨漂破五百余家失三百余人
    　　左补阙毕干泰瀛州任丘人父母年五十自营生藏讫至父年八十五又自造棺稍高大嫌藏小更加砖二万口开藏欲修之有蛇无数时正月尚寒蛰未能动取蛇投一空井中仍受蛇不尽其蛇金色泰自与奴开之寻病而卒月余父母俱亡此开之不得其所也 [108]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [108]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 种黍来蛇烧羖羊角及头发 - Compound phrase describing a method to repel snakes by burning the horns of a goat and human hair while planting millet.
    • 稠如箔上 - Vivid simile comparing the density of the snakes to that of a woven mat (箔).
    • 陈破铸新钱 - Refers to the practice of melting down old coins and casting new ones, often associated with economic manipulation or fraud.
    • 巨富 - Term indicating extreme wealth, often used in a superlative sense.
    • 古铜之精 - Metaphorical expression suggesting that the snakes are manifestations of ancient copper, possibly referencing alchemical or supernatural beliefs.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 隋绛州夏县 - Refers to a specific location in ancient China during the Sui Dynasty, providing a historical context for the events.
    • 符镇 - Refers to the practice of using talismans or charms to ward off evil spirits or supernatural entities.
    • 桃枝 - Symbolizes protection against evil, as peach wood is traditionally believed to have protective properties in Chinese folklore.
    • 鍫掘之 - Refers to the use of a spade or shovel for digging, a common tool in ancient agricultural and construction practices.
    • 开元四年六月 - Specifies the year and month of an event during the Tang Dynasty, providing a precise historical timeframe.
    • 郴州马岭山侧 - Refers to a specific location in the Tang Dynasty, adding geographical context to the narrative.
    • 左补阙毕干泰 - Refers to a specific historical figure, a censor during the Tang Dynasty, who experienced a series of supernatural events.
    • 瀛州任丘 - Refers to another specific location in ancient China, providing additional historical context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 隋绛州夏县 - Specific location in the Sui Dynasty.
    • 符镇 - Practice of using talismans.
    • 桃枝 - Symbolic object with protective properties.
    • 鍫 - Tool for digging.
    • 开元四年六月 - Specific date in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 郴州马岭山侧 - Specific location in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 左补阙毕干泰 - Historical figure.
    • 瀛州任丘 - Specific location in ancient China.

    Planting millet and burning the horns of a goat and human hair would cause snakes to avoid the area.

    During the Sui Dynasty, in the county of Xuxi, the Shuti family had newly built a house and wanted to move it. Suddenly, an endless number of snakes flowed out of the room and covered the ground like a woven mat. At that time, a traveler claimed to understand the use of talismans. He took four peach branches, wrote a talisman on them, and nailed them around the four sides of the house. The snakes gradually retreated, and the talisman also moved with them. The snakes entered the center of the hall, where there was a hole as large as a basin's mouth. All the snakes entered the hole and disappeared. They then ordered a hundred buckets of boiling water to be poured over the area. After a night, they dug with a spade to a depth of one foot and found twenty million old coins. The coins were melted down and recast into new ones, leading to great wealth. The snakes were the spirits of ancient copper.

    In the fourth year of the Kaiyuan era, in June, in the mountains near Ma Ling in Chenzhou, there was a white snake about six or seven feet long and a black snake over ten feet long. After a short while, the two snakes fought, and the white snake swallowed the black snake. When it reached a thick part, both its mouths and throats split open, and blood poured out profusely. The black snake's head entered and bit the white snake's ribs, creating a hole. The black snake's head emerged two feet out, and soon both snakes died. After more than ten days, heavy rain caused a flood, and the rising water destroyed the homes of five hundred families and caused the loss of three hundred people.

    Left Censor Bi Gantai, from Renqiu in Yingzhou, had parents who were fifty years old and had prepared their own burial site. When his father was eighty-five, he built a coffin that was slightly larger, thinking the burial site was too small, and added twenty thousand bricks. When the coffin was opened for repairs, there were countless snakes inside. At that time, it was January, still cold, and the snakes were unable to move. They took the snakes and threw them into an empty well, but the snakes were still not all accommodated. The snakes were golden in color.
