Bi Gantai himself and his servants opened the coffin, and soon after, he fell ill and died. After a month, his parents also passed away. This was because the coffin was not properly closed. [108]

    沧州东光县宝观寺常有苍鹘集重阁每有鸽数千鹘冬中每夕取一鸽以暖足至晓放之而不杀自余鹰鹘不敢侮之
    　　太宗养一白鹘号曰将军取鸟常驱至于殿前然后击杀故名落鴈殿上恒令送书从京至东都与魏王仍取报日往反数回亦陆机黄耳之徒欤
    　　上元中华容县有象入庄家中庭卧其足下有槎人为出之象乃伏令人骑入深山以鼻豗土得象牙数十以报之
    　　吏部侍郎郑愔初托附来俊臣俊臣诛即托张易之易之被戮托韦庶人后附谯王竟被斩
    　　太子少保薛稷雍州长史李晋中书令崔湜萧志忠岑羲等并外饰忠鯾内藏谄媚翕肩屛气防痔折肢附太平公主并腾迁云路咸自以为得志保泰山之安七月三日破家身斩何异鹓鸯栖于苇苕大风忽起巢折卵破后之君子可不鍳哉 [109]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [109]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 常有苍鹘集重阁 - Literal meaning: 'often there are gray hawks gathering on the high pavilion.' Describes a recurring natural phenomenon.
    • 每有鸽数千 - Literal meaning: 'each time there are thousands of birds.' Indicates a large number of birds appearing regularly.
    • 冬中每夕取一鸽以暖足 - Literal meaning: 'in winter, each night they take one dove to warm their feet.' Suggests a specific behavior of the hawks during winter.
    • 至晓放之而不杀 - Literal meaning: 'until dawn, they release it without killing it.' Implies a non-lethal interaction between the hawks and the doves.
    • 自余鹰鹘不敢侮之 - Literal meaning: 'other eagles and hawks dare not bully them.' Indicates a hierarchical relationship among the birds.
    • 取鸟常驱至于殿前然后击杀 - Literal meaning: 'when catching birds, they often drive them to the front of the hall and then kill them.' Describes a method of hunting birds.
    • 名落鴈殿 - Literal meaning: 'named the Goose-Hunting Hall.' A specific name for a hall used for hunting.
    • 象入庄家中庭卧其足下有槎人为出之 - Literal meaning: 'an elephant entered the village and lay in the courtyard, with its feet under the tree; the people drove it out.' Describes an unusual event involving an elephant.
    • 以鼻豗土得象牙数十以报之 - Literal meaning: 'using its trunk to dig up soil, it found dozens of ivory tusks and returned them.' Indicates the elephant's actions and the reward it received.
    • 吏部侍郎郑愔初托附来俊臣俊臣诛即托张易之易之被戮托韦庶人后附谯王竟被斩 - Literal meaning: 'Counselor of the Ministry of Personnel Zheng Yin initially relied on Lai Junchen; when Lai was executed, he relied on Zhang Yizhi; when Zhang was killed, he relied on Wei Shuren; later he attached himself to the Prince of Zhao, and was eventually executed.' Describes Zheng Yin's changing allegiances and ultimate fate.
    • 太子少保薛稷雍州长史李晋中书令崔湜萧志忠岑羲等并外饰忠鯾内藏谄媚翕肩屛气防痔折肢附太平公主并腾迁云路咸自以为得志保泰山之安七月三日破家身斩何异鹓鸯栖于苇苕大风忽起巢折卵破后之君子可不鍳哉 - Literal meaning: 'Grand Tutor Xue Ji, Prefect of Yongzhou Li Jin, Chancellor Cui Shi, Xiao Zhizhong, and Cen Xi, among others, all outwardly displayed loyalty but inwardly harbored flattery, bowing their shoulders and holding their breath to avoid trouble. They supported Princess Taiping and rose to high positions, believing they were secure. On July 3rd, they were destroyed, their families broken, and they were executed. How different is this from a phoenix perched on reeds, where a sudden strong wind causes the nest to fall and the eggs to break. Future scholars can reflect on this, can they not?' Describes the corrupt behavior of several high-ranking officials and their eventual downfall.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 沧州东光县宝观寺 - Refers to the Bao Guan Temple in Dongguang County, Cangzhou.
    • 太宗 - Refers to Emperor Taizong of Tang, a prominent emperor known for his military prowess and cultural achievements.
    • 落鴈殿 - A specific hall used for hunting geese, named after the activity.
    • 上元年 - Refers to the reign title of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, specifically the first year of his reign.
    • 华容县 - Refers to Huarong County, a location in present-day Hubei Province.
    • 象 - Refers to an elephant, which is a rare sight in China.
    • 吏部侍郎 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the Ministry of Personnel.
    • 来俊臣 - A notorious eunuch and political figure during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 张易之 - Another eunuch and political figure during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 韦庶人 - Refers to Empress Wei, a powerful figure during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 谯王 - Refers to the Prince of Zhao, a member of the Tang imperial family.
    • 太子少保 - Refers to a high-ranking official responsible for the education and well-being of the crown prince.
    • 雍州长史 - Refers to the Prefect of Yongzhou, a high-ranking administrative official.
    • 中书令 - Refers to the Chancellor, a high-ranking official in charge of the central government.
    • 太平公主 - Refers to Princess Taiping, a powerful and influential figure during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 鹓鸯 - Refers to a mythical bird, often used as a metaphor for noble or virtuous individuals.

    Proper Nouns

    • 沧州东光县宝观寺 - Bao Guan Temple in Dongguang County, Cangzhou.
    • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 落鴈殿 - Goose-Hunting Hall.
    • 上元年 - First year of Emperor Xuanzong's reign.
    • 华容县 - Huarong County.
    • 象 - Elephant.
    • 吏部侍郎 - Counselor of the Ministry of Personnel.
    • 来俊臣 - Lai Junchen.
    • 张易之 - Zhang Yizhi.
    • 韦庶人 - Empress Wei.
    • 谯王 - Prince of Zhao.
    • 太子少保 - Grand Tutor.
    • 雍州长史 - Prefect of Yongzhou.
    • 中书令 - Chancellor.
    • 太平公主 - Princess Taiping.
    • 鹓鸯 - Mythical bird.

    In Dongguang County, Cangzhou, the Bao Guan Temple often saw gray hawks gathering on the high pavilion, and each time there were thousands of birds. In winter, the hawks would take one dove each night to warm their feet, releasing it at dawn without killing it. Other eagles and hawks dared not bully them.

    Emperor Taizong raised a white hawk, which he named General. When catching birds, he often drove them to the front of the hall and then killed them, hence the hall was named the Goose-Hunting Hall. He would always send the hawk to deliver messages from the capital to the Eastern Capital, and it would return with replies each day, several times a day. This was akin to the story of Lu Ji and his dog, Yellow Ear.

    During the first year of the Shangyuan era, in Huarong County, an elephant entered a village and lay in the courtyard, with its feet under a tree. The people drove it out, and the elephant, using its trunk to dig up soil, found dozens of ivory tusks and returned them.

    Counselor of the Ministry of Personnel Zheng Yin initially relied on Lai Junchen; when Lai was executed, he relied on Zhang Yizhi; when Zhang was killed, he relied on Empress Wei; later he attached himself to the Prince of Zhao, and was eventually executed.

    Grand Tutor Xue Ji, Prefect of Yongzhou Li Jin, Chancellor Cui Shi, Xiao Zhizhong, and Cen Xi, among others, all outwardly displayed loyalty but inwardly harbored flattery, bowing their shoulders and holding their breath to avoid trouble. They supported Princess Taiping and rose to high positions, believing they were secure. On July 3rd, they were destroyed, their families broken, and they were executed. How different is this from a phoenix perched on reeds, where a sudden strong wind causes the nest to fall and the eggs to break. Future scholars can reflect on this, can they not? [109]

    赵履温为司农卿谄事安乐公主气势回山海呼吸变霜雪客谓张文成曰赵司农何如人曰猖獗小人心佞而险行僻而骄折支势族防痔权门谄于事上傲于接下猛若饥虎贪如饿狼性爱食人终为人所食为公主夺百姓田园造定昆池言定天子昆明池也用库钱百万亿斜褰紫衫为公主背挽金犊车险防皆此类诛逆韦之际上御承天门履温诈喜舞蹈称万嵗上令斩之刀劎乱下与男同戮人割一脔肉骨俱尽
    　　天后时张岌谄事薛师掌擎黄幞随薛师后于马傍伏地承薛师马镫侍御史郭霸尝来俊臣粪宋之问捧张易之溺器并偷媚取容实名教之大也
    　　天后时太常博士吉顼父晢易州刺史以赃坐死顼于天津桥南要内史魏王承嗣拜伏称死罪承嗣问之曰有二妹堪事大王承嗣然之遂犊车载入三日不语承嗣怪问之二人曰儿父犯国法忧之无复聊赖承嗣既幸免其父极刑遂进顼笼马监俄迁中丞吏部侍郎不以才升二妹请求承嗣故也 [110]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [110]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 谄事 - To flatter and serve, often implying servility or sycophancy.
    • 气势回山海 - Metaphorically describes overwhelming power or influence.
    • 呼吸变霜雪 - Implies the ability to change nature by mere breath, suggesting immense power.
    • 折支势族 - To undermine or weaken influential families or clans.
    • 防痔权门 - To avoid or prevent offenses against powerful families, metaphorically referring to avoiding trouble with those in power.
    • 猛若饥虎 - Describes someone as fierce as a hungry tiger.
    • 贪如饿狼 - Describes someone as greedy as a starving wolf.
    • 性爱食人 - Literally means 'loves to eat people,' but is likely a metaphor for being extremely greedy or voracious.
    • 终为人所食 - Literally means 'eventually eaten by others,' but is likely a metaphor for being destroyed or punished by others.
    • 定昆池 - Refers to a man-made lake, symbolizing grandiose projects or ambitions.
    • 言定天子昆明池也 - Explains that the man-made lake is a replica of the imperial Kunming Pool, indicating the scale and ambition of the project.
    • 斜褰紫衫为公主背挽金犊车 - Describes a scene where Sīnóng Sī lifts his purple robe and walks behind Princess Anle while pulling a golden ox-drawn carriage, symbolizing his servility and the princess's dominance.
    • 诛逆韦之际 - Refers to the time when the rebels were being punished.
    • 上御承天门 - Means 'the emperor stood at the Chengtian Gate.'
    • 诈喜舞蹈称万岁 - Describes the act of pretending to be happy, dancing, and shouting 'Long live the emperor!'
    • 刀劎乱下 - Describes a chaotic scene of swords falling.
    • 与男同戮 - Means 'was executed along with his son.'
    • 割一脔肉骨俱尽 - Describes the gruesome act of cutting off a piece of flesh until only bones remained.
    • 天后时 - Refers to the time of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 掌擎黄幞 - Means 'holding a yellow silk canopy.'
    • 随薛师后 - Means 'following after Master Xue.'
    • 伏地承薛师马镫 - Describes the act of lying on the ground to hold Master Xue's horse stirrups.
    • 偷媚取容 - Means 'to flatter and seek favor.'
    • 实名教之大也 - Means 'this is indeed a great evil in the teachings of morality.'
    • 犊车载入 - Describes the act of being taken into the carriage.
    • 三日不语 - Means 'three days without speaking.'
    • 儿父犯国法忧之无复聊赖 - Means 'my father committed a crime against the state, and I am worried and have no more hope.'
    • 既幸免其父极刑 - Means 'since he was fortunate to escape his father's extreme punishment.'
    • 遂进顼笼马监 - Means 'then promoted Xu to the position of horsekeeper.'
    • 俄迁中丞吏部侍郎 - Means 'soon promoted Xu to the position of Censor-in-Chief and Deputy Minister of Personnel.'
    • 不以才升 - Means 'not promoted based on talent.'
    • 二妹请求承嗣故也 - Means 'because the two sisters requested Chengsi.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 赵履温 - A historical figure known for his servility to Princess Anle.
    • 司农卿 - A high-ranking official responsible for agricultural and financial matters.
    • 安乐公主 - A princess during the Tang Dynasty, known for her extravagant lifestyle and political influence.
    • 定昆池 - A man-made lake built by Princess Anle, symbolizing her grandiose ambitions.
    • 库钱百万亿 - Refers to the vast amount of money used to build the lake.
    • 诛逆韦之际 - Refers to the suppression of the rebellion led by Wei, a powerful minister.
    • 上御承天门 - The emperor standing at the Chengtian Gate, a significant location in the imperial palace.
    • 来俊臣 - A notorious eunuch and judge known for his cruel methods of interrogation.
    • 张易之 - A close associate of Empress Wu Zetian, known for his influence and power.
    • 偷媚取容 - The act of flattering and seeking favor, often seen as a form of corruption.
    • 实名教之大也 - This is a moral judgment, indicating that such behavior is a grave sin against the teachings of morality.
    • 太常博士 - A scholar-official responsible for religious and ceremonial matters.
    • 易州刺史 - The governor of Yizhou, a region in northern China.
    • 以赃坐死 - Execution for corruption, indicating the severity of the crime.
    • 内史魏王承嗣 - The Chancellor Wei Wang Chengsi, a powerful official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 拜伏称死罪 - Kneeling and claiming guilt, a common act of submission in feudal China.
    • 犊车载入 - Being taken into a carriage, indicating a form of transportation or conveyance.
    • 三日不语 - Three days without speaking, indicating a period of silence or reflection.
    • 儿父犯国法忧之无复聊赖 - My father committed a crime against the state, and I am worried and have no more hope, indicating the emotional state of the speaker.
    • 既幸免其父极刑 - Since he was fortunate to escape his father's extreme punishment, indicating a form of leniency.
    • 遂进顼笼马监 - Then promoted Xu to the position of horsekeeper, indicating a change in status.
    • 俄迁中丞吏部侍郎 - Soon promoted Xu to the position of Censor-in-Chief and Deputy Minister of Personnel, indicating rapid advancement.
    • 不以才升 - Not promoted based on talent, indicating that the promotion was not due to merit.
    • 二妹请求承嗣故也 - Because the two sisters requested Chengsi, indicating the influence of personal connections.

    Sīnóng Sī, serving as the Minister of Agriculture, flattered Princess Anle, whose influence was as overwhelming as the mountains and seas, and whose breath could change the weather to frost and snow. A guest asked Zhang Wencheng, "What kind of person is Sīnóng Sī?" Zhang replied, "He is a rampant and cunning small man, dangerous in his actions and proud in his demeanor. He undermines influential families and avoids offending powerful families. He flatters those in power while being arrogant in dealing with others. He is fierce like a hungry tiger and greedy like a starving wolf, loving to devour people and eventually being devoured by others. For Princess Anle, he seized the farmlands and gardens of the common people to build the Ding Kun Pool, a man-made lake modeled after the imperial Kunming Pool, using a hundred million coins from the treasury. He lifted his purple robe and walked behind Princess Anle, pulling a golden ox-drawn carriage, all of which exemplify his servility. During the suppression of the rebels led by Wei, the emperor stood at the Chengtian Gate. Sīnóng Sī pretended to be happy, danced, and shouted "Long live the emperor!" The emperor ordered him to be executed, and swords fell chaotically, and he was executed along with his son. People cut off pieces of his flesh until only bones remained.

    During the reign of Empress Wu, Zhang Ji flattered Master Xue, holding a yellow silk canopy and following after him, lying on the ground to hold Master Xue's horse stirrups. The imperial censor Guo Ba once cleaned up feces for Lai Junchen, and Song Zhiwen held a chamber pot for Zhang Yizhi, both flattering and seeking favor, which is indeed a great evil in the teachings of morality.

    During the reign of Empress Wu, the scholar-official Jī Xù, whose father was the governor of Yizhou and was executed for corruption, met with the Chancellor Wei Wang Chengsi at the Tianjin Bridge, kneeling and claiming to be guilty of a capital offense. Chengsi asked him, "Do you have two sisters who can serve the great king?" Chengsi agreed and took Jī Xù into the carriage. For three days, Jī Xù did not speak. Chengsi found this strange and asked him, "My father committed a crime against the state, and I am worried and have no more hope." Since Jī Xù was fortunate to escape his father's extreme punishment, he was soon promoted to the position of horsekeeper and then to the position of Censor-in-Chief and Deputy Minister of Personnel, not based on talent but because the two sisters requested Chengsi. [110]

    天后内史宗楚客性谄佞时薛师有嫪毒之宠遂为作传二卷论薛师之圣从天而降不知何代人也释迦重出观音再生期年之间位至内史
    　　天武梁王武三思为张易之作传云是王子晋后身于缑氏山立庙词人才子佞者为诗以咏之舎人崔融为最周年易之族佞者并流于岭南
    　　崔湜谄事张易之与韦庶人及韦氏诛附太平有冯子都董偃之宠妻美与二女并进储闱为中书侍郎平事或有人牓之曰托庸才于主第进艳妇于春宫
    　　燕国公张说幸佞人也前为幷州刺史谄事特进王毛仲饷致金宝不可胜数后毛仲巡边防说于天雄军大设酒酣恩敕忽降授兵部尚书同中书门下三品说谢讫便把毛仲手起舞嗅其鞾鼻
    　　将军高力士特承宗恩宠遭母丧左金吾大将军程伯献少府监冯绍正二人直就力士母丧前披发哭甚于己亲朝野闻之不胜耻笑 [111]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [111]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 天后内史宗楚客性谄佞 - Describes Zong Chuke, an official under the Empress Dowager, as being sycophantic and flattering.
    • 时薛师有嫪毒之宠 - Indicates that Xue Shi was favored by the Empress Dowager, likening his favor to that of Lao Dou, a eunuch in the Han dynasty known for his influence.
    • 遂为作传二卷论薛师之圣从天而降 - Suggests that Zong Chuke wrote a two-volume biography praising Xue Shi, claiming he was a divine being sent from heaven.
    • 不知何代人也释迦重出观音再生 - Expresses uncertainty about Xue Shi's origins, comparing him to the reincarnation of the Buddha and Guanyin.
    • 期年之间位至内史 - Indicates that within a year, Xue Shi rose to the position of Naishi (an important official).
    • 天武梁王武三思为张易之作传云是王子晋后身 - States that Wu Sansi, a prince, wrote a biography of Zhang Yizhi, claiming he was the reincarnation of Prince Ziqing (a historical figure).
    • 于缑氏山立庙词人才子佞者为诗以咏之 - Describes how poets and flatterers wrote poems praising Zhang Yizhi, and a temple was built for him on Mount Gou.
    • 舍人崔融为最 - Indicates that Cui Rong, a scholar-official, was the best among those who wrote about Zhang Yizhi.
    • 周年易之族佞者并流于岭南 - Suggests that after a year, Zhang Yizhi's family and flatterers were exiled to the southern regions.
    • 崔湜谄事张易之与韦庶人及韦氏诛附太平有冯子都董偃之宠 - Describes Cui Shi, who flattered Zhang Yizhi and later allied with Empress Wei and her family, gaining favor similar to that of Feng Zidu and Dong Yan.
    • 妻美与二女并进储闱为中书侍郎平事 - Indicates that Cui Shi's wife and two daughters entered the imperial harem, and he became a high-ranking official.
    • 有人榜之曰托庸才于主第进艳妇于春宫 - Describes a sign that criticized Cui Shi for using his mediocre talent to gain favor and bringing his beautiful wife into the palace.
    • 燕国公张说幸佞人也前为并州刺史谄事特进王毛仲饷致金宝不可胜数 - Describes Zhang Yue, a nobleman, as a flatterer who served Wang Maozhong, a high-ranking official, and received countless treasures.
    • 后毛仲巡边防说于天雄军大设酒酣恩敕忽降授兵部尚书同中书门下三品 - Indicates that after Wang Maozhong went on a border inspection, Zhang Yue was promoted to a high position at a banquet.
    • 说谢讫便把毛仲手起舞嗅其靴鼻 - Describes Zhang Yue kissing Wang Maozhong's boots as a sign of deep respect and flattery.
    • 将军高力士特承玄宗恩宠遭母丧左金吾大将军程伯献少府监冯绍正二人直就力士母丧前披发哭甚于己亲朝野闻之不胜耻笑 - Describes how Gao Lishi, a general, received special favor from Emperor Xuanzong, and how two officials, Cheng Bexian and Feng Shaozheng, showed excessive mourning for Gao Lishi's mother, which was seen as ridiculous by the public.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天后 - Refers to Wu Zetian, the Empress Dowager of the Tang dynasty.
    • 内史 - An important official position in the Tang court.
    • 薛师 - A eunuch who was favored by Wu Zetian.
    • 嫪毒 - A eunuch in the Han dynasty known for his influence.
    • 释迦 - The Buddha.
    • 观音 - Guanyin, the bodhisattva of compassion.
    • 天武梁王武三思 - Wu Sansi, a prince and official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 张易之 - A eunuch who was favored by Empress Wei.
    • 王子晋 - Prince Ziqing, a historical figure known for his virtue.
    • 缑氏山 - Mount Gou, a mountain in Henan province.
    • 崔融 - A scholar-official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 崔湜 - A scholar-official who flattered Empress Wei.
    • 韦庶人 - Empress Wei, a powerful figure during the Tang dynasty.
    • 韦氏 - The family of Empress Wei.
    • 太平 - A title given to Empress Wei.
    • 冯子都 - A historical figure known for his influence.
    • 董偃 - A historical figure known for his influence.
    • 燕国公张说 - Zhang Yue, a nobleman during the Tang dynasty.
    • 并州刺史 - A governor in the Tang dynasty.
    • 特进王毛仲 - Wang Maozhong, a high-ranking official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 天雄军 - A military unit during the Tang dynasty.
    • 兵部尚书 - A high-ranking official position in the Tang court.
    • 同中书门下三品 - A high-ranking official position in the Tang court.
    • 高力士 - Gao Lishi, a general and eunuch during the Tang dynasty.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of the Tang dynasty.
    • 左金吾大将军程伯献 - Cheng Bexian, a high-ranking official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 少府监冯绍正 - Feng Shaozheng, a high-ranking official during the Tang dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 天后 - Wu Zetian.
    • 内史 - An official position.
    • 薛师 - Xue Shi.
    • 嫪毒 - Lao Dou.
    • 释迦 - The Buddha.
    • 观音 - Guanyin.
    • 天武梁王武三思 - Wu Sansi.
    • 张易之 - Zhang Yizhi.
    • 王子晋 - Prince Ziqing.
    • 缑氏山 - Mount Gou.
    • 崔融 - Cui Rong.
    • 崔湜 - Cui Shi.
    • 韦庶人 - Empress Wei.
    • 韦氏 - The family of Empress Wei.
    • 太平 - A title given to Empress Wei.
    • 冯子都 - Feng Zidu.
    • 董偃 - Dong Yan.
    • 燕国公张说 - Zhang Yue.
    • 并州刺史 - A governor.
    • 特进王毛仲 - Wang Maozhong.
    • 天雄军 - A military unit.
    • 兵部尚书 - A high-ranking official position.
    • 同中书门下三品 - A high-ranking official position.

    During the reign of Empress Wu, the Naishi Zong Chuke was known for his sycophancy. At that time, Xue Shi, a eunuch, enjoyed the favor of Empress Wu, comparable to that of Lao Dou in the Han dynasty. Zong Chuke wrote a two-volume biography praising Xue Shi, claiming he was a divine being sent from heaven, unsure of his origin, likened to the reincarnation of the Buddha and Guanyin. Within a year, Xue Shi rose to the position of Naishi.

    Wu Sansi, a prince, wrote a biography of Zhang Yizhi, claiming he was the reincarnation of Prince Ziqing. A temple was built for Zhang Yizhi on Mount Gou, and poets and flatterers wrote poems to praise him. Cui Rong, a scholar-official, was the best among those who wrote about Zhang Yizhi. After a year, Zhang Yizhi's family and flatterers were exiled to the southern regions.

    Cui Shi, a scholar-official, flattered Zhang Yizhi and later allied with Empress Wei and her family, gaining favor similar to that of Feng Zidu and Dong Yan. His wife and two daughters entered the imperial harem, and he became a high-ranking official. Someone posted a sign criticizing Cui Shi for using his mediocre talent to gain favor and bringing his beautiful wife into the palace.

    Zhang Yue, a nobleman, was a flatterer who served Wang Maozhong, a high-ranking official, and received countless treasures. After Wang Maozhong went on a border inspection, Zhang Yue was promoted to a high position at a banquet. Zhang Yue thanked Wang Maozhong, kissed his boots, and danced, showing deep respect and flattery.

    Gao Lishi, a general, received special favor from Emperor Xuanzong. When he lost his mother, two officials, Cheng Bexian and Feng Shaozheng, showed excessive mourning, which was seen as ridiculous by the public. [111]

    前侍御史王景融瀛州平野人也迁父灵柩就洛州于隧道掘着龙窟大如瓮口景融俯而观之有气如烟直上冲损其目遂失明旬日而暴卒

    　　朝野佥载卷五
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　朝野佥载卷六
    　　唐　张鷟　撰
    　　天宝中万年主簿韩朝宗尝追一人来迟决五下将过县令令又决十下其人患天行病而卒后于防司下状言朝宗遂被追至入乌头门极大至中门前一双桐树门边一閤垂幕窥见故御史洪子舆坐子舆曰韩大何为得此来朝宗云被追来不知何事子舆令早过大使入屛墙见故刑部尚书李乂朝宗叅见云何为决杀人朝宗诉云不是朝
    宗打杀县令重决由患天行病自卒非朝宗过又问县令决汝何牵他主簿朝宗无事然亦县丞悉见例皆受行杖木决二十放还朝宗至晚始苏脊上青肿疼痛不复可言一月已后始可于后巡检坊曲遂至京城南罗城有一坊中一宅门向南开宛然记得追来及乞杖处其宅中无人居问人云此是公主凶宅人不敢居乃知大凶宅皆神所处信之 [112]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [112]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 遷父靈柩 - Refers to the act of relocating a deceased person's remains.
    • 大如瓮口 - A simile comparing the size of the dragon's lair to the opening of a jar or pot.
    • 俯而觀之 - Describes the action of looking down and observing something.
    • 有气如烟直上 - Describes a phenomenon where a gas rises like smoke.
    • 旬日而暴卒 - Means 'died suddenly after ten days.'
    • 追一人来迟 - Means 'to summon someone who came late.'
    • 决五下 - Means 'to punish with five strokes of the cane.'
    • 县令又决十下 - Means 'the county magistrate punished him with ten strokes of the cane.'
    • 天行病 - Refers to an epidemic disease.
    • 脊上青肿疼痛 - Describes a condition where the back is bruised and painful.
    • 一月已后始可 - Means 'after one month, it became possible.'
    • 巡检坊曲 - Means 'to inspect the streets and alleys.'
    • 京城南罗城 - Refers to the southern part of the city wall of Chang'an.
    • 凶宅 - In Chinese culture, a house where someone died under unfortunate circumstances is often considered inauspicious and avoided by others.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 前侍御史 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, responsible for overseeing government affairs.
    • 万年主簿 - A low-ranking administrative official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 天宝中 - The Tianbao era (742-756 AD) was a period of prosperity and cultural flourishing in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 朝野佥载 - A historical text that records various anecdotes and stories from the Tang Dynasty.
    • 钦定四库全书 - A comprehensive collection of Chinese literature compiled during the Qing Dynasty.
    • 洪子舆 - A deceased censor, likely a respected figure in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 韩朝宗 - A historical figure, likely a low-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王景融 - A historical figure, likely a former censor.
    • 瀛州平野 - A place name, likely referring to a region in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 洛州 - A place name, likely referring to a region in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 洪子舆 - A historical figure, likely a deceased censor.
    • 韩朝宗 - A historical figure, likely a low-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 李乂 - A historical figure, likely a former minister of justice.
    • 京城南罗城 - A place name, referring to the southern part of the city wall of Chang'an.

    A former censor named Wang Jingrong was from Pingye, Yizhou. He moved his father's coffin to Luozhou. During the excavation, they uncovered a dragon's lair as large as the opening of a jar. Wang Jingrong looked down and observed it, and a gas rose like smoke, damaging his eyes. He then became blind and died suddenly after ten days.

    In the fifth volume of "Chao Ye Qian Zai," under the "Qinding Siku Quanshu," in the sixth volume of "Chao Ye Qian Zai," written by Zhang Zuo of the Tang Dynasty:

    During the Tianbao era, a county clerk named Han Chaozong once summoned someone who came late. He punished the person with five strokes of the cane and handed them over to the county magistrate, who then punished them with ten strokes of the cane. The person died of an epidemic disease. Later, the person filed a complaint against Han Chaozong, who was then summoned. He entered through a large gate, which was marked by two poplar trees. At the entrance, there was a curtain hanging from a door or partition. Han Chaozong peeked and saw the deceased censor Hong Ziyu sitting there. Hong Ziyu asked, "Why have you come here, Han Da?" Han Chaozong replied, "I was summoned. I don't know why." Hong Ziyu told him to hurry and enter the inner courtyard, where he saw the former Minister of Justice Li Yi. Han Chaozong greeted him and asked, "Why did you punish someone for killing?" Han Chaozong explained that he did not kill anyone; the person died of an epidemic disease, and it was not his fault. Hong Ziyu asked why the county magistrate punished him and involved the main clerk. Han Chaozong had no involvement, but the deputy magistrates all saw and were punished with twenty strokes of the cane. Han Chaozong was released back to court and did not recover until late in the evening, with a bruised and painful back. After one month, he was able to resume his duties. Later, he inspected the streets and alleys and came to the southern part of the city wall of Chang'an. In one of the alleys, he saw a house with a southern-facing entrance, which he remembered as the place where he was summoned and punished. The house was empty, and people told him it was an inauspicious house where a princess died. He realized that such inauspicious houses are inhabited by spirits and believed it. [112]

    神鼎师不肯剃头食酱一防每巡门乞物得麄布破衣亦着得防锦罗绮亦着于利贞师座前防问贞师曰万物定否贞曰定鼎曰阇梨言若定何因高岸为谷深为陵有死即生有生即死万物相纠六道轮回何得为定贞曰万物不定鼎曰若不定何不唤天为地唤地为天唤月为星唤星为月何得为不定贞无以应之时张文成见之谓曰观法师即是菩萨行人也鼎曰菩萨得之不喜失之不悲打之不怒骂之不嗔此乃菩萨行人也鼎今乞得即喜不得即悲打之即怒骂之即嗔以此论之去菩萨逺矣
    　　空如禅师者不知何许人也少慕修道父母抑婚以刀割其势乃止后成丁征庸课遂以麻蜡裹臂以火之遂成废疾入陆浑山坐兰若虎不为暴山中偶见野猪与虎鬭以藜杖挥之曰檀越不须相争即弭耳分散人皆敬之无敢议者 [113]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [113]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 神鼎師 - A monk named Shendǐ, likely a title or nickname.
    • 不肯剃头食酱一防 - Unclear phrase, possibly 'refused to shave his head and eat soy sauce' or similar.
    • 每巡门乞物得麄布破衣亦着得防锦罗绮亦着 - Describes the monk's behavior of accepting whatever he receives, whether coarse cloth or fine silk.
    • 于利贞师座前防问贞师曰 - Indicates that Shendǐ asked a question to Master Lìzhēn in front of his seat.
    • 万物定否 - A philosophical question about whether all things are determined or fixed.
    • 高岸为谷深为陵 - A metaphorical expression meaning that high places become valleys and deep places become hills, symbolizing change and impermanence.
    • 有死即生有生即死 - A philosophical statement about the cyclical nature of life and death.
    • 万物相纠六道轮回 - Refers to the interconnectedness of all things and the cycle of rebirth in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 菩萨得之不喜失之不悲打之不怒骂之不嗔 - Describes the qualities of a Bodhisattva, who remains unaffected by gain or loss, praise or blame.
    • 张文成 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official, known for his wisdom and insight.
    • 空如禅师 - A monk named Kōngrú, likely a title or nickname.
    • 少慕修道 - Means 'young and aspiring to practice the Dharma.'
    • 父母抑婚以刀割其势乃止 - Unclear phrase, possibly 'his parents forced him to marry and he was cut with a knife to stop him.'
    • 成丁征庸课遂以麻蜡裹臂以火之遂成废疾 - Describes a person who, upon reaching adulthood, was conscripted for labor and injury, resulting in a disability.
    • 入陆浑山坐兰若 - Means 'entered Luhun Mountain and sat in a hermitage.'
    • 虎不为暴 - Means 'the tiger did not act aggressively.'
    • 山中偶见野猪与虎鬭以藜杖挥之曰檀越不须相争即弭耳分散 - Describes the monk encountering a fight between a wild boar and a tiger and intervening with a staff, causing them to stop fighting.
    • 人皆敬之无敢议者 - Means 'people all respected him and dared not criticize him.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 神鼎师 - Likely a title or nickname for a monk, possibly a historical figure.
    • 利贞师 - A monk or teacher, possibly a historical figure.
    • 菩萨 - Refers to a Bodhisattva, a being who has achieved enlightenment but chooses to remain in the cycle of rebirth to help others.
    • 六道轮回 - Refers to the six realms of rebirth in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 张文成 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official, known for his wisdom and insight.
    • 空如禅师 - A monk or teacher, possibly a historical figure.
    • 陆浑山 - A mountain in China, possibly a specific location.
    • 兰若 - A hermitage or small temple.

    Proper Nouns

    • 神鼎师 - A title or nickname for a monk, possibly a historical figure.
    • 利贞师 - A monk or teacher, possibly a historical figure.
    • 菩萨 - Refers to a Bodhisattva, a being who has achieved enlightenment but chooses to remain in the cycle of rebirth to help others.
    • 六道轮回 - Refers to the six realms of rebirth in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 张文成 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official, known for his wisdom and insight.
    • 空如禅师 - A monk or teacher, possibly a historical figure.
    • 陆浑山 - A mountain in China, possibly a specific location.
    • 兰若 - A hermitage or small temple.

    Shendǐ, the monk, refused to shave his head and eat soy sauce. Every time he went out to beg, he would accept whatever he received, whether it was coarse cloth or tattered clothing, or fine silk and brocade. One day, he asked Master Lìzhēn in front of his seat, "Are all things determined?" Master Lìzhēn replied, "Yes, they are determined." Shendǐ said, "If all things are determined, why do high places become valleys and deep places become hills? Why do the dead come back to life and the living die? All things are interconnected, and there is the cycle of rebirth in the six realms. How can they be determined?" Master Lìzhēn replied, "All things are not determined." Shendǐ said, "If all things are not determined, why not call heaven earth and earth heaven, the moon a star and the star the moon? How can they be undetermined?" Master Lìzhēn had no answer. At that time, Zhang Wencheng saw this and said to Shendǐ, "The Master is a Bodhisattva practitioner." Shendǐ said, "A Bodhisattva is not happy when he gains and not sad when he loses, does not get angry when he is hit, and does not get angry when he is scolded. This is what a Bodhisattva practitioner does." Shendǐ said, "I am happy when I beg and get something, and sad when I do not. I get angry when I am hit and angry when I am scolded. By this reasoning, I am far from being a Bodhisattva."

    As for Kōngrú, the monk, it is unknown where he came from. When he was young, he aspired to practice the Dharma. His parents forced him to marry, and he was cut with a knife to stop him. After reaching adulthood, he was conscripted for labor and injury, resulting in a disability. He entered Luhun Mountain and sat in a hermitage. The tiger did not act aggressively. One day, he saw a fight between a wild boar and a tiger in the mountains and intervened with a staff, saying, "Venerable, you do not need to fight." The two animals stopped fighting and dispersed. People all respected him and dared not criticize him. [113]

    司刑司陈希闵以非才任官庶事凝滞司刑府史目之为高手笔言秉笔支额半日不下故名高手笔又号按孔子言窜削至多面穿穴故名按孔子
    　　衢州龙游县令李凝道性褊姊男年七嵗故恼之即往逐之不及遂饼诱得之齩其胸背流血姊救之得免又乗驴于市中有骑马人靴鼻拨其膝遂怒大骂将殴之马走遂无所及忍恶不得遂嚼路傍子流血
    　　贞观中冀州武彊县丞尧君卿失马既得贼枷禁未决君卿指贼面而骂曰老贼吃虎胆来敢偷我物贼举枷击之应时脑碎而死
    　　开元中萧頴士年十九擢进士至二十余该博三教其赋性躁忿浮戾举无其比常使一仆杜亮每一决责皆由非义平复其指使如故或劝亮曰子佣夫也何不择其善主而受苦若是乎亮曰愚岂不知但爱其才学博奥以此恋恋不能去卒至于死 [114]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [114]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 司刑司 - A title for a high-ranking official in the judicial system.
    • 非才任官 - Means 'incompetent in one's official duties.'
    • 庶事凝滞 - Means 'all affairs are stagnant or delayed.'
    • 高手笔 - A term of contempt, meaning 'a high-ranking official who is actually incompetent.'
    • 按孔子 - A term of contempt, meaning 'a high-ranking official who is actually incompetent.'
    • 面穿穴 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to cut through or pierce the surface.'
    • 褊 - Means 'narrow-minded' or 'petty.'
    • 姊男 - Means 'nephew' or 'niece.'
    • 饼诱 - Means 'to lure with food.'
    • 齩 - Means 'to bite.'
    • 驴 - Means 'donkey.'
    • 市中 - Means 'in the market.'
    • 骑马人 - Means 'a horseman.'
    • 靴鼻 - Means 'the tip of the boot.'
    • 拨其膝 - Means 'to poke or jab the knee.'
    • 大骂 - Means 'to scold loudly.'
    • 将殴之 - Means 'about to hit him.'
    • 马走 - Means 'the horse ran away.'
    • 忍恶不得 - Means 'unable to control one's anger.'
    • 嚼路傍子 - Means 'to chew on something by the roadside.'
    • 失马 - Means 'to lose a horse.'
    • 贼 - Means 'thief' or 'criminal.'
    • 枷禁 - Means 'to put in stocks or restraints.'
    • 未决 - Means 'not yet decided.'
    • 指贼面而骂 - Means 'to point at the thief's face and curse.'
    • 举枷击之 - Means 'to lift the stocks and hit him.'
    • 应时脑碎而死 - Means 'immediately died with his brain shattered.'
    • 擢进士 - Means 'to be selected as a Jinshi (a degree in the imperial examination).'
    • 该博三教 - Means 'well-versed in the three teachings (Confucianism, Buddhism, Taoism).'
    • 赋性躁忿浮戾 - Means 'temperamentally hot-tempered, irritable, and impulsive.'
    • 决责 - Means 'to punish or scold.'
    • 非义 - Means 'unjust or unreasonable.'
    • 平复其指使 - Means 'to restore the order of commands.'
    • 佣夫 - Means 'hired worker.'
    • 择其善主 - Means 'to choose a good master.'
    • 爱其才学博奥 - Means 'to love his extensive knowledge and profound learning.'
    • 恋恋不能去 - Means 'to be reluctant to leave.'
    • 卒至于死 - Means 'eventually died.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 司刑司 - A high-ranking official in the judicial system during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 贞观中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Taizong of Tang (627-649 AD).
    • 冀州武彊县 - A county in the state of Ji during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 尧君卿 - A historical figure, likely a local official.
    • 开元中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (713-756 AD).
    • 萧頴士 - A historical figure, likely a scholar or official.
    • 贞观 - The reign period of Emperor Taizong of Tang (627-649 AD).
    • 开元 - The reign period of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (713-756 AD).

    Proper Nouns

    • 司刑司 - A title for a high-ranking official in the judicial system.
    • 陈希闵 - A historical figure, likely a local official.
    • 李凝道 - A historical figure, likely a local official.
    • 尧君卿 - A historical figure, likely a local official.
    • 萧頴士 - A historical figure, likely a scholar or official.
    • 贞观 - The reign period of Emperor Taizong of Tang (627-649 AD).
    • 开元 - The reign period of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (713-756 AD).
    • 冀州武彊县 - A county in the state of Ji during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 衢州龙游县 - A county in the state of Qu during the Tang Dynasty.

    The High Judge Chen Ximin was incompetent in his official duties, causing all affairs to stagnate. The officials of the High Court referred to him as a "high-ranking official who is actually incompetent," saying that he would spend half a day unable to make a decision, hence the term "high-ranking official who is actually incompetent." He was also called "following Confucius," meaning that he made too many deletions and revisions, thus the term "following Confucius."

    The magistrate of Longyou County, Li Ninding, was narrow-minded and petty. His nephew, who was seven years old, annoyed him, so he went to chase him but failed to catch him. He then lured him with food, bit him on the chest and back, causing him to bleed. His sister saved him. Later, while riding a donkey in the market, he encountered a horseman who poked his knee with the tip of his boot. Li became furious, scolded loudly, and was about to hit him, but the horse ran away, and he could not reach him. Unable to control his anger, he chewed on something by the roadside, causing it to bleed.

    During the reign of Emperor Taizong in the Zhenguan era, Yao Junqing, the county magistrate of Wuchang in Ji State, lost his horse. When he caught the thief, he put him in stocks but had not yet decided on the punishment. Yao pointed at the thief's face and cursed, "Old thief, you have the guts of a tiger to steal my horse!" The thief lifted the stocks and hit him, immediately causing his brain to shatter and die.

    In the Kaiyuan era, Xiao Yingshi, who was nineteen years old and selected as a Jinshi, was well-versed in the three teachings (Confucianism, Buddhism, and Taoism). His temperament was hot-tempered, irritable, and impulsive, and there was no one like him. He often employed a servant named Du Liang, whom he punished or scolded unjustly, but restored the order of commands as usual. Someone advised Du Liang, "You are a hired worker. Why not choose a good master and suffer so much?" Du replied, "How could I not know? But I love his extensive knowledge and profound learning, and thus I am reluctant to leave." Eventually, he died. [114]

    敬宗时高崔巍喜防痴大帝令给使撩头向水下良久出而笑之帝问曰见屈原云我逢楚怀王无道乃沈汨罗水汝逢圣明主何为来帝大笑赐物百段
    　　秋官侍郎狄仁杰嘲秋官侍郎卢献曰足下配马乃作驴献曰中劈明公乃成二犬杰曰狄字犬傍火也献曰犬边有火乃是煮熟狗
    　　吏部侍郎李安期隋内史徳林之孙安平公百药之子性好机警常有选人被放诉云羞见来路安期问从何闗来从蒲津关来安期曰取潼闗路去选者曰耻见妻子安期曰贤室本自相谙亦不笑又一选人引铨安期看判曰弟书稍弱对曰昨坠马损足安期曰损足何废好书为读判曰向看弟判非但伤足兼似内损其人慙而去又选士姓杜名若任芳洲官其人惭而不伏安期曰君不闻芳洲有杜若其人曰可以赠名公曰此期非彼期若曰此若非彼若安期笑为之改注又一吴士前任有酒状安期曰君状不善呉士曰知暗枪已入安期曰为君拔暗枪答曰可怜美女安期曰有精神选还君好官对曰怪来晚安期笑而与官 [115]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [115]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 高崔巍喜防痴 - Playful or teasing expression, possibly a nickname or descriptive phrase. May refer to someone being tall, proud, or perhaps a bit foolish.
    • 沈汨罗水 - Refers to the act of drowning in the Miluo River, a reference to the legendary suicide of Qu Yuan, a famous poet and statesman of the Chu state.
    • 贤室本自相谙 - Literally 'your wife is already familiar with you,' but used ironically to imply that the wife is not impressed by the emperor's actions.
    • 弟书稍弱 - A polite way of saying that the candidate's handwriting is not very good.
    • 损足何废好书为 - A rhetorical question implying that a minor injury like a broken foot does not prevent someone from writing well.
    • 可以赠名公 - A playful suggestion that the candidate's name could be given to someone else, implying that he is not suitable for the position.
    • 此期非彼期 - A pun on the word '期,' which can mean 'period' or 'expectation,' suggesting that this period is not the same as the one referred to earlier.
    • 此若非彼若 - Another pun on the word '若,' which can mean 'if' or 'like,' suggesting that this '若' is not the same as the one referred to earlier.
    • 怪来晚 - A playful or teasing remark, possibly implying that the candidate is surprised by the result.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 敬宗 - Refers to Emperor Jingzong of Tang, a ruler of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 屈原 - A famous poet and statesman of the Chu state, who drowned himself in the Miluo River as a protest against the corruption and incompetence of his rulers.
    • 狄仁杰 - A famous Tang Dynasty official known for his integrity and wisdom.
    • 卢献 - A Tang Dynasty official, possibly a contemporary of狄仁杰.
    • 李安期 - A Tang Dynasty official, the son of李百药.
    • 李百药 - A famous Tang Dynasty scholar and poet.
    • 选人 - Refers to candidates for government positions.
    • 铨 - Refers to the selection or examination process for government positions.
    • 蒲津关 - A historical pass or checkpoint in China.
    • 潼关 - A historical pass or checkpoint in China.
    • 芳洲 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific region or location.
    • 杜若 - A plant or possibly a person's name, used here in a playful or teasing manner.

    Proper Nouns

    • 敬宗 - Emperor Jingzong of Tang.
    • 屈原 - A famous poet and statesman of the Chu state.
    • 狄仁杰 - A famous Tang Dynasty official.
    • 卢献 - A Tang Dynasty official.
    • 李安期 - A Tang Dynasty official, the son of李百药.
    • 李百药 - A famous Tang Dynasty scholar and poet.
    • 选人 - Candidates for government positions.
    • 铨 - The selection or examination process for government positions.
    • 蒲津关 - A historical pass or checkpoint in China.
    • 潼关 - A historical pass or checkpoint in China.
    • 芳洲 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific region or location.
    • 杜若 - A plant or possibly a person's name.

    During the reign of Emperor Jingzong, there was a tall, proud, and somewhat foolish emperor who ordered his servant to dunk his head underwater. After a long time, he emerged and laughed. The emperor asked, "Did you see Qu Yuan? He said, 'I encountered the tyrannical King Huai of Chu and drowned myself in the Miluo River. You, on the other hand, have encountered a wise and benevolent ruler. Why are you here?' The emperor laughed heartily and granted him a hundred pieces of silk.

    The Vice Minister of Justice, Di Renjie, teased the Vice Minister of Justice, Lu Xian, saying, "Your name paired with a horse makes it a donkey." Lu replied, "If you split your name in the middle, it becomes two dogs." Di said, "The character 'Di' has a dog on one side and fire on the other." Lu replied, "If there is fire next to a dog, it means a cooked dog."

    The Vice Minister of Personnel, Li Anqi, the grandson of the former Grand Historian De Lin and the son of the Prince of Anping, Baiyao, was known for his quick wit. Once, a candidate who had been rejected complained, "I am ashamed to return home." Li asked, "From which pass did you come?" The candidate replied, "From Puzheng Pass." Li said, "Take the route through Tongguan Pass." The candidate said, "I am ashamed to face my wife and children." Li replied, "Your wife is already familiar with you, so she will not laugh." Another candidate brought a judgment for Li to review. Li said, "Your handwriting is a bit weak." The candidate replied, "I recently fell off a horse and broke my foot." Li said, "How can a broken foot prevent you from writing well?" The candidate replied, "When I saw your judgment, it was not only about a broken foot but also seemed to indicate an internal injury." The candidate was ashamed and left. Another candidate named Du Ruo was appointed to govern Fangzhou. The candidate was ashamed and refused to accept the position. Li said, "Have you not heard that Fangzhou has a Du Ruo?" The candidate replied, "I can give my name to someone else." Li said, "This period is not that period." The candidate replied, "This 'if' is not that 'if'." Li laughed and changed the annotation. Another man from Wu, who had previously been involved in a drinking incident, was appointed to a position. Li said, "Your record is not good." The man from Wu replied, "I know that an arrow has already been shot." Li said, "I will remove the hidden arrow for you." The man replied, "There is a beautiful woman." Li said, "You have spirit. I will grant you a good position." The man replied, "It is surprising to come late." Li laughed and granted him the position. [115]

    尹神童每说伯乐令其子执马经画様以求马经年无有似者归以告父乃更令求之出见大虾蟇谓父曰得一马略与相同而不能具伯乐曰何也对曰其隆颅跌目脊都缩但蹄不如累趋尔伯乐曰此马好跳踯不堪也子笑乃止
    　　安南有象【阙】　有理者即过负心者以鼻卷之掷空中数丈以牙接之应时碎矣莫敢竞者
    　　安南武平县封溪中有猩猩焉如美人解人语知往事以嗜酒故以屐得之槛百数同牢欲食之众自推肥者相送流涕而别时饷封溪令以帊葢之令问何物猩猩乃笼中语曰唯有仆幷酒一壶耳令笑而爱之养畜能传送言语人不如也
    　　前御史王义方出莱州司户叅军去官归魏州以讲授为业时乡人郭无为颇有法术教义方使野狐义方虽呼得之不伏使却被羣狐竞来恼每掷砖瓦以击义方或正诵读即裂其书碎闻空中有声云有何神术而欲使我乎义方竟不能禁止无何而卒 [116]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [116]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 尹神童 - A proper name, likely a historical figure or a character in a story. The term '神童' means 'prodigy' or 'child prodigy.'
    • 伯乐 - A legendary figure in Chinese history known as the 'Master of Horses,' who was skilled in identifying and training horses. The term '伯乐' is often used metaphorically to refer to someone with exceptional talent or discernment.
    • 执马经画様 - A phrase that likely refers to the practice of drawing or studying the patterns of horses, possibly for the purpose of identifying or breeding them.
    • 隆颅跌目脊都缩 - A descriptive phrase that seems to refer to the physical characteristics of a horse, possibly indicating a specific breed or type. The exact meaning is unclear, but it likely describes a horse with a prominent head, drooping eyes, and a hunched back.
    • 蹄不如累趋 - Another descriptive phrase, possibly referring to the horse's hooves or gait. The term '累趋' could mean 'clumsy gait' or 'unsteady movement.'
    • 安南 - Refers to the historical region of Annam, which is now part of modern-day Vietnam.
    • 虾蟇 - Refers to a type of toad or frog, possibly used metaphorically or in a specific cultural context.
    • 槛百数 - Likely refers to a cage or enclosure for animals, with '百数' meaning 'a hundred or so.'
    • 同牢 - A term that could refer to a shared enclosure or a communal space, possibly for animals or people.
    • 流涕而别 - A phrase that means 'to shed tears and part,' indicating a poignant farewell.
    • 理者 - Likely refers to a person who is rational or sensible, possibly in contrast to someone who is not.
    • 负心者 - Refers to someone who is unfaithful or disloyal, possibly in a romantic or moral context.
    • 卷之掷空中数丈 - A descriptive phrase that means 'to roll it up and throw it into the air several feet,' likely referring to an action involving a toad or another animal.
    • 牙接之应时碎矣 - Means 'to break it with teeth at the right moment,' likely referring to the toad being crushed.
    • 莫敢竞者 - Means 'no one dares to compete,' possibly referring to the toad's dominance or the fear it instills in others.
    • 猩猩 - Refers to a type of ape or primate, possibly used metaphorically or in a specific cultural context.
    • 美人 - Literally means 'beautiful person,' often used to describe a woman of great beauty.
    • 解人语 - Means 'to understand human speech,' indicating that the ape can comprehend human language.
    • 知往事 - Means 'to know past events,' suggesting that the ape has memory or knowledge of history.
    • 嗜酒 - Means 'to love wine,' indicating a fondness for alcohol.
    • 屐 - Refers to a type of wooden clog or shoe, possibly used in a specific cultural or historical context.
    • 帊葢之 - Means 'to cover it with a cloth,' likely referring to the ape being covered or hidden.
    • 何物 - Means 'what kind of thing,' indicating a question about the identity or nature of something.
    • 能传送言语 - Means 'to convey speech,' indicating the ability to transmit or relay messages.
    • 御史 - Refers to an official position in the imperial bureaucracy, often responsible for oversight and investigation.
    • 司户叅军 - Refers to a specific administrative role, likely responsible for household or local affairs.
    • 去官归魏州 - Means 'to leave office and return to Weizhou,' indicating a change in the official's career or location.
    • 讲授为业 - Means 'to make teaching one's profession,' indicating that the person has turned to teaching as a career.
    • 乡人 - Refers to a person from the same village or community, often used to indicate a local or familiar figure.
    • 法术 - Refers to magical or mystical practices, possibly used in a specific cultural or historical context.
    • 教义方使野狐 - Means 'to teach Wang Yifang to use a wild fox,' possibly referring to a specific magical or practical technique.
    • 呼得之 - Means 'to call it and get it,' indicating the ability to summon or capture something.
    • 伏 - Means 'to submit' or 'to yield,' indicating that something has been subdued or controlled.
    • 羣狐竞来恼 - Means 'the pack of foxes comes to bother,' indicating that the foxes are causing trouble or annoyance.
    • 掷砖瓦以击义方 - Means 'to throw bricks and tiles to hit Wang Yifang,' indicating that the foxes are attacking him.
    • 正诵读即裂其书碎 - Means 'when he is reading aloud, the book is torn and broken,' indicating that the foxes are disrupting his reading.
    • 有何神术而欲使我乎 - Means 'What kind of divine skill do you have that makes you want to make me do this?' indicating a question about the foxes' abilities or intentions.
    • 竟不能禁止 - Means 'could not finally stop,' indicating that Wang Yifang was unable to prevent the foxes' actions.
    • 无何而卒 - Means 'not long after, he died,' indicating the end of Wang Yifang's life.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 伯乐 - The legendary figure known as the 'Master of Horses,' who was skilled in identifying and training horses. This figure is often used metaphorically to refer to someone with exceptional talent or discernment.
    • 安南 - Refers to the historical region of Annam, which is now part of modern-day Vietnam. This region has a rich history of interactions with China and its own distinct cultural traditions.
    • 虾蟇 - Refers to a type of toad or frog, which may have specific cultural or mythological significance in the context of the story.
    • 猩猩 - Refers to a type of ape or primate, which may have specific cultural or mythological significance in the context of the story.
    • 御史 - Refers to an official position in the imperial bureaucracy, often responsible for oversight and investigation. This title is associated with the Chinese imperial system and its administrative structure.
    • 司户叅军 - Refers to a specific administrative role, likely responsible for household or local affairs. This title is associated with the Chinese imperial system and its administrative structure.
    • 魏州 - Refers to a historical region in China, likely part of the administrative divisions of the time. This region has a rich history and cultural significance.
    • 王义方 - A proper name, likely a historical figure or a character in a story. The term '义方' could indicate a sense of righteousness or moral integrity.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 尹神童每说伯乐令其子执马经画様以求马经年无有似者归以告父乃更令求之出见大虾蟇谓父曰得一马略与相同而不能具伯乐曰何也对曰其隆颅跌目脊都缩但蹄不如累趋尔伯乐曰此马好跳踯不堪也子笑乃止
    • 安南有象【阙】
    • 安南武平县封溪中有猩猩焉如美人解人语知往事以嗜酒故以屐得之槛百数同牢欲食之众自推肥者相送流涕而别时饷封溪令以帊葢之令问何物猩猩乃笼中语曰唯有仆幷酒一壶耳令笑而爱之养畜能传送言语人不如也
    • 前御史王义方出莱州司户叅军去官归魏州以讲授为业时乡人郭

    Yin Shantong often told the story of Bo Le, who instructed his son to study the patterns of horses to find one that matched a description. After years of searching, the son found no horse that was similar. He returned to tell his father, who then sent him out to look again. This time, he saw a large toad and told his father, "I found a horse that is somewhat similar, but it is not quite the same." Bo Le asked why, and the son replied, "Its head is prominent, its eyes droop, its back is hunched, but its hooves are not as steady as a horse's." Bo Le said, "This horse is good at jumping and leaping, but it is not suitable for the task." The son laughed and stopped the search.

    In the region of Annam, there was an elephant, but the story is incomplete at this point. A sensible person would pass by, while an unfaithful one would be grabbed by the elephant's trunk and thrown into the air several feet before being crushed with its teeth. No one dared to compete with the elephant.

    In the Wuping County of Annam, there was a beautiful ape that understood human speech and knew past events. Because it loved wine, it was captured using wooden clogs. The ape was kept in a cage with a hundred or so others, and they were about to be eaten. The people chose the fattest one to be sacrificed, and they shed tears as they parted. At the time, they offered the ape to the governor of Fengxi, who covered it with a cloth. The governor asked what it was, and the ape, speaking from the cage, said, "I am only a servant and have a bottle of wine." The governor laughed and decided to keep the ape, as it could convey speech better than humans.

    Former Magistrate Wang Yifang, who had served as a county official in Laizhou, left his post and returned to Weizhou, where he made teaching his profession. At the time, a man from his village named Guo Wuwu had some magical skills and taught Wang Yifang to use a wild fox. Wang Yifang could call the fox and get it, but the fox did not submit. Instead, a pack of foxes came to bother him, throwing bricks and tiles to hit Wang Yifang. When he was reading aloud, the book was torn and broken. He heard a voice in the air saying, "What kind of divine skill do you have that makes you want to make me do this?" Wang Yifang could not finally stop the foxes, and not long after, he died. [116]

    并州石艾夀阳二界有妬女泉有神庙泉水沈洁澈千丈祭者投钱及羊骨皎然皆见俗传妬女者介之推妹与兄竞去泉百里寒食不许举火至今犹然女锦衣红鲜装束盛服及有人取山丹百合经过者必雷风电雹以震之
    　　景龙末韦庶人专制故安州都督赠太师杜举时尉济源县为府召至洛城修籍一夕暴卒亲賔具小殓夫人尉迟氏敬徳之孙也性通明彊毅曰公术神妙自言官至方伯今岂长往安然不哭洎二日三夕乃心上稍温翌日徐苏数日方语云初见两人持符来召遂相引出徽安门门隙容寸过之尚寛直北上邙山可十余里有大坑视不见底使人令入举大惧使者曰可闭目执手如飞须防足已履地寻小径东行凡数十里天气昏惨如冬凝隂遂至一墙宇宏壮使者先入有碧衣官出趋拜颇恭既退引入碧衣者踞坐按后命举前傍有一狗 [117]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [117]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.
    • 沈洁澈 - Descriptive phrase meaning clear and deep.
    • 皎然皆见 - Idiomatic expression meaning clearly visible.
    • 犹然 - Idiomatic expression meaning remains so.
    • 锦衣红鲜 - Descriptive phrase meaning splendid red and fresh.
    • 雷风电雹 - Descriptive phrase meaning thunder, lightning, hail, and wind.
    • 具小殓 - Ritual term meaning prepared the minor burial rites.
    • 通明彊毅 - Descriptive terms meaning intelligent, bright, and resolute.
    • 命举前 - Descriptive phrase meaning commanded Du Shuiju to go forward.
    • 傍有一狗 - Descriptive phrase meaning there was a dog by his side.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 并州石艾夀阳二界 - Refers to the boundaries between Shai and Shouyang in Bingzhou, a region in northern China.
    • 寒食 - Refers to the Cold Food Festival, a traditional Chinese festival where no fire is allowed, and people eat cold food.
    • 景龙末 - Refers to the end of the Jinglong era, when Empress Wei held absolute power in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 故安州都督 - Refers to Du Shuiju, a historical figure who served as the governor of Anyang Prefecture.
    • 赠太师 - Refers to Du Shuiju being posthumously promoted to Grand Tutor.
    • 尉迟氏 - Refers to Lady Yidui, the granddaughter of Wei Zhi, a famous general in Chinese history.
    • 敬徳 - Refers to Wei Zhi, a famous general in Chinese history.
    • 方伯 - Refers to the rank of provincial governor.
    • 碧衣官 - Refers to a blue-robed official, a common scene in Chinese historical texts.
    • 趋拜颇恭 - Describes the respectful bowing of a blue-robed official.

    Proper Nouns

    • 妬女泉 - Tudi Spring, a proper noun.
    • 紹興 - Era name of the Southern Song; rendered as Shaoxing.
    • 尉迟氏 - Surname of Lady Yidui.
    • 敬徳 - Proper name of Wei Zhi, a famous general in Chinese history.
    • 杜举 - Historical figure Du Shuiju.
    • 徽安门 - Huian Gate, a proper noun.
    • 邙山 - Mount Mangshan, a proper noun.
    • 碧衣官 - Blue-robed official, a proper noun.

    There is a Tudi Spring between the boundaries of Shai and Shouyang in Bingzhou, with a temple and clear and deep water reaching thousands of feet. Those who offer sacrifices throw coins and sheep bones, which are clearly visible. The local legend says that Tudi is the sister of Jie Zhitui, who quarreled with her brother and left the spring a hundred miles away. During the Cold Food Festival, no fire is allowed, and it remains so to this day. Women wear splendid red and fresh attire. Anyone who takes gentian or lily of the valley will be struck by thunder, lightning, hail, and wind.

    At the end of Jinglong, when Empress Wei held absolute power, Du Shuiju, who was the governor of Anyang Prefecture and was posthumously promoted to Grand Tutor, served as a magistrate in Jiyuan County. He was called to the capital to revise the records and died suddenly one night. Relatives and guests prepared the minor burial rites. His wife, Lady Yidui, was the granddaughter of Wei Zhi. She was intelligent, bright, and resolute. He said he would reach the rank of provincial governor. How could he have passed away so suddenly? She remained calm and did not cry. By the second and third days, his heart began to warm slightly. On the next day, he slowly regained consciousness and spoke after several days. He said: "Initially, I saw two people with talismans calling me. They led me out of the Huian Gate, through a narrow passage, and northward up Mount Mangshan for over ten miles, where there was a large pit that seemed bottomless. I was commanded to enter, and I was terrified. The messenger said to close my eyes and hold hands, and we flew as if in a dream. When we landed, we followed a small path eastward for dozens of miles. The weather was dark and gloomy, as if in winter. We finally arrived at a mansion with grand walls and buildings. The messenger went in first, and a blue-robed official came out, bowing very respectfully. After he left, the blue-robed official sat down and commanded me to go forward. There was a dog by my side." [117]

    人语曰误姓名同非此官也笞使者改符令去有一马半身两足跳梁而前曰往为举所杀今请理寃举亦醒然记之诉云曾知驿敕使将马令杀非某所愿碧衣命吏取按审然之马遂退傍见一吏挥手动目教以事理意相庇脱证既毕遂揖之出碧衣拜送门外云某是生人安州编户少府当为安州都督故先施敬愿自保持言讫而向所教之吏趋出云姓韦名鼎亦是生人在上都务本坊自称向来有力祈钱十万鹏举辞不能致鼎云某虽生人今于此用纸钱易致耳遂许之又嘱云焚时愿以物借之幸不着地兼呼韦鼎某即自使人受鼎又云既至此岂 [118]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [118]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 人語曰 - Means 'someone said' or 'a person reported,' introducing a narrative element.
    • 誤姓名同 - Means 'mistakenly sharing the same name,' a common source of confusion.
    • 非此官也 - Means 'not this official,' clarifying the error.
    • 笞使者 - Means 'to whip the messenger,' indicating a form of punishment.
    • 改符令去 - Means 'to change the seal and order to leave,' suggesting a correction of the error.
    • 有一馬半身兩足跳梁而前 - Describes a horse-like creature with a split body and two legs, leaping forward. '跳梁' means 'to leap about' and often has negative connotations, implying mischief or danger.
    • 往為舉所殺 - Means 'killed by 舉 in the past,' indicating a past event.
    • 今請理寃 - Means 'now requesting to clear the wrong,' suggesting a plea for justice.
    • 舉亦醒然記之 - Means '舉 also clearly remembers it,' indicating a recollection.
    • 訴云 - Means 'to complain' or 'to report,' introducing a statement.
    • 曾知驛敕使將馬令殺 - Means 'once knew that the imperial messenger ordered the killing of the horse,' indicating a past command.
    • 非某所願 - Means 'not my wish,' expressing unwillingness.
    • 碧衣命吏取按審然之 - Means 'the green-clad official ordered the clerk to investigate and confirm it,' indicating an investigation.
    • 馬遂退 - Means 'the horse then retreated,' describing the horse's action.
    • 傍見一吏揮手動目教以事理 - Means 'saw a clerk gesturing and nodding, teaching the principles of the matter,' indicating guidance.
    • 意相庇脫證 - Means 'to mutually protect and escape from the evidence,' suggesting collusion.
    • 遂揖之出 - Means 'then bowed and sent him out,' indicating a formal dismissal.
    • 碧衣拜送門外 - Means 'the green-clad official bowed and saw him off at the gate,' indicating respect.
    • 某是生人 - Means 'I am a living person,' clarifying identity.
    • 安州編戶 - Means 'a registered household in Anzhou,' indicating place of origin.
    • 少府當為安州都督 - Means 'the Small Treasury official should be the governor of Anzhou,' indicating a position.
    • 故先施敬願自保持 - Means 'therefore, first show respect and wish to take care of yourself,' indicating a request for respect.
    • 言訖而向所教之吏趨出 - Means 'after speaking, the clerk who taught the principles hurried out,' indicating a quick departure.
    • 姓韋名鼎 - Means 'name is Wei Ding,' introducing a proper name.
    • 亦是生人 - Means 'also a living person,' clarifying identity.
    • 在上都務本坊 - Means 'in the Wuben Ward of the capital,' indicating location.
    • 自稱向來有力祈錢十萬 - Means 'claimed to have had power in the past and asked for ten thousand coins,' indicating a request.
    • 鵬舉辭不能致 - Means 'Pengju declined, unable to fulfill it,' indicating refusal.
    • 鼎云某雖生人今於此用紙錢易致耳 - Means 'Ding said, 'Although I am a living person, I can easily obtain it here with paper money,' indicating a solution.
    • 遂許之 - Means 'then agreed to it,' indicating acceptance.
    • 又囑云焚時願以物借之幸不着地 - Means 'also instructed, 'When burning, wish to lend something to it, hoping it does not touch the ground,' indicating a specific request.
    • 兼呼韋鼎某即自使人受鼎 - Means 'also called Wei Ding, who immediately sent someone to receive Ding,' indicating immediate action.
    • 既至此豈 - Means 'having come here, how could,' indicating a rhetorical question.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 舉 - A name or title, possibly referring to a historical figure or official.
    • 碧衣 - Refers to a green-clad official, likely a high-ranking official or a supernatural being.
    • 安州 - A place name, indicating a specific region.
    • 少府 - An official title, referring to a high-ranking government official.
    • 安州都督 - A military and administrative position, indicating a high-ranking official.
    • 上都 - Refers to the capital city, likely referring to Chang'an or Luoyang.
    • 務本坊 - A place name, indicating a specific ward in the capital.
    • 紙錢 - Refers to paper money used in rituals, often for the deceased.

    Proper Nouns

    • 舉 - A name or title, possibly referring to a historical figure or official.
    • 碧衣 - A name or title, likely referring to a high-ranking official or a supernatural being.
    • 安州 - A place name, indicating a specific region.
    • 少府 - An official title, referring to a high-ranking government official.
    • 安州都督 - A military and administrative position, indicating a high-ranking official.
    • 上都 - Refers to the capital city, likely referring to Chang'an or Luoyang.
    • 務本坊 - A place name, indicating a specific ward in the capital.
    • 韋鼎 - A name, referring to a specific person.

    Someone reported, "There was a mistake in the name, and it is not this official. The messenger was whipped, and the seal and order were changed to leave." A creature with a split body and two legs leaped forward, saying, "In the past, I was killed by ju. Now I request to clear the wrong." ju also clearly remembered it and complained, "Once, I knew that the imperial messenger ordered the killing of the horse, which was not my wish." The green-clad official ordered the clerk to investigate and confirm it. The horse then retreated. Beside it, a clerk gestured and nodded, teaching the principles of the matter, and they mutually protected and escaped from the evidence. After the investigation was completed, the official bowed and sent him out. The green-clad official bowed and saw him off at the gate, saying, "I am a living person, a registered household in Anzhou. The Small Treasury official should be the governor of Anzhou. Therefore, first show respect and wish to take care of yourself." After speaking, the clerk who taught the principles hurried out, saying, "My name is Wei Ding, also a living person, in the Wuben Ward of the capital. I claimed to have had power in the past and asked for ten thousand coins. Pengju declined, unable to fulfill it. Ding said, 'Although I am a living person, I can easily obtain it here with paper money.' Then he agreed to it. He also instructed, 'When burning, wish to lend something to it, hoping it does not touch the ground.' He also called Wei Ding, who immediately sent someone to receive Ding. Having come here, how could I not help?" [118]

    不要见当家簿书遂引入一院题云户部房廊四周簿帐山积当中三间架阁特高覆以赤黄帏帕金字牓曰皇籍余皆露往架往有函紫色葢之韦鼎云宰相也因引诣杜氏籍书籖云濮阳房有紫函四发开卷举三男时未生者籍名已俱述求茟书其名于臂意愿踟蹰更欲固览韦鼎云既不往亦要早归遂引出令一吏送还吏云某苦饥不逢此使无因得出愿许别去兾求一食但寻此道自至其所留之不可举遂西行道左忽见一新城异香闻数里环城皆甲士持兵举问之甲士云相王于此上天有四百天人来送举曾为相府王官忻闻此说墙有 [119]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [119]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 不要见当家簿书 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'do not concern yourself with trivial matters.'
    • 意愿踟蹰更欲固览 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'wished to linger and look more closely.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 户部房 - Refers to the Treasury Office, a government department responsible for financial matters.
    • 韦鼎 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official or chancellor.
    • 杜氏籍书 - Refers to the Du family's register, a record-keeping document.
    • 濮阳房 - A specific section or room in the register, possibly named after the Puyang region.
    • 紫函 - Purple boxes, often used to store important documents in ancient China.
    • 皇籍 - The Imperial Register, a significant and official record.
    • 相王 - The Prince of the Realm, a title given to a high-ranking prince.
    • 四百天人 - Four hundred celestial beings, often associated with divine or supernatural figures in Chinese mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 韦鼎 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official or chancellor.
    • 杜氏 - The Du family, likely a prominent family in the historical context.
    • 濮阳 - A region or place name, possibly significant in the context of the story.
    • 相王 - The Prince of the Realm, a title given to a high-ranking prince.

    "Do not concern yourself with trivial matters; he was then led into a courtyard where the sign read 'Treasury Office Room.' The shelves around the room were piled high with ledgers, and in the middle, three shelves were particularly tall, covered with red and yellow silk cloths. Gold-lettered signs read 'Imperial Register,' and the rest were open, with boxes covered in purple. Wei Ding, a chancellor, said he was a high-ranking official. He then led the person to the Du family's register, where the label said 'Puyang Room,' and there were four purple boxes. When opened, the names of the three sons who were not yet born were already recorded. The person requested to write their names on his arm and wished to linger and look more closely. Wei Ding said if he didn't go, he should return early, and then led him out, ordering a clerk to send him back. The clerk said he was very hungry and had no reason to come out, hoping to be allowed to leave to find food. Just follow this path, and you will reach your destination. Do not leave without permission. So he went west. On the left side of the road, he suddenly saw a new city with a strange fragrance that could be smelled for several miles, surrounded by armored soldiers holding weapons. The armored soldiers said that the Prince of the Realm would ascend to heaven, and 400 celestial beings came to send him off. The person, who was once an official in the chancellor's office, was glad to hear this, but the wall had..." [119]

    大隙窥见分明天人数百围绕相王满地防云并衣仙服皆如画者相王前有女人执香炉引行近窥帝衣裙带状似剪破一如鴈齿状相王戴一日光明辉赫径可丈余相王后凢有十九日累累成行大光明皆如所戴须防有绨骑来迎甲士令举走遂至故道不觉已及徽安门闭过之亦如去时容易为羣犬遮齧行不可进至家见身在牀上跃入身中遂寤臂上所记如朽木书字尚分明遂焚纸钱十万呼赠韦鼎心知卜代之数中兴之期遂以假故来谒睿宗上握手曰岂敢忘徳寻求韦鼎适卒矣及睿宗登极拜右拾遗词云思入风雅灵通神敕宫人妃 [120]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [120]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 大隙窥见 - Literally 'to look through a large gap and see,' used to describe a sudden or unexpected vision.
    • 分明天人数百 - Means 'a hundred clearly visible people,' emphasizing the clarity of the vision.
    • 围绕相王 - Means 'surrounding the crown prince,' indicating the presence of a group around the main figure.
    • 满地防云并衣仙服 - Describes the scene as 'the ground is full of defensive clouds and the people are dressed in celestial garments,' suggesting a mystical or divine setting.
    • 皆如画者 - Means 'all like paintings,' emphasizing the vivid and artistic quality of the vision.
    • 相王前有女人执香炉引行 - Describes a woman holding a censer leading the way, a common motif in Chinese mythology and literature.
    • 近窥帝衣裙带状似剪破一如雁齿状 - Describes the emperor's clothing as resembling a broken pattern like the teeth of a goose, suggesting a detailed and precise observation.
    • 相王戴一日光明辉赫径可丈余 - Describes the crown prince wearing a sun-like ornament that is brilliant and spans about a zhang (a traditional Chinese unit of length).
    • 相王后凡有十九日累累成行 - Indicates that behind the crown prince are nineteen more sun-like ornaments arranged in a line.
    • 大光明皆如所戴须防有绨骑来迎甲士令举走遂至故道 - Describes a large, bright light and the arrival of a group of horsemen, followed by a command to move along the old path.
    • 不觉已及徽安门闭过之亦如去时容易为群犬遮齧行不可进 - Describes the passage through the Hui'an Gate and the difficulty in continuing due to dogs blocking the way.
    • 至家见身在牀上跃入身中遂寤 - Describes the return home and the realization that the vision was a dream.
    • 臂上所记如朽木书字尚分明 - Describes the writing on the arm as being as clear as if carved into rotting wood.
    • 遂焚纸钱十万呼赠韦鼎心知卜代之数中兴之期遂以假故来谒睿宗 - Describes the burning of ten thousand paper money and the invocation of Wei Ding, indicating a belief in divination and the prediction of a new era.
    • 上握手曰岂敢忘德寻求韦鼎适卒矣及睿宗登极拜右拾遗 - Describes the emperor's response and the subsequent appointment of the person as a Right Censorial Assistant.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 相王 - Refers to a crown prince, likely a historical figure.
    • 帝 - Refers to an emperor, likely a historical figure.
    • 徽安门 - Refers to the Hui'an Gate, a specific gate in a city, possibly Luoyang.
    • 韦鼎 - A historical figure, likely a scholar or advisor.
    • 睿宗 - Refers to a wise emperor, likely a historical figure.

    Proper Nouns

    • 相王 - Crown prince.
    • 帝 - Emperor.
    • 徽安门 - Hui'an Gate.
    • 韦鼎 - Wei Ding.
    • 睿宗 - Wise emperor.

    A large gap allowed me to see clearly a hundred people surrounding the crown prince, with the ground full of defensive clouds and the people dressed in celestial garments, all like paintings. Before the crown prince, there was a woman holding a censer leading the way. I closely observed the emperor's clothes, which were cut like the teeth of a goose. The crown prince wore a sun-like ornament that was brilliant and spanned about a zhang. Behind the crown prince, there were nineteen more sun-like ornaments arranged in a line. The large, bright light was like the ornament he wore. Suddenly, a group of horsemen came to welcome him, and the soldiers ordered us to move along the old path. Unconsciously, we had reached the Hui'an Gate, and passing through it was as easy as when we left. However, we were blocked by a group of dogs and could not proceed. Upon returning home, I saw myself lying on the bed, and suddenly woke up, realizing that the vision was a dream. The writing on my arm was as clear as if carved into rotting wood. I then burned ten thousand paper money and invoked Wei Ding, knowing that this was a sign of the changing era and the time of revival. Using this as an excuse, I went to pay respects to Emperor Ruizong. He took my hand and said, "How could I ever forget your virtue?" I sought Wei Ding, but he had just passed away. When Emperor Ruizong ascended the throne, I was appointed as a Right Censorial Assistant. The words he spoke were: "Thinking of entering the realm of elegance, communicating with spirits, and being favored by the imperial concubines." [120]

    子数十同其糚服令视执炉者举遥识之乃太平公主也问裙带之由公主云方熨龙衮忽为火迸惊忙之中不觉带仓皇不及更服公主歔欷陈贺曰圣人之兴固自天也举所见先睿宗龙飞前三年故举墓志云及睿宗践阼隂隲祥符啓圣期于化元定成拜于幽数后果为安州都督处士萧时和作传　一说举得释后入一院问下者为谁曰魏元忠也有顷敬挥入下马众接拜之云是大理卿对推事见武三思着枷韦温宗楚客赵履温等着锁李峤露头散腰立闻元忠等云今年大计防至六月诛逆韦宗赵韦等并斩峤解官归第皆如其言 [121]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [121]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 子数十同其糚服 - A group of several dozen people wore the same attire, indicating a uniform appearance.
    • 令视执炉者 - Ordered to see someone holding a furnace, likely a servant or worker.
    • 遥识之乃太平公主也 - Recognized from afar that it was Princess Taiping, indicating the recognition of a high-ranking individual.
    • 问裙带之由 - Asked about the reason behind the belt, likely a metaphor for a connection or relationship.
    • 方熨龙衮忽为火迸 - Was pressing the dragon robe of the emperor when suddenly a fire broke out, indicating an unexpected event.
    • 仓皇不及更服 - In a hurry and did not have time to change, indicating a rushed situation.
    • 歔欷陈贺曰 - Sighed and wept, then declared congratulations, indicating a mixture of emotions and a formal declaration.
    • 圣人之兴固自天也 - The rise of the sage is surely due to heaven, indicating a belief in divine will.
    • 所见先睿宗龙飞前三年 - What was seen three years before Emperor Ruizong's ascension, indicating a specific time frame.
    • 墓志云 - Tomb inscription says, indicating a reference to a written record.
    • 及睿宗践阼隂隲祥符啓圣期于化元定成拜于幂数 - When Emperor Ruizong ascended the throne, auspicious signs appeared, marking the beginning of the sage era, and the transformation and establishment of the new era, with the number of officials who were appointed, indicating a series of events and appointments.
    • 果为安州都督处士萧时和作传 - Indeed became a biography of the governor of Anzhou, a scholar who does not seek office, and Xiang Shihuo, indicating the creation of a biography.
    • 一说 - One says or according to one account, indicating an alternative version.
    • 得释后入一院 - After being released, entered a hall, indicating a specific action.
    • 问下者为谁 - Asked who the person below was, indicating a question about identity.
    • 曰魏元忠也 - Said it was Wei Yuanzhong, indicating the answer to the question.
    • 有顷敬挥入下马 - After a while, respectfully gestured and entered, dismounting, indicating a respectful action.
    • 众接拜之云 - The crowd greeted him with bows, saying, indicating a formal greeting.
    • 是大理卿对推事 - This is the Chief Justice in charge of judicial matters, indicating a specific role.
    • 见武三思着枷韦温宗楚客赵履温等着锁李峤露头散腰立 - Saw Wu Sansi wearing a枷, Wei Wen, Zong Chuke, Zhao Lüwen, and others wearing chains, and Li Qiao standing with his head uncovered and loose waist, indicating a specific scene.
    • 闻元忠等云 - Heard Wei Yuanzhong and others say, indicating a report.
    • 今年大计防至六月诛逆韦宗赵韦等并斩峤解官归第 - This year's plan until June to punish the rebels, including Wei, Zong, Zhao, and others, and behead Li Qiao, who resigned and returned home, indicating a series of events.
    • 皆如其言 - All as he said, indicating confirmation of the predictions.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太平公主 - Princess Taiping, a powerful and influential figure during the Tang Dynasty, known for her political influence and involvement in court intrigues.
    • 睿宗 - Emperor Ruizong, a ruler of the Tang Dynasty who reigned briefly before being deposed.
    • 武三思 - A prominent figure during the Tang Dynasty, known for his close relationship with Empress Wu Zetian and his involvement in political affairs.
    • 魏元忠 - A high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his integrity and opposition to corrupt officials.
    • 李峤 - A poet and politician during the Tang Dynasty, known for his contributions to literature and his political career.
    • 韦温宗楚客赵履温 - Names of officials or figures during the Tang Dynasty, likely involved in political or judicial matters.
    • 大理卿 - The Chief Justice of the Tang Dynasty, responsible for judicial matters.
    • 逆韦宗赵韦 - Refers to officials or figures who were considered traitors or rebels.
    • 安州都督 - The governor of Anzhou, a region in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 处士 - A scholar who does not seek office, often living a reclusive life.
    • 萧时和 - A historical figure during the Tang Dynasty, known for his contributions to literature or history.
    • 墓志 - A tomb inscription, a type of memorial text written for a deceased person.
    • 龙衮 - The dragon robe of the emperor, a symbol of imperial authority.
    • 龙飞 - A metaphor for the ascension to the throne.
    • 隂隲祥符 - Auspicious signs or omens, often associated with divine approval.
    • 啓圣期 - The beginning of the sage era, a period of great wisdom and virtue.
    • 化元定成 - The transformation and establishment of the new era, indicating a significant change.
    • 拜于幂数 - The number of officials who were appointed, indicating a specific event.
    • 下者 - Someone below, likely referring to a subordinate or lower-ranking individual.
    • 敬挥 - Respectfully gestured, indicating a formal and respectful action.
    • 接拜 - Greeted with bows, indicating a formal greeting.
    • 着枷 - Wearing a枷, a type of punishment or restraint.
    • 着锁 - Wearing chains, another form of punishment or restraint.
    • 露头散腰立 - Standing with his head uncovered and loose waist, indicating a relaxed or informal posture.
    • 大计 - A major plan or strategy, often related to political or military matters.
    • 诛逆 - To punish the rebels, indicating a severe action against traitors.
    • 解官归第 - Resigned and returned home, indicating the end of a political career.

    Several dozen wore the same attire, and the person ordered to see the one holding the furnace recognized Huiyuan from afar as Princess Taiping. Huiyuan asked the reason for the belt, and the princess replied that she was pressing the dragon robe when suddenly a fire broke out. In the midst of the panic, she did not notice that her belt had been burned, and in her haste, she did not have time to change. Princess Taiping sighed and wept, declaring, "The rise of the sage is surely due to heaven." Huiyuan saw this three years before Emperor Ruizong's ascension, as recorded in his tomb inscription. When Emperor Ruizong ascended the throne, auspicious signs appeared, marking the beginning of the sage era, and the transformation and establishment of the new era were completed with the appointment of officials. Indeed, he later became the governor of Anzhou, and the scholar Huiyuan wrote a biography of him.

    According to another account, after Huiyuan was released, he entered a hall and asked who the person below was. Someone said it was Wei Yuanzhong. After a while, Huiyuan respectfully gestured for him to dismount, and the crowd greeted him with bows, saying, "This is the Chief Justice in charge of judicial matters." They saw Wu Sansi wearing a枷, and Wei Wen, Zong Chuke, and Zhao Lüwen wearing chains, while Li Qiao stood with his head uncovered and loose waist. Huiyuan heard Wei Yuanzhong and others say that this year's plan would reach June, and the rebels Wei, Zong, and Zhao would be punished, and Li Qiao would be beheaded and resign from office, returning home. All of this came to pass as he had said. [121]

    柴绍之弟某有材力轻趫迅防踊身而上挺然若飞十余步乃止太宗令取赵公长孙无忌鞍鞯仍先报无忌令其守备其夜见一物如鸟飞入宅内割讐而去追之不及又遣取丹阳公主镂金枕函飞入房内以手撚上公主面上举头即以他枕易之而去至晓乃觉尝着吉莫靴走上砖城至女墙手无攀引又以足踏佛殿柱至檐头捻椽覆上越百尺楼阁了无障碍太宗竒之曰此人不可处京邑出为外官时人号为壁龙太宗尝赐长孙无忌七宝带千金时有大盗段师子从屋上上椽孔间而下露拔刀谓曰公动即死遂于函中取带去以刀拄地踊身椽孔间出 [122]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [122]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 踊身而上挺然若飞 - Literally means 'leaping up and standing straight as if flying,' describing a sudden, powerful leap.
    • 越百尺楼阁了无障碍 - Means 'crossing over a hundred-foot tower with no obstacles,' indicating extraordinary physical prowess.
    • 割仇讐而去 - Means 'cutting off enemies and leaving,' suggesting an act of self-defense or escape.
    • 举头即以他枕易之而去 - Means 'lifting his head and replacing it with another pillow before leaving,' suggesting a quick, stealthy action.
    • 至晓乃觉尝着吉莫靴走上砖城 - 'By dawn, it was noticed that he had been wearing Gimo boots and walking on the brick wall.'
    • 至女墙手无攀引 - Means 'reaching the parapet without any support,' describing a seemingly impossible feat.
