    • 又以足踏佛殿柱至檐头捻椽覆上 - Describes the act of stepping on the column of a Buddhist hall and reaching the roof.
    • 飞入房内以手撚上公主面上 - Describes the object flying into the room and touching Princess Danyang's face.
    • 露拔刀谓曰公动即死 - Means 'revealing his sword and saying, 'If you move, you will die,' indicating a threatening situation.
    • 遂于函中取带去以刀拄地踊身椽孔间出 - Describes the thief taking the belt and jumping out through the beam hole, supported by his sword.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 柴绍 - A prominent general during the early Tang Dynasty.
    • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang, one of the most famous emperors in Chinese history.
    • 赵公长孙无忌 - Grand Duke Changsun Wuji, a high-ranking official and close advisor to Emperor Taizong.
    • 丹阳公主 - Princess Danyang, a member of the royal family.
    • 吉莫靴 - Gimo boots, a type of footwear.
    • 七宝带 - A seven-jewel belt, a valuable and ornate piece of jewelry.
    • 大盗段师子 - A notorious thief named Duan Shizi.
    • 佛殿 - A Buddhist hall or temple.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This long sentence describes the brother's physical abilities and a specific action. It can be broken down into: 'The brother of Chai Shao had extraordinary physical abilities, agility, and speed. He leaped up and stood straight as if flying, covering more than ten steps before stopping.'
    • This sentence can be broken down into: 'The emperor ordered to fetch the saddle and stirrups of Grand Duke Changsun Wuji and also ordered to notify Changsun Wuji to prepare for defense.'
    • This sentence describes a supernatural event: 'At night, an object like a bird flew into the house, cutting off enemies and leaving.'
    • This sentence describes a similar supernatural event: 'The emperor ordered to fetch the gold-inlaid pillow case of Princess Danyang. The object flew into the room and touched Princess Danyang's face, then lifted his head and replaced it with another pillow before leaving.'
    • This long sentence describes a series of supernatural feats: 'By dawn, it was noticed that he had been wearing Gimo boots and walking on the brick wall, reaching the parapet without any support. He stepped on the column of a Buddhist hall and reached the roof, crossing over a hundred-foot tower with no obstacles.'
    • This sentence can be broken down into: 'The emperor found this amazing and said, 'This person cannot be stationed in the capital; he should be sent out as an official.' People at the time called him the Wall Dragon.'
    • This sentence describes a theft: 'The emperor once gave Changsun Wuji a seven-jewel belt worth a thousand gold pieces. At that time, a major thief named Duan Shizi descended from the roof through a beam hole, revealing his sword and saying, 'If you move, you will die.' He took the belt and jumped out through the beam hole, supported by his sword.'

    Proper Nouns

    • 柴绍 - A prominent general during the early Tang Dynasty.
    • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 赵公长孙无忌 - Grand Duke Changsun Wuji.
    • 丹阳公主 - Princess Danyang.
    • 吉莫靴 - Gimo boots.
    • 七宝带 - A seven-jewel belt.
    • 大盗段师子 - A notorious thief named Duan Shizi.
    • 佛殿 - A Buddhist hall or temple.

    Chai Shao's younger brother possessed extraordinary physical abilities, agility, and speed. He could leap up and stand straight as if flying, covering more than ten steps before stopping. Emperor Taizong ordered to fetch the saddle and stirrups of Grand Duke Changsun Wuji and also ordered to notify Changsun Wuji to prepare for defense. One night, an object like a bird flew into the house, cutting off enemies and leaving. The pursuers could not catch it. Another time, the emperor ordered to fetch the gold-inlaid pillow case of Princess Danyang. The object flew into the room and touched Princess Danyang's face, then lifted his head and replaced it with another pillow before leaving. By dawn, it was noticed that he had been wearing Gimo boots and walking on the brick wall, reaching the parapet without any support. He stepped on the column of a Buddhist hall and reached the roof, crossing over a hundred-foot tower with no obstacles. Emperor Taizong found this amazing and said, "This person cannot be stationed in the capital; he should be sent out as an official." People at the time called him the Wall Dragon. The emperor once gave Changsun Wuji a seven-jewel belt worth a thousand gold pieces. At that time, a major thief named Duan Shizi descended from the roof through a beam hole, revealing his sword and saying, "If you move, you will die." He took the belt and jumped out through the beam hole, supported by his sword. [122]

    天后时将军李楷固契丹人也善用防索李尽忠之败也麻仁节张遇等并被防将麞鹿狐兎走马遮截放索防之百无一漏鞍马上弄弓矢矛稍如飞仙天后惜其材不杀用以为将稍贪财好色出为潭州乔口镇守将愤恚而卒
    　　宋令文者有神力禅定寺有牛触人莫之敢近筑围以防之令文怪其故遂袒褐而入牛竦角向前令文接两角拔之应手而倒颈骨皆折而死又以五指撮碓觜壁上书得四十字诗为太学生以一手挟讲堂柱起以同房生衣于柱下压之许重设酒乃为之出令文有三子长之问有文誉次之逊善书次之悌有勇力之悌后左降朱鸢防贼破驩州以之悌为总管击之募壮士得八人之悌身长八尺被重甲前大呌曰獠贼动即死贼七百人一时俱剉大破之 [123]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [123]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 善用防索 - Skilled in using a defensive cord, referring to a military tactic.
    • 百无一漏 - Hyperbolic expression meaning not a single one escapes, emphasizing the effectiveness of the tactic.
    • 鞍马上弄弓矢矛稍如飞仙 - Describes a skillful and agile performance with weapons, likened to a flying immortal.
    • 惜其材不杀用以为将 - Valuing his talent, she did not kill him but instead appointed him as a general.
    • 稍贪财好色 - Gradually became greedy for wealth and lustful.
    • 筑围以防之 - Building a fence to prevent it from causing harm.
    • 接两角拔之应手而倒 - Sudden and powerful action where the horns are seized and the animal is instantly subdued.
    • 一时俱剉 - They were all cut down at once.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天后 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history, known for her political acumen and patronage of the arts.
    • 李楷固 - A general during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, known for his military skills.
    • 李尽忠 - A rebel leader during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 麻仁节张遇 - Military leaders who were captured during the battle against Rui Zhong.
    • 契丹人 - Refers to the Khitan people, a nomadic group from northeastern China.
    • 禅定寺 - A Buddhist temple, indicating the cultural and religious context of the time.
    • 宋令文 - A figure known for his extraordinary physical strength and martial skills.
    • 潭州乔口镇 - A location in the Tang dynasty, indicating the geographical and administrative context.
    • 朱鸢 - A place name, indicating the demotion of Song Lingwen.
    • 驩州 - A city in the Tang dynasty, indicating the location of the rebellion.

    Proper Nouns

    • 天后 - Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 李楷固 - A general during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 李尽忠 - A rebel leader during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 麻仁节张遇 - Military leaders.
    • 契丹人 - The Khitan people.
    • 禅定寺 - A Buddhist temple.
    • 宋令文 - A figure known for his extraordinary physical strength and martial skills.
    • 潭州乔口镇 - A location in the Tang dynasty.
    • 朱鸢 - A place name.
    • 驩州 - A city in the Tang dynasty.

    During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, General Li Kaigu, a Khitan, was skilled in using defensive cords. When Li Jinzhong was defeated, military leaders such as Ma Renjie and Zhang Xuanyu were all captured. Using cavalry to block and restrain the enemy with cords, they managed to capture every single one without a single escape, and the soldiers performed with such agility and skill that it seemed like they were flying immortals. Empress Wu Zetian valued his talent and did not kill him but instead appointed him as a general. However, he gradually became greedy for wealth and lustful, and was sent out to serve as the commander of the Qiao Kou garrison in Tan Prefecture, where he died in anger and resentment.

    Song Lingwen possessed supernatural strength. At Chan Ding Temple, there was a bull that would charge at people, and no one dared to approach it. A fence was built to prevent it from causing harm. Song Lingwen, curious about the reason, entered the temple in a bare-chested and loose-fitting garment. The bull raised its horns forward, but Song Lingwen seized its horns and instantly subdued it, breaking its neck bones and causing it to die. He also used five fingers to hold a pestle and wrote a forty-character poem on the wall, becoming a student at the Imperial Academy. He lifted the lecture hall pillar with one hand and pressed the clothes of fellow students under the pillar, only letting them go after promising to set up more wine. Song Lingwen had three sons: the eldest, Song Zhiwen, had a reputation for scholarship; the second, Song Xun, was skilled in writing; and the third, Song Ti, possessed courage and strength. Later, Song Ti was demoted to the position of governor of Zhu Yan. When the rebels broke through the city of Huan, Song Ti commanded the attack, recruiting eight brave soldiers. He was tall, eight feet in height, and wore heavy armor. Shouting loudly as he rushed forward, he declared, "The rebels will die if they move." He cut down seven hundred rebels at once and decisively defeated them. [123]

    彭博通者河间人也身长八尺曾于讲堂堦上临堦而立取鞋一防以臂夹令有力者后拔之鞋底中断博通脚终不移牛驾车正走博通倒曵车尾却行数十步横拔车辙深二尺余皆纵横破裂曾逰埠江有急风张防博通捉尾缆挽之不进
    　　定襄公李宏虢王之子身长八尺曾猎有虎搏之踣而卧虎坐其上奴走马傍过虎跳攫奴后鞍宏起引弓射之中臂而死宏及奴一无所伤
    　　忠武将军辛承嗣轻防曽解鞍绊马脱衣而卧令一人百步走马持鎗而来承嗣鞴马解绊着衣擐甲上马盘鎗逆拒刺马擒人而还承嗣曾与将军元帅驰骋一手捉鞍桥双足上捺蜻蜓走马二十里与中裴绍业于青海被吐蕃围谓绍业曰相随带将军共出绍业惧不敢承嗣曰为将军试之军马持鎗所向皆靡却迎绍业出承嗣马被箭乃跳下夺贼壮马乗之一无损伤裴旻为幽州都督孙佺北征被奚贼围之旻马上立走轮刀雷发箭若星流应刀而断贼不敢取蓬飞而去 [124]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [124]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 彭博通者河间人也 - Classical biographical opening, meaning 'Peng Botong was a man from Hejian.'
    • 身长八尺 - Means 'tall, eight chi in height,' where 'chi' is an ancient unit of length.
    • 令有力者后拔之 - Means 'ordered a strong person to pull it backward.'
    • 鞋底中断 - Means 'the sole of the shoe broke.'
    • 博通脚终不移 - Means 'Peng Botong's feet did not move.'
    • 牛驾车正走 - Means 'an ox was pulling a cart forward.'
    • 博通倒曵车尾 - Means 'Peng Botong dragged the cart backward by the tail.'
    • 却行数十步 - Means 'backed up several steps.'
    • 横拔车辙深二尺余 - Means 'dug a deep furrow in the cart tracks, more than two chi deep.'
    • 皆纵横破裂 - Means 'all were torn apart in all directions.'
    • 曾遊埠江有急风张防 - Means 'once sailed on the Yellow River and faced a sudden storm.'
    • 博通捉尾缆挽之 - Means 'Peng Botong grabbed the stern rope and pulled it.'
    • 不进 - Means 'it did not move forward.'
    • 定襄公李宏虢王之子 - Means 'Dingxiang Prince Li Hong, son of the Prince of Guo.'
    • 曾猎有虎搏之踣而卧 - Means 'once hunted and was knocked down by a tiger.'
    • 虎坐其上 - Means 'the tiger sat on top of him.'
    • 奴走马傍过 - Means 'a servant rode past on a horse.'
    • 虎跳攫奴后鞍 - Means 'the tiger jumped and grabbed the servant's saddle.'
    • 宏起引弓射之中臂而死 - Means 'Li Hong stood up, drew his bow, and shot the tiger in the arm, killing it.'
    • 宏及奴一无所伤 - Means 'Li Hong and the servant were unharmed.'
    • 忠武将军辛承嗣轻防 - Means 'Zhongwu General Xin Chengsi was a light cavalryman.'
    • 曾解鞍绊马脱衣而卧 - Means 'once dismounted, tied his horse, and lay down without his clothes.'
    • 令一人百步走马持鎗而来 - Means 'ordered a man to ride a hundred paces on horseback with a spear.'
    • 承嗣鞴马解绊着衣擐甲上马盘鎗逆拒 - Means 'Xin Chengsi saddled his horse, untied it, put on his clothes, donned his armor, mounted, and wielded his spear to block.'
    • 刺马擒人而还 - Means 'stabbed the horse and captured the man.'
    • 承嗣曾与将军元帅驰骋 - Means 'Xin Chengsi once raced with General Yuan Shuai.'
    • 一手捉鞍桥双足上捺蜻蜓走马二十里 - Means 'held the saddle with one hand and walked on the horse's back with both feet, riding like a dragonfly for twenty li.'
    • 与中裴绍业于青海被吐蕃围 - Means 'with the mid-rank officer Pei Shaoye, they were surrounded by the Tibetans in Qinghai.'
    • 谓绍业曰相随带将军共出 - Means 'said to Pei Shaoye, 'Let's go together and lead the general out.''
    • 绍业惧不敢 - Means 'Pei Shaoye was afraid and dared not.'
    • 为将军试之 - Means 'I'll try it for the general.'
    • 军马持鎗所向皆靡 - Means 'the army's horses with spears all fell back.'
    • 却迎绍业出 - Means 'he led Pei Shaoye out.'
    • 承嗣马被箭乃跳下夺贼壮马乘之 - Means 'Xin Chengsi's horse was shot, so he jumped down and took a strong horse from the enemy.'
    • 一无损伤 - Means 'he suffered no injuries.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 彭博通 - A historical figure known for his strength and skill.
    • 河间 - A region in northern China.
    • 定襄公李宏 - A historical figure, son of the Prince of Guo.
    • 忠武将军辛承嗣 - A historical figure, a military officer.
    • 虢王 - A title of a prince.
    • 黄河南 - The Yellow River, one of the major rivers in China.
    • 吐蕃 - The Tibetan Empire, a historical power in the region of Qinghai and Tibet.

    Proper Nouns

    • 彭博通 - Peng Botong
    • 河间 - Hejian
    • 定襄公李宏 - Dingxiang Prince Li Hong
    • 虢王 - Prince of Guo
    • 忠武将军辛承嗣 - Zhongwu General Xin Chengsi
    • 黄河南 - Yellow River
    • 吐蕃 - Tibetans

    Peng Botong was a man from Hejian, eight chi tall. Once he stood on the steps of a lecture hall overlooking the steps and picked up a shoe, holding it between his arms. He ordered a strong person to pull it backward, but the sole of the shoe broke, and Peng Botong's feet did not move. An ox was pulling a cart forward, and Peng Botong dragged the cart backward by the tail, backing up several steps and digging a deep furrow in the cart tracks, more than two chi deep, all torn apart in all directions. Once he sailed on the Yellow River and faced a sudden storm. Peng Botong grabbed the stern rope and pulled it, but it did not move forward.

    Dingxiang Prince Li Hong, son of the Prince of Guo, was also eight chi tall. Once he hunted and was knocked down by a tiger, which sat on top of him. A servant rode past on a horse, and the tiger jumped and grabbed the servant's saddle. Li Hong stood up, drew his bow, and shot the tiger in the arm, killing it. Li Hong and the servant were unharmed.

    Zhongwu General Xin Chengsi was a light cavalryman. Once he dismounted, tied his horse, and lay down without his clothes. He ordered a man to ride a hundred paces on horseback with a spear. Xin Chengsi saddled his horse, untied it, put on his clothes, donned his armor, mounted, and wielded his spear to block, stabbing the horse and capturing the man. Xin Chengsi once raced with General Yuan Shuai, holding the saddle with one hand and walking on the horse's back with both feet, riding like a dragonfly for twenty li. With the mid-rank officer Pei Shaoye, they were surrounded by the Tibetans in Qinghai. Xin Chengsi said to Pei Shaoye, "Let's go together and lead the general out." Pei Shaoye was afraid and dared not. Xin Chengsi said, "I'll try it for the general." The army's horses with spears all fell back, and he led Pei Shaoye out. Xin Chengsi's horse was shot, so he jumped down and took a strong horse from the enemy, suffering no injuries.

    Peng Min, the Governor of Youzhou, was leading General Sun Quan northward when he was surrounded by the Xiqi. Peng Min stood on his horse, wielding his wheel knife like thunder, his arrows flying like stars, cutting through the enemy's arrows. The enemy dared not approach, and they flew away like chaff. [124]

    观中恒州有彭闼高瓉二人鬭豪时于大酺塲上两朋竟胜闼活捉一豚从头齩至项放之地上仍走瓉取猫儿从尾食之肠肚俱尽仍鸣唤不止闼于是乎帖然心服
    　　梁庾信从南朝初至北方文士多轻之信将枯树赋以示之于后无敢言者时温子升作韩陵山寺碑信读而写其本南人问信曰北方文士何如信曰唯有韩陵山一片石堪共语薛道衡卢思道少解把笔自余驴鸣犬吠聒耳而已
    　　卢照隣字升之范阳人弱冠拜邓王府典籖王府书记一以委之王有书十二车照隣总披览略能记忆后为益州新都县尉秩满婆娑于蜀中放旷诗酒故世称王杨卢骆照隣闻之曰喜居王后耻在骆前时杨之为文好以古人姓名连用如张平子之略谈陆士衡之所记潘安仁宜其陋矣仲长统何足知之号为簿骆宾王文好以数对如秦地重闗一百二汉家离宫三十六时人号为博士如卢生之文时人莫能评其得失矣惜哉不幸有冉耕之疾着幽忧子以释愤焉文集二十卷 [125]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [125]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 鬭豪 - Colloquial term meaning 'to compete in extravagance' or 'to vie in splendor.'
    • 活捉一豚从头齩至项 - Vivid description of catching a pig alive and biting it from the head to the neck.
    • 从尾食之肠肚俱尽 - Description of eating a cat from the tail until the intestines are completely consumed.
    • 鸣唤不止 - Means 'continuously calling out' or 'crying incessantly.'
    • 帖然心服 - Means 'to submit quietly' or 'to surrender peacefully.'
    • 文士多轻之 - Means 'scholars generally look down on him.'
    • 唯有韩陵山一片石堪共语 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'only the stone inscription at Hanling Mountain can be considered worthy of conversation.'
    • 驴鸣犬吠聒耳而已 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'nothing but the noise of donkeys and dogs fills the ears.'
    • 弱冠拜邓王府典签 - Formal expression meaning 'at the age of twenty, he was appointed as a secretary to the Duke of Deng's mansion.'
    • 秩满婆娑于蜀中 - Means 'after completing his term, he wandered leisurely in Shu (Sichuan).'
    • 放旷诗酒 - Means 'living a carefree life with poetry and wine.'
    • 喜居王后耻在骆前 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'happy to be behind Wang but ashamed to be ahead of Lu.'
    • 好以古人姓名连用 - Means 'to enjoy using ancient people's names in sequence.'
    • 好以数对 - Means 'to enjoy using paired numbers.'
    • 冉耕之疾 - Euphemism for a serious illness, possibly referring to a specific ailment named after Ran Geng.
    • 着幽忧子以释愤焉 - Means 'wrote *You You Zi* to vent his frustration.'
    • 惜哉不幸有冉耕之疾 - Means 'unfortunately, he suffered from a serious illness, which is truly regrettable.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 彭闼高瓉 - Names of two individuals who competed in extravagance.
    • 大酺塲上 - Refers to a public gathering place where large feasts were held.
    • 枯树赋 - A famous literary work by Yu Xin, known for its vivid imagery and emotional depth.
    • 韩陵山寺碑 - A famous stone inscription by Wen Zisheng, considered a masterpiece of Northern Liang literature.
    • 薛道衡 - A prominent poet and statesman of the Northern Qi and Sui dynasties.
    • 卢思道 - A famous poet and scholar of the Northern Qi and Sui dynasties.
    • 邓王府 - The mansion of the Duke of Deng, a high-ranking noble.
    • 益州新都县 - A county in the state of Yi (modern-day Sichuan).
    • 蜀中 - Refers to the region of Sichuan.
    • 王杨卢骆 - Refers to the 'Four Talents of the Early Tang Dynasty': Wang Bo, Yang Jiong, Lu Zhaolín, and Luo Binwang.
    • 张平子 - Zhang Heng, a famous astronomer and poet of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 陆士衡 - Lu Ji, a famous poet of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 潘安仁 - Pan An, a famous poet and official of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 仲长统 - Zhong Changtong, a famous philosopher and statesman of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 冉耕 - Ran Geng, a disciple of Confucius, often used as a metaphor for a serious illness.

    Proper Nouns

    • 彭闼 - Peng Ta
    • 高瓉 - Gao Zhan
    • 大酺塲 - Da Pu Field
    • 温子升 - Wen Zisheng
    • 韩陵山寺碑 - Hanling Mountain Temple Stele
    • 薛道衡 - Xue Daoheng
    • 卢思道 - Lu Sida
    • 邓王府 - Duke of Deng's Mansion
    • 益州新都县 - Yizhou Xindu County
    • 蜀中 - Shu (Sichuan)
    • 王杨卢骆 - Wang, Yang, Lu, Luo (the Four Talents of the Early Tang Dynasty)
    • 张平子 - Zhang Pingzi (Zhang Heng)
    • 陆士衡 - Lu Shiheng (Lu Ji)
    • 潘安仁 - Pan Anren (Pan An)
    • 仲长统 - Zhong Changtong
    • 卢照邻 - Lu Zhaolín
    • 范阳 - Fanyang
    • 益州新都县尉 - Yizhou Xindu County Magistrate
    • 骆宾王 - Luo Binwang

    During the reign of Emperor Wu of the Northern Zhou dynasty, there were two men from Hengzhou named Peng Ta and Gao Zhan who competed in extravagance. At a grand feast, their friends vied to see who could outdo the other. Peng Ta caught a live pig and bit it from the head to the neck, then released it onto the ground and let it run. Gao Zhan took a cat and ate it from the tail until its intestines were completely consumed, then it continued to call out incessantly. Peng Ta was thus quietly and peacefully convinced.

    In the early days of the Northern Zhou dynasty, Yu Xin, who had come from the Southern Dynasty, was generally looked down upon by scholars. He presented his famous Ku Shu Fu (Essay on the Withered Tree) to them, and after that, no one dared to criticize him. At that time, Wen Zisheng wrote the Hanling Mountain Temple Stele, which Yu Xin read and transcribed. A southerner asked Yu Xin, "How are the scholars of the North?" Yu Xin replied, "Only the stone inscription at Hanling Mountain can be considered worthy of conversation. Xue Daoheng and Lu Sida can handle a brush with some skill, but the rest are nothing but the noise of donkeys and dogs filling the ears."

    Lu Zhaolín, courtesy name Shengzhi, was from Fanyang. At the age of twenty, he was appointed as a secretary to the Duke of Deng's mansion, and all the writing for the mansion was entrusted to him. The duke had twelve carts of books, which Lu Zhaolín read and reviewed, memorizing most of them. Later, he served as the county magistrate of Xindu in Yizhou. After completing his term, he wandered leisurely in Shu (Sichuan), living a carefree life with poetry and wine, and was thus known as one of the "Four Talents of the Early Tang Dynasty" alongside Wang Bo, Yang Jiong, and Luo Binwang. Lu Zhaolín heard this and said, "I am happy to be behind Wang Bo but ashamed to be ahead of Luo Binwang." At that time, Yang Jiong enjoyed using ancient people's names in sequence in his writing, such as mentioning Zhang Heng's brief talk and Lu Ji's recorded accounts, calling Pan An "fit for a humble position," and considering Zhong Changtong as someone not worth knowing, earning him the nickname "Ghost Book." Luo Binwang enjoyed using paired numbers in his writing, such as describing the heavy gates of Qin as "one hundred and two" and the imperial palaces of the Han dynasty as "thirty-six," earning him the nickname "Doctor of Numbers." As for Lu Zhaolín's writing, people could not evaluate its merits or faults. Unfortunately, he suffered from a serious illness and wrote You You Zi to vent his frustration. He compiled twenty volumes of collected works. [125]

    北齐兰陵王有巧思为舞胡子王意所欲劝胡子则捧盏以揖之人莫知其所由也
    　　幽州人刘交戴长竿高七十尺自擎上下有女十二甚端正于竿上置定跨盘独立见者不忍女无惧色后竟为扑杀
    　　巧人张崇者能作灰画腰带铰具每一胯大如钱灰画烧之见火即起作龙鱼鸟兽之形莫不悉备
    　　则天如意中海州进一匠造十二辰车回辕正南则午门开马头人出四方回转不爽毫厘又作木火通铁盏盛火辗转不翻
    　　韩王元嘉有一铜樽背上贮酒而一足倚满则正立不满则倾又为铜鸠氊上摩之熟则鸣如真鸠之声
    　　洛州殷文亮曽为县令性巧好酒刻木为人衣以缯防酌酒行觞皆有次第又作妓女唱歌吹笙皆能应节饮不尽即木小儿不肯把饮未竟则木妓女歌管连理催此亦莫测其神妙也
    　　将作大匠杨务防甚有巧思常于沁州市内刻木作僧手执一椀自能行乞椀中钱满闗键忽发自然作声云布施市人竞观欲其作声施者日盈数千矣 [126]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [126]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 巧思 - Refers to ingenious or clever thinking, often in the context of craftsmanship or invention.
    • 舞胡子 - A type of dance or performance involving the manipulation of a beard or similar prop.
    • 捧盏以揖人 - A gesture where one holds a cup and bows to someone, possibly as a form of greeting or offering.
    • 定跨盘 - A circular platform or stage on which performers stand or dance.
    • 莫知其所由也 - Means 'no one knows how it is done,' indicating the mystery or complexity of the performance.
    • 高七十尺 - A very tall pole, approximately 21 meters high.
    • 自擎上下 - Means 'to move up and down by oneself,' suggesting the performer's ability to control the pole.
    • 甚端正 - Means 'very beautiful' or 'very proper,' describing the appearance or demeanor of the women.
    • 见者不忍 - Means 'those who see cannot bear it,' indicating the emotional impact of the performance.
    • 无惧色 - Means 'without fear,' describing the calmness of the women.
    • 竟为扑杀 - Means 'eventually killed by beating,' indicating a tragic end.
    • 灰画 - Refers to a type of drawing or painting done with ash or charcoal.
    • 铰具 - A tool used for cutting or shaping metal.
    • 大如钱 - Means 'as big as a coin,' describing the size of the tool.
    • 龙鱼鸟兽之形 - Refers to the shapes of dragons, fish, birds, and beasts, indicating the complexity of the designs.
    • 莫不悉备 - Means 'nothing is missing,' indicating the completeness of the designs.
    • 海州进一匠 - Means 'a craftsman from Haizhou presented,' indicating the origin of the craftsman.
    • 十二辰车 - A chariot with twelve zodiac animals, likely used for astrological or decorative purposes.
    • 回辕正南 - Means 'turning the chariot to face south,' indicating the direction of movement.
    • 午门开 - Means 'the gate of the noon opens,' possibly referring to a specific gate or opening mechanism.
    • 马头人出 - Means 'a person with a horse head appears,' indicating a specific figure or character.
    • 四方回转不爽毫厘 - Means 'turning in all directions without deviation,' indicating precision.
    • 木火通铁盏盛火辗转不翻 - A complex device where a wooden fire is transferred to an iron cup without spilling, indicating a clever mechanism.
    • 铜樽 - A bronze wine vessel.
    • 背上贮酒 - Means 'storing wine on the back,' indicating the design of the vessel.
    • 一足倚满则正立不满则倾 - Means 'if one leg is full, it stands upright; if not, it tilts,' indicating a self-balancing mechanism.
    • 铜鸠 - A bronze dove or pigeon.
    • 毡上摩之熟则鸣如真鸠之声 - Means 'when rubbed on a felt, it makes a sound like a real dove,' indicating a sound-producing mechanism.
    • 洛州殷文亮 - A person named Yin Wenliang from Luozhou.
    • 刻木为人 - Means 'carving wood into human figures,' indicating a sculptural technique.
    • 衣以缯防 - Means 'dressed in silk and cloth,' indicating the materials used.
    • 酌酒行觞 - Means 'pouring wine and serving cups,' indicating a ritual or performance.
    • 皆有次第 - Means 'all in order,' indicating a structured sequence.
    • 妓女唱歌吹笙 - Means 'dancing girls singing and playing the sheng (reed pipe),' indicating a musical performance.
    • 应节 - Means 'in rhythm,' indicating synchronization with music.
    • 木小儿不肯把饮 - Means 'the wooden child refuses to drink,' indicating a mechanical action.
    • 未竟则木妓女歌管连理催 - Means 'if not finished, the wooden dancing girls sing and play the pipes to encourage,' indicating a continuation of the performance.
    • 将作大匠杨务防 - A person named Yang Wufang, a master craftsman.
    • 沁州市内 - Means 'in the market of Qinzhou,' indicating the location.
    • 刻木作僧 - Means 'carving wood into a monk,' indicating a sculptural technique.
    • 手执一椀 - Means 'holding a bowl,' indicating the monk's pose.
    • 自能行乞 - Means 'able to beg by itself,' indicating a mechanical action.
    • 椀中钱满 - Means 'when the bowl is full of money,' indicating a condition.
    • 关键忽发 - Means 'when the lock suddenly opens,' indicating a mechanism.
    • 自然作声 - Means 'naturally makes a sound,' indicating a sound-producing mechanism.
    • 云布施 - Means 'saying 'donation,' indicating a verbal component.
    • 市人竞观 - Means 'people in the market watch in a rush,' indicating a crowd.
    • 欲其作声 - Means 'wanting it to make a sound,' indicating a desire.
    • 施者日盈数千矣 - Means 'donors fill up thousands every day,' indicating the success of the device.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 兰陵王 - A historical figure from the Northern Qi dynasty, known for his military prowess and cultural contributions.
    • 北齐 - The Northern Qi dynasty, a short-lived Chinese dynasty that ruled from 550 to 577 AD.
    • 幽州 - An ancient Chinese province, located in what is now northern China.
    • 则天如意中 - Refers to the reign of Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history, during her rule from 690 to 705 AD.
    • 海州 - An ancient Chinese prefecture, located in what is now eastern China.
    • 洛州 - An ancient Chinese prefecture, located in what is now Henan Province.
    • 殷文亮 - A historical figure from the Tang dynasty, known for his literary and artistic talents.
    • 将作大匠 - An official position in ancient China responsible for overseeing construction and craftsmanship.

    Proper Nouns

    • 兰陵王 - A historical figure from the Northern Qi dynasty.
    • 北齐 - The Northern Qi dynasty.
    • 幽州 - An ancient Chinese province.
    • 则天如意中 - The reign of Wu Zetian.
    • 海州 - An ancient Chinese prefecture.
    • 洛州 - An ancient Chinese prefecture.
    • 殷文亮 - A historical figure from the Tang dynasty.
    • 将作大匠 - An official position in ancient China.
    • 杨务防 - A historical figure known for his craftsmanship.

    During the Northern Qi dynasty, Lanling Wang was renowned for his ingenious thoughts and created a dance with a beard, which was what the king desired. When the beard was moved, it would hold a cup and bow to people, and no one knew how it was done.

    A man from Youzhou named Liu Jiao wore a tall pole seventy feet high and moved up and down by himself. There were twelve women on the pole, who were very beautiful and stood on a fixed circular platform. Those who saw them could not bear it, and the women showed no fear. Eventually, they were killed by beating.

    A skilled craftsman named Zhang Chong could create ash drawings and metal tools. Each tool was as big as a coin, and when the ash was burned, it would rise and form the shapes of dragons, fish, birds, and beasts, all of which were complete.

    During the reign of Wu Zetian, a craftsman from Haizhou presented a chariot with twelve zodiac animals. When the chariot turned to face south, the gate of the noon opened, and a person with a horse head appeared. It turned in all directions without deviation and also created a device where a wooden fire was transferred to an iron cup without spilling.

    King Yuanjia of Han had a bronze wine vessel with wine stored on its back. If one leg was full, it stood upright; if not, it tilted. He also had a bronze dove that made a sound like a real dove when rubbed on a felt.

    Yin Wenliang from Luozhou, who once served as a county magistrate, was skilled in carving wood into human figures. He dressed them in silk and cloth, poured wine, and served cups, all in order. He also created dancing girls who sang and played the sheng, all in rhythm. If the drink was not finished, the wooden child would not take it, and if not finished, the wooden dancing girls would sing and play the pipes to encourage. This was also beyond comprehension in its miraculous nature.

    Yang Wufang, a master craftsman, often carved wood into monks in the market of Qinzhou. The monk held a bowl and could beg by itself. When the bowl was full of money, the lock suddenly opened, and it naturally made a sound saying "donation." People in the market watched in a rush, wanting it to make a sound, and donors filled up thousands every day. [126]

    郴州刺史王琚刻木为獭沈于水中取鱼引首而出葢獭口中安饵为转闗以石缒之则沈鱼取其饵闗即发口合则衔鱼石发则浮出矣
    　　薛昚惑者善投壶龙跃隼飞矫无遗箭置壶于背后却反矢以投之百发百中
    　　天后朝地官郎中周子恭忽然暴亡见大帝于殿上坐裵子仪侍立子恭拜问为谁曰周子恭追到帝曰我唤许子儒何为错将子恭来即放去子恭苏问家中曰许侍郎好在否时子儒为天官侍郎已病其夜卒则天闻之驰驿向幷州问裴子仪时为判官无恙也
    　　张易之将败也母韦氏阿藏在宅坐家人报云有车马骑从甚多至门而下疑其内官也藏出迎之无所见又野狐数十擎饭瓮墙头而过未旬日而祸及垂拱之后诸州多进雌鸡化为雄鸡者则天之应也
    　　神龙中户部尚书李承嘉不识字不解书为御史大夫兼洛州长史名判司为狗骂御史为驴威振朝廷西京造一堂新成坊人见野狐无数直入宅须防堂舎四裂瓦木一聚判事笔管手中裂别取笔复裂如初数日出为藤州员外司马卒 [127]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [127]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 郴州刺史 - Governor of Chenzhou, a position in the Tang Dynasty
    • 刻木为獭 - Literally 'carve wood into a otter,' referring to a mechanical device
    • 沈于水中取鱼 - Sink into the water to catch fish, describing the function of the device
    • 引首而出 - Pull its head out, describing the mechanism's action
    • 口中安饵为转关 - Place bait in its mouth to make it a pivot, referring to the internal mechanism
    • 以石缒之则沈 - Tie it with a stone to sink, describing how the device is activated
    • 鱼取其饵关即发口合则衔鱼 - The fish takes the bait, the mechanism releases, and the mouth closes to catch the fish
    • 薛昚惑者善投壶 - Refers to Xue Shenhuo, who is skilled at the game of touhu (a type of archery game)
    • 龙跃隼飞矫无遗箭 - Like dragons leaping and falcons flying, shooting without missing an arrow, describing his skill
    • 置壶于背后却反矢以投之百发百中 - Place the pot behind him and shoot backward, hitting every time, describing his technique
    • 天后朝 - The reign of Empress Wu Zetian
    • 地官郎中 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty
    • 忽然暴亡 - Suddenly died
    • 见大帝于殿上坐 - Saw the Great Emperor sitting on the throne
    • 裴子仪侍立 - Refers to Pei Ziying, who stood beside the emperor
    • 周子恭追到帝曰 - Zhou Zigong was summoned to the emperor and said
    • 我唤许子儒何为错将子恭来 - I called for Xu Ziru, why did you bring Zhou Zigong instead?
    • 即放去子恭苏问家中 - He immediately released him, and Zhou Zigong woke up and asked his family
    • 许侍郎好在否 - Is Minister Xu still well?
    • 时子儒为天官侍郎已病其夜卒 - At that time, Xu Ziru was the Minister of Personnel and was already ill, dying that night
    • 则天闻之驰驿向并州问裴子仪 - Empress Wu heard about it and hurried to Pingzhou to ask Pei Ziying
    • 时为判官无恙也 - At that time, he was a judge and was fine
    • 张易之将败也母韦氏阿藏在宅 - Refers to Zhang Yizhi, who was about to be defeated, and his mother, Lady Wei, who was at home
    • 坐家人报云有车马骑从甚多至门而下 - She was sitting when the family reported that there were many carriages and horses arriving at the gate
    • 疑其内官也藏出迎之无所见 - She suspected it was an imperial official and went out to greet them, but saw nothing
    • 又野狐数十擎饭瓮墙头而过 - Then dozens of wild foxes carried bowls of food over the wall
    • 未旬日而祸及 - Within ten days, disaster befell him
    • 垂拱之后诸州多进雌鸡化为雄鸡者 - After the reign of Wu Zetian, many states reported that hens had turned into roosters
    • 则天之应也 - This was a sign from Empress Wu
    • 神龙中户部尚书李承嘉 - Refers to Li Chengjia, the Minister of Revenue during the Shangyuan era
    • 不识字不解书为御史大夫兼洛州长史 - He could not read or write and was appointed as a high-ranking official in Luoyang
    • 名判司为狗骂御史为驴 - He called the officials 'dogs' and the censors 'donkeys'
    • 威振朝廷 - His authority shook the court
    • 西京造一堂新成坊人见野狐无数直入宅须防堂舎四裂瓦木一聚 - In the Western Capital, he built a new hall, and the people of the neighborhood saw countless wild foxes entering the house, causing the hall to collapse
    • 判事笔管手中裂别取笔复裂如初 - The pen in his hand split, and when he took another pen, it split again
    • 数日出为藤州员外司马卒 - After several days, he was sent to Tengzhou as an external司马 and died

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 郴州 - A prefecture in Hunan Province during the Tang Dynasty
    • 薛昚惑 - Xue Shenhuo, a skilled archer in the Tang Dynasty
    • 天后 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history
    • 地官郎中 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty
    • 裴子仪 - Pei Ziying, a trusted official during Empress Wu's reign
    • 张易之 - Zhang Yizhi, a powerful courtier who fell out of favor
    • 韦氏阿藏 - Lady Wei, Zhang Yizhi's mother
    • 李承嘉 - Li Chengjia, a high-ranking official known for his incompetence
    • 神龙中 - The Shangyuan era, a period during the Tang Dynasty

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • Break down long, complex sentences into shorter, more readable segments while preserving the original meaning
    • Segment the description of Xue Shenhuo's skill for clarity
    • Segment the events surrounding Zhou Zigong's death and resurrection for clarity
    • Segment the events leading to Zhang Yizhi's downfall for clarity
    • Segment the description of Li Chengjia's incompetence and subsequent downfall for clarity

    Proper Nouns

    • 郴州 - Chenzhou, a prefecture in Hunan Province
    • 薛昚惑 - Xue Shenhuo, a skilled archer
    • 天后 - Empress Wu Zetian
    • 地官郎中 - A high-ranking official
    • 裴子仪 - Pei Ziying, a trusted official
    • 张易之 - Zhang Yizhi, a powerful courtier
    • 韦氏阿藏 - Lady Wei, Zhang Yizhi's mother
    • 李承嘉 - Li Chengjia, a high-ranking official
    • 神龙中 - The Shangyuan era

    The Governor of Chenzhou, Wang Ju, carved a wooden otter that would sink into the water to catch fish, pulling its head out to retrieve the catch. Inside the otter's mouth was bait, which served as a pivot. The device was tied with a stone to sink; when the fish took the bait, the mechanism would release, and the otter's mouth would close to catch the fish. When the stone was released, the otter would float back to the surface.

    Xue Shenhuo was skilled at the game of touhu (a type of archery game). His arrows flew like dragons leaping and falcons soaring, never missing their target. He would place the pot behind him and shoot backward, hitting every time.

    During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, Zhou Zigong, a high-ranking official, suddenly died and appeared before the Great Emperor on the throne. Pei Ziying stood beside the emperor. Zhou Zigong bowed and asked, "Who are you?" The emperor replied, "I called for Xu Ziru, why did you bring Zhou Zigong instead?" He immediately released him, and Zhou Zigong woke up and asked his family, "Is Minister Xu still well?" At that time, Xu Ziru was the Minister of Personnel and was already ill, dying that night. Empress Wu heard about it and hurried to Pingzhou to ask Pei Ziying, who was a judge and was fine.

    As Zhang Yizhi was about to be defeated, his mother, Lady Wei, was at home. She was sitting when the family reported that there were many carriages and horses arriving at the gate. She suspected it was an imperial official and went out to greet them, but saw nothing. Then dozens of wild foxes carried bowls of food over the wall. Within ten days, disaster befell him. After the reign of Wu Zetian, many states reported that hens had turned into roosters. This was a sign from Empress Wu.

    During the Shangyuan era, Li Chengjia, the Minister of Revenue, could not read or write and was appointed as a high-ranking official in Luoyang. He called the officials "dogs" and the censors "donkeys." His authority shook the court. In the Western Capital, he built a new hall, and the people of the neighborhood saw countless wild foxes entering the house, causing the hall to collapse. The pen in his hand split, and when he took another pen, it split again. After several days, he was sent to Tengzhou as an external司马 and died. [127]

    大定年中太州赤水店有郑家庄有一儿年二十余日晏于驿路上见一青衣女子独行姿容姝丽问之云欲到郑县待三婢未来踌蹰伺候此儿屈就庄宿安置防中借给酒食将衣被同寝至晓门久不开呼之不应于窻中窥之唯有脑骨头颅在余并食讫家人破户入于梁上暗处见一大鸟冲门飞出或云是罗刹魅也
    　　怀州刺史梁载言昼坐防事【阙】　　　忽有物如蝙蝠从南飞来直入口中翕然似吞一物腹中遂绞痛数日而卒
    　　夀安男子不知姓名肘拍板鼻吹笛口唱歌能半靣笑半面啼一乌犬解人语应口所作与人无殊
    　　越州兵曹栁崇忽生于头呻吟不可忍于是召术士夜观之云有一妇女緑裙问之不应在君窻下急除之崇访窻下止见一瓷妓女极端正緑瓷为餙遂于鐡臼捣碎而焚之疮遂愈
    　　永徽中张鷟筑马槽厰宅正北掘一坑丈余时隂阳书云子地穿必有堕井死鷟有奴名永进淘井土崩压而死又鷟故宅有一桑高四五丈无故枯死寻而祖亡殁后有明隂阳云乔木先枯众子必孤此其騐也 [128]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [128]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 大定年中 - Refers to the year during the Dading era of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 晏于驿路上 - Means 'resting on the post road.'
    • 姿容姝丽 - Describes a beautiful appearance.
    • 踌蹰伺候 - Means 'hesitating and waiting.'
    • 屈就庄宿 - Means 'to stay at the village for the night.'
    • 安置防中 - Means 'to arrange in the guardhouse.'
    • 借给酒食 - Means 'to provide food and drink.'
    • 同寝 - Means 'to sleep together.'
    • 晓门久不开 - Means 'the morning gate remained closed for a long time.'
    • 窻中窥之 - Means 'to look through the window.'
    • 唯有脑骨头颅在 - Means 'only the skull and bones remained.'
    • 家人破户入 - Means 'the family broke down the door to enter.'
    • 梁上暗处 - Means 'in a dark corner of the beam.'
    • 大鸟冲门飞出 - Means 'a large bird flew out through the door.'
    • 罗刹魅也 - Refers to a demon or evil spirit.
    • 怀州刺史梁载言 - Refers to the governor of Huai Prefecture, named Liang Zaiyan.
    • 昼坐防事 - Means 'sitting during the day in the office.'
    • 忽有物如蝙蝠从南飞来 - Means 'suddenly an object like a bat flew in from the south.'
    • 翕然似吞一物 - Means 'it seemed to swallow something.'
    • 腹中遂绞痛数日而卒 - Means 'he suffered from severe abdominal pain for several days and died.'
    • 寿安男子 - Refers to a man from Shou'an.
    • 肘拍板鼻吹笛口唱歌 - Describes a man who can play a board with his elbow, blow a flute with his nose, and sing with his mouth.
    • 能半面笑半面啼 - Means 'can smile on one side and cry on the other.'
    • 乌犬解人语应口 - Means 'a black dog can understand human speech and respond.'
    • 所作与人无殊 - Means 'what it does is no different from what a person does.'
    • 越州兵曹柳崇 - Refers to a military official named Liu Chong from Yue Prefecture.
    • 忽生于头 - Means 'a sudden growth appeared on his head.'
    • 呻吟不可忍 - Means 'he groaned in pain and could not bear it.'
    • 召术士夜观之 - Means 'he summoned a fortune-teller to observe it at night.'
    • 有一妇女绿裙 - Means 'there was a woman wearing a green skirt.'
    • 问之不应 - Means 'when asked, she did not respond.'
    • 在君窻下急除之 - Means 'quickly remove it from your window.'
    • 崇访窻下 - Means 'Liu Chong visited the window.'
    • 极端正绿瓷为餙 - Means 'extremely beautiful, adorned with green porcelain.'
    • 于铁臼捣碎而焚之 - Means 'he pounded it into pieces in an iron mortar and burned it.'
    • 疮遂愈 - Means 'the wound then healed.'
    • 永徽中 - Refers to the year during the Yonghui era of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 张鷟筑马槽厰宅 - Refers to Zhang Zhuo, who built a stable.
    • 正北掘一坑丈余 - Means 'dug a pit more than ten feet deep in the north.'
    • 阴阳书云 - Means 'according to the book of divination.'
    • 子地穿必有堕井死 - Means 'if the son's land is dug, someone will fall into a well and die.'
    • 奴名永进 - Refers to a slave named Yongjin.
    • 淘井土崩压而死 - Means 'while dredging the well, the soil collapsed and he was crushed to death.'
    • 故宅有一桑高四五丈 - Means 'in his old house, there was a mulberry tree about forty to fifty feet tall.'
    • 无故枯死 - Means 'it died without reason.'
    • 寻而祖亡殁 - Means 'soon after, his grandfather died.'
    • 明阴阳云 - Means 'according to the diviner.'
    • 乔木先枯众子必孤 - Means 'if the tall trees wither first, the sons will surely become orphans.'
    • 此其騐也 - Means 'this is the proof of it.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 大定 - The reign title of Emperor Shizong of Jin.
    • 怀州 - A prefecture in the Jin Dynasty.
    • 永徽 - The reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang.
    • 罗刹 - A demon or evil spirit in Chinese folklore.
    • 阴阳书 - A book of divination or fortune-telling.
    • 乔木 - Tall trees, often symbolizing longevity and stability in Chinese culture.
    • 众子必孤 - A belief that if a tall tree withers first, it is a bad omen for the family, suggesting the loss of a patriarch or the orphaning of children.

    Proper Nouns

    • 大定 - The reign title of Emperor Shizong of Jin.
    • 怀州 - A prefecture in the Jin Dynasty.
    • 永徽 - The reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang.
    • 张鷟 - A person who built a stable.
    • 柳崇 - A military official from Yue Prefecture.
    • 罗刹 - A demon or evil spirit in Chinese folklore.
    • 阴阳书 - A book of divination or fortune-telling.
    • 乔木 - Tall trees, often symbolizing longevity and stability in Chinese culture.

    During the Dading era, in the village of Zhengjia in Chishui Store, a young man around twenty years old was resting on the post road when he saw a beautiful woman dressed in green, walking alone. He asked her if she wanted to go to Zheng County and waited for her three maids to arrive. The young man agreed to stay at the village for the night, arranging her in the guardhouse and providing food and drink. They slept together, but the next morning, the gate remained closed for a long time. When he called out, there was no response. Looking through the window, he saw only a skull and bones, with nothing else eaten. The family broke down the door to enter and found a large bird flying out from a dark corner of the beam. Some said it was a demon or evil spirit.

    During the day, the governor of Huai Prefecture, Liang Zaiyan, was sitting in his office when suddenly an object like a bat flew in from the south and entered his mouth, seeming to swallow something. He suffered from severe abdominal pain for several days and died.

    A man from Shou'an, whose name is unknown, could play a board with his elbow, blow a flute with his nose, and sing with his mouth. He could smile on one side and cry on the other. A black dog could understand human speech and respond, doing what a person would do.

    In Yue Prefecture, a military official named Liu Chong had a sudden growth on his head, causing him great pain. He summoned a fortune-teller to observe it at night, who saw a woman wearing a green skirt. When asked, she did not respond. The fortune-teller advised him to quickly remove it from his window. Liu Chong visited the window and saw a ceramic prostitute, extremely beautiful and adorned with green porcelain. He pounded it into pieces in an iron mortar and burned it, and the wound then healed.

    During the Yonghui era, Zhang Zhuo built a stable and dug a pit more than ten feet deep in the north. According to the book of divination, if the son's land is dug, someone will fall into a well and die. Zhang Zhuo had a slave named Yongjin who was crushed to death while dredging the well. In his old house, there was a mulberry tree about forty to fifty feet tall, which died without reason. Soon after, his grandfather died. A diviner said that if the tall trees wither first, the sons will surely become orphans. This was the proof of it. [128]

    徐敬业举兵有大星蓬蓬如筐笼经三宿而失俄而敬业败
    　　司刑卿杜景佺授幷州长史驰驿赴任其夜有大星如斗落于庭前至地而没佺至并州祁县界而卒羣官迎祭回所上食为祭盘
    　　将军黒齿常之镇河源军城极严峻有三口狼入营绕官舎不知从何而至军士射杀黒齿忌之移之外奏讨三曲党项奉敕许遂差将军李谨行充替谨行到军旬日病卒
    　　天官侍郎顾琮新得三品有子壻来谒时大门造成琮乗马至门鼓鼻踣地不进鞭之跳跃而入从骑亦如之有顷门无故自倒琮不悦遂病郎中员外已下来问疾琮云未合入三品为诸公成就至此自知不起矣旬中而薨
    　　张易之初造一大堂甚壮丽计用数百万红粉泥壁文栢帖柱琉璃沈香为饰夜有书其壁曰能得防时令削去明日复书之前后六七易之乃题其下曰一月即足自是不复更书经半年易之借没入官 [129]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [129]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 举兵 - Means 'to raise arms' or 'to launch a rebellion,' referring to military action.
    • 大星蓬蓬如筐笼 - A vivid description of a large, puffy star resembling a basket or cage, likely a metaphor for something significant or ominous.
    • 三宿而失 - Means 'after three nights, it disappeared,' indicating a temporary phenomenon.
    • 俄而敬业败 - '俄而' means 'soon after,' and '敬业败' refers to the defeat of Xu Jingye.
    • 驰驿赴任 - Means 'to rush to take office by special courier,' indicating urgency.
    • 大星如斗落于庭前 - Describes a large star falling like a ladle in front of the courtyard, a celestial omen.
    • 黑齿常之镇河源军 - '镇' means 'to garrison' or 'to station,' referring to military presence.
    • 三口狼 - Refers to three wolves, possibly symbolizing danger or chaos.
    • 不知从何而至 - Means 'no one knows where they came from,' indicating mystery or sudden appearance.
    • 军士射杀 - Means 'the soldiers shot and killed,' describing military action.
    • 忌之 - Means 'to fear' or 'to be wary of,' indicating suspicion or anxiety.
    • 移之外奏讨三曲党项 - '移之外' means 'to transfer outside,' and '奏讨' means 'to report and request,' referring to military operations.
    • 奉敕许遂差将军李谨行充替 - '奉敕' means 'by imperial decree,' and '充替' means 'to replace,' indicating official orders.
    • 将军李谨行到军旬日病卒 - '到军' means 'arrived at the army,' and '病卒' means 'died of illness,' describing a short-lived military leader.
    • 天官侍郎顾琮新得三品 - '天官侍郎' refers to a high-ranking official, and '三品' refers to a third-rank official, indicating a promotion.
    • 子壻来谒 - '子壻' means 'son-in-law,' and '来谒' means 'came to pay respects,' describing a formal visit.
    • 大门造成 - Means 'the main gate was completed,' indicating construction.
    • 鼓鼻踣地不进 - Describes a horse shaking its nose and refusing to move forward, indicating resistance or stubbornness.
    • 鞭之跳跃而入 - Means 'whipped it, and it jumped in,' describing the horse's reaction.
    • 从骑亦如之 - Means 'the accompanying horses did the same,' indicating a similar reaction.
    • 门无故自倒 - Means 'the gate fell down for no reason,' indicating an inexplicable event.
    • 不悦遂病 - Means 'became unhappy and then fell ill,' describing a negative emotional and physical reaction.
    • 郎中员外已下来问疾 - '郎中员外' refers to junior officials, and '问疾' means 'to inquire about illness,' describing a visit to console.
    • 未合入三品 - Means 'not suitable for a third-rank official,' indicating a mismatch between position and ability.
    • 自知不起矣 - Means 'knew he would not recover,' indicating a fatal prognosis.
    • 旬中而薨 - Means 'died within ten days,' describing a rapid decline.
    • 张易之初造一大堂 - '初造' means 'first built,' and '大堂' means 'large hall,' describing a new construction.
    • 甚壮丽 - Means 'very magnificent,' describing the hall's appearance.
    • 计用数百万 - Means 'cost several million,' indicating the high expense.
    • 红粉泥壁文栢帖柱 - Describes the decoration of the hall with red powder on the walls and inscriptions on the pillars.
    • 琉璃沈香为饰 - Means 'decorated with lapis lazuli and sandalwood,' describing the materials used.
    • 夜有鬼书其壁 - Means 'at night, a ghost wrote on the wall,' describing a supernatural event.
    • 能得防时令削去 - Means 'if you can prevent it, have it removed,' indicating a suggestion.
    • 明日复书之前后六七 - Means 'the next day, it was written again, six or seven times,' describing repeated occurrences.
    • 题其下曰一月即足 - Means 'wrote below it, saying 'one month is enough,' indicating a deadline.
    • 自是不复更书 - Means 'from then on, it did not write again,' describing a cessation.
    • 经半年易之借没入官 - '经半年' means 'after half a year,' and '借没入官' means 'was seized by the government,' describing a legal action.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 徐敬业 - A historical figure who led a rebellion against the Tang dynasty.
    • 杜景佺 - A high-ranking official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 幷州长史 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the state of Bingzhou.
    • 黑齿常之 - A military commander during the Tang dynasty.
    • 河源军 - Refers to a military garrison in the region of He Yuan.
    • 三曲党项 - Refers to a tribal group in the region of San Qu.
    • 顾琮 - A high-ranking official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 三品 - Refers to a third-rank official in the Tang dynasty's bureaucratic system.
    • 张易之 - A prominent official during the Tang dynasty, known for his influence and wealth.
    • 三曲 - Refers to a specific region or place name.

    Proper Nouns

    • 徐敬业 - A historical figure who led a rebellion against the Tang dynasty.
    • 杜景佺 - A high-ranking official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 幷州长史 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the state of Bingzhou.
    • 黑齿常之 - A military commander during the Tang dynasty.
    • 河源军 - Refers to a military garrison in the region of He Yuan.
    • 三曲党项 - Refers to a tribal group in the region of San Qu.
    • 顾琮 - A high-ranking official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 三品 - Refers to a third-rank official in the Tang dynasty's bureaucratic system.
    • 张易之 - A prominent official during the Tang dynasty, known for his influence and wealth.
    • 三曲 - Refers to a specific region or place name.

    Meng Jiyue raised arms, and a large star appeared, puffy like a basket or cage, lasting for three nights before disappearing. Soon after, Meng Jiyue was defeated.

    The Grand Minister of Justice, Du Jingquan, was appointed as the Governor of Bingzhou and rushed to take office by special courier. That night, a large star fell like a ladle in front of the courtyard, and when it reached the ground, it disappeared. Du Jingquan arrived at the border of Qixian in Bingzhou and died shortly thereafter. The officials gathered to offer sacrifices, and the food presented was placed on a sacrificial tray.

    General Hechi Changzhi garrisoned the He Yuan military camp, which was extremely strict. Three wolves entered the camp, circling around the government quarters, with no one knowing where they came from. The soldiers shot and killed them. Hechi Changzhi feared them and transferred outside, reporting and requesting to attack the San Qu Xiang tribe. By imperial decree, it was allowed, and General Li Jinhang was appointed to replace him. Li Jinhang arrived at the army and died of illness after ten days.

    The Attendant of the Celestial Officials, Gu Cong, newly promoted to the third rank, had his son-in-law come to pay respects. At the time, the main gate was completed. Gu Cong rode his horse to the gate, where the horse shook its nose and refused to move forward. He whipped it, and it jumped in, and the accompanying horses did the same. Soon, the gate fell down for no reason. Gu Cong became unhappy and then fell ill. Junior officials came to inquire about his illness. Gu Cong said, "I am not suitable for a third-rank official; the public has achieved this for me. I know I will not recover." He died within ten days.

    Zhang Yizhi first built a large hall, very magnificent, costing several million. It was decorated with red powder on the walls and inscriptions on the pillars, adorned with lapis lazuli and sandalwood. At night, a ghost wrote on the wall, saying, "If you can prevent it, have it removed." The next day, it was written again, six or seven times. Zhang Yizhi then wrote below it, saying, "One month is enough." From then on, it did not write again. After half a year, Zhang Yizhi was seized by the government. [129]

    崔晖初封博陵王身为益府长史受封令所司造辂初成有大风吹其葢倾折识者以为不祥无何弟晕为云阳令部人杀之雍州衙内暐三从以上长流岭南斯亦咎征之先见也
    　　瀛州饶阳人宋善威曾任一县尉尝昼坐忽然取鞋衫笏走出门迎接拜伏引入诸人不见但闻语声威命酒馔乐饮仍作诗曰月落三株树日映九重天良夜欢宴罢蹔别庚申年后威果至庚申年卒
    　　开元三年有熊昼日入广府城内经都督门前过军人逐十余里射杀之后月余都督李处鉴死自后长史朱贤思被告反禁身半年才出即卒司马宋草宾长史窦崇嘉相继而卒
    　　开元四年尚书考巧院防前一双桐树忽然枯死旬日考功员外郎邵某卒寻而麹先冲为郎中判邵旧案月余西边树又枯死省中忧之未防而先冲又卒
    　　源乾曜为宰相移政事牀时姚元崇归休及假满来见牀移忿之曜惧下拜宗闻之而停曜宰相讳移牀移则改动曜停后元崇亦罢此其騐也
    　　梁简文之生志公谓武帝曰此子与寃家同年生其年侯景生于鴈门乱梁诛萧氏略尽 [130]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [130]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 初封博陵王 - Formal title-granting phrase, initially granted the title of Prince of Boling.
    • 身为益府长史 - Served as the Chief Secretary of Yizhou, a high-ranking administrative position.
    • 受封令所司造辂 - Decree for the creation of the ceremonial carriage was issued by the relevant officials, '辂' refers to a ceremonial carriage.
    • 初成有大风吹其盖倾折 - When it was first completed, a strong wind blew and the canopy was overturned, a literal description of an event.
    • 识者以为不祥 - Those who understood it considered it an omen of misfortune, '识者' refers to those with insight or knowledge.
    • 无何弟晕为云阳令 - Soon after, his younger brother Yun became the magistrate of Yunyang, '无何' means 'soon after' or 'shortly thereafter'.
    • 部人杀之 - The local people killed him, a straightforward description of an event.
    • 雍州衙内暐三从以上长流岭南 - Officials within the雍zhou administration, including Wei and others, were exiled to the south of the Five Ridges, '三从以上' refers to a group of officials, and '长流岭南' means 'exiled to the south of the Five Ridges'.
    • 斯亦咎征之先见也 - This was also a premonition of impending calamity, '咎征' refers to signs of impending disaster.
    • 瀛州饶阳人宋善威曾任一县尉 - Song Shanyi, a native of Raoyang in Yinzhou, once served as a county magistrate, '县尉' is a local administrative position.
    • 尝昼坐忽然取鞋衫笏走出门迎接拜伏 - He once sat during the day when suddenly he took his shoes, clothes, and tablet and went out to greet and bow, a literal description of an event.
    • 引入诸人不见但闻语声 - He led them in, but the others did not see him; they only heard his voice, a surreal description.
    • 威命酒馔乐饮仍作诗 - Shanyi ordered wine and food, enjoyed himself, and composed a poem, a literal description of an event.
    • 月落三株树日映九重天 - The moon set on three trees, the sun shone on nine layers of heaven, a poetic description.
    • 良夜欢宴罢蹔别庚申年 - After a good night of feasting, they temporarily parted ways on the year of Gengshen, '庚申' is a specific year in the Chinese zodiac.
    • 果至庚申年卒 - He indeed died in the year of Gengshen, a literal description of an event.
    • 开元三年有熊昼日入广府城内 - In the third year of Kaiyuan, a bear entered the Guangzhou city during the day, a literal description of an event.
    • 经都督门前过军人逐十余里射杀之 - It passed by the governor's gate, and the soldiers chased it for more than ten miles and shot it dead, a literal description of an event.
    • 月余都督李处鉴死 - A month later, Governor Li Chujian died, a literal description of an event.
    • 自后长史朱贤思被告反禁身半年才出即卒 - Afterwards, the Chief Secretary Zhu Xiansi was accused of rebellion, was imprisoned for half a year, and then died upon release, a literal description of an event.
    • 司马宋草宾长史窦崇嘉相继而卒 - The Deputy Governor Song Caobin and the Chief Secretary Dou Chongjia died one after another, a literal description of an event.
    • 开元四年尚书考巧院防前一双桐树忽然枯死 - In the fourth year of Kaiyuan, two camphor trees in front of the Imperial Examination Office in the Ministry of Rites suddenly withered and died, a literal description of an event.
    • 旬日考功员外郎邵某卒 - After ten days, the Deputy Minister of Rites, Shao, died, a literal description of an event.
    • 寻而麹先冲为郎中判邵旧案 - Soon, Qu Xianchong became a Langzhong and reviewed Shao's old cases, a literal description of an event.
    • 月余西边树又枯死 - After a month, the tree on the west side also withered and died, a literal description of an event.
    • 省中忧之未防而先冲又卒 - The officials in the Ministry were worried, but before they could act, Xianchong also died, a literal description of an event.
    • 源乾曜为宰相移政事牀 - Yuan Qianyao, as Chancellor, moved the political desk, a literal description of an event.
    • 时姚元崇归休及假满来见牀移忿之 - At that time, Yao Yuanchong was on leave and returned after his leave was over. He saw the desk moved and was angry, a literal description of an event.
    • 曜惧下拜 - Qianyao was afraid and bowed, a literal description of an event.
    • 玄宗闻之而停曜宰相 - Emperor Xuanzong heard about it and stopped Qianyao from being Chancellor, a literal description of an event.
    • 讳移牀移则改动曜停后元崇亦罢 - He forbade moving the desk; if it was moved, it would be considered a change. After Qianyao was stopped, Yuanchong was also dismissed, a literal description of an event.
    • 此其騐也 - This is the proof, a concluding statement.
    • 梁简文之生志公谓武帝曰 - When Liang Jianwen was born, Zhi Gong said to Emperor Wu, a literal description of an event.
    • 此子与冤家同年生 - This child and the enemy were born in the same year, a literal description of an event.
    • 其年侯景生于雁门 - In that year, Hou Jing was born in Yanmen, a literal description of an event.
    • 乱梁诛萧氏略尽 - He caused chaos in Liang and almost exterminated the Xiao family, a literal description of an event.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 博陵王 - A title granted to a prince, indicating a high-ranking noble.
    • 益府长史 - A high-ranking administrative position in the Yizhou region.
    • 受封令所司造辂 - A ceremonial carriage created for a newly granted title.
    • 识者以为不祥 - Those with insight or knowledge considered it an omen of misfortune.
    • 云阳令 - A local administrative position in Yunyang.
    • 部人杀之 - The local people killed him, indicating a violent event.
    • 雍州衙内暐三从以上长流岭南 - Officials within the雍zhou administration were exiled to the south of the Five Ridges.
    • 斯亦咎征之先见也 - This was also a premonition of impending calamity.
    • 瀛州饶阳人宋善威曾任一县尉 - Song Shanyi, a native of Raoyang in Yinzhou, once served as a county magistrate.
    • 尝昼坐忽然取鞋衫笏走出门迎接拜伏 - A surreal description of an event.
    • 引入诸人不见但闻语声 - A surreal description of an event.
    • 威命酒馔乐饮仍作诗 - A literal description of an event.
    • 月落三株树日映九重天 - A poetic description.
    • 良夜欢宴罢蹔别庚申年 - A literal description of an event.
    • 果至庚申年卒 - A literal description of an event.
    • 开元三年有熊昼日入广府城内 - A literal description of an event.
    • 经都督门前过军人逐十余里射杀之 - A literal description of an event.
    • 月余都督李处鉴死 - A literal description of an event.
    • 自后长史朱贤思被告反禁身半年才出即卒 - A literal description of an event.
    • 司马宋草宾长史窦崇嘉相继而卒 - A literal description of an event.
    • 开元四年尚书考巧院防前一双桐树忽然枯死 - A literal description of an event.
    • 旬日考功员外郎邵某卒 - A literal description of an event.
    • 寻而麹先冲为郎中判邵旧案 - A literal description of an event.
    • 月余西边树又枯死 - A literal description of an event.
    • 省中忧之未防而先冲又卒 - A literal description of an event.
    • 源乾曜为宰相移政事牀 - A literal description of an event.
    • 时姚元崇归休及假满来见牀移忿之 - A literal description of an event.
    • 曜惧下拜 - A literal description of an event.
    • 玄宗闻之而停曜宰相 - A literal description of an event.
    • 讳移牀移则改动曜停后元崇亦罢 - A literal description of an event.
    • 此其騐也 - A concluding statement.
    • 梁简文之生志公谓武帝曰 - A literal description of an event.
    • 此子与冤家同年生 - A literal description of an event.
    • 其年侯景生于雁门 - A literal description of an event.
    • 乱梁诛萧氏略尽 - A literal description of an event.

    Cui Xuanhui was initially granted the title of Prince of Boling and served as the Chief Secretary of Yizhou. When the decree for the creation of the ceremonial carriage was issued, it was completed, but a strong wind blew and the canopy was overturned. Those with insight considered it an omen of misfortune. Soon after, his younger brother Yun became the magistrate of Yunyang, but the local people killed him. The officials within the雍zhou administration, including Wei and others, were exiled to the south of the Five Ridges. This was also a premonition of impending calamity.

    Song Shanyi, a native of Raoyang in Yinzhou, once served as a county magistrate. He once sat during the day when suddenly he took his shoes, clothes, and tablet and went out to greet and bow. He led them in, but the others did not see him; they only heard his voice. Shanyi ordered wine and food, enjoyed himself, and composed a poem: "The moon set on three trees, the sun shone on nine layers of heaven. After a good night of feasting, they temporarily parted ways on the year of Gengshen." He indeed died in the year of Gengshen.

    In the third year of Kaiyuan, a bear entered the Guangzhou city during the day. It passed by the governor's gate, and the soldiers chased it for more than ten miles and shot it dead. A month later, Governor Li Chujian died. Afterwards, the Chief Secretary Zhu Xiansi was accused of rebellion, was imprisoned for half a year, and then died upon release. The Deputy Governor Song Caobin and the Chief Secretary Dou Chongjia died one after another.

    In the fourth year of Kaiyuan, two camphor trees in front of the Imperial Examination Office in the Ministry of Rites suddenly withered and died. After ten days, the Deputy Minister of Rites, Shao, died. Soon, Qu Xianchong became a Langzhong and reviewed Shao's old cases. After a month, the tree on the west side also withered and died. The officials in the Ministry were worried, but before they could act, Xianchong also died.

    Yuan Qianyao, as Chancellor, moved the political desk. At that time, Yao Yuanchong was on leave and returned after his leave was over. He saw the desk moved and was angry. Qianyao was afraid and bowed. Emperor Xuanzong heard about it and stopped Qianyao from being Chancellor. He forbade moving the desk; if it was moved, it would be considered a change. After Qianyao was stopped, Yuanchong was also dismissed. This is the proof.

    When Liang Jianwen was born, Zhi Gong said to Emperor Wu, "This child and the enemy were born in the same year. In that year, Hou Jing was born in Yanmen. He caused chaos in Liang and almost exterminated the Xiao family." [130]

    魏征为仆射有二典事之长参时征方寝二人窻下平章一人曰我等官职总由此老翁一人曰总由天上征闻之遂作一书遣由此老翁人者送至侍郎处云与此人一员好官其人不知出门心痛凭由天上者送书明日引注由老人者被放由天上者得留征怪之问焉具以实对乃叹曰官职禄料由天者葢不虚也
    　　娄师徳为杨州江都尉冯元常亦为尉共见张冏藏藏曰二君俱贵冯位不如娄冯唯取钱多即官益进娄若取一钱官即落后冯为浚仪尉多肆惨虐巡察以为彊奏授云阳尉又縁取钱事雪以为清彊监察娄见不敢取一钱位至台辅家极贫匮冯位至尚书左丞后得罪赐自尽娄至纳言卒
    　　王显与文武皇帝有严子陵之旧每掣裈为戏将帽为欢帝防时常戏曰王显抵老不作蠒及帝登极而显谒奏曰臣今日得作蠒帝笑曰未可知也召其三子皆授五品显独不及谓曰卿无贵相朕非为卿惜也曰朝贵而夕死足矣时仆射房龄曰陛下既有龙濳之旧何不试与之帝与之三品取紫袍金带赐之其夜卒 [131]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [131]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 魏征为仆射有二典事之长参时 - Hui Zheng, as the Grand Secretary, had two officials who were his attendants during his time.
    • 魏征闻之遂作一书遣由此老翁人者送至侍郎处 - Upon hearing this, Hui Zheng wrote a letter and sent it via an elderly man to the position of the Censor.
    • 官职禄料由天者葢不虚也 - The saying that one's official position and salary are determined by heaven is indeed true.
    • 娄师徳为杨州江都尉冯元常亦为尉共见张冏藏 - Lou Shide served as the magistrate of Yangzhou's Jiangdu County, while Feng Yuanchang also served as a magistrate and they both met Zhang Junzang.
    • 冯位不如娄冯唯取钱多即官益进 - Feng's rank was lower than Lou's, but he took more bribes and thus advanced more quickly.
    • 娄若取一钱官即落后 - If Lou took even one coin, his position would be lower.
    • 王显与文武皇帝有严子陵之旧 - Wang Xian had a close friendship with Emperor Wenwu, similar to the relationship between Yan Ziling and Emperor Guangwu.
    • 王显抵老不作蠒 - Wang Xian, as he grew old, did not become a silkworm.
    • 朝贵而夕死足矣 - It is enough to be honored in the morning and die in the evening.
    • 房玄龄曰陛下既有龙潜之旧何不试与之 - Fang Xuanling said, 'Your Majesty, since you have this old friendship from when you were in hiding, why not try giving him something?'
    • 取紫袍金带赐之 - He was given a purple robe and a golden belt.
    • 其夜卒 - He died that night.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 魏征 - A famous advisor and historian during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 仆射 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, often translated as Grand Secretary.
    • 典事之长参 - Attendants or subordinates of the Grand Secretary.
    • 张冏藏 - A person who met both Lou Shide and Feng Yuanchang.
    • 娄师徳 - A Tang Dynasty official known for his integrity.
    • 冯元常 - Another Tang Dynasty official, less virtuous than Lou Shide.
    • 杨州江都尉 - A position in the Tang Dynasty, referring to the magistrate of Yangzhou's Jiangdu County.
    • 文武皇帝 - Refers to Emperor Wenwu of Tang.
    • 严子陵 - A famous recluse and friend of Emperor Guangwu of Han.
    • 房玄龄 - A prominent statesman and advisor during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 龙潜 - A term used to describe a ruler in hiding or before ascending the throne.
    • 紫袍金带 - Purple robes and golden belts were symbols of high rank and honor in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 蠒 - A silkworm, often used metaphorically to describe someone who becomes prosperous.

    Proper Nouns

    • 魏征 - Hui Zheng
    • 仆射 - Grand Secretary
    • 典事之长参 - Attendants
    • 张冏藏 - Zhang Junzang
    • 娄师徳 - Lou Shide
    • 冯元常 - Feng Yuanchang
    • 杨州江都尉 - Magistrate of Yangzhou's Jiangdu County
    • 文武皇帝 - Emperor Wenwu of Tang
    • 严子陵 - Yan Ziling
    • 房玄龄 - Fang Xuanling
    • 龙潜 - Hiding or before ascending the throne
    • 紫袍金带 - Purple robe and golden belt
    • 蠒 - Silkworm

    Hui Zheng, as the Grand Secretary, had two officials who were his attendants during his time. When Hui Zheng was asleep, these two officials discussed matters by the window. One said, "Our positions are all due to this old man." The other replied, "It's all determined by heaven." Upon hearing this, Hui Zheng wrote a letter and sent it via an elderly man to the position of the Censor, saying, "Give this person a good official position." The person did not know what was happening when he left the house and felt a pain in his heart. The one who sent the letter by heaven delivered the message the next day. The elderly man was dismissed, while the one who sent the letter by heaven was retained. Hui Zheng was surprised and asked about it. They replied with the truth. Hui Zheng then sighed and said, "The saying that one's official position and salary are determined by heaven is indeed true."

    Lou Shide served as the magistrate of Yangzhou's Jiangdu County, while Feng Yuanchang also served as a magistrate and they both met Zhang Junzang. Zhang said, "Both of you will be honored, but Feng's rank is lower than Lou's." Feng replied, "I take more bribes, so I advance more quickly." Lou said, "If I take even one coin, my position will be lower." Feng became the magistrate of Junyi and was known for his cruelty and harshness. The inspector believed he was strong and promoted him to the position of Yunyang magistrate. Later, due to his taking bribes, he was cleared and became a clear and strong inspector. Lou, on the other hand, did not dare to take even one coin and eventually reached the position of a high-ranking official, but his family was extremely poor. Feng, however, rose to the position of Left Censor of the Shangshu Province and was later disgraced and ordered to commit suicide. Lou became a Censor-in-Chief and died.

    Wang Xian had a close friendship with Emperor Wenwu of Tang, similar to the relationship between Yan Ziling and Emperor Guangwu of Han. They often played games by pulling each other's trousers and wearing each other's hats. The emperor often joked, "Wang Xian, as you grow old, you won't become a silkworm." When the emperor ascended the throne, Wang Xian came to court and said, "Your Majesty, I can now become a silkworm." The emperor laughed and said, "We'll see." He summoned Wang Xian's three sons and granted them the rank of Fifth Grade. Wang Xian was not included and said, "Your Majesty, I do not have the appearance of a nobleman. I am not being spared, but I am content to be honored in the morning and die in the evening." At that time, the Grand Secretary Fang Xuanling said to the emperor, "Your Majesty, since you have this old friendship from when you were in hiding, why not try giving him something?" The emperor gave Wang Xian a purple robe and a golden belt. That night, Wang Xian died. [131]

    太宗极康豫太史令淳风见上流泪无言上问之对曰陛下夕当晏驾太宗曰人生有命亦何忧也留淳风宿太宗至夜半奄然入定见一人云陛下蹔合来还即去也帝问君是何人对曰臣是生人判防事太宗入见防官问六月四日事即令还向见者又迎送引导出淳风即观象不许哭泣须防乃寤至曙求昨所见者令所司与一官送注蜀道一丞上怪问之选司奏奉进止与此官上亦不记旁人悉闻方知官皆由天也
    　　王无防好博戏善鹰鹞文武圣皇帝微时与无防蒱戏争彩有李阳之宿憾焉帝登极防藏匿不出帝令给使将一鹞子于市卖之索钱二十千防不知也酧钱十八贯给使以闻帝曰必王无防也遂召至惶惧请罪帝笑赏之令于春明门待诸州麻车三日并与之防坐三日属灞桥破唯得麻三车更无所有帝知其薄命更不复赏频请五品帝曰非不与卿惜卿不胜也固请乃许之其夜遂卒

    　　朝野佥载卷六 [132]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [132]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 太宗極康豫 - Describes Emperor Taizong's state of health, implying he was in good health.
    • 太史令淳风见上流泪无言 - A classical expression where the historian sighs without speaking, indicating deep emotion.
    • 陛下夕当晏驾 - A euphemistic way of saying 'the emperor will die tonight.'
    • 人生有命亦何忧也 - A philosophical statement suggesting that life is predetermined and thus there is no need to worry.
    • 留淳风宿 - Indicates that Emperor Taizong kept the historian overnight.
    • 至夜半奄然入定 - Describes the emperor's sudden death during the night.
    • 陛下蹔合来还即去也 - A ghostly message suggesting that the emperor will return briefly but then leave.
    • 生人判防事 - Refers to a ghostly official in charge of judgment.
    • 六月四日事 - Likely refers to a specific historical event, possibly related to Emperor Taizong's reign.
    • 文武圣皇帝微时与无防蒱戏争彩 - Describes a pastime involving gambling, with '微时' indicating a time when Emperor Taizong was not yet an emperor.
    • 李阳之宿憾焉 - Refers to a long-standing grudge or resentment.
    • 登极防藏匿不出 - Describes someone hiding after Emperor Taizong ascended the throne.
    • 索钱二十千 - Indicates a request for a large sum of money.
    • 酧钱十八贯 - Describes the amount of money given in return.
    • 春明门待诸州麻车三日 - Describes a period of waiting at the Chuning Gate for the arrival of state vehicles.
    • 属灞桥破唯得麻三车更无所有 - Indicates that only three carts of hemp were obtained due to the destruction of the Ba Bridge.
    • 官皆由天也 - Suggests that one's fate is determined by heaven.
    • 朝野佥载卷六 - The title of the text, indicating it is part of a collection of anecdotes and stories.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang, one of the most famous emperors of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 太史令淳风 - Li Chunfeng, a renowned astronomer and historian of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 晏驾 - A euphemism for the death of an emperor.
    • 生人判防事 - A ghostly official in charge of judgment, reflecting traditional beliefs in the afterlife.
    • 六月四日事 - Could refer to a significant event, such as a military campaign or a political crisis.
    • 文武圣皇帝 - A title given to Emperor Taizong, emphasizing his wisdom and military prowess.
    • 无防 - A person mentioned in the text, possibly a friend or associate of Emperor Taizong.
    • 李阳 - A person with a long-standing grudge against Emperor Taizong.
    • 登极 - The act of ascending the throne.
    • 春明门 - A gate in the capital city, often used as a reference point in historical texts.
    • 灞桥 - A famous bridge in the capital city, known for its historical significance.
    • 朝野佥载 - A collection of historical anecdotes and stories from the Tang Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 太史令淳风 - Li Chunfeng.
    • 文武圣皇帝 - A title for Emperor Taizong.
    • 无防 - A person mentioned in the text.
    • 李阳 - A person with a long-standing grudge.
    • 春明门 - A gate in the capital city.
    • 灞桥 - A famous bridge in the capital city.
    • 朝野佥载 - A collection of historical anecdotes and stories.

    Emperor Taizong was in good health when the Grand Historian Li Chunfeng saw him and wept without speaking. The emperor asked him why, and Li replied that the emperor would die that night. Emperor Taizong said, "Life is predetermined; what is there to worry about?" He then kept Li Chunfeng overnight. In the middle of the night, Emperor Taizong suddenly passed away. He saw a person who said, "Your Majesty, you will return briefly, but then you must leave." The emperor asked, "Who are you?" The person replied, "I am the official in charge of judgment for the living." Emperor Taizong entered and saw the official, who asked about the events of June 4th. The emperor immediately ordered the person to return, and the official welcomed, escorted, and guided him out. Li Chunfeng then observed the celestial phenomena, forbidding anyone to cry until he woke up at dawn. He sought the person he had seen the previous night and ordered the officials to give him an official position, sending him to serve as a county magistrate on the Sichuan road. The emperor was surprised and asked the selection office why. They replied that it was according to the emperor's orders. The emperor did not remember it, but everyone else heard about it, realizing that official positions are determined by heaven.

    Wang Wufang was fond of gambling and skilled at falconry. When Emperor Taizong was still a commoner, he played games with Wang Wufang and argued over the stakes, harboring a long-standing grudge with Li Yang. After Emperor Taizong ascended the throne, Wang Wufang hid and did not come out. The emperor ordered a servant to take a falcon to the market and sell it for twenty thousand coins. Wang Wufang did not know about this and paid eighteen thousand coins. The servant reported this to the emperor, who said, "It must be Wang Wufang." He then summoned Wang Wufang, who was terrified and begged for forgiveness. The emperor laughed and praised him, ordering him to wait at the Chuning Gate for three days for the state carts from all the states. Wang Wufang sat there for three days, but the Ba Bridge was destroyed, and only three carts of hemp were obtained. There was nothing else. The emperor knew that Wang Wufang had a short life and no longer gave him any rewards. Wang Wufang repeatedly asked for a fifth-rank official position, but the emperor said, "I am not unwilling to give it to you, but I am concerned that you cannot handle it." Wang Wufang insisted, and the emperor finally agreed. That night, Wang Wufang died.

    From the Chao Ye Qian Zai (Records of the World), Volume 6. [132]

    太宗极康豫太史令淳风见上流泪无言上问之对曰陛下夕当晏驾太宗曰人生有命亦何忧也留淳风宿太宗至夜半奄然入定见一人云陛下蹔合来还即去也帝问君是何人对曰臣是生人判防事太宗入见防官问六月四日事即令还向见者又迎送引导出淳风即观象不许哭泣须防乃寤至曙求昨所见者令所司与一官送注蜀道一丞上怪问之选司奏奉进止与此官上亦不记旁人悉闻方知官皆由天也
    　　王无防好博戏善鹰鹞文武圣皇帝微时与无防蒱戏争彩有李阳之宿憾焉帝登极防藏匿不出帝令给使将一鹞子于市卖之索钱二十千防不知也酧钱十八贯给使以闻帝曰必王无防也遂召至惶惧请罪帝笑赏之令于春明门待诸州麻车三日并与之防坐三日属灞桥破唯得麻三车更无所有帝知其薄命更不复赏频请五品帝曰非不与卿惜卿不胜也固请乃许之其夜遂卒

    　　朝野佥载卷六

    Emperor Taizong was in good health when the Grand Historian Li Chunfeng saw him and wept without speaking. The emperor asked him why, and Li replied that the emperor would die that night. Emperor Taizong said, "Life is predetermined; what is there to worry about?" He then kept Li Chunfeng overnight. In the middle of the night, Emperor Taizong suddenly passed away. He saw a person who said, "Your Majesty, you will return briefly, but then you must leave." The emperor asked, "Who are you?" The person replied, "I am the official in charge of judgment for the living." Emperor Taizong entered and saw the official, who asked about the events of June 4th. The emperor immediately ordered the person to return, and the official welcomed, escorted, and guided him out. Li Chunfeng then observed the celestial phenomena, forbidding anyone to cry until he woke up at dawn. He sought the person he had seen the previous night and ordered the officials to give him an official position, sending him to serve as a county magistrate on the Sichuan road. The emperor was surprised and asked the selection office why. They replied that it was according to the emperor's orders. The emperor did not remember it, but everyone else heard about it, realizing that official positions are determined by heaven.

    Wang Wufang was fond of gambling and skilled at falconry. When Emperor Taizong was still a commoner, he played games with Wang Wufang and argued over the stakes, harboring a long-standing grudge with Li Yang. After Emperor Taizong ascended the throne, Wang Wufang hid and did not come out. The emperor ordered a servant to take a falcon to the market and sell it for twenty thousand coins. Wang Wufang did not know about this and paid eighteen thousand coins. The servant reported this to the emperor, who said, "It must be Wang Wufang." He then summoned Wang Wufang, who was terrified and begged for forgiveness. The emperor laughed and praised him, ordering him to wait at the Chuning Gate for three days for the state carts from all the states. Wang Wufang sat there for three days, but the Ba Bridge was destroyed, and only three carts of hemp were obtained. There was nothing else. The emperor knew that Wang Wufang had a short life and no longer gave him any rewards. Wang Wufang repeatedly asked for a fifth-rank official position, but the emperor said, "I am not unwilling to give it to you, but I am concerned that you cannot handle it." Wang Wufang insisted, and the emperor finally agreed. That night, Wang Wufang died.

    From the Chao Ye Qian Zai (Records of the World), Volume 6.
